item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Ancient Tradeoff Behind Being Intelligence – David Reich
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个被我们误以为永恒的价值观，其实只有几代人那么年轻

最反直觉的观察来自Reich的一个时间错位：当《旧约》被写下时，恰恰是人类历史上对"智力"自然选择压力最强的时期之一——然而这部文本通篇不歌颂智力，它歌颂力量、勇气、虔诚。 这是一个深刻的悖论。我们今天把"聪明"当作不言自明的最高德性，但写下人类最古老经典的那些社会，明明正活在智力被高度筛选的环境里，却选择不去赞美它。

这暗示了一件令人不安的事：一个性状被自然选择，和一个文化去崇拜它，是两件完全脱钩的事。 我们对智力的崇拜不是因为我们"终于看清了真相"，而是一种极其晚近的、可能不到两百年的文化突变——它和大规模识字、考试制度、学历筛选同时诞生。换句话说，IQ崇拜不是人类的觉醒，而是工业社会的一个局部时尚。我们误把自己房间里的墙纸当成了宇宙的颜色。

"鱼与哺乳动物"——藏在生育策略里的同一个开关

Reich把人类家庭决策和鱼类产卵放进同一个框架，这个跨领域连接值得停下来品味。生态学里有个经典的 r/K 选择谱系：鱼把成千上万颗卵撒进河里、几乎不投资（r策略）；哺乳动物一胎少子、重度投资（K策略）。Reich的大胆之处在于——他说冰岛过去一百年的"反智力基因选择"，本质上就是这个开关在人类身上被拨动了一下。

这里有个让人意外的推论：那个被我们浪漫化为"少生优生、精心培养"的现代中产育儿哲学，在演化的账本上并不比"多生少养"更高级，它只是 K 端的一种下注。而所谓"智力相关基因被选择掉了"，并不意味着人类在变笨——意味着环境（20世纪富足的冰岛）暂时奖励了 r 端策略。潮水会回来。 同一个基因组合，在资源紧缺时是劣势，在资源过剩时也可能是劣势，只是劣在不同的地方。这不是退化，是钟摆。

精神疾病可能是"创造力"的体检报告——以及它对AI的隐喻

最具颠覆性的一段是Reich对精神分裂和躁郁症的重构：这些"疾病"也许不是纯粹的故障，而是某个性状光谱的临床读数——焦虑、想象力过剩、神经质，在萨满传统、宗教异象、艺术创造被珍视的社会里，曾经是亚临床的优势。我们今天叫它"病"，是因为现代环境恰好惩罚这个谱系的那一端。

这里我想做一个视频之外的连接：这与当代关于人工智能的争论结构惊人地一致。 我们正在拼命优化大模型的"幻觉"——把模型偏离事实的倾向当作纯粹的缺陷来消除。但Reich的逻辑提醒我们："幻觉"和"创造力"很可能是同一个谱系的两端，一个能编造不存在引文的系统，和一个能写出前所未见隐喻的系统，底层是同一种"愿意偏离训练分布"的倾向。把谱系的一端压到零，另一端往往一起塌掉。

Reich真正的核心命题，因此可以提炼成一句他自己没明说的话：凡是被进化长期保留下来的复杂性状，几乎都不是"越多越好"，而是一根两端都有奖赏的杠杆。 任何只朝一个方向优化的人——无论是优化孩子的IQ、优化基因、还是优化一个神经网络——都在无意中砍掉了自己看不见的那一半价值。多样性不是冗余，它是把未来的不确定性提前储存起来的方式。

📝 转录文本

智力，真的在所有时代都是好东西吗？

And so you might wonder isn't intelligence good — you know, in all contexts and places of time? And I think that there's a number of ways to think about that.

First of all, I think we are speaking from the point of view of a society which intensely values this particular trait — you know, ability to score well on IQ tests or things like them, or to go to school for a long time, or whatever it is. And I think this is unprecedented in human history that we live in a time like this.

It's like, if you look at the Hebrew and Christian Bible, and you look at how much intelligence is valued, it's basically not at all.

But that — when the Bible is being written, especially the Old Testament — is exactly when selection for intelligence is the highest point it's apparently ever been.

Yeah. Exactly. But there, it's about strength, or courage, or religiosity. Right? Those are the values, right? Or if you read Homer, or the other texts of other religions, it's not intelligence. It's beauty. It's other things.

And so this value system which has a hyperfocus on smarts is not obviously a trait value that's been common in the past. You might think that in certain communities there might be valuation of things that are more approximate to years of schooling, but really, broadly, it's not been a high value in the population.

IQ 测验的智力，和"在新世界活下来"的智力

But obviously the thing we're referring to — or the thing we care about — is not direct performance in IQ tests, especially in the past.

The thing I'm trying to understand better is intelligence more broadly. And maybe just that IQ-test intelligence is not that correlated with: here is a new world environment, and go figure out how to process food there, and make shelter, and everything else — all the things which your colleagues like Joseph Henrich talked about, like how modern people underestimate the difficulty of doing this kind of thing with a small band of people.

Anyways, maybe that's not IQ-test intelligence, and that's why we don't see that strong a selection effect on this thing. But intuitively it seems like, regardless of the value system, it just seems very valuable to have this trait maxed out.

推测一：两种投资孩子的方式

So I'm being very speculative, and let me give you two examples about what this is about in my head, how I'm thinking about this — not that I'm a particularly good authority on these things.

As I mentioned, a lot of these traits which are quite disparate are highly correlated to each other — obesity, years of schooling, walking pace, performance in IQ tests, household wealth — all these crazy traits all seem to be governed to a substantial extent by a shared combination of genetic variants.

And let's just think about what this might mean. In Iceland, in the last 100 years, there's been selection against this combination of variants. And one possible interpretation is: it's basically selection for two ways of investing in your children.

Having many kids and not investing a lot in them, or having few kids and investing more in them, right? So if you defer having kids — becoming wealthier, having more resources, and putting more into each kid — you're going to have lower fertility, you're going to have fewer kids. And that's going to result in lower fertility, but those kids might survive more and do better in society.

Alternatively, you can just have as many kids as you can and invest less in them. They might have individually less good outcomes, but in a time of plenty — which is potentially Iceland in the 20th century — it might make sense to have more kids and invest less in them.

从哺乳动物到鱼：环境决定的生育开关

And so you can imagine that actually, at different times and in different places in ecology, there are different ways. Mammals often invest a lot, with a pregnancy and a small number of children, whereas fish will spawn huge numbers of offspring into the river — the great majority of whom will be eaten. But that is an effective way to produce offspring in certain conditions.

So there'll be a toggle, depending on the environmental conditions, back and forth between investing in large numbers of offspring with less investment, or smaller numbers of offspring with more investment. And maybe we're just seeing that move back and forth over different places and times.

推测二：精神疾病作为创造力光谱的"读数"

Similarly for schizophrenia and bipolar disease — how could this ever be advantageous? But maybe what we're seeing with these diseases is a kind of readout of some kind of spectrum of traits that actually, in some context, might be advantageous.

Maybe being anxious, or being imaginative, or being neurotic, might be helpful in a shamanistic tradition — you know, in a religious tradition which values people who can have visions, or values people who can be creative. And maybe these are subclinical versions of schizophrenia or bipolar disease that, in certain times, may be advantageous and in other times may be disadvantageous.

Maybe you're just seeing selection for different types of creativity or other thinking that can be valuable in different contexts.

核心观点：复杂性状是两端都有优势的光谱

I'm waving my hands here, but my sense is that these complex traits have not pushed in one direction because there's advantage. There are spectrums where there's advantages to both ends of the spectrum, and there's multi-dimensional impacts of these different traits.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Powell Is Out, Warsh Is In: What It Means for Crypto
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、比特币最大的悖论：它是做空美联储的资产，却靠美联储的"恐慌反射"而活

整集节目里最被低估的一句话，是 Noelle 对 2023 年银行业危机的复盘。当时所有人欢呼"比特币上涨是因为人们终于看清了银行系统的腐败与脆弱"，而她冷冷地纠正：不，比特币上涨是因为人们预期央行会出手放水。

请把这句话和她后面讲的 BLIS 交易（Big, Large and Lasting Stimulus）并排放在一起看，你会得到一个令人不安的结论：比特币诞生的叙事是"对抗美联储的法币滥发"，但在真实的资金流里，比特币其实是做多美联储那只随时会按下去的恐慌按钮。它不是法币的反义词，它是法币救助机制的杠杆化衍生品。换句话说——这是本期最反直觉的观察——加密世界一边在意识形态上诅咒央行,一边在仓位上押注央行永远不会让系统真正崩溃。一个真正"去中心化、抗审查、对抗印钞"的资产，它的最佳买点恰恰是"印钞机即将启动"的时刻。比特币的信徒和美联储的救市派，本质上是同一笔交易的两端。

二、"通行费"是贯穿整集的隐藏母题——从霍尔木兹海峡到 Hyperliquid 的 90% 分成

注意一个文字上的巧合：节目里中国油轮要驶出霍尔木兹海峡，需要向伊朗交一笔 "fee / toll"（通行费）；而 Ram 评价 Hyperliquid 时也用了完全相同的词——Hyperliquid 拿走 90% 的收入，是它多年苦功"exact 的那笔 toll"。两处用的是同一个英文单词，绝非偶然。

这揭示了一个跨领域的结构真相：地缘政治的咽喉要道经济学，和加密市场的基础设施经济学，是同一套逻辑。 谁卡在"必经之路"的节点上，谁就收税。伊朗卡的是物理海峡；Hyperliquid 卡的是链上交易场所；而 Coinbase 这次最精明的动作，恰恰是把自己安插成 USDC 的"抵押品管理者"——一个新的咽喉节点。它表面上是在和竞争对手"对齐合作"，实质上是花钱买下一个未来的收费站。

更妙的跨域连接在于：沃什想要的"减少点阵图、减少前瞻指引"，以及 SEC 想要的"减少财报披露频次"，同样是一种收费站的建立。当信息从"被免费喂养"变回"稀缺品"，谁能解读信息谁就重新拥有了护城河。 节目里 Noelle 半开玩笑地问"这是不是意味着分析师终于得真正干活了"——这句玩笑底下是严肃的产业重组：过去十年央行的"信息透明化"实际上摧毁了卖方分析的价值，而沃什正在悄悄把这个行业的定价权还回去。霍尔木兹、Hyperliquid、美联储——三个看似无关的场景，都在做同一件事：在必经之路上重新设卡收费。

三、美联储真正的产品不是利率，而是"信任"——它和它鄙视的加密 KOL 卖的是同一样东西

Noelle 反复强调一句话：鲍威尔的首要工作是"管理信任"（manage trust），美联储 most of what it does is 管理信任。她甚至毫不留情地拆解了"鲍威尔人格崇拜"——那个"你愿意一起去喝棉花糖拿铁的爷爷"形象。

这里藏着一个会让人愣一下的观察：美联储的核心产品，和这期节目广告时段里那些"KOL activations"、那些靠人格魅力带货的加密影响者，是同一种东西——被精心管理的、半准社交化（parasocial）的信任。 区别只在于美联储管理的是对一个机构的信任，KOL 管理的是对一个个人的信任。鲍威尔的 FOMC 发布会，本质上是一档运营了七年的、收视极高的"信任内容节目"。这也正是为什么 Steve 顺口说"SNL 第 52 季应该演一集 FOMC 会议"会显得如此自然——FOMC 早已经是一档有固定角色、固定剧本、固定观众的连续剧了。

由此引出本集最深的一处张力：当一个机构的主要资产是"信任"，它就再也无法承认自己错了。 这正是 Noelle 解释为什么美联储不能把通胀目标从 2% 改成 3% 的原因——"三是新的二"人人心知肚明，但一旦官方承认够不到 2%，被摧毁的不是某个数字，而是机构信任本身。于是美联储被锁进了一个荒诞的处境：它必须假装追求一个所有人都知道它达不到的目标。这和加密世界的某些项目方何其相似——叙事一旦立起来，就成了不能拆的承重墙。Noelle 留下的真正问题不是"独立性好不好"，而是那个更锋利的反问：当"独立"意味着"无需对任何决策负责、可以无视传票"，独立性还是优点吗？ 一个把信任当作产品来经营的机构，最终会发现"维护信任"和"承认真相"是直接冲突的——而这，恰恰也是整个加密行业还没想明白的那道题。

📝 转录文本

Powell Is Out, Warsh Is In: What It Means for Crypto

Bits + Bips — The Interview

【冷开场片段：Coinbase 与 Hyperliquid 的"对齐"】

So I was wondering what it said to you that Coinbase is, you know, through this gesture, aligning more closely with Hyperliquid. And if that, you know, kind of move there to increase its position of stake type even shed any further light on the stablecoin deal.

Yeah, Laura, I was thinking the same thing. It feels like in many ways, Hyperliquid and Coinbase are both going after this sort of everything-exchange situation — you know, spot trading, perpetuals trading, equities, prediction markets. And it's true, like, you know, they have to acquire a significant amount of HYPE and stake it to be able to offer this, you know, AQA. And so this shows significant alignment between the two businesses, even though there's so much competition.

To me, what this shows is that Coinbase is very certain in its growth as a primarily US-based compliant institution, whereas Hyperliquid, I think they see as more of this outside-of-US-jurisdiction, no-KYC platform — which I think Coinbase can't really compete as much in. But they can leverage the usage of Hyperliquid globally to grow distribution of USDC, which I think ultimately in the long term will positively affect their bottom line.

We're at a critical point. And so you're spot on in that, for the longest time, I would always say: Econ 101, Bitcoin has all the attributes of a successful currency. But the one thing it's been missing is a clear set of legal and regulatory guidelines. So if we have clarity in the mix, that's the puzzle piece. Like, that's the last piece to unlock all of the attributes of a successful currency.

Ram, let me actually throw this ball to you on the market side of things. Does this make you more or less bullish on Coinbase, on Circle in terms of the stocks, and on the deployment of stablecoins versus banks overall?

It's a win for all of them. It's an especially big win for Hyperliquid. For Coinbase and Circle, they were able to neutralize an emerging competitor. Very strategic move to get USDC involved — and with Coinbase essentially this collateral manager, installing themselves at a very key point in this new infrastructure.

For Hyperliquid, it's a major win — like 90% of the revenue here. So that's the toll that they exact for having accomplished a tremendous amount over the last several years. We talked about Hyperliquid, I think, three or four weeks ago. This is one of the assets that you wanna own this cycle. And it was very prophylactic of Coinbase to get ahead of that, because Hyperliquid is emerging as a decentralized trading venue. Obviously, Circle generates a material amount of recurring revenue, net interest income. So it's a win for everybody, but it's especially a big win for Hyperliquid.

I agree with you on distribution, but I think the other end of the barbell is energy. He who has access to unlimited energy and effectively cheap compute probably gets a win — and I think it's pretty fair to say that Elon has an edge there. I know that we were talking about this morning, the physics, the science of getting your energy pretty much for free in space ain't easy. But if anyone can do it, it's the guy who has the — I don't want to say the M word, but he has a very, very robust capability across the globe in getting things into outer space. So I think that's Elon's unfair advantage at the very back of the stack.

【广告时段】

A 3% top-off on my entire portfolio? Uh, yes. Who is saying no to that? That's free money. I don't care what's in your portfolio. That's a yes. If you've been looking for an excuse to consolidate your assets, this is it. Until May 31st, Coinbase is giving a straight 3% Bitcoin boost on any crypto or cash deposits all month. Coinbase One is the ultimate membership to make the most of your money, which, as you know, is how I optimize my finances. Zero trading fees on thousands of crypto assets, 3.5% APY on USDC, boosted staking and lending rewards, and up to 4% Bitcoin back with the Coinbase One card. Plus, you can still claim 20% off the first year of Coinbase One annual plans, a $50 Bitcoin bonus when you spend $100 on a new Coinbase One card in the first 30 days through May 31st. Plus, your share of 5 Bitcoin if you out-predict pro basketball coach Lethal Shooter, and a chance at a private shooting session with him. It's the perfect time to centralize your assets and maximize your earnings. Get your 3% boost at Coinbase.com/unchained. Visit Coinbase.com/unchained — that's Coinbase.com/unchained — to claim your Bitcoin bonus. No purchase necessary. See rules and other ways to enter. Terms apply to other offers. Futures swaps via Coinbase Financial Markets. Risk of 100% loss. Payouts event-based. Not investment in — not available in Nevada. Coinbase One Card is offered through Coinbase Inc. and Cardless Inc. Cards issued by First Electronic Bank. Bitcoin-back rates are based on cardholders' assets on Coinbase.

Borrow against them without selling. Trade a wide range of cryptocurrencies, all in one platform. Now available in the U.S. with 30 days of exclusive privileges for new clients. Experience Wealth Club Premier. Access enhanced interest rates, reduced borrowing costs, and crypto cashback on swaps. Get started today at nexo.com/unchained.

They've worked with some of the largest and most impactful companies in the space. They're the partner you want when you're navigating markets and trying to break out from the noise. They help navigate TGEs, go-to-market, BD and partnerships, capital markets advisory, PR, media placements, KOL activations, and more — driving execution from launch to scale. Their results are measurable. To learn more and start building real traction today, visit multichainadv.com.

You can borrow against your holdings at 4% or less, which is super useful if you don't want to sell your assets. You can also earn on all major assets, up to 8% APY, just by holding. And moving money is just easy. No hidden transfer fees, no friction. It just works globally. If you want to check it out, go to ether.fi.com.

【冷开场片段（重复）：Coinbase 与 Hyperliquid】

Coinbase also said that it significantly increased its position of stake type, and that was interesting to me because I view Hyperliquid in some ways as a competitor to Coinbase. So I was wondering what it said to you that Coinbase is, you know, through this gesture, aligning more closely with Hyperliquid — and if that, you know, kind of move there to increase its position of stake type even shed any further light on the stablecoin deal?

Yeah, Laura, I was thinking the same thing. It feels like in many ways, Hyperliquid and Coinbase are both going after this sort of everything-exchange — spot trading, perpetuals trading, equities, prediction markets. And it's true, they have to acquire a significant amount of HYPE and stake it to be able to offer this, you know, AQA. And so this shows significant alignment between the two businesses, even though there's so much competition.

To me, what this shows is that Coinbase is very certain in its growth as a US-based — primarily US-based — compliant institution, whereas Hyperliquid, I think they see as more of this outside-of-US-jurisdiction, no-KYC platform, which I think Coinbase can't really compete as much in. But they can leverage the usage of Hyperliquid globally to grow distribution of USDC. But I think ultimately in the long term, will positively affect their bottom line.

We're at a critical point. And so you're spot on in that, for the longest time, I would always say: Econ 101, Bitcoin has all the attributes of a successful currency. But the one thing it's been missing is a clear set of legal and regulatory guidelines. So if we have clarity in the mix, that's the puzzle piece. Like, that's the last piece to unlock all of the attributes of a successful currency.

Ram, let me actually throw this ball to you on the market side of things. Does this make you more or less bullish on Coinbase, on Circle in terms of the stocks, and on the deployment of stablecoins versus banks overall?

It's a win for all of them. It's an especially big win for Hyperliquid. For Coinbase and Circle, they were able to neutralize an emerging competitor. Very strategic move to get USDC involved — and with Coinbase essentially this collateral manager, installing themselves at a very key point in this new infrastructure. For Hyperliquid, it's a major win — like 90% of the revenue here. So that's the toll that they exact for having accomplished a tremendous amount over the last several years. We talked about Hyperliquid, I think, three or four weeks ago. This is one of the assets that you want to own this cycle. And it was very —

【节目正式开场与嘉宾介绍】

Hi everyone. Welcome to another episode of Bits and Bips the Interview, the show where we explore crypto and macro colliding one basis point at a time. Once again, my name is Steve Ehrlich. I'm the head of research at SharpLink, and I'm also your host. And we've got a terrific show for you today.

Lots going on in the macro world. Stocks and bonds are going in entirely different directions. Crypto is getting caught in the wake. We're going to have a new Fed chair tomorrow, and much, much more.

But as always, before we begin, just a little bit of housekeeping. One: nothing that you'll hear on this show should be considered investment or financial advice. For full disclosures, please see unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips. And then second, let's take a brief pause so we can hear from some of the sponsors who make this show possible.

If you've been loving Bits and Bips, don't forget that the show is transitioning to its own feeds on X, YouTube, and your favorite podcast player. If you're not already subscribed to Bits and Bips on its own channels, go there now and hit that subscribe button so you can keep up with our twice-weekly live streams and Macro-Meets-Crypto breakdowns. Bits and Bips will only be on the Unchained feed for a few more weeks, so subscribe today to be ready for launch. You can get all the links at unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips.

All right, welcome back. So let me bring in my guest. She's someone that will be very familiar to a lot of longtime listeners: Noelle Acheson — formerly at Genesis, I believe head of research at CoinDesk, and currently the author of a very widely read and influential newsletter, Crypto Is Macro Now. So welcome, Noelle.

Noelle: Hi, Steve. So good to be here today.

Steve: How are you doing today?

Noelle: I am good. I'm recovering from being about 95 degrees Fahrenheit in Philadelphia — a little summer weather in May.

Steve: Yeah, I hear you. I gather you're going to have to get used to that.

Noelle: Yeah.

【股债背离：两个不同的鼓点】

Steve: But I think like a lot of people watching and listening, I'm also curious to try to figure out what's going on in the markets today. And that's why you're actually the perfect guest to have on. Because as I said in my opening, stocks continue to do well — I know — but there are some warning signs. I mean, Nvidia announced another very big quarter, but the response was muted. But there's a lot of fear permeating, especially through the bond market — I know that's something you've followed very closely. And then on top of all that, we got our first inflation print post the Iran war really beginning. No one quite knows exactly what's going to happen there.

And Jerome Powell is enjoying, I guess — taping this on Thursday — his last day as Fed chair, although he's going to be staying on the board to continue voting, I guess, for the foreseeable future. And again, crypto is getting caught up in all of this. Bitcoin had been up to about 82,000, 83,000 in recent weeks. ETH was, I believe, in the 2400s. That's all since retreated.

So I want to kind of get into all of it. So let's really kind of just start right now, Noelle, with my first question. How are you reading especially some of the fear that is pushing through bond markets, pushing up yields — the 10, the 30-year? To me, these would seem to be worrying signs. But for the most part, the market is kind of taking it in stride.

Noelle: You're right. They are definitely worrying signs. What's more, they're worrying signs at a global level. You look at global bond yields — they are climbing. That's global tightening. That is not good for markets.

But stock markets move to a different drummer. This isn't new. They always have moved to a different drummer. What is new is the divergence — the scale of the divergence. It's astonishing. You probably remember back in the day, everyone was excited about the 60-40 portfolio because, in theory, you'd have bonds and stocks moving in separate directions. Well, you know, we're seeing that, but not quite at this scale.

The stock market is being driven — we can talk more about this later — by intrinsic and temporary factors: excitement about AI mainly. Look at the chip sector performance. Whereas the bond market looks at the macro outlook, what's ahead. The bond market traditionally has been known as — and I'm going to use big air quotes here — the "smart money," because they do focus on nothing but macro data and narratives and trends, whereas the stock market can get swept up, as we know, in some of the hype cycles that we seem to be diving into with greater frequency.

So we've got the stock market following the hype cycle, which may have some foundations, may not — we can get into that as well. And we've got the bond market following the macro indicators, which are not great right now. So that's why: two very different drummers, not painting the same story. But they don't need to.

【通胀：缓慢移动、且早在霍尔木兹危机之前就开始抬头】

Steve: Yeah. Well, let's talk about some of those macro indicators. I mean, the inflation print is something that I know people are paying attention to. I think that the producer price index is another one that is starting to creep up. But what else are you seeing? And how are you interpreting a couple of those sort of inflationary signs — given the fact that, I don't want to use the word "transitory," but theoretically, if the strait reopens, if there is some sort of deal for Iran, energy markets are going to go back to at least immediately back to where they were before February 28th, when the attack happened. But things should calm down a little bit now.

Noelle: Things should come down, certainly in terms of the oil price, but that's not necessarily going to bring inflation down right away, for two reasons.

One: inflation moves slowly. I mean, we've seen an uptick in the core index, which is what the Fed focuses on, but it's been small — because oil prices do affect everything, but it takes time to filter through.

And we are also going to see increased volatility in the expectations. And this is interesting, especially in the American, the U.S. economy: the impact of gas prices on inflation expectations. You fill up your car at the pump, you see the tick, tick, tick of the numbers going up, and that event — that feels like it's draining out of your bank account almost. So with gas prices climbing, even though they don't impact the core inflation, investors or savers, consumers feel that inflation is going higher. That's going to influence their expectations, which influences their behavior, which influences actual inflation.

So we could have an end to the Hormuz crisis tomorrow, and one, it will take quite a while for energy prices to resettle, and two, it'll take even longer for that resettling to filter through to the inflation indices and the expectations. So in other words, this trend of inflation is not going to be over anytime soon, no matter what happens in Hormuz — because it actually also isn't new. Inflation was building well before the Hormuz crisis started.

Steve: Can you double-click on that a little bit? Because, I know — you're based in Spain, you're European, obviously I'm American. Inflation — the Fed has not — inflation had been going down, especially since like the COVID inflationary boom occurred and the Fed was raising rates to kind of ratchet it down a little bit. But it didn't get close to the 2% target, but it had been coming down. So what do you mean when you say it had been creeping up even before this happened?

Noelle: I'm going to push back on the assumption that it had been coming down. It actually hadn't. If you look at the graph since '24, it's been flat. The core CPI has been hovering between 2.6 and 3. It really hasn't been coming down.

And even just a year ago, year and a half ago, many of us were saying, "Okay, this is it. Inflation is done. The disinflation is done, and we're going to be steady for a while and then start to take up again." Why are expectations of continued uptick in inflation — which we have seen, but since even before the Hormuz strike, through the PPI, through import prices, through export prices as well — why? Because of the deglobalization trend that even predates Trump's administration. This started during the Biden administration. So that's a longer-term trend. Inflation will be heading up because of that. And Trump is just accelerating and turbocharging that.

We had the tariffs flip-flopping. We don't really know what's going on with the refunds there, but prices were going up as a result of tariffs. And then the Hormuz crisis just lit a match underneath all of that. But seriously, if you look back, if you look at the charts, you see that, no, inflation hadn't been coming down for a while.

Steve: Yeah, no, I actually — I think you're right. I mean, I remember even some discussions about whether or not the 2% number actually had to be adjusted for, like, the kind of like reset, a neutral — sort of like a neutral Fed target rate, et cetera. So I think you're —

Noelle: Three is the new two, right?

Steve: Three is the new two.

Noelle: And this is something that many have been arguing — even many Fed officials have suggested — but they can't change the expectations because of the underlying and perennial problem of credibility, of trust in the institution. A large part of what the Fed does is manage trust. And if suddenly, "Hey, we can't reach two, so we're going to change our target," well, that's going to impact the trust in the Fed's ability to reach the target that they set.

Steve: Gotcha. Okay. Thank you for that. And we're going to get a lot more into the idea of Fed and trust in probably about 10 minutes or so.

【"BLIS 交易"：永久性的看跌期权已嵌入系统】

Steve: But I do want to kind of press you a little bit on sort of the whole, like, immovable force versus unstoppable — or immovable — you know what I mean — the whole idea of stocks versus bonds. Because in your newsletter this week, you pointed out a really interesting op-ed that was written by — I think it was a former deputy managing director of the IMF, Gita Gopinath — about sort of the BLIS trade, and kind of how it's perhaps a corollary, or even like a more sustainable version of sort of the taco trade. And it sort of fits into the same family as the Fed put.

And a really interesting book that I read a couple months ago about sort of, like, the rise of carry — the idea that there's always going to be a backstop, and how that got turbocharged, especially during COVID, when, understandably, central banks all around the world went into excessive accommodation in order to support economies that were grinding to a halt.

Can you please kind of explain — like, what do you think is going to give first? And I'd love for you to just also explain sort of this BLIS trade and whether or not that's going to be a determining factor.

Noelle: Well, the BLIS trade, for those who aren't familiar with it — it stems from a really interesting opinion piece published in the Financial Times a couple of weeks ago by, as you said, Steve, a former IMF chief economist and deputy managing director, Gita Gopinath, who's now a Harvard professor. And it has to be read through the lens of someone that comes from the IMF.

But she makes the excellent point that the market assumption that there will be a put, if you will, or a safety net, is not to do with the taco trade anymore. That's part of it, for sure — and Trump certainly has given the market many things to hang the taco expectation on, many things we want him to walk back. But her point is that this goes beyond that. The taco trade is temporary. It's limited to Trump's term.

But the BLIS trade — which is the acronym for Big, Large and Lasting Stimulus or Support — is structural. Her point is that there is no administration now that will not spend to get people out of difficulties, whatever those difficulties may be — be it a market crash, be it a banking crisis, be it high energy prices. We saw this in 2020, we saw this in 2022 with the energy prices. We're seeing this again, especially here in Europe anyway, with the Hormuz crisis. Governments are not going to be voted out of office because they are not spending to save people from whatever difficulty they may be in.

And this is irrelevant — it's irrelevant which political party you're talking about. It's actually also irrelevant whether you're talking about a democracy or not, because we've seen coups across — especially across the middle of the equator economies.

Now, this is relevant for the outlook for currency debasement, obviously. Where is the funding for the stimulus going to come from? Well, it's going to come from somewhere. They're going to simply make it happen through one of the many tools in their box. Longer term, this does add moral hazard, if that's the right term. It does add froth to the speculative side of the market. It's one of the reasons, I think, why we're just seeing so much risk-on appetite in this very uncertain environment, as you pointed out earlier. But it's structural. The put is now part of the system. That, of course, will add a whole different layer of fragility.

【AI 股票：被忽视的脆弱性】

Steve: Yeah, and I'm really curious to see, like, when or if something might come to a head — because every time anyone has been trying to kind of predict the doom loop ends up missing out, because the market always recovers, often with like a K or a V or some type of letter that shows divergent returns.

And one area I'm really focused on, I'd like to ask you about before we kind of move on to the next segment, is: what do you think all this means in particular for the AI stocks? I mean, OpenAI, I think, is expected to file for a confidential IPO perhaps as soon as tomorrow. Anthropic is on the hook likely later this year. I mean, these companies need to raise hundreds of billions of dollars in funding to build out all this infrastructure, buy chips from the likes of Nvidia. Actually, I think Anthropic — it was reported they made an operating profit this quarter, which is pretty impressive. But OpenAI loses a ton of money. And they're relying on all this debt to build out for the future. But they're also the ones — like, this whole segment, this is the segment that's driving stock markets higher. How do you think about that particular segment and its broader implications for the market?

Noelle: I follow the reports that insist that the earnings multiples — the PE, forward PE — are actually very reasonable right now. And what drives me mad is that everyone's assuming that the earnings are going to be met or exceeded. And while that has historically been the case, we can't assume that will always continue. Because, again, what is the basis of these earnings expectations? In many cases, it's the company guidance. In many cases, it's just simply extrapolating demand. We're assuming a ton of demand for chips and for AI infrastructure that may or may not appear.

Again, it may. I'm not going to claim to be an AI expert here, but we've seen before, Steve, many times, that technological innovations have their hype period in which the expectations are out of proportion to the reality, and they do eventually suffer a correction. Maybe this is going to be the very first exception in history — possibly. But it's rash to bet everything you have on that. And right now, everything the market has is betting on that. And that's an underlying fragility, which I think is being very overlooked.

You mentioned earlier Nvidia's earnings were good and the stock sold off. This has actually happened every single time Nvidia reports, for the past, I think, eight quarters. And every single time everyone's like, "Okay, this is over, the whole AI play is over." But no — what it is, is a sell-the-news event. This has become fairly normal. It's related to expectations: expectations before the report, run-up after the report, "Okay, they were good, we're done, we're out," and people sell. So I wouldn't read anything into it yet.

But you're still right — eventually you think something has to tip. And I will just throw out there that historically, market tops have been tipped by very large IPOs.

Steve: Yeah, that is a good point to pay attention to. And Nvidia, as you mentioned, is a really kind of funny case, because I think I read that there had been analyst expectations, I think, for either 14 or 15 quarters in a row. But analyst expectations are, I guess, theoretically should be rooted in reality, whereas the Twitter hype machine can just speculate wildly. And those are the ones that are the momentum traders.

So it is kind of funny — I found it interesting that Jensen Huang, Nvidia CEO, and the company, they pointed out how there's so much concern about, like, a degree of incestuousness between all these different AI companies, the chip manufacturers, the consumers, et cetera, that — I think they tried to point out that, like, half of their clients are not, like, the big hyperscalers, to maybe assuage concerns about too much concentration in a few big companies. But it is really interesting to see how that's going to play out.

Noelle: The concentration of clients in these companies — and these clients are no doubt also themselves dealing with higher input costs and increasing cost of debt. Are we certain the health of these clients is going to continue to be strong enough to maintain the earnings forecast that these hyperscalers and the chip manufacturers are selling to?

Steve: Okay, great.

Noelle: But I've been wrong. I've been wrong before. I've been expecting a market correction for a while now, and I've been wrong on the timing. So, to be taken with a big grain of salt.

Steve: Yeah. Well, I've never been wrong ever in anything I've written. So I don't know — if that feels like — just kidding.

Okay. With that, let's take a very quick break so we can hear from some sponsors who make the show possible.
