Our first products actually has a meaningful revenue boost. We launched our uh first AI product actually two weeks before ChatGPT happened. Um, it's like AI writing product, that has a boost. But after that, we've been pretty much want to build agent product in 2022, end of 2022, and that's a slope — that took us a year and a half to really —

Didn't really work back then?

Didn't work at all. Like, Simon — I would say my problem and Simon's problem, we might be living too much into the future.

Yeah.

It bites you with new technology. Like, we try everything, built a model for us, work OpenAI fine-tuning or another — just none of them work. That was, like, a year and a half just go with no error, and morale is definitely low. Only start to work a year and a half — our revenue grow inflection, this around the same time, right, when the AI product —

The underlying got better.

Yeah. It's like, um — but that was a pretty painful period.

Was that the most painful period in Notion history for you?

Different type of pain.

What was the most painful?

Just when those two, three early days, pre-product market fit — is despair. I just like —

Despair is that the word. Okay.

You don't know where it's coming from, you don't know where to go, right? Um, when your growth rate low, like during that period of time where we were try to rebuilding our AI foundation multiple times — it's, yeah, that's not great. Um, right now, just everything on fire — it's a different type of pain.

Advice for SAS Companies Doing AI

Nice. So a lot of people, 500-person companies, a lot of SAS companies are doing the AI thing, but certainly haven't refounded. Advice?

Start with your product. We just talk about — treat it like brewing beer, not building bridges, because that's, end of the day, most tech companies is the product, right? So um, start with the products, cuz then you the founder has to be really hands-on to experience that way of building things.

Can non-founder-led companies pull it off?

I don't know. Would be tough. I think founder has the moral high ground to change things. People are more tolerant and forgiving with founders, right? Um, I think the person need to be use language modeling, in just know it. Like, you got to feel the AGI, feel the AI.

Yeah. You can't read about it, you can't watch YouTube. You got to build.

Feel — you have build it. Building is one of the best way.

For your product, or build for your internal systems?

Build for yourself. Yeah. Tinker on the weekend, tinker on the side. You have to do that, because, like, at the end of the day, business are kind of like pathfinding entities, right? Like, we're in this thing called a market, there's equilibrium for your company with the market, with your shareholder, with your company. Like, we're just finding local optimas, local maximums for different — our niches.

When this new brand new ingredient got introduced into the market — in this case language model — doors are, path that are opened up. And we are — each company at different point in the market, each you have different angle, you have different niche, you have to taking this, internalize this new ingredient, figure out which path got opened for you. The truth is, with the customer, with yourself — if you don't know how this can do, you cannot find a new path. I think you have to do something, you have to feel this — you feel the AI, feel AGI.

Decalcifying: Acquisitions and Founders

I think you're right. You use a word that I cringe at, but I think is good, called — a lot of companies calcify as they get older. Let's just say you're a company that's calcified. How do you get uncalcified?

We intentionally do a lot of acquisitions. We have 50, 60 founders at Notion.

And were they aqua-hires?

Aqua-hires, acqui-hires, acquisitions. Um, founders are kind of this kind of like little decalcified meathead machinery, just trying to break things, right? So —

Keep your regenerating.

I agree with that. Yeah, um, I find that really helpful.

So, founders found for a reason, and now all of a sudden they're in a thousand-person company. How do you keep them motivated and not leaving and starting something new?

Well, first, some of them work on a domain that they were working on before. Like, the person who leading our AMO has a startup of AMD notes before the company. The person leading our enterprise search is a founder of enterprise search product. So you give actually give them a better platform support to do this. They people feel appreciated. Like, I just had lunch with the founder who start enterprise search product before — it's like —

You give them leverage, they give them —

You give them leverage. They actually people care about the thing they're building, right? Versus fighting for product market — think really crowd the market, they don't. You can argue that it might be more difficult to start new companies today compared to even a year ago.

Okay.

Because the world's getting more noisy. Like, the shipping cadence from existing companies are just increasing. It's like the — everybody got Twitter fatigue, right? So —

And the SAS companies have got the memo. Most of them got the memo and are shipping fast now. You were ahead of everyone, but —

Yeah, people like — it's still —

Like HubSpot's cranking.

That's good. No, that — people are still kicking that — they have to kick in different gear, you know.

My thing these days is like, it could — it's never been easier to start, man, has never been harder to scale. Because, like, in every little micro segment, if there's a little traction, like there's a hundred competitors two days later. And the thing I've also noticed about the startup land is, almost in every one of these verticals, it's a duopoly. There's like a one and a two, and then 50 tied for third — they're way off.

When you look back in time — the great companies were often founded in shitty times, because people who are really grinders and people who really want to start a company and really have a vision, didn't start in like 2022, 2023, a terrible time to start. In times like 2026, you get a lot of tourist entrepreneurs. And so I've heard people say, you know, 2026 ventures is going to be crap, even though the valuations are —

Like crypto, crypto vibe energy.

Yeah, a little bit.

A little bit. Um, I don't disagree. Market will do the thing. Market is kind of beautiful in that way.

Yeah. Advice to founders of reasonable-sized companies who are a little scared to refound? You know — I'm CEO of a 500-person company, it's going pretty well, I'm doing some AI stuff, my board's kind of happy with me. Um, but there's a birdie inside of me listening to Ivan, and maybe I should refound. Advice? Does a person need to feel the AGI first?

If you feel — you realize most tech industry is support different flavor knowledge work, that we're at the beginning of how this can do to this industry.

The Future of Organizations

I agree with you. How do you think organizations look like? So, they're changing a lot now. What do you think they look like in like three, four years? It's your knowledge work — you're right at the center of this.

I think truth is probably somewhere there's a Dorsey, Jack Dorsey's thing. Uh, there is the traditional hierarchical ones. Um, I like to think, what are invariables? Like, let's look invariable. In this case, human nature are rather static. We all —

We don't change that fast.

We don't. Human don't change. Humans as a species doesn't change. That's where I think it should — it will be a hierarchical organization. If just observe bonobo, chimpanzees, right? It's like, very — if you watch nature program, there's hierarchy. That doesn't change, this everything hasn't changed of us since we left Africa.

Um, division and labor make sense. Um, so people have different interests. You look at all the different, like, personality tests, right? They're just different way to saying, like, people are different. They're not 10,000 flavor of them there, but there are probably a dozen flavor of humans. They cluster around this different dozen things.

And we'll know that — our legal system, well, at least right now, there's no autonomous companies, right? It's the CEO, CFO will have to sign up for something. So it will be a small team of human to start. So those to me are the invariables. So this invariable tells me, you need to figure out how those group of human collaborate. The group of human all have different biases, preferences, different values. Um, and it will be somewhat a small, some kind of hierarchical system there. Then, how do we use this new technology, new ingredient to passing information, decision around a small, midsize group of humans? That's how I think about it.

I kind of think about it as like, right now in companies the AI system makes very few of the decisions. Let's call it 1%. But as that system gets more context — using Notion for example — it'll make better decisions than humans. It'll have more context and more history. And then humans will be more training that system and giving it context and giving it taste. And I think over time the system makes more and more of the decisions instead of the humans, but the humans are still really necessary.

Yeah. People say AI researchers, in 15 months, will not be able to make decision to improve AI models, right? Kind of the same thing, but they're just in a bleeding edge for this.

Yes. By the way, I think you're incredibly well positioned for all this.

Oh yeah.

I worked for a while with this guy Ray Ozzie. It was a company called Groove Networks. We were in the knowledge management industry, which is a [ __ ] industry, cuz chief knowledge officers have no money and it was never urgent. In theory, it sounded great. Now, knowledge management is actually incredibly important and works incredibly well.

I know. It's like — what's that industry called in the '90s? People hire consultant to map out their org chart and information flow — that become like what AI and for deployment.

Totally. And your platform is ideally suited for it.

Yeah. Um, I think we always forget that modern knowledge work is only about 150 years old.

It's invented. It's not as old as fire or language. It's only 150 years old.

Yeah, right. So why can it be a new flavor of it?

On Ivan: Introvert, Schedule, All-Hands

Okay, I want to talk about you.

Okay.

I don't know you super well. You're soft-spoken, and I thought you were an introvert, but in getting to know you better — was I wrong about that?

I think I'm still introverted.

Okay.

Yeah, I like one-on-one conversation, one too many. I train myself to do company all-hands just by doing a lot.

A lot of founders are introverts. The world is sort of built for — I'm kind of extroverted, extroverts. Advice for founders who just don't like talking to other — you don't like the whole extrovert game.

I have to evolve a lot. Like, I hate doing —

Force yourself? Like, how — to work, a significant other push you?

No, I force myself. Okay. Um, to lead the group of human, you need to do one-to-many communications, otherwise people don't trust you. They want to hear from you. Writing is one mode, which is like, sometimes long enough. But even more so is face-to-face, to the all-hands. So for a while I try to delegate all-hands to my co-founders, to other execs — no way. I have to, now, at every all-hands, I'm open the mic. I do it. I lead all-hands. Now I'm pretty comfortable with it. Uh, I still don't love it, but — well, it's not, I don't dread it.

Okay. Advice for people doing all-hands? Everyone seems to do it differently.

Oh, get a teleprompter.

I'm the opposite. I can't read fast enough for the teleprompter.

Really?

Yes. I hate a teleprompter.

Well, that changes — me, because, like — well, English is my second language, so it's like, if I have to think and speak, it's hard. So I just prefer to spoken before, and right now all those, like, uh, speech-to-text model locals are so good.

They're so good.

So good. I literally, like, okay, the night before or then, I just like talked to myself and got what I'm roughly want to say, and the time doing all-hands, teleprompter there.

Do you do them once a week, once a month, once — like, what's your cadence?

We're actually changing our cadence. Used to be once a month. Right now it's every other week, uh, for all-hands, and every other week again for AMA. AMA. So every week now.

So, you ever step on yourself on an AMA? You ever say something you're like, I gotta crush for that?

Yeah, everybody has it, right? Or just you miss a good opportunity. You can actually rally people about something, but you give a non-answer. That's the worst — and non-answers.

We, in — at least at HubSpot, in the company meetings, in the AMAs, there would be a Slack channel on it, and you go back and look at it and be like — what — just like, if I said something stupid or I missed an opportunity, like it would really show up in there.

That's good.

Schedule and Energy

How do you manage your time in your schedule and your deep work? Like, how do you manage your energy? Like, for me, my energy is pretty good right now. It kind of goes up and down during the day more than I would like. Is that the case for you? How do you deal with that? How do you deal with your introvert versus extrovert? Like, how do you do it? What's your schedule like?

It's actually not that crazy. Um, wake up 7, 7 a.m., make coffee for my wife. Um, I usually like to do work in the morning, do some thinking, writing at home, come to office. I come to work every day. Uh, come to office. Um, sometimes — a new habit I'm trying right now is doing the gym during the day.

Okay, that's a good idea. Perk you up.

Elevate my energy during the middle of the day, and work in the office till 7-ish. Usually social dinner with my wife, back to work, sleep out till midnight, around midnight.

When do you have time to get down the Claude rabbit hole?

We can't — we can't. My happy time. No. Um —

You describe that as your habitat.

Yeah. You can be led by your curiosity rather than by your responsibility, right? And like, usually Saturday, Sunday morning, I have a bit time to read the books I want to read. Um, sometimes it's for work-related, sometimes purely just for my guilty pleasure.

And during the day, like half hour — like, I'm seeing — found one of my founders here in New York — say who it is — he's got his schedule in 10-minute blocks.

Meeting-wise.

Yes. Like, what does your day look like?

A lot of 25-minute blocks. I usually like five minutes between meetings, and 25, 25. My team pack my meetings together, so I have one or two-hour chunk to think. But most my thinking time is in the morning or in the evening. Usually I get too tired in the evening, I can't think really well. Um, morning is better. And I do a lot of Slack. My wife joke, my Slack is my social media.

Why doesn't Notion build a Slack killer?

We're — shh. Definitely. Yeah.

Truth-Seeking and Pluralism

A lot of your team said you're humble, and I think you are too. Why?

I wouldn't use the word humble. I use the word —

Your team uses that.

— truth-seeking, probably.

They use humble.

I think you seek truth, you know you're just one perspective of the truth, or pluralism. There are multiple flavor of truth in the world. You know the story of blind people touching elephant.

Yes.

As — where, just different blind people touching different part of elephant. Sometimes could be the tail, the elephant's a snake; sometimes the trunk is a tree, right? That's my worldview.

Advice to Founders: The Social Game

Okay. Tonight we're going to have dinner with a bunch of founders, and some are somewhere like 10 employees, and they hope to go and build a thousand-person company in the next several years. What advice do you have for them?

It's a game. It's a game that's a social game. It's like there's all the good part and bad part of social game. Like humor or status is human thing, right? Like, um, power is this — the CEO game is full of that game. Just like entertainment, just like sports, you have that, like, high school, college lunch sessions, right? You watch that, in real, in the tech scene.

It also could be a pursuit of your personal values. This is something I realized more in the past three, four years. It's like, I need to be in equilibrium with my own value of what this company I want to build. With that, give me more sustainable energy source. I cannot just chase something or competition for competition's sake. Like, I'm really competitive, I need to compete to win, that's part of me. But at the same time, I need to be true to my own values — the value of building good tools, the craft, the human side of things, the social part of being a CEO, the market dynamic, just compete for competing sake.

And the value, the person — I wouldn't say there are — this is somewhat a zero-sum game. So you need to figure out where do you want to sit in this spectrum, and your personality might be different. Some people might just compute for compute sake, doesn't care about what they're building. Some people just want to be popular. If you just only care about craft, that you don't build a business, it might as well just be an artist. Took me a while to realize where's my equilibrium.

Strengths vs. Weaknesses

Are you someone that tries to fix your weaknesses, or you just lean into your strengths? And what's your advice on that?

Definitely lean on your strength. There's a necessary thing you have to do, like one-to-many communication — likely you need to do with your company scale. Maybe you can lean more on writing rather than speaking. But there's certain invariable that works, right? Um, within the boundary of that, you should amplify what you're good at, because capabilities are become more and more commoditized with machines. So it's your own point of view, your strength, that you can live with — that's more lasting.

Building Enterprise Sales

Okay. Something I assume that isn't your strong suit is building an enterprise sales — which seems like you're in the process of building that. And when I hear it's going exceptionally well — how did you get your head around it? Um, how did you hire? Sounds like you hired the right person. So many companies are PLG and are desperate to move to the enterprise, and the founders are similar mold to you. Can you talk about that?

We had a mistake. I mentioned, like, we sort of did not buy traditional sales motion for a good two-ish years, right? If we — because Notion sales could start earlier, but we want to first-principle our way into it, which is, we're going to design our own system. That doesn't work. Like, again, it's a reflection of human nature. A lot of things, you want to talk to a seller. There's a lot of — the reason the modern sales playbook has been around for two-ish decades, right? So —

I think enterprise sales is like the last function.

I agree with you. It's like, that you want to see a human, you feel comfortable.

You don't want to have — be a doctor from a robot.

No. Then why should you buy an expensive thing from a robot, right? So, um, it's not that hard, I would say. But you need to know — like, a lot of smart people might think they can create something new. Fundamentally you should not reinvent new things unless it's really absolutely necessary. I was trying to reinvent a new go-to-market motion. That's stupid.

Okay. Like, I did mine — you really built HubSpot.

Yeah, right. Maybe — that's the thing made HubSpot, right? Like this inbound.

That was the thing.

You should only — each company should only preserve your innovation point to few places, go a couple places. You cannot spread everywhere, like otherwise you're spread too thin, you're trying to reinvent the wheel too many times. We try to put our innovation point onto sales back then — just go classic sales, it works. Um, but you make a lot more efficient today with a lot of tools we're building internally. Um, that's different.

Okay. So you woke up one day — one day you're like, we got to get good at enterprise sales. What'd you do? What are the mistakes everyone makes when they do it? Did you miss a hire or two before you got the right person?

I thought this somewhat solved today, right?

I kind of do, but people struggle with it. Um, enterprise sales has changed very little since I did it in the 1990s.

Yeah. So we tried, um, initially only half — uh, PLG flavor of sales, like system thinker sales, system sales leader. Um, didn't quite work. It's just like, then you're just taking orders, and no — like, the PLG give you the luxury that your customer want to buy your product, you just —

Easy to sell that, it's like —

Fulfilling demand. You cannot get your sellers to do something difficult when there's so much easier thing they can do. Just doesn't work.

Then we realize, okay, yes, we still need to respect the system. So our CRO is Erica — she was CRO of GitHub before. It's a system thinker, right? We pair Erica with our head of sale, Pravesh, which is the rah-rah, like the —

Meat eater.

Meat eater, right? So, beans in his teeth.

Perfect pairing.

And so we have sellers with rah-rah, Pravesh, but also respect working with Erica's overall system. This is when things start to work in the past a year and a half.

Got it. And so many of the organizations I deal with, the engineers look at how much money the salespeople make, and look at the rah-rah, and they roll their eyes and they complain. I assume that happened here.

No, we actually — Notion culture is always pretty receptive for different people are different. So we never have an organ rejection of sellers in the company. Our engineering team love work with sales team.

Okay, awesome.

Cult Leader and Religion

Speaking of culture, I saw online some people refer to you as a cult leader, and some people referred to me as a cult leader back in the day. I didn't particularly like that. How does that land with you?

I like that.

Okay.

A company, it's sort of a religion. Is — you have a point of view, you have your value system, project to the world through a business, through a product. Like, my favorite saying is, Catholic Church is one of the most successful companies of all time — 2,000 years. They have their ritual, their business models. They're still around kicking. Great founder in Jesus, and a great head of sales in Paul, right?

Yeah.

And there are a lot of sellers and uh, um, referral system.

Viral marketing.

Viral marketing. OG viral marketing.

Yeah.

And to me, religion or belief system or value system are, again, one of the few dozen thing that's ingraining human nature. Like, people want to believe in something, to find meaning and purpose. That's why wartime make this more fun, right? Your purpose and meaning got amplified. Um, work and company give people that camaraderie, give people that. That's why we play sports, right? It's the same energy.

The first four words in culture are cult.

Yeah, I probably — I need to look up the roots. But yeah, it's like, what is all-hands? People go to church every Sunday, all-hands every week — same thing.

We used to have a thing with our all-hands, that Dharmesh and I wanted it to be so inspirational that people would well up or cry during the all-hands. Like, we really really worked on our all-hands.

Yeah, nothing — I don't think anything wrong with that.

No. Okay. Thank you for coming on the pod. Thank you for building Notion. Thank you for being inspiration for tons and tons of founders.

I'm glad, hopefully to be helpful for others.

Appreciate you.

Thank you for your time.

Host Outro: Three Takeaways

Okay, I hope you like that episode with Ivan.

One of the things I like about him — there's a famous quote by Oscar Wilde: "Be yourself, everyone else is taken." I like to think I am myself. Like, I'm pretty quirky and I've embraced that quirkiness. And I think at the end of the day, companies really reflect those CEOs. And I don't think CEOs should be afraid to really be themselves and kind of build the company around them. Ivan's really done that nicely. That might be a good plan for you, too.

The second kind of takeaway I had is — you know, I work with lots of startups, and there are so many companies, let's say less than 500 million in revenue, growing like 30%. They're kind of stuck, and there's infinite numbers of stuck companies out there. If you're in that boat, I'd recommend a refounding moment. And the refounding is harder than it looks. It typically involves like a big step back and two steps forward. It's going to be hard. Your numbers will look worse in the short term. Ivan didn't do this, but when we wanted to do a refounding at HubSpot at one point, we started a new company inside of HubSpot that was completely independent from it, and that served us well. That's how Apple did it back in the day. But if you're stuck — and a lot of companies are stuck — be risky and do what Ivan did. Like, he was a ye olde SAS company and now he's a rocket ship AI company. I think he's onto it.

The last takeaway I have on Ivan kind of starts back in a podcast interview I did with Jack Dorsey a few weeks ago. And it does seem to me there's a new way to build and run companies today. That's a pretty big departure from the way people have done it the last 30 years. Jack's iterating on it. Ivan's iterating on it. A lot of the call-it vintage-2025 startups are iterating on it. And this is something I'm thinking a lot about. I think there needs to be a new playbook on how to be a CEO and AI native in the kind of era we're in.

Um, and I'm early in the iteration, but just roughly the way I kind of think about the way they're organized is — historically there is a triangle-shaped org chart. Those org charts have been around a long, long time, since the Roman Empire. They're a very inefficient way to run a company, but they've been the best way to run a company, uh, because there's been no better one. I think the problem with them is, the bigger that org chart gets, the more that information gets distorted across and up and down, and the slower it gets. And so it's pretty inefficient.

The new way that Dorsey described — and I didn't quite get into it with Ivan, but I suspect it's similar — is it's a circle, and in the middle is all of the context in the AI system. And initially humans are making all the decisions, but over time, as you give that kind of centralized AI system more context, it gets smarter, and you're feeding in context, and you turn over more and more of the decisions to them. I don't think it happens overnight, I think it's gradual, but it's an IT task to get it right. You have to be legible — I don't love that word, but you have to have a legible set of systems, you have to kind of record everything. But I think this is the new way.

Companies who are doing this seem to be just moving a lot faster than the companies that aren't. Um, and Ivan is moving at breakneck speed. And I think there's a lot of things that fall out of it. They don't need as many people. Um, they move much faster because of that. They have much shorter planning cycles. All of those kind of one-way doors that Bezos used to talk about are really two-way doors, cuz nothing takes that long to do.

Anyway, I think there's this new playbook. I'm interested in it. I'm going to be talking about it in the podcast and on X. If you're interested in it too, hit me up on Bcolligan. Thanks for tuning in.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 最反直觉的一点：Notion 的两次"重founding"不是战略决策，而是身体决策
注意 Ivan 描述重大转折时用的语言系统——几乎全是身体词汇。决定裁掉团队搬去京都，是"你的身体会告诉你必须做""一种熊猫般的内在能量";第一次见到 GPT-4，是"a full body religious experience";给同行的建议是"feel the AGI";甚至 wartime 的方法论被他总结成"我必须照顾我的身体，每天健身"。这里藏着一个系统性真相：**Notion 的组织战略，和京都那些做刀、做陶杯、做榻榻米匠人的认识论，是同一套**——隐性知识，靠手和身体去"知道"，而不是靠计划书去推演。他说软件分两种，"造桥"（可预测、可演绎）和"酿啤酒"（不可预测、靠经验逼近）。这看似是产品比喻，其实是 Ivan 决策风格的自画像：他本人就是用酿啤酒的方式经营公司。所以他去京都不是巧合——他需要一个用身体而非用 KPI 思考的城市来给自己背书。
## 跨领域连接：被忽视的"钢铁悖论"
Ivan 说"语言模型 + 软件是组织的钢铁"，钢铁让大楼能盖得更高;可下一句他又说组织

item.title: [Mercatus Center] Pratap Bhanu Mehta on Liberalism, Nihilism, and the Collapse of Sincerity
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"真诚的崩塌"其实是一个密码学问题

Mehta 谈"sincerity collapse"时，把它归因于语境的崩塌——全球化的传播让一句话脱离了"谁在说、对谁说、为何说"的坐标系。这里有一个让人意外的角度：他描述的现象，结构上和零信任网络安全模型一模一样。

在一个语境完整的世界里，每条信息都带着"签名"——发言者的职业身份、关系史、场合，构成了可验证的来源凭证（provenance）。当语境崩塌，信息变成了未签名的数据包：你无法验证发送者意图，于是系统的理性反应只能是默认敌意。Mehta 那句"knowledge is made increasingly for cutting, not for understanding"，翻译成工程语言就是：当无法做身份认证时，每个节点都进入防御模式。这不是道德滑坡，而是信任基础设施失效后的理性退化。他自己也给了最好的类比——印刷术革命也曾让权威与可信度的归属机制整体崩溃，然后用了几代人重建。我们现在缺的不是善意，而是"签名协议"。

二、被掏空的"职业身份"与被掏空的"真诚"，是同一件事

对话里有一个隐藏的连接，Mehta 把它分成两段讲、却没有点破：职业身份的空心化（医生变股东、资深律师不再接政治敏感案件）和真诚的崩塌，本质是同一个机制。

一个职业身份（"我是法院的官员"）是一种预先承诺装置——它在你开口之前就替你担保了行为边界。一句真诚的话同样是一种预承诺——它担保了"我说的就是我想的"。两者都是 Mehta 所说"thickening context"的载体。资本主义所要求的极端流动性与一切皆可商品化，同时溶解了这两种担保：职业沦为另一种财产持有形式，话语沦为纯粹的表达或战斗口号。所以"职业危机"和"沟通危机"不是两个并列的章节，而是同一根承重墙的两道裂缝。Durkheim 当年押注职业是"现代性的核心道德"——他押的其实就是：现代社会需要可被外部识别的、带签名的身份。这一注，正在输。

三、最反直觉的一点：右翼赢，是因为它偷走了左翼的理论

Mehta 抛出一个极易被忽略却极锋利的判断——BJP 和美国的右翼是"真正的后现代主义者"。这是整场对话里最大的"啊哈时刻"。

我们习惯把右翼民族主义想象成"本质主义、回到血缘与传统"。但 Mehta 指出：恰恰是右翼接受了身份可以被政治建构这一彻头彻尾的后现代命题——把一个被排斥群体的身份，通过"政治的辛苦工作"重新编码进印度教民族主义。而左翼与中间派反而退守到本质主义："人投票投种姓"、"人口结构即命运"（demography is destiny）。理论上的左右翼，在实践中完成了一次彻底的角色对调。

这里可以做一个跨领域连接：这正是生物学意义上的单一栽培（monoculture）与多样性的对抗。Mehta 说种姓作为"自然政治板块"正在内部碎裂、产生策略多样性——这是一个生态系统的抗逆性在上升。而 Vishwas 政治（把对领袖的垂直信仰，替换公民之间的横向信任）做的恰恰相反：它用一个单点身份取代了多样的认同网络。从韧性工程的角度看，垂直 Vishwas 是一种单点故障架构——效率极高，崩溃时也极彻底。左翼的失败不在于价值，而在于它放弃了"重新培育多样性"这门手艺，把战场让给了唯一一个还在做身份农业的玩家。

四、种姓是一种"制度空缺的变通方案"——把它当作内生变量

最后一个值得停留的洞见，是 Mehta 那句几乎一带而过的话：应该像对待制度一样，把种姓当作内生变量来处理。在缺乏正规金融、缺乏正式制度的条件下，"在网络内交易"其实是完全理性的选择。

这等于把种姓从"古老偏见的残留"重新定义为国家失败的症状——它和非正规借贷、宗族担保是同一类东西：当正式制度缺位，人们用现成的社会网络来填补合约执行与风险分担的真空。这个重构有一个尖锐的政策含义：你无法靠"道德劝诫"消解种姓，正如你无法靠劝诫消灭高利贷；你只能通过把它所替代的那些制度（金融、教育、公共空间）真正建起来，让"网络内选择"不再是理性的。而 Mehta 同时给了一记警告——印度的话语病是把解释一切的懒惰标签从"殖民主义"换成了"种姓"。一个变量一旦能解释一切，它就什么也没解释。

📝 转录文本

Pratap Bhanu Mehta 论自由主义、虚无主义与真诚的崩塌

节目开场

to Ideas of India, a podcast where we examine academic ideas that can propel India forward. My name is Shruti Rajagopalan and today my guest is Dr. Pratap Bhanu Mehta, who is the Lawrence Rockefeller Professor for Distinguished Teaching at Princeton and former President and Chief Executive of the Center for Policy Research New Delhi. He's the author of various books and edited volumes, has served on various government committees and is a columnist for the Indian Express newspaper.

We talked about the return of nihilism in political life, the hollowing of professional identities, the politics of vishwas, Adam Smith on concentration of power, what it takes to build lasting institutions, the assumptions behind non-alignment, and much more. For a full transcript of this conversation, including helpful links of all the references mentioned, click the link in the show notes or visit mercators.org slash podcasts.

Welcome back to the podcast and it's a pleasure to have you here in the studio in person as opposed to doing this online during COVID times. So I'm thrilled that you're here.

It's such a privilege to be here and you know to think together actually in a sense more than just talk.

I will think with a little bit of lag compared to you I have a feeling.

几十年专栏写作的弧线：从批判全球化到担忧规范的侵蚀

I've been reading a lot of your older columns again recently. you know, in general, both to prepare for this conversation and also to make sense of the world, which is in so much flux right now. And across all your writing, whether, you know, even if I go as far back as the financial crisis, when you talk about how we should think about repairing globalization, especially to make it a little bit more of a level playing field, you know, across different stakeholders in the world, whether it's arguments about the war in Ukraine and Gaza, whether whether it's what's happening now with the entire WTO liberal order being upended, the sense I got from the arc of your columns is that you've moved away from very specific critiques of globalization, you know, the failures of the old liberal order, And your deeper worry is the broader norms, like the erosion of restraint, the erosion of legitimacy, of leadership, of a kind of moral authority in the international order. And is this the correct way to read you across the decades and the columns? And perhaps you can help us make sense of where are we right now? Like, what is this new world we're entering and what is a frame through which we can enter it?

Gosh, first of all, thank you for reading things so closely. I'm not sure they merit as much close attention. But I think you have characterized, I think, the arc correctly. And one way of describing that arc, which might be an entry point into the current moment, is, as you know, 15, 20 years ago, we used to discuss things like institutions, incentives, questions of distributive justice, questions of what's the basic architecture of a global economic order. But I think all of those questions and the way we frame that did not reckon with one historical possibility, which I think we are having to face up to now. And liberalism has probably had to face up in its past as well. What do you do if there are in society forces that that have a degree of ruthlessness and a willingness to, as it were, burn the house down rather than merely disagree or let the other side win.

虚无主义的回归：Leo Strauss 与"去抑制化"

I was going back to an old essay by Leo Strauss, you know, on German nihilism and his characterization of nihilism, and you can add it to it, which is to say that, look, nihilism for him was a condition where a political party, a political dispensation is willing to, in a sense, call for radical change without the foggiest idea of what that new change is, but more importantly, in the process, willing to jeopardize the gains and potentialities of the current order. And if you add on to that, I think the word you used or something similar, which is this complete lack of kind of disinhibition, in doing it, right? So, disinhibition in the sense that a certain valorization of violence and a propensity to engage in it both internationally and domestically. I think the construction of your opponents as existential enemies. And once you have done that, then, of course, everything becomes permissible.

I think the deep skepticism that our institutions as they are currently configured... can actually do the democratic job of producing regimes of truth that are actually widely accepted. And this is not just a critique from the right. I think there are sections of the left. I think liberals have lost kind of, I think, courage of their own convictions in some ways, right? So you have that kind of deep skepticism about truth itself. Skepticism about authority would have been an absolute wonderful thing. And I think this amalgam of dispositions is a kind of existential condition, which I don't think liberals know quite how to respond to.

And it's not the kind of condition that will be easily addressed just by recourse to, you know, the very kind of comfortable bland things we used to talk about, tinkering of institutional design, incentives. I mean, those things do matter. I mean, I think for a world that we deconstruct after this, those things will continue to matter. But at this moment, diagnosing the why we are in this condition. You know, people used to say that, or historians used to say that if you want to understand the prelude to World War I, don't read The Social Scientist, read Robert Musil, right? The Man Without Qualities, that kind of depiction of nihilism. And cultures have been through this. So I think that's our bigger challenge. And to that extent, it's not that I've given up on those convictions or actually changed my mind about those institutions. But the battle on which we'll have to fight is of a very qualitatively different nature.

不只是好人与坏人：精英也失去了道德权威

So when I was writing my dissertation, my dissertation advisor, Peter Bedki, used to say that, you know, as economists, we never rely on saying bad people do bad things and good people do good things. And when bad people do bad things, bad things happen. And when good people do good things, good outcomes happen. And yet, That's what the nihilistic framework seems to suggest, like either accidentally or through some emergent process, we have landed on a particular set of actors who either lack moral authority or leadership or just, you know, a long term view of how to build the world.

I think the counterweight to that seems to me to also be that liberals and elites have also lost that moral authority. So I In earlier cycles of this, when terrible people came and tried to do terrible things, there was another group that was ready to absorb the burden of that. And because of their moral authority or leadership, they were willing to be heard or people were willing to hear them. Now that seems to be lost. So what explains this erosion on all sides?

So, you know, it's wonderful. You're going to begin with Peter Botke. I mean, as you know, you know, my dissertation was on Adam Smith and one of the great virtues of the Scottish Enlightenment was that it never fell into the simplistic trap that good people do good things and bad people do bad things. In fact, you know, the dynamics of history are always more complicated. Sometimes bad people actually end up producing policies that are you know, beneficial in the long run. History is very paradoxical.

So they all believed in progress, but with a very skeptical way. I mean, you know, right. So I think that's absolutely right. And I think that sensibility is very important to understanding politics, actually. Politics is not just about drawing these lines between good people and bad people. Politics has to firmly keep its eye on consequences. I think, again, as any serious student of politics from Max Weber onwards has told you.

So I don't think, so I agree. I mean, I don't think the issue is sort of who are the good people and who are the bad people, right? You can certainly map on weaknesses, mistakes, terrible moral judgments on a wide range of the political spectrum. I mean, it's very hard to kind of argue at this point that there are political dispensations that are immune from that kind of moral abdication. I think the question in a sense, I think in a sense is even deeper and more troubling, which is, do we even have a sense of what the standards of right and wrong are, independently of who actually lives up to them and in a sense, who betrays them.

And that's, I think, the nihilism I was kind of, in a sense, hinting at, which is there is such a radical instrumentalization of almost every value. In a sense, the challenge of dealing with good people and bad people is easier if you can make the argument that, look, if you want to critique something, by all means critique it, but critique it in good faith and then live up to the principles on the basis of which you are critiquing, right? Don't use critique merely as a pretext to be down on somebody else, right? And I think the disinhibition we are, you know, experiencing at the moment, I mean, take the issue of war, for example. I mean, it's very hard to argue that there is only a particular political dispensation, whether it's the right, the centrist, in some cases, the left's abdication has been as extraordinary, right? How can we in democracies not ask tough questions about our own moral complicity? I think sometimes people accuse others of acting out of self-interest. And yet the most secure classes in both the democracies we care about, exemplified traits that Montesquieu described as, you know, security often beats timidity, right? A kind of lack of conviction and courage. So I think that landscape is actually in the sense, I think all over the place. But my worry is that I think our sense of where the red lines are is actually eroding. And that sense is much harder to reconstruct once that breaks down.

微观的进步与宏观的失序：一个悖论

You know, here I have a difficulty between matching what's happening in the micro sense and the macro sense. I feel like if I just look at the data across on, you know, how much violence we commit against each other as individuals, against women, how we treat children, how we treat animals, do we care, you know, about, you know, the simplest of things like, you know, conservation of extinct species or animals, the big rise in vegetarianism and against this kind of, you know, industrial complex that's cruel. On all of those margins, we have made progress, right? People are actually kinder to each other in a very literal sense of, you know, not being violent. They are kinder to each other within the household. So those norms have all changed and they've largely stabilized. But that doesn't emerge into a macro order of where we know what is right and wrong at that level. But we seem to know what's right and wrong at the lower level. And in fact, we're sticking with it quite well. So what causes this?

Yeah, that's a wonderful observation. But if you take the long arc of history, right, there's nothing anomalous about this. I mean, you know, even if you look at debates in late 19th century, right? So there's one kind of long term story about in a way, what Norbert Elias would call this kind of civilizing process, right? Where lots of different forms of violence, in a sense, are tamed and actually no longer found acceptable. I mean, we don't do duels anymore as a way of settling disputes, right? We don't do an eye for an eye. I mean, there's a whole range of things. And in many ways, a lot of the phenomenon that you describe, right, a certain kind of revulsion against certain forms of violence. I certainly think, for example, the discourse on animal rights, the turn towards vegetarianism, if you place it in that long arc of societies trying to curtail and eclipse violence, right, these are of a piece.

二十世纪唯二胜出的意识形态：民族主义与女性主义

I think the norm towards a certain kind of moral egalitarianism, you're also right, I think is a long-term trend that is going to be hard to reverse. I mean, in some senses, I think if you look at the 20th century, there are only two ideologies that have unequivocally triumphed. One is nationalism and the other is feminism, which is feminism is actually the frame of reference to which every other ideology has to adapt. Yes, sometimes there are pockets of resistance. And it's really in a kind of extraordinary achievement if you actually think about it, right? But part of the answer is is actually the fact, is the tension between these two ideologies, right? Which is, if you think of what produces or licenses a certain kind of moral disinhibition, the claim that nationalism is the one ideology that allows you to do that much more easily is actually a very powerful win.

It's true that nationalism and the modern nation state form has enabled liberal democracy, at least as we understand it. It created the idea of the people, popular sovereignty. And yet it is also the one ideology that is actually the biggest competitor of religion. It's the only modern ideology that can consecrate death. It's an absolutely astonishing psychological fact about nationalism. It is the one ideology that can colonize our sense of meaning. I mean, you know, even our sense of economic meaning actually comes in national categories, you know, GDP growth rate for my country, as it were, right? It is so pervasive in some ways, right, in its ability to actually create meaning. And through its ability to create that meaning, license a kind of instrumentality in its cause. Once you say this is for the nation, right, it's extraordinary how many barriers you can actually break, right?

And if you look at the current moment, I think one of the things that is actually very striking is almost every form of authoritarianism is having to rely on nationalism to give it that cloak and cover, right? not just in a sense to, you know, create an electoral majority, not just in purely instrumental terms, right, but also in moral terms to say that, look, this is the highest God we'd worship. Once you worship this God, right, you are allowed to take all of these necessary, what we think are necessary steps to this. And I think I think that tension, in a sense, if you use them as two archetypes, right? Feminism is representing progress, equality, a certain kind of softening of public mores, dismantling of patriarchy, that modern impulse towards liberation, and nationalism as that modern ability to mobilize collective power, right? in all its most murderous forms. That's the tension I think we are experiencing.

全球化、共同体的瓦解与民族主义这个"便利"的身份

So, you know, if I had to connect this to how some of the other scholars have made sense of it, to me, it seems like, and I'm drawing the very simple version of the story here, We had globalization with its extraordinary success. But one of the outcomes of globalization was people were less connected to their local communities. They were less connected to their local identities. And, you know, local identities, as Ambedkar pointed out, can sometimes be very problematic because of those hierarchies and sometimes be wonderful to give you a sense of reciprocity and really who you are in the world.

So if I look at, say, you know, what Raghuram Rajan had to say about what happens to community, when we are thinking about the broader sort of global economic order, that erosion has led to people floundering for new identities. And nationalism seems to be quite convenient in some sense, right? It's still rooted in localism, because if you belong to a particular country, then of course, that gets subsumed in it. But at the same time, you get to keep all the other good things. You don't have to do this around religious lines or gender lines or caste lines or ethnic lines, you know, or state lines. You still get to pretend to be modern and liberal while having a single point of identification, which can then unleash these consequences. Is that just too simple a frame? Or is that a good way of thinking about what's going on? Because nationalism has not always had this kind of power.

So I am a little skeptical of the story. I mean, I actually agree with the kind of underlying premise, right? That there is a kind of experience of a social lack, right, in terms of kind of community, in terms of actually relationships. And in fact, that is actually being radicalized. I mean, all kinds of evidence, not just Robert Putnam's kind of bowling alone stuff, but if you actually think of what's happening to family structures, I mean, there is a sense in which the sociological atomism of society is actually quite deep and persuasive.

I think the question is, is whether that has something to do with globalization or to use that old word capitalism, right? Because remember, this is a debate that begins again in the late 18th century, right? In fact, somebody like Edmund Burke, I mean, I think it's the last moment where conservatives have this illusion that you can actually hold on to stable, fixed institutions in the social realm. and yet give markets, in a sense, free reign. And that's been the biggest tension and challenge for conservative thought, right?

The reason, in part, I'm skeptical about the globalization story is, you know, the best literature on this crisis of relationships, of crisis of communities, is actually not the literature that's being produced now. It was the literature that was produced in the 1970s. I mean, I think Alistair McIntyre, Christopher Lash were probably the most prescient and deepest diagnosticians in a sense of this phenomenon. And it's the 70s, you know, which was in a sense, given that 50s and 60s was the kind of the high tide of what we now think of as, you know, at least in the Western world, right? The nostalgia for the 50s and 60s as forms of industrial production, forms of national identity and community.

So the deeper question might be, is it to do in a sense with globalization or is it, you know, to do with the fact that capitalism requires both commodification but also such extraordinary mobility, right, that it is just very difficult. I mean, I'm sure at personal level, you know, I mean, at least almost everybody in the younger generation, we know, I mean, just this whole idea of what it might even actually mean to create strong relationships in a world where everybody is kind of constantly mobile, right? And so I actually think it's, in a sense, roots, you know, in a sense, its roots are deeper.

The turn to nationalism at the beginning of the 20th century, again, was in part a response to that. I mean, this idea that culture in the German sense was, some idea of culture could actually be an antidote to atomization. Actually, Matthew Arnold is already talking about in the late 19th century, where he thinks of culture as an antidote, both against kind of mass democracy and what he thinks is the leveling down. But it's also, again, a kind of revolt against atomization. And I think this inherent tension between the scale effects, the productivity effects, which are genuinely enchanting. I mean, the reason this is a difficult problem is, it's again, it's not as simple as this is good and that's it, that, you know, in a sense that is bad.

The benefits are so extraordinary, yeah. Is how do we find win-win solutions that actually mitigate some of the tensions between these?

从 Rawls 到 Smith：思想的弧线与"真诚的崩塌"

Yeah. And I just want to zoom back a little bit. And I'm going to be a little clumsy in how I phrase this because I want to reference a number of different works of yours. to look at not just the economic component of what you talked about, but also the cultural component. So if I go back and look at your 2003 piece, Passion and Constraints, you look at Rawlsian public reason against, you know, what's happening in India, Indian electoral law, and you find the Rawlsian framework useful, but also insufficient in some sense. Then if I look at your 2012 column, Public Reason Indian Style, and I've gone back to revisit that column multiple times. Actually, that column, I recommend all the listeners go back and read because it's very prescient in some sense. You talk about what's going on with media and media capture and the problems of social media and the 140 character limit and what that does to us. But everything you warned us about in that column has in some sense... come to pass, right?

And in your more recent columns, like you've argued that sincerity is not just an inner mental state, but it's also about social legibility, you know, or legibility of at least how we interact socially or social practice. And, you know, you mentioned McIntyre, you also wrote a recent column on McIntyre, where you argue that we've lost a shared moral language. Now, I don't know if I'm jumbling too many things together. But to me, this again seems like a very clear arc on how you've progressed. Like to me, it seems like you've gone from a Rawlsian framework where we look at institutions and test them and where they're sufficient, and then move closer and closer in some sense towards Smith's where we're now talking about the collapse of sincerity, a shared moral language, and without sincerity and a shared moral vocabulary, how can we even think of, you know, what is right or wrong or who has moral authority or who doesn't have moral authority? Is this a good way to both think of your own thinking or am I jumbling too many things together?

I mean, I just think you're being very, very generous in characterizing what was in those columns. So I think here's how I'd put it. I have to say, I've grown even fonder of Rawls, actually. I mean, despite kind of what I'm writing, I think I actually discover even more depth in him now than I probably did 10, 15 years ago. I always kind of loved both Rawls and Smith and in a sense thought you needed to, in a sense, engage with both.

I think what has changed or one way of telling the story is thinking about the sociological preconditions. And I think the column that you, one of the columns that you defer to the collapse of sincerity, if one just takes that as a kind of reference point. So the puzzle behind that column was actually a very simple one. And I'm sure any of you have engaged in public writing, writing, public discourse, which everybody does these days, right? That's what social media has done. It has empowered everybody to be a public figure in some ways. One of the very simple experiences you have is you will almost always be misinterpreted.

Now, you can make a philosophical argument about, you know, all act of interpretations are misinterpretations, right? But you know what I mean in a much more colloquial sense, right? And there used to be all of these arguments in the philosophy of language, going back to Paul Grice and so forth, which is, what are the conditions under which we even actually understand each other as engaging in acts of communication and meaning, right? One of the conditions used to be you actually attribute sincerity to somebody, right?

One of the things globalization has done with communication in particular is that it has completely collapsed all contexts. Right. And without, in a sense, any context, the basic requirements of what it means to make for do intelligible communication. Yeah. Right. How is this word actually being used? What is it's actually its function? Who the target is? Right. It's much harder to make these kinds of judgments. And. I often say that, at least in a sociological sense, Derrida has worn out that all signs have become indeterminate. Right? I mean, if you praise somebody in a very different context, somebody is asking, oh, but why hasn't, you know, this column praised so-and-so, you're reading silences?

So, it was actually just an observation of a kind of, almost a phenomenological kind of experience of why communication across contexts is actually becoming, in a sense, so much harder. Right? And once you find it difficult to attribute sincerity to any act of communication, and you find it difficult to construct or reconstruct the context, which actually gives that communication intelligibility, then the only function left for communication is either pure expression or, you know, in a sense, kind of fighting words, as it were, right? I mean, knowledge is made increasingly for cutting, not for understanding, right?

So at one level, you know, I think our challenge with this new information order is, I think, in a sense, even a deeper one, right? And I still like to think that it is much more of an institutional and sociological condition that it's maybe the kind of thing that people experienced when the printing revolution came, where structures that allowed you to attribute context, the trustworthiness of authorities and credibility, you went through a phase where all of that collapsed. And that there will be, hopefully through time, some kind of reconstitution of norms.

公共与私人界限的崩塌

Another example, I mean, for liberals, this is absolutely central, is, you know, the complete collapse of the distinction between the public and private. Yeah. Yeah. I mean, what we implicitly ended up doing was taking norms that were appropriate to private conversation and taking them to the public sphere. And I actually think it was, you know, by and large, even if it was not okay, but one of the advantages of a private sphere was that you could actually say things without those being your public identity. There was an opportunity for self-correction.

Right. I mean, you know, you observe that among students in a very trivial sense, which is, you know, people talk a lot about political correctness and censorship. The worry is not that there is actually an authority censoring you or that in classrooms there is a, you know, a monocultural ideology or the professors won't let you speak. The worry simply is that even a private conversation is can be put on meta or X, oh, so-and-so said this, and you are then stamped for life. In fact, your only option then is to kind of double down on that.

So a lot of these institutional norms that liberalism had supposed about the intelligibility of communication, context actually thickening our understanding of each other, the distinction between public and private, The modern information order has made those distinctions much, much more difficult to, in a sense, hold on to. So I'd still like to kind of, you know, defend the Rawlsian impulse in some ways, right, which is the fundamental question of society, which is how do we reconcile the terms of social cooperation with institutional conditions that acknowledge us as free and equal? I think that still remains the central question. But now doing this across these extraordinary disparate contexts all at once, I think has made our challenge harder.

制度设计之前：意识形态的力量

If I look at your earlier work, the way I think about it is you were really thinking about institutions from the point of view of design, capacity, and therefore what are the reforms that or, you know, sometimes major reform, sometimes minor tinkering, that will get us to the appropriate institution design, institutional capacity, and thereby incentives and outcomes. And this you did at the level of the constitution for all major, you know, central institutions in India, because that was where most of your work was rooted, and also local governments and things like that.

And now again, when I read your more recent work, the thread I see is that's not enough. It may be necessary, but it's nowhere close to enough because in all this, you know, institutional capacity, tinkering, reform discussion, we lost track of what are the broader ideological forces that both bind us and animate us and mobilize us in some sense. And we perhaps need to first understand the that ideological sense first, or what it means to be a citizen or what it means to be governed by a particular institution before we actually start fixing the institution. So one, again, is this a good way of thinking about how you're approaching these questions? Not to say you've abandoned the institutional agenda, but something else needs to precede that agenda to make it sensible again.

I think you're exactly right. Look, post-1989, you know, even if you didn't subscribe to the literal thesis, I think we all acted, not all, you know, many acted as if we were in a kind of end of history world, right? Which is, there is a broad consensus on evolving meta-norms of conduct particularly around the pacification of violence, the delegitimization of large wars, the delegitimization of sort of civil strife, you know, a whole range of kind of political phenomenon. So the main issue for democracies was, in a sense, how do you make them amelioratively better over time? And you took it for granted that almost all the actors in democracy were operating within certain bounds of conduct. I mean, they just did not have the will to ruthlessness that you're seeing in a sense, many actors now, right?

And so it was a tremendous time to think about institutions. You know, what are the incentives for judges? What are the rules of parliamentary procedure that might actually make, in a sense, deliberation, I think, you know, more effective? Now, Two things have been since. Once, as you have, in a sense, characterized, right, which is the attack, in a sense, on institutional life came not from where we thought it would come, which is institutions will internally get dysfunctional, the incentives will, you know, change, and the answer to that will just be better institutional design. The attack actually came from quarters which basically said, we actually don't care what the institutional design is. And moreover, if we can't win, we are actually willing to burn the house down with it.

职业身份的空心化

The second thing that I think that happened, and this is just a piece of kind of, you know, intellectual biography. So when we did the institution's work, and particularly some work with Divesh Kapoor, one of the projects we had in mind, which we never actually, which never got going, was, partly just turned out to be difficult to do, was actually a project on the creation of professional identities.

So many years ago when I used to teach modern political thought, what I was very struck by in the late 19th and early 20th century, there was extensive discussion of the professions. And not just in terms of service delivery norms, but in you know, for people like Durkheim, actually to some extent, even Max Weber, professions were actually going to be the central morality of modernity because they would actually give modern subjects a very specific identity. So if I say I'm a lawyer, I'm an officer of the court. If I'm an officer of the court, I'm how do I understand that phrase and internalize that phrase that actually disciplines me, commits me to certain moral norms, and commits me to defending certain kinds of institutions, right?

They were also interested in the professions because for them, professions were a kind of interesting class because, you know, the professions could be a bulwark both against the state and the market. Not that professions... are not embedded in a market. Obviously, professionals work for a fee and they are involved in market relations. But the basic idea was that the professions, the source of their legitimacy is modern forms of knowledge. Yeah. Right. With capital, it's capital creating more capital in some senses. With state, there's coercive power and the power that in a sense comes from legitimation in mass publics, right?

And so if you could, through these sociological experiences of the professions actually create a large class of middle-class professionals across a range of professions, right, who made their institutionals their professional identity. And therefore, were willing to protect those institutions, right? And through protecting them, actually decentralizing them. power, right? So if universities protect their autonomy, if courts of law protect their autonomy, lawyers historically were at the forefront of thinking of a constitutional culture, you achieve a lot of things simultaneously. You actually achieve a decentering of power. You actually give people a source of meaning because it has become your moral identity in a sense. And It's also the nice confluence of something that has become your moral and institutional identity also allowing you to sustain a living.

And I was actually very struck by the fact that, you know, maybe the post-1940s, 1950s, I mean, if you look at old journals, American Political Science Review and stuff, lots of articles in the professions. And then that kind of drops out, or rather it becomes much more a narrower question of very technical questions about professional ethics. There's some work in the 70s, I think a wonderful book by Abbott. It was partly on the psychology profession, but the theoretical frameworks there were quite interesting. There was this kind of missing middle, as it were, of what actually made institutions work.

And it came home to us. I mean, you know, I know it's something you are concerned with, which is I can speak of India, but I suspect there are parallels in the United States. One of the most disappointing things, as you know, over the last 10 years, has been the abdication of the judiciary. But the disappointment has not just been the abdication of judges and absolutely scandalous performance of the Indian Supreme Court. It has been the abdication of the senior bar, which which we always thought was one of the most powerful groups in Indian society. I mean, you know, who could touch, possibly touch them, right? That they would forget rising to the cause of liberty. They have reached a point where they're not even willing to do the sort of quotidian things that lawyers used to do, which is actually accept cases of people who are, in a sense, being targeted. I mean, you know, Ram Jaitpalani used to very proudly say, you know, I will defend anyone, right? And what is it about those professional identities that have actually become hollowed out?

I think the crisis of healthcare. I mean, one of the things I've been really struck by, I'm not a health economist and so will be agnostic on what organizational forms healthcare should take. But the number of medical professionals that in this country who we increasingly meet, but who at one level will say, look, there's been a lot of progress in a number of fields. But the sense of satisfaction they're deriving from their professions because of the institutional conditions under which they work. So should doctors be shareholders, right? What does that do to professional identity and that hope Durkheim had, right? That actually the basis of your legitimacy is your knowledge. The basis of your legitimacy is not that you're just another property holder. In fact, signaling that was actually extremely important, right?

So I think, again, that sociological story about what made institutions function, When he talks about lawyers in America, right, he says this is going to be the aristocracy American democracy needs, right? I doubt lawyers think of themselves in aristocracy in that sense, in aristocracy as in something that is actually a professional identity, right, that commits them to defending certain kinds of institutions.

民主化与"门槛"的张力

So is this just a byproduct of democratizing politics? education, professional services too much too quickly, I don't want to lean towards that explanation because for me, you know, at a principal level, it is we need to lower barriers to entry to make sure that anyone can be anything. But at the same time, you use the word aristocracy. And I like that better than the word elite because it has this intergenerational element as if we are custodians of a particular set of shared values and norms that we will make sure gets passed on. And that seems to have just completely disappeared. So I'm not surprised it's disappeared. It's difficult to have that kind of guild or shared sense of norms if anyone can be allowed to enter. And at the same time, we need that for these professional services, not just to provide identity and satisfaction and a sort of greater purpose, but also you know, to produce the next layer of knowledge and make it more accessible. So is this a fundamental tension and trade-off or can this actually be resolved?

So, look, like you, I agree with you both normatively and also I think empirically. I'm not entirely convinced that you can be in kind of premature democratization. I think it's become very fashionable. It's an easy, easy explanation in some ways. And I completely agree with you that, in fact, If you did not democratize, the profession would have been undermined in another way, which is why should anybody go along with an arrangement that actually closes off avenues and opportunities to them?

I think what happened in India, I mean, I think I can speak to that context a bit more. And I have one or two things about the kind of legal profession in the U.S. that might be worth thinking about. But I think in India, what happened is, in a sense, we've got the worst of both worlds, which is We did democratize, but democratized under conditions of institutional design that were designed to produce maximum inequality within that framework of inclusion.

I think the Indian legal profession is a perfectly good example. I don't think the problem with Indian legal profession was that we got large numbers of lawyers getting educated. In fact, that could have actually created a middle class of lawyers on the front lines of defending democracy. The problem we got with the Indian legal profession is that it is extraordinarily stratified and polarized. And a lot of this has to do with very quotidian incentives. I mean, it is still very hard for a first-generation lawyer to actually break into litigation and profession. Practices, particularly at the level of the Supreme Court, are extremely concentrated. Judicial norms where judges deferring to senior lawyers and enhancing their power. I mean, there's a whole range of things we can talk about. And so what you got is a kind of extraordinarily dual structure within what was supposedly a framework of inclusion, which is a few elite lawyers at the top and a mass who are being formally included, but not substantively included.

I think the same is true of Indian higher education, right? Which is we wanted inclusion on the cheap. Inclusion on the cheap was we'll just give everybody admission. Absolutely the right thing to do. But not asking the subsequent question, which is, if you're going to make this work, what else do you actually have to put in place? I mean, when I think the reservations were kind of expanded post-Mandal, I mean, I remember this conversation where Indian universities were asked to expand their seats and capacity 25% in a year. no education system in the world can do. And without any extra money, without any. And of course, the quality would get compromised in a meaningful way then. So you increased formal inclusion. You did not increase the commitment you made about how much you will spend this proportion of GDP. And again, we know spending is not always the answer. But capacity building, it's a proxy for that.

So what we got was We got formal democratic inclusion under conditions of extraordinary inequality. The same is true, by the way, of the Indian business sector, as you know, right? Extremely concentrated capital at the top, right? And we formally liberalized. We formally have done all the ease of doing business and stuff. And yet the structures of our institutional life, right? produce this incredible inequality within this broad frame of inclusion, right? And so you end up having actually the disadvantage of both worlds, which is you've not reaped the political gains of inclusion as much as you might have, right? And you still experience the pathologies of extraordinary inequality within the paradigm of these institutions.

亚当·斯密论权力的集中

You know, this concentration of PowerPoint, initially, I wanted to talk to you about that just in the context of economics. But I think you've given me something broader to think about. So this is the 250th year of the Wealth of Nations. You wrote a wonderful column about it. Of course, your dissertation was, you know, on the theory of moral sentiments. And we've talked about this before on the podcast. You boil it down to capture by special interests and concentration of power. Now this concentration of power is usually in terms of capital when we're talking in the business setting, but everything else that you just discussed.

So now it becomes that every regulation is a rent-seeking system. opportunity, right? Every subsidy is a transfer. Every sort of welfare entitlement, even when it is completely justified, becomes a vote bank concentration of power, which is at a different level than, you know, just business. And the logic of Smith points that points to the fact that the answer has to be a more limited state. As long as the state has this power to redistribute, the state has the power to grant favors and draft regulation in a particular way, you're always going to get some attempts at capture successful or unsuccessful.

So is there a tension within your Smithian analysis that If you have a large state that has the capacity to bring about all the other social changes want, then that large state is automatically at tension because it's going to create these opportunities. And if we have that state retreat, then it creates a vacuum. So my question is not so much should we go back to a night watchman state, but more... Like, what is the Smithian answer to this question? Or is there no Smithian answer? Smith just gives us a good diagnosis.

No, so I mean, I actually think there is a Smithian answer. I think it's just, I think it's more, it has more layers than what are kind of normally attributed to it. So the basic premise, I think, of Smith, and I think this should be a mantra for all liberals, which is a kind of a presumptive suspicion against concentrations of power anywhere and everywhere. I mean, in fact, I think if you ask the question, what defines a liberal sensibility? It is actually that, including, by the way, concentration of power in a single interpretation of the demos. I mean, fragmented power is in a sense, you know, and I think that is a sensibility, I think, consistently, right?

And you're right, right? Which is that, of course, if the state is empowered to do certain things legally, and if the ambit of that empowerment expands, you will get all kinds of rent-seeking. But I think Smith also, in a sense, had a parallel argument. And this one's slightly tricky because Smith is writing before the Industrial Revolution. I think the Industrial Revolution poses a challenge to Smith, not in terms of his principles, but in terms of how we think of power institutionally.

And so the second kind of compliment to thinking hard about what should the state and state should not do is Smith also thinks about the concentration of social power, right? So you can actually reverse the equation as well. On the one hand, you can say, if you have a limited state, there will be less rent-seeking. You can also reverse the equation by saying, if you have specially empowered groups in society who have a lot of power because they want a lot of capital or something, they will actually also get the state to, in a sense, produce rents for them. Right. That's what he thinks was partly happening with mercantilism. Yes. Right. That it's always, he was consistent. He said, look, it's always easy for small groups of capital to organize, right? The collective action problems are much lower, right?

If you look at our contemporary crisis, right, we talk a lot about the rent-seeking that might come from this combination of democratic populism and an expanded ambit for the state, right? Whether it's kind of cash transfers, the expansion of a welfare state. honestly, in the Indian context, it completely pales in comparison with what big capital has been able to extract. Absolutely. Right.

And so Smith's other kind of the other question he wanted you to ask was not what powers the state have, but what is the distribution of social power that actually ensures that the state does not get captured by one particular set of interests or forces. And of course, that That will vary by context. I mean, I think his image, and I think it's been true, actually, of liberals mostly. I mean, I think it's even true of Rawls, right? I mean, Rawls' model, if you want to call it an economic model, was property-owning democracy, but where it is very widely distributed. And that was for this political reason, right? That the minute you get certain kinds of concentration of power and capital, they will distort the state one way or the other. So you need a social force to counteract another social force.

同情之环、种姓与 Vishwas 政治

You know, on the social force to counteract another social force, this is where I want to think about your arguments about citizenship, right? In the burden of democracy, you know, you had argued that, you know, sort of caste-based or caste-organized inequality prevents these horizontal bonds. When we talked last about, you know, the theory of moral sentiments, you talked about how, and you've written about this many times, how poverty actually excludes people from the circle of sympathy, you know, as Smith discussed it. And this is really important in the Indian context, because when we interact caste and poverty relations, the number of people who are excluded from this circle of sympathy becomes larger and larger.

If I had to connect that then to your, you know, recent column, you had written wonderfully about Vishwas, which has become this sort of, I don't know, national tagline almost. You wrote about how the vertical faith, you know, or Vishwas, almost blind faith in political leadership or a particular leader has been substituted or replaced this horizontal trust that we have between citizens. But that horizontal trust is very difficult to form if there are people outside the circle of sympathy. So, one, is this a good way to think about where we are and why that's going on? And then I have a few follow-up questions on, you know, where we go from there.

So, This is a tremendously challenging question. I'll try not to be too long on this. No, no, take as much time as you need. I know I'm trying to connect so many dots in your work. So, in fact, you're making connections I haven't seen.

So, I think a couple of distinctions, right? So, as you rightly said, India had, in a sense, this twin problem, which is appalling rates of poverty and the existence of one of the most appalling social institutions in the form of caste, particularly for Dalits. I mean, it's just, even now, it's just hard to kind of imagine, actually, what that condition is, despite all the kind of progress we have, in a sense, made, right? And they produced both a form of invisibility in the policy process, which I think still exists, right? I mean, in a sense, the poor are almost always fending for themselves at the end of the day. But they also created a particular dynamics of the politics of exclusion and inclusion.

Vishwas 的三个来源

Now, we can have a kind of long conversation of where we went wrong in addressing these twin exclusions. But the politics of Vishwas... I think it had, in a sense, two sources. One, that phrase was actually, I think, first used by my CPR colleague, Nilan Jan Sarkar. I think he had done some empirical kind of work on this politics of Vishwas and the politics of faith, which is, it seems to me that it has three different sources in contemporary politics. And they've kind of aligned in a fortuitous way to kind of reinforce each other.

So let's first of all not forget that the biggest proponents of soul leadership, cult of leadership, are actually elites. And that's also true of the United States, right? And if you want to put the more charitable gloss on that elite support, it came from a place of saying, you know, all these things that you liberals care about, checks and balances, democracy, balance of power, separation of powers, these have become a hindrance in democracy being able to do things together, right? So this, you know, people don't openly say it, but somewhere if you scratch the right? The argument basically comes out, actually, these institutions are a problem. And they say it in the United States. There's an equivalent here as well. And again, as a descriptive level, you can see there is something to it, right? That there is a form of institutional complexity, the jostling of regulatory norms that takes place once institutions proliferate. The sense that that actually made the it's very difficult to do things. You know, you can't build railroads anymore. You can't, you know, build energy plants as quickly. So there's a kind of elite buy-in to the proposition that if somebody can just cut through this Gordian Knot, right, just the very thing that Tocqueville said was the strength of democracy, which is forms protect us. This was an argument that these forms of government actually disable us in some ways, right? That is, in a sense, one source of the politics of Vishwas.
