Host: I'd imagine it's such a key part of you being able to do what you do, having that kind of partnership away from the game that allows you to actually do this — because it's a massive sacrifice on her side if she's got a career and a job as well, being at the front end of childcare when you are off to the Caribbean for four weeks on a jolly.

Tymal: Literally. It's not easy and it's hard, and it puts a strain on you at times. But she's amazing, and she puts up with a lot. And I'm sure you've spoken to so many sports people, and I'm sure every one of them says, "we're selfish and we have to be, and we can always be more selfish." I could always do more if I really went 100% in on sport and recovery and everything else. I could literally never see my family if that's how I wanted to be. But yeah, we're selfish, but you justify it because it's a short career. You think, okay, I can pay now and get the rewards later. And also we love what we do. Like, I love playing cricket.

And I know when I retire, I won't be going to Barbados and Cape Town and Australia and getting the opportunity to do these amazing things. Well, hopefully I will — but chances are…

Host: You'll just have to take them with you.

Tymal: Exactly. And you've got to pay for a full holiday for everybody.

女儿中风：流动人生的脆弱断点

But yeah, I'm very lucky to have a wife who's supported me through that time. She met me, I guess, at the height of it. She knew what I was doing and stuff. It didn't come out of the blue, I guess. But as soon as you chuck kids into it…

And yeah, our daughter got sick. She had a stroke a couple of years ago. We were at the airport about to fly to Australia to play in the Big Bash. We were all going as a family to Perth, and she collapsed. And obviously I had to cancel that contract and we were in hospital for a few weeks. And that puts a whole other strain on things. You've got health [issues] — now, am I going away and leaving a child who, luckily she's recovered brilliantly, and she's amazing? But at the time you don't know what the future is going to look like. And that's more of a strain on, "okay, can I leave, or should I now be at home, and how do you juggle these things?"

So yeah, we've been through a lot, but we'll look back on this chapter of our lives as a really fun time and be better off for it.

Sussex合约：流动人生里的"国债"

Host: And the realities of that, I guess — it's a pressure on also the fragility of the contracts that you're working with and everything from a financial perspective.

Tymal: I've been lucky in terms of — I've had security in the UK in terms of my Sussex contract. And then now that we have The Hundred — even though The Hundred is a one-year contract — so I've done well in The Hundred and I've always had a good contract in The Hundred. So from April through to August, I know where I'm going to be. I know who I'm playing for. I'm at home and I'm playing cricket.

And then in the winter, that's the Wild West, I guess. Who knows what's going to happen. Hopefully I can get a repeat contract somewhere, but probably it's just going to be a case of seeing what I can get. And hopefully I get a contract with enough notice that my wife knows where I'm going to be — but it might be a case of, "can you fly tomorrow and go and play?"

But my county contract with Sussex has been pretty much the foundation of everything I've been able to achieve. Because it gives you just a security. It gives me a base. Financially, it's one of my lower contracts — or probably is my lowest contract that I'll earn throughout the year — but you get 12 months of the year coverage. So if I get injured in South Africa in January, I'm coming home to Sussex and I'll get rehabbed there. And I've got a gym and a physio and the support and everything I need, which as a player has been invaluable.

And if I need to go training, like before here — we're about to start the season for Sussex — but for example, last September, before I'm about to go off and play franchise cricket, I'm in at Sussex every day, in the gym, in the indoor school, in the nets, just practicing, training. I have access to the ground. So I'm lucky — a lot of players don't have that. If they go full franchise, full freelance, they don't necessarily have that. And it's 100% on them. Whereas what Sussex have given me has been paramount to everything that I've been able to achieve in cricket.

Host: And amongst all of the movement around the world, when you have that stability — what are the obligations from you to Sussex for that contract?

Tymal: So I have to get, for every contract that I apply for, I have to get an NOC — a no objection certificate — from Sussex, essentially, from your home board. One from the ECB and one from your county who you're employed by. So if I get picked up in the SA20, I need an NOC from Sussex to say "yeah, no problem." So they have to sign that.

And then now — they've only actually introduced it a couple of years ago — we have to take insurance out against our county contracts. So if I was to get injured playing in the SA20 and missed games for Sussex, Sussex wouldn't lose out financially on that. So they'd be able to claim on the insurance against my contract. You have to take insurance personally — we have to pay that.

And to be honest, it's PCA — the Personal Cricket Association — handle all that, and it's a pretty good deal. It's not hurting you too much, really, financially. And it makes sense. Fair play. We can go and earn whatever we want in the winter, but you have to give that security to your county. That's a trade-off right there.

Host: They're giving you the solidity. But also for you, as someone who is a specialist, to have that with a county where so much of county cricket has been in four-day games — that's great loyalty.

Tymal: Yeah, and as I said, I'm into my 12th year now with Sussex. I had a testimonial year last year. And the biggest message I had to give was: "I'm not retiring." Like, it was just — okay, they've given me a testimonial year, it's great, but every event I'm at: "okay, I'm still playing. I've still got two years left of my contract. I feel great."

But yeah, the club just have given me an awful lot, and I like to think I give back as well — with the foundation at Sussex and doing bits around. Because we live in Hove as well — I live five minutes from the ground, so I'm always around, I'm always available to do bits. Like tomorrow, I'm filming something for one of the club sponsors and those little things — they use me as and when they need.

英格兰国家队生涯：16场国际赛与两次世界杯

Host: Can we talk about England? We've moved all around the franchises, and international cricket is a really important part of that discussion because congested calendars and trying to align everything seems like one of the hardest jobs to do. You've also had a significant England career. You've been part of World Cup winning squads in probably the most successful short-form era of cricket that England's seen. But at the same time, when I was looking at the stats, I was like, "he's only played 16 games." But in my head, you played way more than that.

Tymal: Yeah, I don't really see myself as an England cricketer, if I'm being honest. So I've barely played.

Basically, I got brought in for two World Cups, but aside from that, haven't really played an awful lot.

I made my debut, as I mentioned earlier. Basically, when I made my debut, there wasn't an awful lot of international T20 cricket played. It was still of the era of 50-over cricket. So if I remember correctly, the summer I made my England debut, there was a one-off T20 and five ODIs. So I played the T20 and then I wasn't playing 50-over cricket. Again, then I think later in the summer they played Pakistan — I think, again, a one-off T20 and maybe three ODIs. So I'd kind of dip in for a squad, play a game and then be gone again.

And then to be fair, during my mid-twenties, I had a lot of injuries. I was injured quite a lot and not selected. So that was no real kind of gripe there.

And then the first year of The Hundred came about and I bowled really well. I was 28 maybe then, and I was one of the best bowlers in the competition. Jofra Archer got injured that year, unfortunately, so there was a space in the England team ahead of the World Cup, and I got picked for the World Cup. So that was a really nice moment, because that was about five years apart from my last England appearance to the next one.

And it was a World Cup in the UAE, it was just after COVID, and it was amazing. So I felt like an England player then. I played the first game against the West Indies in Dubai, played against Australia, was a leading wicket-taker for us. And then I pulled my quad in the last group stage game, I think it was against Sri Lanka, and had to sit and watch when we lost in the semifinal to New Zealand in Abu Dhabi. And it was terrible. It was only a minor injury, but I was out for a few weeks and that was that.

In the meantime, in the subsequent years, I had some other really good summers for England — particularly in The Hundred, performed really well — and then didn't get picked for England on the back of it. Kind of messaging was always around, "okay, we're going to have a look at other guys, younger guys, we know what you can do," that type of thing — which I personally pushed back on, because that doesn't help me at all. I want to play, I haven't played a lot.

And then I got picked for another World Cup in Australia that we won, but I didn't play. I was 12th man for the whole tournament. Myself and David Willey didn't play a game. So whilst you can say you're a World Cup winner, you're not really. In my eyes, you weren't on the pitch.

Host: No kid dreams about being on the bench for a World Cup final, do you?

Tymal: You dream about scoring the winning goal or taking the winning catch, bowling the winning wicket, whatever it might be. But you really don't consider yourself a World Cup winner because — you're a part of the squad, and the squad wins a tournament. It does, yeah. But as a selfish sports person, you want to — still, for that month, it was an amazing month or however long it was in Australia, an amazing country, the World Cup. I loved it, it was great. But yeah, you just — it's not the same.

It's not the same when you're not — especially when you play a single [game]. It's not like I played one of the group stage games. It's not like in football, where you can even say, "okay, I came off the bench three times." I literally just ran drinks for five weeks and trained. That was really my contribution to the squad. But I was ready if the opportunity would have come.

I was still proud to have been part of the squad. I'm not saying I didn't want to be there, but you'd rather have been there in that 11. It hits a little bit differently.

And then since then, I haven't featured for England.

Host: Eoin Morgan said he'd only consider you in a World Cup year, right?

Tymal: Yeah, again, it's frustrating. That was when I was a bit — yes, that was during the time where I was injured quite a lot. And for right or for wrong, I back myself. Even now, I feel like I'm still good enough to play for England. I don't know whether — I haven't had any contact with England for a few years now. But honestly, I'm okay with it. I just want to play as much cricket as I can, stay healthy, play at a high level, perform well.

Host: But you'd want to — you keep yourself available?

Tymal: Yeah, I haven't retired. I'm available if anybody wanted to pick me. But also, if that ship sailed, then that's fine. You can only control what you can control. But I still feel really good. My pace feels good. I'm confident. Like, look, we've got another busy summer coming up now — the Blast about to start, straight into The Hundred. That's all I'm focusing on right now, and what will be will be after that.

板球能承受多少板球？三种格式的角力

Host: We heard so much about the demands on players, and you mentioned previously about how many months of the year you're away. Beforehand when we were downstairs talking, we were talking about just the amount of cricket that some of these guys are having to play now. Do you think something has to give? Are we sustainable in what we're really asking for? And what I really want the response on — if sport (and you're a big NFL fan, right) — if sport is about the quality of a product, having more and more of it may be great for some means because you can sell more TV rights and you get more fans in the stadium. But if the product suffers and the cricket isn't as good because the players can't perform at the highest level, eventually that's going to have a negative impact on the whole cricket product.

Tymal: Yeah, I think the problem cricket has is that there's three formats of cricket. It's not just one game. There's T20 cricket, 50-over cricket and Test match cricket. So there's essentially three different sports within a sport. So naturally, you're going to saturate it, aren't you? Because it's not just one product. So that's the main issue, I think.

And players — there's so much choice now. But as I said, within most of the players you speak to in England, if you offer them a Test cap, they'd bite your hand off. And that still holds a lot of value, without a doubt. When I was younger, I wanted to play Test cricket for England. My decision was made for me due to injury. Whether I would have achieved it or not, who knows. But most players still want to play Test match cricket.

But the challenge is now that you can earn a lot of money not doing so and going off and doing T20s. 50-over cricket is obviously the format that's probably the most under pressure. The format players enjoy the least. There's less reward there. There's no domestic 50-over cricket really being played at all. Certainly none that pays as well as T20 cricket.

Host: It does just feel — I'm again a huge fan. Now I watch — I used to love it — and now I watch it and the first 30 overs, the way we're playing our Test match cricket, we're sometimes scoring quicker in Test matches than we are in the first 30 overs of ODIs. And then the last 20, I'm just watching a T20 game. So you kind of look at it and go, it's just really now become a combination of both of those.

Tymal: Yeah. And as you said, because now in England, obviously, the schedule is the most talked about thing in English cricket — and The Hundred taking primetime. And now no 50-over cricket is played at the highest level because it's played at the same time as The Hundred. So it's been neglected. And it just had to [give], because you can't fit all the cricket into the English summer. The English summer isn't long enough that you can fit, with enough space, a good product. So 50-over cricket was what had to kind of get pushed by the wayside. And unfortunately, we can't fit everything, and you can't do something that just fits everything and pleases everybody. And I think rightly they've made the decision to prioritize short-form cricket and Test match cricket.

灰飞烟灭的Ashes与新一代英格兰投手

Host: Observer's, fan's hat on. Pretty depressing winter for England cricket fans. There's been lots of talk of change. Now continuity's been confirmed. We look at different types of players coming in. The bowling department's obviously one that's got a significant impact shift and kind of change coming to it. You have Broad and Anderson holding down so much of that for so many years. It's inevitable, right, sport, when it happens. What do you think about the kind of depth and the quality of the bowlers that we have at the moment to try and fill some of that excitement within the Test squad?

Tymal: I think the irony is, I think Jimmy Anderson's the leading wicket-taker in the County Championship this year. Still bowling all the overs and taking loads of wickets for Lancashire.

But yeah, going back to the winter, I think with the Ashes — it was a shame because this Ashes was really built up to be: "okay, England have got a really good chance here. And if England are at full throttle here, they should win the Ashes." And I think the thing that probably I found, straight out — so I was in the UAE for most of it, and then South Africa — because the games were so short, there wasn't anything to watch. It got built up to this massive occasion. And then the first Test was over in two days. And then there was two weeks or whatever it was until the next Test. It loses momentum. And then the next Test doesn't go very long. And then another two-and-a-half-day Test. So it just felt really disjointed, which was a shame because I thought England were going to go there and do really well. I think everybody did. And it wasn't misplaced judgment at the time, I don't think.

But looking to where they're at now — yeah, it's a tough… It's always tough because the English Test summer now comes straight after the IPL. So some of these players are going to come in not ready, in terms of having not played and practiced four-day cricket. The bowlers — Jofra, for example — is going to have a pure diet of T20 cricket. Well, they put him straight into a Test match. They talk about bowling loads and red zones and spikes.

So then you're looking at county cricket and who's performing well. Ollie Robinson, my teammate down at Sussex, he's taking wickets pretty regularly down now at Sussex. He's just had a good game whilst we're recording now.

Host: Wants to come back as well, doesn't he?

Tymal: Yeah, of course he does. He's had a taste of it. He's done well. He just hasn't stayed fit when he has played. So from what I understand, he's been told that the door's open to him. We'll find out if he gets selected ahead of this summer. But he's saying all the right things and doing all the right things. He's captain now for the four-day stuff at Sussex and he's bowling his overs. He's taken wickets. He scored 100 at the Oval last week, so that helps.

But there's a lot of exciting players. Sonny Baker is bowling fast at Hampshire. Josh Tongue, who was probably one of the bowlers to come out of the Ashes with a bit of credit — he's now doing really well. Unfortunately… I said it. So Tongue's probably your one that he's hanging a hat on. He's going to play because he looks [the part] and he's doing well this summer.

But there's spots up for grabs, which as a player is exciting. And the England hierarchy, I guess, are making the right noises that they're going to start looking at county cricket a bit more and taking those performances more seriously now when picking the international side.

200分时代：当击球员把比赛搬到新维度

Host: Style in cricket has changed. I don't want to use "Bazball" because now we know what it means, but the style of our Test match cricket has changed dramatically. The style of even short-form cricket has changed dramatically. I had a stat here that I was looking at, which was ridiculous. I saw a video — it said in, I think it was 2009, there was one score over 200 in the IPL. Last year, there were 52. There are 46 already this year of scores over 200. So you've got England scoring 400 in a day in Test match cricket, you've got scores of 260 being chased in T20 cricket. This kind of shift is incredible on one side from a viewer's perspective. What does it do for a bowler?

Tymal: Yeah, it's hard, because it's about scoring runs now. The mindset you need to try and have with it is that it does create an even bigger opportunity — because as a bowler, if you can be the one bowler to do well in a game where all the bowlers have done poorly, it makes you stand out even more.

And it also — it makes the game, you stop looking at the game from 20 overs and you start looking at it in two-over blocks. Because say my team bats first and we score 250. Really, if you can bowl two good overs — if you can have two overs that go for five in that 20 overs — you're probably going to win. Because the run rate for the rest of those overs has to be so high when it's already high. It has to be astronomical. Does that make sense?

So there's opportunity there. It's just managing — I guess you have to focus on the small details so much. Your margin for error becomes so small, because in this situation, the conditions are clearly so against you. If a team is able to score 240, 260, that clearly the batters are on top here. And then also it won't be like that every game. You will have games where it's your day, or it should be your day — but it's making sure you capitalize on them, because you need that credit in the bank for when you do have a bad day, which is coming.

You need those good memories from your coaches, your captains, the selectors, whoever it might be. Because if you're constantly just going around the park, then you're not going to find yourself in the team.

Host: Do you think it's changed Test match bowling? If I look at the aggressive format that England have played now, do you think that changes in the requirements for bowling in a longer-form game when batters are being far more aggressive?

Tymal: I think it tests your tactics and your relationship with your captain. Travis Head was brilliant with it in Australia. He put the bowlers under pressure and forced you to change — change the field. Because in the Test — I've never played Test match cricket, so it's difficult for me to say — but you want to have four slips or three slips and a gully, a couple of men around the back. That's for most of the day. That's what you want. That's traditional Test match cricket, isn't it? And then, okay, they might hit you for a cover drive through the covers, they might hit you for a pull shot, but okay, we'll live with it.

Whereas now the ball's flying early and suddenly, okay, well, we can't just keep all the men in because then it's going to keep scoring boundaries. So then you have to spread the field. Then it gives them easy singles everywhere. Then you can't build any pressure because they're either able to hit a boundary or just get off strike. Because what a bowler wants to do — you want to bowl dot balls. You want to think, "I want to bowl a good ball, and it's either going to get them out or they're not going to score." That's the fundamentals of bowling, of Test cricket really.

Whereas now you get thrown off your length, or the field has to spread, you bowl your really good ball and they're just getting an easy single, and then all of a sudden they're going at five an over without even having to take a risk. So that's where the successful aggressive batters in the modern game, I think, have challenged the bowlers most.

数据与背景：经济率 vs 平均数

Host: I mean, I was looking at — you played 259 T20s. You got an average of 23.36. That was according to Wiki, so maybe — I mean, give or take. That's outrageously good for T20 cricket, right?

Tymal: Yeah, but you take more Wickis. I think for me, anything under 25, I think, is a good average in T20 cricket. Anything under 20 is excellent. I'm sure Bumrah and these type of guys — I don't have his numbers to hand — but I'm sure he'd be under 20. So that's, yeah, I'm happy with that. You're happy with 20. I'm happy with 20.

Host: It's your economy rate now, though, to be honest. I'd rather have a better economy rate [than a] better average.

Tymal: That's so hot. Yeah, again, as we were talking about with economies now, I mean, surely isn't it a wicket-taking — you'd maybe give 20 runs in an over if you can get Travis Head out.

Yeah, so that's where context is massive. The game, who you're bowling at — so that's where stats can be tough. Because you can look at a bowler — could bowl four overs and take one for 50, which is a bad day by anybody's metric. But if that one wicket was the batter that was on 100 or 90 at the time and he still had four overs left, that one wicket is really valuable. And even though you've had a bad day there — or even if you're bowling second, you've had a bad day, you've bowled three overs for 40, but you've got to defend 11 off the last over. If that last over goes for 10 and you win, you've bowled four overs for 50 — but you won the game because of that last over. And that won't come up on the scorecard or in the Wikipedia. So context is really important within cricket to take stats away a little bit.

死亡之球：最后一个over的剖析

Host: Okay, so you've been doing some really interesting stuff alongside the game and I want to use that as a segue to talk about it. Because I saw — I'm going to hijack your really good content and have you explain it for everyone, because I loved it, it was great. You're bowling the last over in a T20 game. How are you approaching those six balls?

Tymal: Again, ball one — it doesn't even start there. So because by the time you've bowled the first ball of your last over, you've already bowled three overs in the game. So you're already in a position where either you're going well, you're going badly, or you're in the middle. So your confidence — that affects your confidence. If I bowled three overs and I've already been hit for 40 runs and then I've got to go bowl that last over, you need to be able to push those three overs away. That's really important. If you're coming into it with your head down, your shoulders a little bit down, that's going to translate into your performance.

That's really important because T20 is such a short game. You've got to be able to identify who you're bowling at. So I'm quite big on doing my homework before the game. I go out onto the pitch with a piece of paper that I've written down with the analyst — the names of the batters, whether pace-on or pace-off is a good option, and whether they ramp (so whether they play a scoop shot, a tickle across).

So I've got that, so I know that — because once you're in the game, you might forget it. We'll have an analysis meeting the day of the game or the night before the game, but when you're out in the middle, you might forget it. So I actually write it down on a bit of paper. So: pace-on or pace-off? Are their strike rates higher against pace-off or pace-on bowling? Because that gives you an inclination of whether to bowl slower balls or whether to bowl faster balls.

So those are little things that I do on the pitch to help me. And then gut instinct's a big part of it as well. You need to analyze the wicket. Because by the time it gets to the end of the over, end of the innings, some of the game has already happened, so you'll know — okay, slow balls are working on the wicket today, or slow balls aren't working on the wicket today.

The boundary sizes play a big [role]. If you've got a short leg-side boundary, you need to go wide. So that's the hardest thing about T20 cricket at the moment — you don't always play in the middle of the pitch. So you often have a short boundary on one side. So the batters will target that short boundary wherever they can. So there's a lot of times in cricket where you know what's going to happen. I'm bowling to a short boundary on the leg side. The batter's going to try and hit it there. I'm going to try and bowl it wide to try and get him to hit to the long boundary. And it's just a case of who can execute the best.

And those are the tough situations as a bowler because you don't really have anywhere to go. You can't really bowl a bouncer to the short boundary because you're probably going to get hit for six. Unless you can bluff. And then it's just a roll of the dice.

So sorry, to answer it — we've got all of that and you haven't even bowled a ball yet.

Host: Haven't bowled a ball yet.

假设场景：在RCB群星里选一个对手

Host: So you've got to assess all of that. Who are you bowling at? Are they in good touch? Are they going well? Who's at the other end? Firstly, okay — on the other end of this, you're back to your RCB team. You have to choose one of the following to face: Head, Kohli, AB, Gayle.

Tymal: Oh, Gayle and De Villiers.

Host: As in to be on my team?

Tymal: No, they're who you're bowling at right now.

Host: Okay, it's a tough day out. Because obviously by the time you're bowling at them, they're probably in. De Villiers was probably the worst because he was known as Mr. 360 — he could hit you, he could manipulate the ball to anywhere he wanted to on his day. If you typed in his highlight reel on YouTube, it'd be amazing — just the entertainment value. And I was lucky enough to play with him and taste a little bit of it first-hand and see it and experience it. And yeah, he was special.

Host: Okay, so you're bowling this last over — who do you want to bowl at in this situation? I'm giving you, you've got the best chance of winning a game. I know there's not many positives in there.

Tymal: Yeah, it's tough because you'd probably say Travis, but at the moment he smokes it everywhere. But Chris Gayle in his pomp could literally hit every ball out of the ground. He's such a big man and hit the biggest [sixes], because those guys don't need to get the middle of the bat. They just need to get a bit of it. You know, as long as it's not the edge, it's probably going to go for six.

That's one thing we say in analysis — is conditions dictate your plan. Because it doesn't matter who you're bowling at. If you've got a short leg-side boundary, you have to bowl wide most of the time. You can throw the odd ball in straight, but if you keep feeding it in straight, they're just going to hit you to the leg side. So you try as a bowler — you try to not bowl to the man and you've got to focus on yourself and execute. Because ultimately we are the ones in control because we are the ones delivering the ball. The batter has to then react to what we do. So in theory, if I nail my skill, I should win. It doesn't always work like that, but at least we're the ones in control of what we do as a bowler.

Host: And the skill you need now for that — let's just use again, like a last over — you're looking at bowling a mixture of back-of-the-hand slow balls, you're looking at cutters, you're looking at like Yorkers. I mean, there's a lot of skill, a lot of options to this, right?

Tymal: A lot of options as well. You need a lot of options. So it's choosing the right option at the right time. You need luck as well. Like, T20 cricket is a massive game of luck. I could bowl the same ball back-to-back. One could take a wicket and one could go for six. Because then it comes down to the batter's execution.

I could bowl you a ball on the stumps and you could just miss it and I look great because I've taken a wicket. Or I could bowl that exact same ball and you can hit it for six. So cricket's a funny game. I've had some games where I bowled rubbish and not executed where I wanted to execute, but you end up with four wickets because the batter hasn't quite timed it and you've got them caught out on the boundary somewhere. And then you'll have other games where you've bowled really well, or feel you've bowled really well, and the batter's just beaten you and you've been out-executed.

自我分析：在变量风暴中保持诚实

Host: In all seriousness, the mentality of that is quite challenging, right? Because you probably can't be too self-critical to some extent. There's an element of the controllables you can control, and then the things that are just part of the game. How do you compartmentalize that as a player?

Tymal: That's really important. I think self-analysis, being honest with yourself in cricket, in T20 cricket, is really important. Because whether I have a good game or whether I have a bad game, I'll watch it back after. I'll watch my bowling back, because in your mind's eye, it's often different to how it actually looks. You might think, "I executed that really well," but then you watch it back and you think, "actually, no, I missed by half a foot and that's too much."

So that's a really important part of it. Being honest with yourself — okay, did I have a good performance there because I bowled well, or was it because I got a bit lucky and the batter on another day probably could have hit me for six? Or likewise, my figures are really bad today, but did I actually execute really well? Were my plans the right plans?

That's one thing we speak about a lot in cricket is: you can't always control the outcome, but you can control the process. Okay, have you set the right field? Is that the right plan to these conditions? And have you just lost out to the batter?

That's really important in cricket because it's such a variable sport. A batter can — big swing of the bat and it's an edge and it goes over the keeper. That's not — you haven't done anything bad, but that's just bad luck. You've executed well, the batter got the luck on that occasion, but it's gone down as four runs or six runs against you. And that's why I say context is so important within cricket. But yeah, self-analysis and honest self-reflection in cricket is massive.

OnlyFans之约：把内容当成第二事业

Host: So the reason I asked you that question about the last over was because you put up something online not long ago about breaking down the anatomy of bowling a final over. And you've been doing a lot of work — or should we say, what looks like an increasing amount of work away from the pitch, doing things with some really cool content. You've done some interesting partnerships that I'm sure you can bring up now. Why is it so important for you to be doing work alongside your cricket, away from the pitch, while you're still playing?

Tymal: I think it's important in all sport. I think you speak to people from all forms of sport and it's the way the world is at the moment. I want to be successful. I want to be a successful cricketer for as long as I can. And then whatever I do after cricket, I want to be successful — or as successful as I can be. I don't know exactly what that looks like yet, but I know I want to do cool things, go to cool places, meet cool people, be able to provide a good life for my family. Those are things I know I want to do. And social media and the internet, it's a huge part of it.

And I've always kept one eye on it. So I've done commentary and broadcast work alongside playing pretty much my whole career. So since I retired from four-day cricket back in 2015, I've been doing commentary. I work with the BBC a lot during The Hundred on TV, on the radio. I've done work with Sky, talkSPORT, those companies. Because I've got time. I'm not playing four-day cricket. I'm only playing T20, so I physically have the hours to go and do it. It's not like I'm playing a four-day game and that takes up your whole week and then you're knackered and you don't want to do anything else. I'm playing a T20. I might have three weeks off where there's no T20. I can go do a bit of broadcast work.

But social media — I've just been a bit vanilla. Just done the bare minimum. Accepted the posts, just the basic stuff. My wife's always saying, "you should do more, your social media could be a lot better." Because I've never been one of the big names in cricket. I've never had an England contract — well, I have, but not been an IPL regular. You know, these guys that have millions of followers on Instagram — it's achievable. I've never been in that realm. So for me to do something on social media needs to be impactful and worthwhile.

So, yeah — to what you alluded to — obviously an opportunity came about with OnlyFans last year, which, yeah, I ummed and ahhed about for a while. But that kind of triggered and gave me no excuse really to push what I'm doing on socials and just be brave really and try some new stuff.

Host: And this was a different type of opportunity? OnlyFans content strategy to the one that everyone I'm sure is thinking about?

Tymal: Very much so, yes. The deals I had — the contracts expired now. But yeah, it was all obviously just safe-for-work content. It came about — my brother-in-law runs an influencer marketing company, mainly in football, was working with OnlyFans, who wanted footballers on the platform, were struggling to do so. And then he put me forward and eventually got on a meeting with the OnlyFans people, told them about cricket, what I do, where I go, kind of what the life looks like. They thought, "that sounds quite interesting." And yeah, so I kind of eventually signed this deal with OnlyFans to be their first cricketer endorsed athlete. Yeah — sponsored by OnlyFans.

体育内容的下一站：旅行、分析、自我曝光

Host: But there's so much right behind the scenes that people will want to see now. And that's, if you're looking at where that type of partnership can go — you can create content around your life that people don't see yet, it's probably actually now some of the most interesting parts of being a professional sports person. How do you train? How do you eat? What's the travel like? Where do you stay? Like, especially when you're on the move for so much of the year and you're going to some really cool places. But then also, it's like we just talked about — again, the anatomy of bowling a death over — you can go and explain it. I've seen you do stuff highlights and you'll talk people through that over. So an OnlyFans deal opening up the industry to the work that you're doing and kind of giving a lens into that world, right? That's the next evolution of athletes really taking control of their own content.

Tymal: Yeah, and I think it's value added, isn't it? And cricket's a bit behind — similar to I'm sure other sports — in terms of it's quite a safe, you know, a bit backwards, in terms of we're not pushing the envelope really. Or teams maybe are playing catch-up now. But again, if I can try and be ahead of it and get ahead of the game in terms of what I'm producing, and if people find it interesting, that's the main thing.

And I think the low-hanging fruit is the travel side of things. Because traveling isn't that exotic really, but it's just — okay, it's tricky because you can't… You've got to be careful because there's always other people at stake. So I can't stick a camera in the changing room whilst we're having a team talk or whatever because there's other people's [privacy]. I can only speak for myself. I can't expose other people potentially or get other people in situations that they don't want to be in. But I can speak for myself.

It's just a case of being brave and putting myself out there, and understanding what the repercussions are going to be. And, you know, affiliating with OnlyFans for example, I knew that there would be backlash on that side of things, but kind of weighing everything up and also just being comfortable enough that I'm not doing anything untoward. I'm doing cricket content, lifestyle content.

And I think the analysis stuff is probably the bit that I've found was the most successful, because we actually have really good access to our footage and we're allowed to share it. And talking through — because if I play a game on TV, you might do a flash interview after the game, you give pretty generic answers on TV. You could probably predict what I'm going to say before I'm going to say it. And then I'll go back into the changing room. But what I found was I actually really enjoyed — okay, open up my laptop, playing the footage, and talking about what I'm doing: "okay, this was the scenario, this was the equation, they needed X off X, I'm bowling at him." And just talking and trying to give a bit of insight into what we're actually thinking whilst we're out there. And being honest, and showing the bad as well as the good. Because it's easy for us to just put all the good stuff out there and how great we are. But okay, what happens when you get hit for six off that last ball and you lose a game? What you're feeling.

So I'm going to now migrate and start doing stuff on YouTube instead of OnlyFans, which is obviously behind a paywall. But really grateful for that partnership because it gave me that kick up the arse, really, to confidence to — yeah, just to do it. I basically used the money from OnlyFans to help pay for a team to help me do the editing and all the stuff that I'd never do on my own, and to make it look good and to take away the hard work of it, and try and just make it — as I said, whatever I do, I want to do it well, and I want it to look good and look like it's not just been hashed together quickly on my iPhone whilst I'm on the train or something.

But yeah, I'm excited about it and trying to just be brave. What's the worst that could happen?

板球与OnlyFans：当ECB打来电话时

Host: OnlyFans in cricket aren't the most natural partners. What was it like when you announced the deal?

Tymal: Yeah, so we announced it — the timing, not coincidentally, was right at the start of The Hundred last year. And yeah, so a lot of banter, obviously, between the boys. I told a few of the guys that knew me — I told them and it was fine. But then obviously we announced it on socials, and then obviously all the other players in the tournament — it was a lot of banter, all the everything you'd expect, all the questions that have been asked.

I got asked, "what's that group?" Yeah, it was all the standard stuff. But it obviously rang some alarm bells at the ECB as well. I had some phone calls from those guys.

I'm not an England contracted player, so I didn't have to. So I spoke to Sussex, because Sussex I am contracted by. They're my employers. They're the people that, as I said earlier, have looked after me for a long time. So I spoke to them beforehand to make sure that they were comfortable with it. And they were great. Sussex were outstanding. The then chief exec and the commercial officer were sweet. They backed me, because at the end of the day, they knew I'm not doing anything on the channel that's going to cause anybody any problems. That's the fundamental, that's the bottom line.

But yeah, when we announced it with The Hundred, that morning of our first game, I had quite a few phone calls from the ECB and various different people. Wanting clarification and yeah, maybe flapping a little bit.

Host: I'm just picturing the boardroom when the message comes through to the powers that be in cricket.

Tymal: I was wearing an OnlyFans hoodie, cricket bat, sticker — you know, OnlyFans on my bat and stuff.

Host: But the stick is the one thing — you said that that's the thing that you have to negotiate, or that's the say that they're allowed to have over what you do advertise and what you don't.

Tymal: Yeah, so cricket is very limited in terms of what you're allowed. You're not allowed — you know, a UFC fighter can slap labels all over his shorts, can't he, or a boxer, or whoever. But cricket, we only have one thing, and it's: you can have a sticker on the back of your cricket bat. That's your only advertising space, really. I didn't realize you actually had to get approval for that, because it's kind of an unwritten — it's not a rule that gets forced through, because normally insurers and stuff don't require [approval]. It's normally pretty friendly.

So yeah, I had to take the sticker off the back of my bat. The ECB asked me to take the sticker off, because it goes against the values of the competition, et cetera, et cetera. Which was fine, no complaints from my side, understood. Took the sticker off and that was as much as it went. And like all these things, it's news one day, for a couple of days, and then, you know, it's fish and chip paper in the days after.

即将到来的赛季：Sussex队长与London Spirit

Host: Can we finish off on the last section? You — what do you got now coming up in the summer? You're still very much playing. None of this — I want it to seem like it's kind of fading out. You're fit, which is really important, touch wood. Always. You've signed a two-year deal again.

Tymal: Yep, so that's my Sussex contract.

Host: So we've got the Blast — that's still the major feature. And also we've got The Hundred. And there's been so much about The Hundred. This is the first year that we're playing — The Hundred is playing with new investment, which has obviously been massively talked about. But you've been in it literally since the first iteration. You won the first title with Southern Brave. You're the leading wicket-taker in The Hundred. From a player's perspective, how have you kind of enjoyed and approached the competition?

Tymal: I've loved it. And I think 90% of the players would say they've loved it as well. I saw Jimmy Anderson did a quote not so long ago saying he hated every second of it — which obviously got a lot of traction. You wouldn't expect anything else. I know, and that's fine. It isn't for everybody, clearly. But I know a lot of the players — A, obviously we love it because the pay is good, and the pay is extra money that wouldn't have been there otherwise. Let's just be honest. We're getting paid more to play in another competition in the height of our summer, in front of great crowds. And there's not many negatives from a player's point of view, if you're involved.

Obviously, the players that aren't involved in The Hundred might look at it and think it's rubbish because they haven't been able to access it. But pretty much every game I've been at for five years has been at least 70% full, if not sold out. At Southern Brave, we had great attendance. The women's games were largely sold out even before our games in the evening. I've broadcast on games as well as playing and being around the ground. So many kids are there.

And I understand it takes away a chunk of the summer that other people want to see used for other forms of cricket. But as somebody who's been involved in it and actually played in it, it's comparable to other high-level T20 tournaments I've played in around the world. Unfortunately, the Blast just can't replicate [that], just with how stretched out it is, with 18 Blast teams. You can't replicate the quality that you can with eight teams. You condense the talent, you funnel everything into something a bit more condensed. And you can't televise the Blast — like, you can televise every game of The Hundred. I understand how it's not for everybody.

It's not that different to T20, once you start. That first year, we all came into it like, "okay, what is this going to be like? We're all five-ball overs, we're not changing ends after the first five, we're staying there." And everyone thought it's going to be alien. But actually once we played maybe two games, you actually pick it up pretty quickly. And everyone now is pretty seamless in how they go about it.

London Spirit、拍卖会的等待与被忽略的滋味

Host: And you played, as I said, Southern Brave all the time up to this year. And then now you've just been signed by London Spirit. It's down the road.

Tymal: Yes. So The Hundred — so I played for Southern Brave for five years and I loved it. And I'd have happily gone back. We were successful. So basically us and Oval Invincibles were probably the two best teams over the five years. Oval Invincibles were the best team. We were probably the second best. And that's because they nailed the year one. It was just a complete open slate draft, your team. And we drafted really well. And as a result, we kept our team together. Because we did well, nobody wanted to leave, and we kept the core of our team together. Other teams had a lot of churn, a lot of change in those five years, and we were always kind of chasing it. Whereas we were able to keep our team together.

But this year, with all the new investment, all the teams with new ownerships, there was a natural time to reset things. So each team could only go into the auction with four players, essentially two retentions and two signings. I wasn't retained or signed by Southern Brave. You couldn't be signed by another domestic team — you can only be retained by your current team or go back in the auction. So yeah, I ended up back in the auction a couple of months ago now, which was a horrible day, actually. I hated it. I've been in a lot of auctions that have gone well. I've been in a lot that have gone badly. And yeah, it was a tough day. It was a long day. I came up late in the auction.

Host: Can you explain how it works? Because the structure of that — you have 50 top players, right? And then there's different categories?

Tymal: Yeah. So the auction day itself is a long [day]. It was all day. And yeah, they kind of ranked it — or it was done on a nomination system. The first — there was the top 50 players obviously came up first, and when that list came out, I wasn't a part of it. And yeah, I had my nose put out a little bit, if I'm being honest. Leading wicket-taker, yeah. I was a bit upset and a bit frustrated. Spoke to my agent about it — typical agent just trying to calm you down, "it'll play out, yada, yada, yada." But still, it was a tough day.

But obviously I knew I wasn't in that first 50, but then after that it's kind of done behind the scenes and you're not quite sure when you're going to come out after that first 50. So the morning was fine and I watched it in the gym or wherever I was and watched guys go for various prices. And then you're just kind of waiting all afternoon for your face to appear at the bottom of the screen. And it just took a while. And it got to the mid-afternoon, I'm still not coming up, and I'm panicking, I think. Because obviously by this time teams are punching players and their purses are going down, and other bowlers are being signed, and you don't know how it's going to go.

And then eventually I came up. And yeah, it was London Spirit and Manchester Originals for me. And then yeah, I ended up with London Spirit. And yeah, I can't wait. I think they put together a really strong side. And I can't wait for the middle of July to roll around.

Blast + Hundred = 一个完整的英格兰之夏

Host: How does it compare playing The Hundred and Blast? Because you've now pretty much got those right from what — the Blast starts next week.

Tymal: Next week. Friday. So this Friday when this will probably release.

Host: Does it kind of feel like one continuation?

Tymal: Yeah, it does, yeah. It's just a summer of cricket now. I still love playing in the Blast because I'm captain for Sussex. This is my 12th season at the club. I live in Hove. It's home. I was there this morning. I'm there most days of the week training. And it's one competition I've never won. Lost in the final, lost in a few semifinals. So I'd love, before I stopped playing, to win the Blast for Sussex. So I have a vested interest in Sussex — it still means a lot to me.

And it's great. As I said, I get to be at home. I'm sleeping in my own bed. I'm still taking my kids to school. The kids can come and watch the afternoon games that we have. And it's really nice.

And then that leads into The Hundred, which is a bigger deal broadly, widely. I know if I do well in The Hundred, that will help for franchise stuff in the winter. There's more eyeballs on it. Your teams are now owned by IPL teams or powerful people — the guys that own London Spirit are Microsoft and Adobe, these really cool people from another world that you're going to get to meet and interact with and open up a few more doors, hopefully.

So yeah, it's just — the English summer is the highlight of my year, because I get the best of both worlds. I get to be at home for the most part in my own bed, as I said, with my kids around, and just have a good fun and then still play high-quality cricket. And then in the winter, go off and see what happens.

The Hundred 的未来：是否走向T20化？

Host: It's made the splash it needed to. Do you think The Hundred becomes eventually England's T20 IPL, and do you think it goes to 20?

Tymal: Maybe. I don't know. I personally, as a player, don't see the need to. I think the one thing that would make it tricky would be — it works really well at the moment because you have the women's games and the men's games. So you have a women's match normally in the afternoon and then the men's match in the evening. Or the women on the weekend play late morning and the men in the afternoon. That works really well time-wise because obviously a game of The Hundred is shorter than a game of T20 by nature.

So I don't know how it would work if all of a sudden you changed it to T20, because that makes a longer day, longer broadcasting day, longer day for everybody. Whereas The Hundred does work pretty well from a timeframe point of view. So I'll be interested to see what they do with that. It'd be a shame if they split the men's and the women's competitions up, because I feel that's the one thing that they stumbled across. That was never the plan, but because of COVID and they needed to condense everything a bit easier — having the women's games and the men's games together has worked really well.

Like, we get to know the women's team better. I know more about women's cricket now than I did before The Hundred, because you're around it more. You obviously get to the ground and watch the end of the women's game because you're straight on after. As soon as that women's game's finished, you're on the pitch to warm up for your game. So there's that synergy there. You're in the hotel with the women — like Southern Brave, the Southern Brave women were excellent. And quite often they'd play, they'd win, and then they'd sit up in the changing room on the beers watching us. And then after the game, hopefully we'd won, and then you'd be in the hotel bar and stuff. And it was nice. It created a nice atmosphere.

So I hope they don't change it from that point of view, because these people that have bought the teams — they've bought two teams, they've bought the women's team and the men's team. It's not a men's product or just a women's product. So I hope it stays as The Hundred, but I think still a couple more years left on the broadcast cycle where things stay in place. But I've got no idea.

Host: Do you think the international players, from the ones that you've spoken to — you said you loved it from an international standpoint, the ones that are moving around — are they enjoying it the same?

Tymal: Similarly. So I said how I love the SA20 because going to South Africa for four weeks in the summer is amazing. Foreign players love coming to England. You said it, not me. But London, for example — you're not going to find too many guys from South Africa, Australia, say, "I'll come to London for four weeks to play in The Hundred." Guys love coming to England in July and August and traveling around and playing cricket. It's still a desirable thing for most people to do. So yeah, we're not going to struggle to get good quality overseas, especially now the money's gone up as well from all the new investment. So I don't see it taking a backward step in that regard.

退役不远，但远未到来

Host: So, you know, all of this put together is incredible. It's a very, very different type of story. It's a different story coming into cricket in the first place. And then it's a very different story having to pivot and be a relatively early adopter of, shall we say, a more focused and targeted professional career. While that may have been really cool, you know, I've no doubt that has presented lots of different challenges. What now — you know, you're 33 years old, you've got multiple interests, different passions, you've got family. What's the plan?

Tymal: I want to play for as long as possible. I still love playing. Because at some point, for a lot of people, it becomes a job and it becomes a chore. But I still love it. I still love standing at the top of my mark in control of my own destiny. And it sounds a bit cheesy, but those moments when you win a game, I bowl that last over and defend a score, or I take that wicket to remove a batter — I know I will never get those moments again when I retire. So as soon as I stop playing, that part of my life stops. And hopefully you can replicate it elsewhere. But I want to play for as long as possible.

And also financially — my earning potential won't be as high. Well, hopefully it is, but probably won't be as high as what it is as a player. And I want to capitalize on both of them as much as possible. I'm a typical sportsman — I want to have my cake and eat it too. I want to play at the highest level, do all these amazing things, take my family around with me where I can, let them see these cool things, start doing other things around it. I want to do it all.

And I think you can, if you've got a good support network around you. I spoke about my wife earlier and she plays a massive part in that and supporting me, whilst also trying to advance her own career. And it's hard. But no, I want to play. I want to play until the wheels fall off, until I can't play anymore — or until an opportunity presents to me that's too good to turn down. And hopefully I'm putting myself in a situation where, off the field, I won't be struggling when I stop playing cricket. I'll have something immediately to build upon or to work towards, or I've met [people].

I think with cricket, we're so fortunate — we meet a lot of cool people around the world. It's such a global game. You never know who you'll meet in what pocket of the world who could lead you into something down the line. Because I'll be retired at some point before I'm 40, I'd imagine. And then I've got a long time to do something else. And you never know — that person will highly likely come from my cricketing network.

板球迷的世界与最后的余味

Host: The most surprising cricket fan you've met — oh, you didn't know you, so passionate about… you replied to my message like, "yeah, I absolutely love it."

Tymal: I absolutely love it. It is the sport. I think it's a sport that if you like it, you love it.

Host: Yeah, like, it's so unique and intricate and there's so many intricacies to it. If you meet a cricket fan, they're mad about it. You don't get many casual cricket fans, I don't think. No. But I also, I've become very aware, like recently — I was lucky enough to grow up playing cricket. Like, I played it all the time. I was lucky enough to get that exposure. And you definitely take that for granted if you just had it and done it.

And it was when my wife, who loves cricket now — but that was kind of a late thing in the last seven, eight years, probably, because she had to put up with the amount of times it was on the TV. Whether it's women not getting the same access that we had — and she just said, you know, she was going through BBC commentary, and the language was so advanced. As in, the words they were using, how the language was being put forward. It was like, if I'm just coming to the game, if I don't know what a Yorker is or a googly…

Tymal: So I worked with the BBC. So during The Hundred, when The Hundred started, every game's on Sky, and then the BBC have a free-to-air package — 10 games maybe. So I've been broadcasting with the BBC around my playing commitments. And especially in those early couple of years, we were literally walked through how we needed to explain everything and strip it back. Because, you know, going to free-to-air and a new audience. So things that we take for granted as cricket people, that we assume everybody knows, most people won't know. So when you're talking about fielding positions, you're having to explain what it is. You're talking about the off-side, the leg side — you need to explain what that is. And it took a while to get used to.

But even as you were saying, "oh yeah, no, we're bowling, we want to have four slips and a gully," and we go, "yeah, yeah, yeah" — there'll be people listening to this going, "no idea."

Host: But that is a really important part of the development of the game — just by making it more accessible, both to play, to understand, to watch. And from my side, I think when you do have that kind of exposure when you're young, it's great — you get into it. I mean, Test match cricket to me is still — I will sit down and watch five days of it. If you get an enthralling game of Test cricket, it trumps any T20 game.

Tymal: Even now, I miss [it]. So at Sussex, they obviously play championship cricket, four-day cricket. When they come off — if they've won on that day four, they've bowled a team out in the last session on day four — that's one thing that I'll never be able to replicate in a T20 game. Because you've worked for four days with your mates, your body's hurting, and those beers you have in the changing room, they're the best beers you'll ever have. And I'll go in just for those beers. So I'll be watching on the stream or whatever and I'll say, "okay, the boys have just won. I'm going to go and have a beer with them in the changing room." Because that's one thing — not many sports test you for that long, and that's why it's called Test cricket. But, you know, the payoff and the reward of long-form cricket is still something that, as a short-form player, I wish I still had that little bit of taste.

Host: You did play your first four-day game recently, in a long time.

Tymal: No, I bowled four overs in a game. So it was a preseason friendly. I was in to train and have a bowl, but it rained, so I couldn't bowl. So I said, "oh, can I get the lights on and bowl in the game? Because it's a friendly, you can just hop in." So I literally bowled four overs and then walked straight off the field and went home. So I nipped back in, big time.

Host: People won't know what that means. Sorry — "nipped back in."

Tymal: Yeah, exactly. Started outside off, came in. Oh, it was good.

结尾：长寿、YouTube、与下一段故事

Host: Again, it's very easy to talk about these different formats of cricket and it's very easy to just talk about players and going, "oh, you know, moving for the money and protecting certain formats, doing all this." But as with everything, I think it's really important to understand that outside of the top, top [tier], this is a job as well. So hearing that from you is really cool and really good. And even just seeing you doing those different things — OnlyFans, you know, as I said, laugh because of the name, and then you understand actually what it's about, and the importance of athletes doing that now more and putting themselves in a window for longevity beyond those playing days.

Tymal: And hopefully now that it's not behind a paywall on OnlyFans, hopefully — well, I'll get a real understanding now if people want to see it.

Host: Yeah, and YouTube's a great way to do that.

Tymal: Yeah, so we'll see.

Host: So when is it? YouTube this summer?

Tymal: Soon. Next couple of weeks, I think. We're launching end of May, I think, the first episode. We'll share some content.

Host: But no, in all seriousness, thank you. It's really, really cool to do it with you, and I'm so pleased that you enjoy the show firstly as an athlete. It's partly meant for like you, so it's really great to have you on and looking forward to watching you out throughout the rest of summer.

Tymal: Thanks for having me on. I really enjoyed it.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：他的"无知"是他最大的资产
Tymal 14岁才第一次摸板球——这通常会被视为致命缺陷。但仔细听他叙述，你会发现一个惊人的悖论：**正是这种"晚到"，让他在每一次本该击垮职业球员的危机中毫发无损地走出来。**
- 22岁脊髓被压迫、被告知可以退役时，他没有哀悼"不能打Test的人生"，因为**他根本没有这个童年梦想可供哀悼**。 - 在RCB拍卖会上以150万英镑被买下、瞬间从穷小子变身豪门外援时，他没有像同龄人那样被"维拉·科利、AB德维利尔斯就在更衣室"的压力击垮——因为他根本没把这些人当偶像。 - 被全网嘲讽是"IPL最贵水货"时，他的反应不是崩溃，而是淡然接受。
这里隐藏着一条系统性真相：**心理学里的"沉没成本依赖"在精英体育里被严重低估。** 大多数同龄球员从7岁开始日复一日训练，到20岁已经被"我必须成为Test球员"这个身份完全占据；而Tymal的身份是模糊的、可塑的、未被殖民的。当命运强行让他转弯（脊髓损伤→T20→自由球员→OnlyFans合作），他几乎不需要支付身份重组的心理成本。
**他不是"克服了起步晚"，他是"靠起步晚才活下

item.title: [a16z] Is Defense the Next Trillion-Dollar Category? | a16z American Dynamism Summit
item.dc:creator: a16z
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的核心观察

最让人意外的不是"用机器人替代人"，也不是"重建工业基础"，而是 Dino 抛出的那个数字对比：Marauder 5万工时 vs 驱逐舰 7-9百万工时——这是 140到180倍 的差距。但真正的洞见藏在这个数字背后：他们不是把传统造船变快了，而是从"造船"这个范畴里跳了出去。当你不再受制于"船必须由有15年经验的焊工建造"这个隐含前提，造船业突然变成了一个"IKEA组装问题"。这是一种范畴跃迁——把高门槛的工艺问题，重新定义为低门槛的装配问题。这种思维转换在历史上极为罕见，上一次大规模发生是亨利·福特把"制造汽车"从"工匠手艺"重新定义为"流水线组装"。

一个隐藏的系统性悖论

Dino 反复强调"商业市场是国防的救赎"，这听起来像投资人话术，但其中藏着一个深刻的系统性悖论：国防工业越纯粹，就越脆弱。 传统逻辑认为"专为国防设计=更可靠"，但事实恰恰相反——单一买家+定制设计+不盈利的供应商，构成了一个负反馈生态。Duffy 一句话点破了这个范式："commercial first ... creates all kinds of resilience"。这与生物学中的单一栽培脆弱性（monoculture fragility） 完全同构：爱尔兰土豆饥荒、香蕉枯萎病、以及今天的国防供应链危机，都是同一种数学结构。专一性是效率的友军，却是韧性的敌人。

跨领域连接：这是一场"软件吞噬硬件"的延迟回响

注意 Dino 是怎么解释成本压缩的逻辑链的：用更少的钢 → 因为设计为自主而非载人 → 因为软件吃掉了原本由人执行的功能。这其实是 Marc Andreessen 2011 年那篇"Software is Eating the World"的滞后15年的国防业回响。但有一个更微妙的层次：在消费互联网时代，软件吞噬硬件意味着 Uber 不拥有汽车；在 American Dynamism 时代，软件吞噬硬件意味着军舰因为不需要容纳人，物理形态本身被重新定义。船的"形状"是船员的人体工学决定的——走道宽度、舱室高度、生命维持系统、逃生路径——一旦移除人，整个几何约束坍塌。这不是把船自动化，这是船这个物体的本体论被改写。

一个被忽视的二阶效应

Duffy 提到"激励产业用自己的私人资本扩产"——表面上是财政纪律，但这实际上是在用资本结构重塑产业的心理结构。当一家公司用补贴扩产，它学到的是"等订单"；当它用自己的钱扩产，它学到的是"造市场"。这是激励设计如何雕刻一个行业的灵魂。结合 Dino 的"必须进入商业市场否则我们终将回到原点"——两条看似不同的话，实际上在描述同一个系统性真相：当一个行业失去了商业肌肉记忆，再多的国防订单也救不了它的长期生命力。

📝 转录文本

开场：一代人的机会

There's a real generational opportunity to build what this country needs for the next 100 years, and we need more founders. We need more builders, and we need more folks in government pushing for change.

It's really frustrating how much fragility we encounter within the traditional defense industrial base, because we have a sole supplier that's not really that profitable, that was bespoke for the defense industry — and all of a sudden we've really created our own set of vulnerabilities here.

This room is filled with PEOs and PMs from across the Pentagon. What's one key message you'd like to send this group as we look to build together and build faster in 2026?

The only way to succeed is really to —

自主性如何转化为合同与速度

How have you translated this alignment between Saronic's autonomy and the Navy's "no man left behind" ethos into contracts? And how does — how do autonomy breakthroughs actually allow you to build more, faster products for the Navy?

So, that's one I'll come back to. What autonomy enables is — and I'm a huge believer in this — we're unlocking capability that didn't exist before. Like, warfare will require humans' involvement at some point, but I've seen it: and you should never send a human if you have the ability to send a robot. You just shouldn't do it. So, let's leverage robotics and protect human life where we can.

The real unlock to autonomy is speed and scale. Right? If I was sitting up here telling the secretary how we're going to build the same ships that we've been building for the last 50 years, or the same ships that they're building in China, we're just going to do it better, faster, and cheaper — I mean, he should just politely ask me to leave.

弯折经济成本曲线

You have to bend the economic cost curve. You have to build ships cheaper. You have to build ships faster. The only two levers you have to pull are material costs and labor costs.

We're not going to buy steel cheaper than they're buying it in China. So, you have to take a first-principles design to the ship and just fundamentally use less steel. How do you do that? You build for software, autonomy, and digitization.

Same thing on labor hours. We're not going to acquire a per-hour labor rate cheaper than they're paying in China. So, let's build ships much faster.

For example, Marauder is about a 50,000 labor hour on our first ship. Just for order of magnitude, put it in frame of reference — apples-and-oranges comparison here, I'm going to acknowledge that up front — but a destroyer is 7 to 9 million —

Wow.

— labor hours. So that's the scale and speed that autonomy unlocks, because you can redesign the whole platform and make it much simpler to build. And then you have the capability at the scale that you need to keep as many people as possible out of combat scenarios.

重建美国工业基础与劳动力

You mentioned the sort of factories and bringing autonomy into the actual manufacturing processes. You've put real money behind rebuilding the American industrial base, most notably a major expansion of your Louisiana shipyard, as well as getting prepared to launch Port Alpha — we're excited for news there at some point soon.

How are you thinking about Saronic investing in some of these forgotten areas, forgotten demographics, as you build out manufacturing across the US? And how has scaling been similar, different, complimentary for the different customer bases that you're targeting?

I mean, the workforce is critical, right? The secretary and I were talking about the workforce earlier. We have to rebuild the workforce to support the maritime industrial base. You can't create people with 15 years of experience overnight. So it goes back to the first-principles approach design of the ship. Design the ship so you actually don't need 15 years of welding experience to build it in the first place. Then you can actually rebuild and retrain the workforce.

I'll steal one of the lines from our head of manufacturing. He says: "Less like an encyclopedia, more like IKEA." Everybody can build furniture. If somebody can come out of building a car for Ford or General Motors, or an airplane at Boeing, or a rocket ship for SpaceX, and we can't get them highly effective building ships quickly — that's our fault. That means our design isn't simple enough, or we don't have the processes, or the work instructions, or the training materials, or something else.

So, it's rebuilding and recreating the workforce and changing the culture: what it actually means to work at a shipyard again. Making it cool, building for the future, giving people a mission, and giving people job security behind that.

私人资本与生产能力

You mentioned the job security piece. So following up generally on your investments — Honorable Duffy, what investments do you want to see companies making as we look to rebuild the defense industrial base? And maybe I'll expand that to companies and private capital.

Well, I think Dino's got it right in terms of how we can leverage modern manufacturing. One of the things that we're finding as we dig down into traditional industry — and as we look at, you'd mentioned the transition of Defense Security Cooperation Agency under my jurisdiction now in the Pentagon — production really is the biggest constraint for us when it comes to fulfilling our foreign military sales orders. And the rate of production is generally calibrated to what the US Department of Defense budget looks like.

We can no longer kind of live within those constraints. And you've seen some of the deals that we've been able to do with traditional industry in the last two months, where we've incentivized them to expend their own private capital to expand production with their own funding. Which I think then, instead of being a handout from the US government as it's traditionally been, incentivizes industry to do what Dino is doing with his company — which is really modernize, take ownership on the actual efficiency and production capacity that they're producing.

I think that's a fundamental shift that we want to see industry-wide, which I think levels the playing field for both the new startups that are already practicing this way, and bringing our traditional defense contractors who have capability that we need into that construct as well.

Port Alpha 与商业市场的战略价值

Yeah, completely. Maybe Dino, moving back to you — what role will Port Alpha play in supporting your growth and expansion as you continue building for both the defense and commercial markets at speed and scale? And in particular, how have you found the commercial market to be important to support your defense market?

Yeah. To put it bluntly: Port Alpha is a generational project. We're looking at building one of, if not the largest shipyard in the world, focused on autonomous platforms, focused on the future of the maritime industry — unlocking the commercial market, building larger vessels: cargo containers, bulk carriers, oil tankers, things that enable things like energy dominance.

It's absolutely critical to have that commercial market because, at the end of the day, what our goal is is to provide the Department of War wartime production capacity during peacetime. And if we can't enter the commercial market in a viable, economic manner, then we're just looking to the government for the next contract vehicle and the next paycheck to support our company's longevity. And maybe it's 5 years from now, maybe it's 10 years from now, maybe it's 20, maybe it's 30 — but eventually we may end up right back where we are.

That's why the commercial market is so important. And you see a big focus with the maritime action plan is on the commercial shipping industry within the United States.

"Commercial First" 与工业基础的韧性

Yeah, I think if I can jump in on that — commercial first is a major element of our acquisition transformation strategy and the resilience it provides the industrial base. It's really frustrating how much fragility we encounter within the traditional defense industrial base, because we have a sole supplier that's not really that profitable, that was a feeder to a design that was bespoke for the defense industry, and all of a sudden we've really created our own set of vulnerabilities here.

If we can prioritize commercial first and producibility as a primary input to the development of a design, that creates all kinds of resilience and benefits for the Department of Defense as well. So that's, I think, key to our success.

Yeah, on our team we often say: there's no defense industrial base without an industrial base. So the two really go hand in hand.

给 PEO/PM 与建设者的最后寄语

Honorable Duffy, this room is filled with PEOs and PMs from across the Pentagon, as well as many founders that are building for the department. What's one key message you'd like to send this group as we look to build together and build faster in 2026?

Well, let me start with an expression of gratitude for the leaders in this room who are driving innovation, who are fighting through the bureaucracy to deliver the capability that the warfighter needs on schedule.

And I think the other message I would say is: it's really up to everybody in this room. We recognize, I think — as a part of this acquisition transformation strategy — the only way to succeed is really to let a thousand flowers bloom, and ensure that we are removing the obstacles to companies and program leadership to deliver the best capability the warfighter needs on schedule.

And that's going to take time and it's going to take cooperation. One of the things that we've been trying to do as we try to change culture within the Pentagon is maximum communication throughout the hierarchy of the acquisition system — directly with industry — and ensuring that there's clear understanding what's getting in the way. We want to continue to expand on that. And I invite everybody in this room to engage with my team or others and just say, "Hey, this is not working. How can we remove this barrier?" Because we're committed to making sure we can go as fast as possible.

Thank you. And Dino — advice to other builders who might be a click or two behind you?

Yep. Find what you believe in and then go for it. We're in a — there's a real generational opportunity to build what this country needs for the next hundred years. And we need more founders, we need more builders, and we need more folks in government like the secretary pushing for change.

This isn't a one-company, one-person is going to solve this problem. We need a partnership across the country. And folks that are thinking about getting into this space, building a company — I would say, you know, build the conviction and what you believe in, and then go all in on it. Because the country needs you, and the partners are here to support you.

Amazing. Thank you both so much, Honorable Duffy, Dino. It's been a real pleasure.

I can't do it.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个反直觉的核心观察
最让人意外的不是"用机器人替代人"，也不是"重建工业基础"，而是 Dino 抛出的那个数字对比：**Marauder 5万工时 vs 驱逐舰 7-9百万工时**——这是 **140到180倍** 的差距。但真正的洞见藏在这个数字背后：他们不是把传统造船变快了，而是**从"造船"这个范畴里跳了出去**。当你不再受制于"船必须由有15年经验的焊工建造"这个隐含前提，造船业突然变成了一个"IKEA组装问题"。这是一种**范畴跃迁**——把高门槛的工艺问题，重新定义为低门槛的装配问题。这种思维转换在历史上极为罕见，上一次大规模发生是亨利·福特把"制造汽车"从"工匠手艺"重新定义为"流水线组装"。
## 一个隐藏的系统性悖论
Dino 反复强调"商业市场是国防的救赎"，这听起来像投资人话术，但其中藏着一个深刻的**系统性悖论**：**国防工业越纯粹，就越脆弱。** 传统逻辑认为"专为国防设计=更可靠"，但事实恰恰相反——单一买家+定制设计+不盈利的供应商，构成了一个负反馈生态。Duffy 一句话点破了这个范式："commercial first ... crea

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Rebuilding IT From the Ground Up for the AI Age: Serval's Jake Stauch
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

关于 Serval 访谈的几个非常规洞察

一、最反直觉的发现：AI 公司正在变成"反 AI 的护城河生意"

Jake 在访谈中说了一句被忽略却极其重要的话——"产品就是边界（The product is the boundaries）"。这是一个惊人的反转：

大众叙事：AI 公司的价值 = 让 AI 能做更多事。 Serval 的真实价值：让 AI 能"少做事"——即在企业里给狂奔的模型套缰绳。

换句话说，他们卖的不是智能，而是对智能的克制能力。这与所有"AI 替代人类"的宣传形成鲜明反差：企业付钱给 Serval，本质上是为了让 AI 不要太自由。这暗示了一个被低估的市场结构——未来最赚钱的 AI 应用层公司，可能恰恰是那些"驯化 AI"而非"释放 AI"的公司。

二、一个跨领域的隐藏连接：Serval 的"双 Agent 架构"和宪政分权惊人地同构

Jake 描述的架构： - Admin Agent：定义工具、权限、审批（立法权） - Help Desk Agent：在被授权的边界内自由推理（行政权）

这其实就是孟德斯鸠的三权分立移植到 AI 系统：让一个 Agent 拥有"立法权"去约束另一个 Agent 的"行政权"。更惊人的是——这种架构解决的不是"AI 能不能做"，而是"AI 该听谁的"。

跨领域连接：这与宪法 AI（Constitutional AI）的思路在哲学上同源——通过显式定义边界来释放内部的自由探索。Serval 不是在写代码，他们是在为企业写"AI 宪法"。这或许预示着未来企业里会出现一个全新职位：AI 制宪者（不是 prompt engineer，而是 boundary architect）。

三、一个让人脊背发凉的观察：Anthropic 几个月的 ARR 增长 ≈ Service Now 二十年的总和

Jake 用这个数字论证 OpenAI/Anthropic 不会下场做 ITSM。但这个数字真正的含义远比他说的要颠覆——

它意味着：传统 SaaS 公司用二十年砌起的"系统记录护城河"，正在被基础模型公司用几个月的有机增长跨越。 整个企业软件的"时间杠杆"正在崩塌。

但 Jake 提出了一个反向的、几乎是禅意的命题："焦点本身就是稀缺资源"。基础模型公司有无限的钱和算力，唯独没有的是"愿意花十年精雕一个细分领域"的耐心。这暗示了一个全新的创业法则：

在 AI 时代，公司之间的差距不再由"能做什么"决定，而是由"愿意只做什么"决定。

四、一个被埋藏的悖论：AI 公司比任何时代都更依赖"非 AI 的东西"

Jake 自己说："Hiring is still our biggest issue. 所有 AI 公司都在比以往更疯狂地招人。"

这是一个深刻的悖论——号称要替代人类劳动的行业，自己却最饥渴地需要顶尖人类。这暗示：

AI 时代的真正稀缺品不是 token，而是"判断什么不该被自动化"的人类品味（taste）。

Jake 提到 Serval 的招聘策略叫"Dream Team Draft"——员工推荐他们一生中最想合作的人，由 Serval 自动化追踪。这是一个值得停下来想想的瞬间：他们用 AI 自动化的，不是工作，而是"识别杰出人类"的过程。这是一个非常优雅的反转——AI 不是用来代替人，而是用来更精准地找到正确的人。

五、最隐秘的结构性洞察

Jake 说："我们必须能比以往任何企业软件公司都更快地颠覆自己。也许这个软件只能活六个月。"

这句话与传统 SaaS 行业的所有商业逻辑相悖——SaaS 的护城河本来就建立在"换不动"上（switching cost）。如果 Serval 自己每六个月就要重写产品，那么它和客户的关系本质上变成了"咨询关系"而不是"软件关系"。

换句话说，AI 原生 SaaS 可能根本不是 SaaS，而是一种穿着 SaaS 外衣的、可编程的持续咨询服务。这是一个让传统 SaaS 估值模型整体失灵的潜在范式转移。

📝 转录文本

Rebuilding IT From the Ground Up for the AI Age: Serval's Jake Stauch

开场：理想与现实之间的鸿沟

You know, I think that there's always a gap between the idealized vision of what you think your job's going to be and then what your job actually is. I think it's true for every profession. You idealize, and kids do this the most, right, when they're like, want to be a firefighter, an astronaut — um what that job looks like. And then you get in that job and you realize, oh, there's actually a lot in this that I don't like. And we want to be the tool that actually closes the gap between what you think your job's going to be and what your job actually is.

All right, I'm here with Jake, the founder and CEO of Serval. Jake, welcome to the show. Thank you so much. Great to be here.

为什么世界需要一个 AI 原生的 Service Now？

All right, we're going to start with a high level question. You guys are building kind of the next generation Service Now, like the AI native Service Now, so to speak. That is right. Why does the world need an AI native Service Now?

Yeah, employees need help at work. That's kind of the idea here is we are a platform for employee support. The technical term is enterprise service management, but what it really means is getting help at work. And the ideal way to get help at work is you ask for something and you get it instantly automatically. You don't have to wait on somebody to track down a ticket and assign a ticket to somebody. You just get help immediately.

That requires you to have some kind of automated support, and that automation is best built with AI. So we think about this from first principles. How do we support employees? You automate all the requests. How do you automate all those requests? AI is really a good tool to bring that automation to employees.

与 Service Now 的本质差异：用 AI 来构建工作流和数据库

Awesome. Let's say more about that.
