So I remember back in the day when we first met Fred Luddy who was the founder of Service Now in like 2007–2008 sort of time frame, and at that time people thought that Service Now was amazing because it was this big step function change over BMC Peregrine and Remedy. And the key thing that they got right was to think about enterprise software as an abstraction — it's just workflows on top of a database, right? So they built kind of this flexible workflow configuration engine on top of a database. And at the time that was amazing, and people loved it, and they were off to the races.

What I'm hearing you say is that that's not enough. With AI there's kind of a new generation of automation that can be done. Can you just say more about the thing that Service Now built and the thing that you guys have built and kind of the side by side — like what is truly different this time around?

They got it right. We also built workflows on top of databases, and that is the right abstraction — those are the right primitives. The problem with their workflows and databases is that they require a lot of manual effort to build and maintain. So building those workflows often requires dedicated development resources to put those together. And while that sounds fine — you invest those resources and you get a beautiful automation on the end of it — that can take weeks to months. And in an era where business processes are changing very rapidly, by the time you get that workflow implemented, your business may have changed and moved on and you want a different workflow. And so your automation kind of runs behind where you want it to be. And that's more true now than ever before.

Same with the database. If you need to manually update those entries and look at your IT assets and make sure they're up to date in those systems, that's going to be very painful — to have to bring in consultants or internal developers to update.

We took this unique approach of: let's keep those primitives, workflows on top of databases, but allow you to use AI to build the workflows and use AI to update the databases. And the way we do that is this codegen engine where you describe the workflow that you want — all the different steps and permissions and approvals and logic — and we take your natural language description, we turn that into code, and so your workflow appears instantaneously. There's practically zero time to develop those workflows. And the same thing goes for our databases. You can describe exactly what data you want to take from which sources, and our system will actually generate the code to fetch that data and keep it up to date without you having to do any kind of manual intervention.

让构建自动化比手动操作更简单

I remember you said months ago — this is one of the things you said when we were first getting to know each other that really piqued my interest — something along the lines of: if you really want to drive enterprise automation, you have to make the process of building the automation just as simple, if not simpler, than the workflow being automated. Exactly. Do you still believe that? Is that still true?

I still believe that. And that insight came from just putting yourself in the shoes of someone in IT or another function and you're presented with a task. Somebody asks you to reset their password and you've got two options. You can go into Google Workspace, find the user, hit a button that says reset password. Yeah. Or you can open up a workflow builder and you can drag the trigger and then you can drag the response and then you can build out this custom workflow.

When you're presented with that choice, you're just going to reset the password. You're going to do the manual thing. But if it were actually easier to build the automation, you would build the automation — because why wouldn't you?

And I think it comes down to: people in the moment have to make that decision. And you want that decision to be very, very easy — to opt for the automation, not opt for the manual step.

自动化的"垃圾代码"问题

Now, is there such a thing as being too simple to automate? Like, people talk about vibe coding and all the slop that it produces. Is there such a thing as slop automation?

Yeah, it is. It's real. And we've had to build some really interesting things around that because — yeah, when you make it so easy to automate, somebody might build the 20th password reset workflow this week. Yeah. And it's basically the same as the 19 that came before it. And now you've got all these duplicate workflows and the AI gets confused on which one to run.

Yeah. How do you guys manage that?

We built a new agent basically on top of Serval — that we're really excited about — that has full contextual awareness of all the workflows you've ever built before, how they work, what they're going to do. So when you say, "Hey, I want this workflow that does X Y and Z," it says, "Hey, actually you've got 19 that already do that. I could modify one of these, but here's what I think you should do. I think you should actually delete 10 of them, separate these remaining nine into these different categories, add these approval steps." So it actually walks you through the system and is kind of your assistant that's an expert in our product to help you translate your business requirements into the actual product implementation.

极致客户驱动：每天在每个客户 Slack 频道里

Speaking of the product, you're a product guy, and one of the things that we've heard about you consistently from every possible source is that you're extremely customer focused and really good at listening to customers and figuring out what they need. Do you have a North Star metric that you rely on that just tells you the product is getting better and more useful, or is it more — you collect all the anecdotes, you synthesize them, you kind of have an intuitive sense? Is there a number that you can look at?

I think it's the latter. Like, I try to be embedded with the customers — full immersion. I am in every single customer Slack channel. Yeah. I think most of our customers will get a Slack from me in that channel every single day. Wow. And just to set the context — a listener might think you probably have four customers. How many customers do you have?

Over a hundred customers, and a lot of large enterprises. And it is overwhelming at times to be in all those conversations all the time, and sometimes I feel like, oh man, am I wasting my time? But I feel like I really understand what's going well, what's not going well, and I just have my finger on the pulse. And there's just no substitute for that — especially when a lot of the implementation work has gotten very fast, more and more of the moat for any startup is the customer insight, the empathy of actually understanding what they want.

And if we can have a differentiated advantage around the customer insight, that's going to be much more valuable than having a product advantage, which is copyable overnight.

应用层公司在基础模型时代的护城河

Let's talk more about that. This has been a hot topic for a few years now. ChatGPT comes out in November of 2022 and immediately people start deriding application layer companies as "wrappers" on top of a foundation model. And what you just said kind of plays directly into this theme. There's always a school of thought that says the foundation model is going to just do everything, and then there's a school of thought that says: sure, but you can build a company on top to close the gap between raw capabilities and actual customer problems.

How do you view your role running an application layer company versus the role of the foundation models? Where do you think the moats form around your business over time? To the extent somebody listening is interested in how to build an application layer company in this era — what are your thoughts on that?

I think you have to be happy when new models come out. That is the guiding principle: how do we make sure whatever we're building is actually not made obsolete by whatever the labs and hyperscalers come out with, but actually is made better.

What's a good example?

For us, we think the product is the boundaries. The product is the controls. The product is actually what limits the capabilities of the model. Because the question now is not "can Opus, can GPT-5.5, do these amazing things, can they do the things I want to do in my enterprise environment?" — the capabilities are practically unlimited. The limitation now is: how do I get comfortable as a large enterprise that cares about security and deploying this companywide without elevating my security risk?

So we think about it from the boundaries. And that means really boring, old-school enterprise software things — around permissions and approvals, limiting the scope of your API integrations, having visibility, audits and reporting and logs and alerts, and just all of the things that make you feel comfortable letting the models run wild in your environment.

双 Agent 架构：Admin Agent 与 Help Desk Agent

So one of the fundamental things we did from an architecture perspective is divide the agents into two parts. Our customers, when they experience Serval, there's really two agents they work with. One is the admin agent that helps build all of these tools and skills that configure what the help desk agent can do and what it knows about. The admins build that. Then there's a help desk agent that end users talk to that resolves their problems.

The help desk agent can only use the tools and skills that have been expressly built, published with approvals and permissions and all of that by the admins. And that two-pronged architecture ends up being really, really powerful because you can let the help desk agent run wild — right? Because the end user can ask it anything, and it can use its reasoning ability and its full intelligence to be able to solve a user's problems —

But it can only use the tools that the IT admin has expressly said are okay to use, and those may have approvals attached, permissions that gate certain users from doing certain things. All of that is done on the admin side, but then you get the full ability and intelligence of the help desk agent to use those tools appropriately.

模型选择：OpenAI 与 Anthropic 各擅胜场

What's under the hood? Is there anything you can say about which models you guys are having luck with, and maybe how that's evolved over time?

Yeah, we use OpenAI and Anthropic models today. Always experimenting with the latest models from all providers to make sure that we're on the cutting edge, running evals on everything that we do. Today it's still OpenAI and Anthropic models. We find different models are better at different applications.

So for the interaction with the end user, we're seeing the most luck with OpenAI models, and that has remained consistent for quite a long time — actually calling the correct tools and responding to the user in the appropriate way. We're having a lot of success with various GPT models, and always keeping those up to date depending on the latest release.

But on the automation side, which is mostly codegen automation, we're having the most success with Anthropic models. So interesting — we continue to use Sonnet, Opus.

Tons of trade-offs between the different models. And what's interesting in recent times is the new releases oftentimes are not just plug-and-play. Sometimes you get some advantages and some things that were working really well don't work as well anymore. So that's become an interesting challenge as things go.

Yeah. How do you guys manage that? How long does it take to incorporate new models into the production version of your product? How much of that process is automated? How much is somebody just has to sit down and figure it out?

It's not as automated as I'd like. We have the evals, but in every situation when we have a new model that we're testing, some things get better and some things get worse. And a lot of things that get worse — not necessarily the model got worse, but we built a lot of prompt tuning and infrastructure around the known quirks or behaviors of that model. And those make less and less sense when the new model comes out, or where you're swapping models. And so that's where a lot of the adjustments have to be made. Then you run it through the evals and then you do a slow release across customers.

So we're getting better at this. But there's certain cases where the trade-offs have not been worth it, where we've actually upgraded models and then downgraded the models and said, "you know what, the old models are maybe a little bit faster, or maybe they're reliable in a way that the new models aren't." The new models are a little bit smarter, but they misbehave in ways that are less predictable, and we have less predictable guardrails to prevent against. And so we're like, hey, this model might not be as smart, but we know it's going to behave the right way for these customers. So — it's been an interesting challenge and it's changed over time.

成本经济学：不靠倒卖 token 赚钱

How much are you guys factoring cost into the equation right now? Step one, you've got to make sure the product does something magical. Yeah. Step two, okay, now let's make sure we've got a business. Do you guys think much about cost at this point, or — we kind of know where that's going over time; let's make sure the product is magical and we'll figure out the cost element later?

It's the latter. And one of the reasons that gives us this flexibility is that our unit economics end up looking much better than a lot of AI companies, because we are not in the business of reselling tokens. The way that our product works is you build these automations — which are basically TypeScripts. And once they're built, you don't have to rebuild that. So every time the end user asks for a password reset, it's not going and regenerating code to reset a password — it's actually just running the code that's already been generated.

Over time, users have to generate less and less actual code, because we have a growing library of automations that cover the very long tail of things that you might want to do. So there's not as much, especially in the very expensive codegen token consumption, as you'd expect. The unit economics are very strong even though we haven't done a lot of optimizations around that.

I tried to tell the team: spend more money, use the best possible product. We know long-term where this is going. We know that there are all kinds of optimizations we can make down the line. So that's been our focus to date.

Where it starts to get more interesting is as we explore more and more applications of background agents — long-running agents that are not just responding to help desk requests or building quick scripts for you, but investigating all of your historical tickets, or investigating logs from devices, doing all this work in the background, and maybe generating solutions to problems you didn't know you had. That's where it becomes a little bit more interesting, where maybe the costs become more relevant. So that's where we'll probably start to think about cost a little bit earlier in the journey — because those could run away pretty quickly if you're not keeping an eye on it.

假如 OpenAI 或 Anthropic 决定自己做 Service Now？

Yeah, that makes sense. Let's say somebody at OpenAI or Anthropic wakes up tomorrow and they're like, "Wait a minute, I found this company called Service Now and it seems to be a major system of record, major center of gravity inside the enterprise. I think we should build, as a first party product, the OpenAI Service Now or the Anthropic Service Now." If they set their sights on you and come after you directly, or come after your category directly, what would that mean for Serval?

Yeah, this is always a really tough question, because on the one hand, any response to "okay, this company with infinite money and the best engineering talent and AGI wants to do what you do — how are you better?" is going to sound pretty naive. Like, "well, we're just going to beat them."

But the history of startups tells us that often the smaller company does beat them. I mean, the existence of OpenAI and Anthropic are kind of proof that you can beat the entrenched incumbent with infinite resources. And I think it comes down to maybe divine providence favoring startups, or maybe a lack of focus is often what makes it hard to execute.

When I was starting my first company, every VC would ask me, "Won't Google just do this?" And yes, Google will do a lot of those things — but it actually is very hard to do a lot of different things really, really well. It's hard to divert your focus into all these categories.

I don't think ITSM makes the most sense as a focus area. One reason is that I think in the past couple months, Anthropic has added more ARR than Service Now has in the past 20 years.

Good point. Does it really make sense for them to take their best and brightest people and throw them at this problem, that even if they are very successful would take them years to get what they could get out of the rest of their product portfolio in a matter of months? I don't think they're — they're probably going to look at this category. I wouldn't be surprised if they built some kind of simple version, maybe a more mid-market or SMB focused version. But to really devote the time and energy to master the complexities of enterprise service management — not to say they couldn't, but the focus that would require would be a bad use and bad prioritization of their resources. And I don't think that's going to happen.

客户图景：AI 原生公司 vs. 大型企业

Yeah, I think you're right. Let's talk about your customers for a minute. You got a bunch of the really nice AI native logos, and you're also starting to have some big enterprises. How do the needs differ from the AI native crowd to the big enterprise crowd? And if you had to pick from each of those, what's the nicest thing that they would have to say about you? "Serval is amazing because…" — what would they say?

Yeah, what's been the biggest learning is how similar they are relative to how different. I expected them to be much more different, but the pain points and the problems end up looking remarkably alike from the AI natives to the large enterprises. We work with companies as small as a few hundred employees up to companies as large as a few hundred thousand employees.

The difference ends up being how many people it takes to make a decision. And that's what actually makes things really challenging — because:

It's more of a go-to-market thing. It's a go-to-market. It's an implementation thing more than that, though.

When you're working with a company with a few hundred employees, there's probably an IT leader that can say "this is how we're going to do things, this is how onboarding works, this is how we're going to reset passwords." Whatever. If you're working with a company with a few hundred thousand employees, no one even knows who that person is, if that person exists. So you end up in all these committees trying to figure out, "what should we do here?" That's actually what makes it very, very challenging. You see that in these labs building out consulting businesses and more and more services and deployment resources, because that ends up being a lot of the rate-limiting step in adoption — coordinating all these folks.

客户最喜欢说的两句话

So what would they say nice things about Serval?

In the AI native, early-stage companies: We take an IT person that's passionate about technology and we let them spend their time building. They got into it because they love technology, and so much of their job before Serval was: experience technology. My favorite example is a customer we had that spent a lot of their day fielding Service Now requests to provision someone's access to Cursor.

Yeah. And that juxtaposition was just so sad. "I am in this ancient ticketing system helping somebody else at the company get access to a really cool AI tool, and my tools are still stuck in the past, and I don't get to use the cool stuff." With Serval they get to use the cool stuff. There's actually an AI tool for IT built for them. That's what we see on the native side.

In the large enterprise, generally they're thinking about it at just a higher level of business transformation. We hear a lot more about the end employee experience. Because at a small company, even if your IT processes aren't perfect, no one's waiting weeks to get a response back from IT. But at a large organization, you could send a ticket into the abyss.

Yeah. And just have no idea where it's at, if it's being worked on. So then you'll send another ticket, and then there's confusion on the other side because now you've got two tickets from the same person — and there's actually people that are blocked for weeks from getting back to the thing they're trying to do. In a large enterprise, we actually change the employee experience and what it feels like to be an employee, and have this more broad impact, because the problems are actually a lot deeper in those big organizations.

内部用 Serval 自己做了什么？Dream Team Draft 招聘

What is a delightful or surprising use of Serval at Serval? How do you guys use it?

Oh man, I force the team to use it for everything. So every time — Where do they really push the boundaries? Exactly. I mean obviously things like office requests, like snacks — all of that has to go through Serval.

But one of the coolest things we do for Serval: we have a channel called Dream Team Draft. We take this very person-first approach to recruiting where we want to identify the best people in the world and bring them to Serval, versus just cast a wide funnel, host an open tryout, and see who makes it.

So people put the best people they've ever worked with in this channel. They post to LinkedIn. That's a Serval channel. So Serval will take that profile and then run a series of automations. One, it'll run them into all of our outbounding campaigns, our nurturing campaigns. It'll also do a lot of things that my marketing team won't let me talk about — making sure that they are seeing Serval everywhere they go, and Serval becomes very top of mind for them. We basically warm this audience to make sure they know about Serval, and we're playing the long game, because these are generally not people that are going to leave their job tomorrow, but people we'd love to work with one day.

I love the idea of going in and saying, "Hey, all I have to do as an employee is just say, 'I love this person, they're great,'" and I'm done. And the talent team will be able to, through Serval, have all these automations that get them into the system.

为什么要加入 Serval？以及它不是什么

That is very cool. What's the best reason to work at Serval? Like, why do people join your team?

I think it is the greatest group of people I've ever worked with. When you walk into our office and you meet our team, it's just a group of so kind and so talented and so fun to be around. And that's what's really unique — I didn't even know I was selecting for this when we started the company. But something candidates started telling me is, "wow, your team is so nice and they're so fun to be around. I walked in the office and the energy was just contagious, and I wanted to work there."

And obviously there's really interesting technical problems. Building these really complex enterprise automations, getting to touch HR, legal, finance, IT, security — it's kind of a training ground. We often think about this as a training ground for anyone who wants to do anything in AI. You get to touch all these different departments, build all these cool workflows. But if I'm being honest, the reason you join Serval is because you meet the team and you realize this is the place for you.

它不是什么

Yeah. Very cool. We heard some of the words that you used to describe your culture — fun and nice and high-energy people. What is it not?

It is not a good place for, let's say, a lot of training or mentorship. Everyone is kind, but everyone's doing their job, and there is not a lot of "hey, you're going to be onboarded through this program and you're going to learn how to do these things, and we're going to train you really well, and we're going to pair you with some resources and coaching and mentorship." There are great companies that do that really well. We are not one of them.

We hire people to come in and basically be productive on day one, and who like that ambiguity of "I've got to show up and figure out what I'm supposed to do, and then do it really, really well." So it's not a place for coaching, mentorship. It's not a place for very clearly defined career paths. We don't know where a lot of these functions are going to go, so there's not going to be this clear progression up the org chart. There's not really an org chart. I don't actually know who reports to me at the company. I am blissfully unaware of who technically reports to me versus other people, because we try to keep everything as flat as possible. So if you're looking for that kind of natural progression, it's just not going to be a good place for you.

作为 AI 原生组织的运营方式

Yeah. One of the big topics we've had a lot of conversations on recently — amongst founders and folks — is this idea of living in the future, and not only does the product that you build need to be AI native, but the way that you build it and the way that you manage your organization needs to be AI native. Beyond using Serval itself, what does that mean for you guys? How has the way that you operate changed this time around versus Vicarious or your prior companies?

In so many ways. One is: we are questioning everyone's role, every department — we're wondering if it needs to exist anymore and if it still makes sense. Oftentimes it does. We relearn the necessity of some departments that we thought maybe we didn't need. But we start with the assumption that maybe this doesn't need a person anymore, maybe this could be AI. So AI almost gets the right of first refusal for every job or every department: "Hey, maybe we don't need this at all. Maybe this can be much smaller than" — maybe this.

没有 SE，没有 SDR

Is there a good example of something that fully went to AI?

Solutions engineering. We don't have SEs. We also don't have SDRs — but I think that's less of a controversial take over the past few years. We don't have SDRs and we don't have solutions engineers. Our reps are not necessarily more technical than reps in past generations have been, but they have access to Serval. Yeah. So any question they have about how the product works, or that a customer or a prospect comes up with, Serval is going to give them an instant answer to that question. Serval can even build them decks, one-pagers, quick battle cards and comparison sheets — all of that on the fly in the middle of a call.

So we expect more out of our AEs, and they're not going to have the SE resource. We do have forward deployed engineers that assist with the actual pilot implementation. But that's been one example where we didn't need that.

We also delayed hiring in a lot of domains. Sometimes not by choice, just by the slowness of our hiring — but we've discovered how far we can get. On the enablement side, we have someone in product marketing who's done an incredible job of building out enablement and all these automated resources and scaled that quite far. We definitely need more help there, but a lot of times you get further than you think with these AI tools. RevOps is another one where we've gotten pretty far without a RevOps hire, but then discovering, actually, you do need somebody eventually. So there's a lot of these things where you delay it a little bit and you're like, "actually we need somebody, but maybe this department is a little bit smaller than we thought it was going to be."

创始人的角色演变

Company is about two and a half years old thereabouts. Just two years old. Two years old. Okay. In the last — and you guys have grown like crazy. More people, more customers, all that good stuff. How has your job changed in the last two years?

In the very early days, I felt very useless, honestly. My CTO is building the product, and it's very clear what we need to build. There's so much to build, and I'm trying desperately to hire, set up customer calls, and maybe try to sell this thing — and it's just not working. So in the early days it was kind of trying to figure out how I can be most useful when we don't really have a business yet.

Then over time it switches, and it becomes a business, and stops being me pushing the boulder up the hill and more of the business dragging me along.

What hasn't changed: I'm still very involved with customers, both on the sales side, but also in long-term success — talking to customers every day. I'm still very involved in recruiting.

What I'm not as involved in, that I'd like to be, is a lot of product. In the early days, I am thinking non-stop about the product direction. And now it feels like the product direction is kind of emerging from our customers, through our forward deployed engineers, and going right into the product. Which is in many ways great, because you have this nice closed loop of customers talking to forward deployed engineers, and then the product is getting implemented and getting better all the time. I've often referred to this as "gradient descent for product improvements", because our forward deployed engineers are swarmed with all this feedback from customers, and they're like, "oh, I'll fix this, I'll make this better, I'll change this." And you fast forward a week and — wow, the product is a lot better than it was a week ago. And it keeps going that way.

But I get to spend a lot less time thinking about the future and where we want to be. And that's something I need to spend more time on, because this stuff is changing so quick.

必须比以往更快地颠覆自己

To your question earlier about how we are different as an organization, an AI native organization — I think a big difference is you have to be willing to reinvent yourself so much faster and disrupt yourself so much faster. We are thinking about just uprooting things that we were convinced were true months ago, and going in completely different directions in all these different ways, and we have to have that flexibility. We're going to be renaming parts of the product. We're going to be completely shifting how we do certain things in the product, and we have to be willing to do that over and over and over again.

There's going to be less of this idea of "I am building software that's going to last for 20 years," and more "I'm gonna build software that hopefully will last six months, and then I might have to rebuild it once the paradigm shifts and the markets have changed."

处于客户与基础模型之间的"翻译层"

Yeah. Let me ask you about that. There's a foundation model on one side, or a set of foundation models and capabilities. There's a customer on the other. And there's Serval in the middle. These capabilities are changing at a very rapid pace. The customer is probably not changing all that fast. So you guys in the middle are kind of this buffer that is trying to take all these capabilities and put them to work for the customer. So the question is really a change management question: how do you keep the customer from drowning in this downpour of capabilities coming out of the foundation models?

I think that's exactly the right way to think about it. We are kind of that translation layer. And we have to meet customers where they are. We very much have to understand their business problems — and that's what really helps with the forward deployed engineers: we are starting with their business problems. What are they trying to solve for? And then we are helping them discover how those solutions are implemented in Serval. We are on the other side figuring out how to take in the latest advances in AI to be able to deliver those solutions.

So that is the role we serve. We're often educating them: "Hey, here's how we do this. And by the way, it's changed since how we would have done this three months ago. We have these new tools at play, but we will be the ones to help figure this out for you."

One of the things we're working on is: how can we bridge that gap a little bit more succinctly? How can we make an agent available to the end user that says, "okay, what are your business problems? Cool. Here's what we're going to do. Here's how we're going to solve them." So you don't really have to think about the latest advances. Serval just takes care of that for you, and you just have to focus on: what are your problems? What are you trying to achieve?

反共识观点：自治 vs. 控制的张力

What's your most contrarian take on the world of AI?

One take that I have — I don't know how contrarian this is — but I think there's this big gap emerging between who wants autonomy and who wants control of these agents. The individuals in an enterprise, they want autonomy. They want their Claude agent to do everything for them and have access to everything. The organization itself doesn't want their employees' agents to have all this autonomy. And there is this interesting tension emerging.

I often see this in consumer versus enterprise products, where the consumer products obviously are built for a world where you want it to do everything, and the enterprise products are built more with this control layer. What's happening in the enterprise is that the individuals are adopting these tools and wanting them to be able to do more, and the security organization, the IT organization, is very worried about this — and for good reason.

I think that's something interesting to track — this tension between autonomy. Do organizations actually want autonomy, or do just individuals want autonomy? And how do you navigate that tension? That's where we're thinking a lot about the Serval product and how we help solve that.

影子 IT 的历史回响

It reminds me of shadow IT back in the day, when consumers started to adopt iPhones and enterprises still had BlackBerries because they could lock down the BlackBerry. And the iPhone started to show up with work information and then work applications, and they were kind of sneaking their way into the enterprise. The instinct of most CISOs was protect, protect, protect. And then I think eventually they realized: well, wait a minute, this is kind of the leading indicator on what my employees need to be productive. And they started to embrace it. Yep. Right. It feels like the same thing's happening now with AI, where employees kind of know what they need to be productive, and as long as you can just systematically follow those signals and embrace it, you're more likely to end up on the right side of history.

Yeah. And I think the companies that basically say yes as the default are going to be way ahead of the companies that say no as a default. We're seeing this with the companies that just embrace it — they will also be leading the charge, and face a lot of the consequences. There are going to be security incidents, there are going to be problems, and we're going to learn a lot from those. But that is what we're going to see: the companies that take those risks are going to get ahead. Yeah. But those risks are real, and they're also going to be some of the ones that suffer some of the consequences on the security incidents.

当下最大的难题：招聘

What's your biggest issue at the moment?

Our biggest issue is hiring, still. Every AI company I talk to is in the same situation. Even though AI is allegedly supposed to automate all this work and take away all these jobs, we're all still hiring more than ever before, and it's still our number one priority and our number one concern.

People remain the biggest moat you can have, and having better people in the room is really the only thing that will keep you ahead of the competition. It's the only moat that's left — the people in your organization. So hiring and scaling and keeping the bar incredibly high as we hire are the things that keep me up at night and worry me — especially as the business grows so much that I'm less involved. There's a lot of people that I only meet in their interview and their onboarding, but don't have a lot of one-on-one interactions after that.

"Fewer, better"

Yeah, the talent density point is a good one. We think about that a lot too, because we're sort of in this world where cost goes down, capabilities go up, and the result is accelerating change. If the world is changing quickly around you, you have to optimize for agility. Yes. The best way to optimize for agility as an organization is to have the smallest possible number of the best possible people, right? So I feel like the returns to talent density have never been higher than they are today.

And so, like, you guys could go hire a million people, but doing so with the sort of quality and culture fit that you need — that's tricky. And to your point, it makes it harder to turn the ship. Yeah.

Our mantra on hiring is "fewer, better, fewer, better." We say this over and over again. Fewer, better. How can we make this department fewer and better? Yeah. And that's going to be really important, because again, I think we're going to have to reinvent ourselves more frequently than companies have ever had to do this before. I can imagine a future version of Serval where it's unrecognizable from what we do today, because we just had to adapt so quickly. And that's much easier to do with a small agile team than if we scale very rapidly — you become thousands and thousands of employees and you say, "hey, by the way, we're changing everything about our go-to-market, we're changing everything about what our product does."

Yeah.

You know, we're making some big changes to the product, and it's nice to have a relatively small organization that can embrace that shift almost overnight. I can't imagine what we'd do if we had to convince thousands of people that this is the new direction.

终局：希望被人记住的影响力

Yeah. Let's assume — and obviously this is not something we can take for granted, but just for fun — let's assume that Serval becomes a monster company. You know, gazillion customers and employees and revenue and market cap and free cash flow and all that great stuff. Let's assume you're a monster company. At that point, what would you want people to say about you? What else would you want to be true, that is not contained in one of those traditional metrics of scale or success?

I would want the impact of Serval to be very clear in terms of what it did for the world. The impact that I think is most important of what we do today is: we unlock meaningful work for people.

You know, I think that there's always a gap between the idealized vision of what you think your job's going to be, and then what your job actually is. I think it's true for every profession. You idealize — and kids do this the most, right, when they're like, "I want to be a firefighter, an astronaut" — what that job looks like. And then you get in that job and you realize, oh, there's actually a lot in this that I don't like.

And we want to be the tool that actually closes the gap between what you think your job's going to be and what your job actually is. And we do that by automating away all this repetitive, menial work that you don't want to do — and that is not productive and is not part of the reason you took this job. We've done that in many ways for IT. And as we unlock automation for the entire organization, that's what we do for people: we get them back to the work that they actually signed up for, and the work they actually enjoy.

I would hope that there is clarity that that was the impact that we had.

Yeah. Yeah. And it wasn't just like, "oh, Serval automated away a bunch of IT jobs." It's: now I want people to feel like Serval made people's work lives a lot better.

Awesome. That feels like a great place to end it. Jake, thank you so much for joining us today. Thank you so much for having me. This is a blast.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
# 关于 Serval 访谈的几个非常规洞察
## 一、最反直觉的发现：AI 公司正在变成"反 AI 的护城河生意"
Jake 在访谈中说了一句被忽略却极其重要的话——"产品就是边界（The product is the boundaries）"。这是一个惊人的反转：
**大众叙事**：AI 公司的价值 = 让 AI 能做更多事。 **Serval 的真实价值**：让 AI 能"少做事"——即在企业里给狂奔的模型套缰绳。
换句话说，他们卖的不是智能，而是**对智能的克制能力**。这与所有"AI 替代人类"的宣传形成鲜明反差：企业付钱给 Serval，本质上是为了**让 AI 不要太自由**。这暗示了一个被低估的市场结构——**未来最赚钱的 AI 应用层公司，可能恰恰是那些"驯化 AI"而非"释放 AI"的公司**。
## 二、一个跨领域的隐藏连接：Serval 的"双 Agent 架构"和宪政分权惊人地同构
Jake 描述的架构： - **Admin Agent**：定义工具、权限、审批（立法权） - **Help Desk Agent**：在被授权的边界内自由推理（行政权

item.title: [Bits + Bips] The Fed, China, and CLARITY + Coinbase Eats USDH
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、USDH 之死：一场关于"货币摩擦力"的精妙战争

表面上，Coinbase + Circle 吞并 USDH 是一笔流动性整合交易。但更深的真相是：这是一场关于"摩擦力归属权"的争夺。

传统金融中，最赚钱的从来不是交易本身，而是资金在两种状态之间转换时被收的过路费——这就是 NIM（净息差）。Chris 点破了一个被加密行业长期忽视的事实：Hyperliquid 此前把这块"无摩擦的浮存金收益"白白让给了第三方。这本质上是一种协议级别的"租金外溢"。

而更让人意外的是：90% 的储备收入回流给 Hyperliquid——这意味着 Circle 主动放弃了大部分息差收益，换取的是"USDC 成为新兴霸权链的官方语言"。这不是一笔金融交易，这是一场殖民地的语言征服——就像英语之所以是商业通用语，不是因为它最好，而是因为它在足够多的关键节点上被默认安装。Circle 在买"默认安装权"，代价是放弃短期 NIM。

二、一个隐藏的跨领域连接：稳定币正在悄悄重塑美债收益率曲线

Gordon 抛出了一个被严重低估的洞察——稳定币正在成为美债曲线的"形态塑造者"，而不仅仅是"边际买家"。

这是一个跨越货币史和材料科学的奇妙映射：稳定币偏好持有极短久期 T-bills 和反向回购。当它们规模膨胀时，美国财政部就获得了在"短端发更多债、长端发更少债"的隐性许可证。换句话说，稳定币不只是债主，它在默默改变债务的几何形状——就像水流改变河床一样，不是通过冲击，而是通过持续的偏好选择。

更深的讽刺在于：传统银行游说团体在 CLARITY 法案中拼命阻击稳定币付息，但他们没意识到自己正在打的是一场结构性后卫战——他们捍卫的"无息存款"模式，正在被一种甚至能影响主权债务曲线形状的新货币形态绕过。这就像马车夫工会反对汽车的安全带条款，却没注意到柏油马路本身已经被铺设。

三、最反直觉的洞察：OpenAI 案的"无聊判决"与 Apple 的"沉默"，揭示了同一个真相

人人都在争论 Musk vs Altman 谁是英雄谁是反派。但真正的信号被埋藏在两个完全不相关的事件里：

  1. OpenAI 案以"诉讼时效"这种程序性理由结案——法院根本懒得审实质问题。
  2. Apple 在 AI 军备竞赛中保持诡异的沉默——当 Meta、Google、Microsoft 烧掉数千亿做 capex 时，Apple 几乎一言不发。

把这两件事合起来看，市场和法律系统都在用不同的方式说同一句话：基础模型层不值得为之打架，因为它最终捕获不了价值。Rahm 直言：LLM 公司是在"为人类免费提供公共服务"。

这与稳定币的故事惊人地同构：USDC 本身不是赢家，能在 USDC、货币市场基金、银行存款之间无缝切换的"形态转换器"才是赢家。Circle 同时拥有 USDC（支付工具）和 USYC（收益工具），正是在赌这个"transformer 假说"。

最深的洞见：未来十年最赚钱的位置，可能既不是"创造者"也不是"分发者"，而是"形态切换器"——无论是 AI agent 在 credit card / 稳定币 / 银行转账之间切换，还是资本在 stable coin / money market / 银行存款之间切换。优化跨边界的人，比优化任何单一系统的人都更值钱。这与你之前关注的"内容管道在 cron / vault / obsidian 之间流转"的本质是一样的——价值不在节点，而在节点间的相变能力。

📝 转录文本

开场与赞助商介绍

availability layer. As Bitcoin's application layer, Catraa gives you access to the first trust minimized BTC on a fully programmable platform and a native stable coin for Bitcoin CTUSD. You can now participate in Bitcoin capital markets with lending, privacy, payments, Bitcoin yield, trading, and predictions. You get expanded Bitcoin utility without sacrificing its security. Catra mainet is live. Put your BDC to work at chatraa.xyz/unchained.

Coinbase 增持 Hype 的战略意图

Coinbase also said that it significantly increased its position of stake hype and that was interesting to me because I view Hyperlid in some ways as a competitor to Coinbase. So, I was wondering what it said to you that Coinbase is, you know, through through this gesture aligning more closely with Hyperlid and if that, you know, um, kind of move there to increase its position of stake hype even shed any further light on the stable coin deal.

Yeah, Laura, I was thinking the same thing. Um, it feels like in in many ways, Hyperlid and Coinbase are both going after this sort of everything exchange. um you know spot trading, perpetual trading, equities, prediction markets. Um and it's true like you know they they have to acquire significant amount of hype and stake it to be able to offer this uh you know AQA. Um and so this shows significant alignment between the two businesses even though there's so much competition.

To me, what this shows is that Coinbase is very certain in its, you know, growth as an as a US-based, primarily US-based compliant institution. Um, whereas Hyperliquid, you know, I think they see as more of this uh like outside of US jurisdiction, no KYC uh platform. which you I think Coinbase can't really compete as as much in uh but they can leverage the usage of Hyperlid globally to grow distribution of USDC. Uh but I think ultimately in the long term will you know positively affect their bottom line.

比特币与监管清晰度

We're at a critical point and I it's so you're spot on in that for the longest time I would always say econ 101 Bitcoin has all the attributes of a successful currency but the one thing it's been missing is a clear set of legal and regulatory guidelines. So if we have clarity in the mix that's that's the puzzle piece like that's the last piece to unlock all of the attributes of a successful currency.

Why is there uh non-interest bearing deposit accounts alongside with you savings accounts? There's always some type of delimination uh even if it's offered by the same institution. And you know, I think you you always going to have both uh not just one versus the other.

Chris 回应

And >> Chris, what do you think to throw that ball right back to you?

No, I I think you're right. I think you're going to see an interoperable uh capability I I do think you'll see, you know, there's there's a lot of of there's a network effect when enough people have accounts with money market funds that you'll see more um I guess ability to to to not I don't want to say pay but but uh exchange them for value and goods or whatever. Um but yeah, I think that that intersection is going to be very important going forward and the agents are going to be very good at it.

主题四：CLARITY Act 与稳定币收益之争

All right. So, let's talk about clarity then, which is part of the key to unlocking this. Senate Banking advanced uh the Digital Asset Market Clarity Act to the floor on a 15-9 bipartisan vote. It would need 60 votes on the floor to proceed. Also, an open question on if we're going to get more amendments as we land on the floor.

Um, there are many core mechanics in here. We've talked about this before. decentralization tests, assigning duties to the SEC and CFTC, what tokens or what kinds of entities. I think everybody has agreed that this bill is not perfect, but it is at least decently good. And I want to throw two things out here to discuss for the group.

被动收益 vs 活动型奖励的妥协

Let's start with the first one. There's still an ongoing fight around stable coin yields, which I think consumers, retailers, and the crypto industry are calling working as intended. The bank lobby is still calling a loophole. But senators Tillis and also Brooks have cut a compromise where strictly passive yield is banned, but activity- based rewards are allowed. The ABA is upset about this, but several others seem relatively happy with it. And the senators have indicated, quote, "We're not open for renegotiation."

So, I want to start with that issue, which is what do we think of where it's landed? Let's assume that what Senator Tillis and also Brooks said is correct, that there will not be renegotiation. How do we feel about where that is if the bill passes with the exact language in it?

Gordon 论新旧金融中介模式

I can jump in there. uh you know I think the tois and also broken compromise is is actually you know it has a academic nature in it right at least from the economist perspective there is uh multiple facets of money as I mentioned uh it's a settlement instrument but it's also store value in some ways this is separating the store of value component from the settlement value and from the uh uh from the union account

and um it also speak to a larger I movement um about how intermediation happens in finance. Traditionally we think about intermediation being very balance sheet heavy right that that's why we have all the banking rules that is focused on stress testing the balance sheets of banks right in that world it is about growing the size of the balance sheet and making as big as possible and having the right regulatory structure around it but if you look at how you know even just decentralized finance have evolved onchain finance have evolved quite a bit of it is activity based it is uh not necessarily about having the largest balance sheet but having the set of intermediaries sometimes are mediated by uh smart contracts that's a set of activities

uh so I think this is this compromise is actually speaking very well to that divide between the old and the new the tri-y view of balance sheet heavy intermediation to the newer world of smart contract agentic mediated intermediation in finance and um I I think there's tremendous opportunities uh for those who want to focus on the activity based uh you know type of rewards activity based services inter newer form of intermediation that will take over the older form.

Chris 论参议院领导层与法案前景

Uh let me start by saying uh Senator Tim Scott um Senator Lumis Republican side leadership's been amazing. And I also wanted to give a special shout out to Senator Ggo um and also Brooks of course. But but Senator Ggo uh is a Marine and dude fought HadithA when I was in Ramani. The guy has some courage. I just thought it was awesome for him to to make it bipartisan coming out of committee.

Um I see that we're now like you ever climb Everest Austin and Rahm Gordon? It's like we're approaching Hillary's step on this bill, right? We're approaching Hillary's step. You still got commissioner issue. I'm sure you're going to talk about that, Austin. You've got ethics, which is going to be really hard to overcome. And then you've got the interesting where the banks aren't really happy. So, what's going to happen? Like the banking lobby was pulling on the national security string today because maybe they're not happy about other aspects.

So, it gets really weird towards the end. I'm still in the camp that it gets done. Um, I would love to know where you guys are because I think we diver we've been diverging for a while. I think it gets done barely. It gets really weird between now and and and when it gets done. Uh, but I still think it gets done because when Epic Fury started and the bullets were flying, what did Trump do? He tweeted out about the Clarity Act saying he's going to put the chips on the table to get this thing done. I also think as midterms approach, there's not a lot of upside for not making this happen. There's more downside than upside, right? And I I still think we it squeezes by.

Austin 论伦理风险与党派立法

So, I guess I would say I remain more skeptical than average, though. I'll say obviously getting to the floor is a very positive sign. Whatever your previous estimate was of probability, you need to have moved it up based on that happening. To me, Chris, the one that I have not heard somebody give me a truly credible solution to yet is the ethics part.

And I think there's two potential ways that that ends up. One, which is probably a temporary good, but extremely long-term bad, is you would not want clarity passing on a purely partisan vote in the House and the Senate. And that is possible with the current framework if you can get to a vote. But I think if it's Republican only, this probably ages like the Affordable Care Act where the other side just takes it apart over time. When you jam through something transformative on purely partisan lines, the history in United States legislation is pretty poor.

Now, the other hand is do we just crash on the ethics rocks completely? If it dies, that's where it's dying. I think every single other issue we have there's a solution to and many of the arguments being made like with the bank lobby around yield are quite frankly special pleading by industries. It's not in good faith for the average consumer, the American economy, the national security apparatus who is growing more and more interested and positive on digital assets as they see the capabilities. The ethics part is the one that as I've talked to people on both sides I still have some doubts about. So, I guess that's why I keep pumping the brakes.

What What are the specific issues on the SX side that you're alluding to?

I I think basically here's the question. Are the Democrats going to vote for something that doesn't force Trump's family to divest world liberty, the meme coin etc.

And would the Republicans send a bill to the president that forces him to do all of that? That to me is like the break point right in the middle. I don't see a great answer. But also, as Chris said, now that it's on the floor, things get weird at the finish line. Like there is a nonzero chance that it does move.

One of the >> it can compromise somewhat like totally away from from crypto,

right?

There's chips.

Gordon 论"活动型"原则的边界

It makes sense. Guess the distinction between uh paying interest and activity is a very clever distinction. It's a good principle. I like the principle. Um the question is going to be like how many edge cases come out of activity based regulation? It looks like a duck, it quacks like a duck. Well, it starts looking at interest again.

Chris 论后 Chevron 时代的立法

So, so this is the problem. So, so what we're living now is we're living in a post Chevron difference world. And so, in the past, it'd be like, hey, you know, tie goes to the regulator. Regulator, you figure it out. We're going to give you a little bit more um leeway to kind of fill in the gaps. right

now you have legislation that has to be much more rigid and prescriptive which makes the legislation worse frankly

uh so it's going to be and then it just goes to the court and like so that that you know in a way we needed the Chevron deference to come away but there were some benefits to it I'm not saying it's time to bring it back by all means

but it's sort of the complexity we're dealing

Austin 论银行游说团体的战略错误

I'll go back the other way though and say the original problem with this is the call it Gordian, not of financial regulation at large because totally

if you want to prevent money market funds from paying yield, you're going to have to rewrite the 40 act because I will remind everybody they are legally obligated to pay people yield right now. You can't hold that money inside of the vehicle and just compound. So, as long as we have tokenized securities on one side and stable coins on the other side and reserves that look like the tokenized securities, there's kind of no way to close this door, it's more a question of form over function. Otherwise, you're rewriting the banking act and the 40 act. And I don't think Congress has the appetite or willingness to do that right now. I mean, they could barely confirm WASH, right? And that's like getting a guy in a seat to do one specific job. it. We think they're going to rewrite the fundamental structure of US securities regulation. No. So, this is part of why I thought this fight was quicksic for the banks in the first place is like, what do you guys get out of this? Like, are you handing essentially a giant win to asset managers by stabbing each other to death? You might have done that.

Rahm 论 Gillibrand 参议员的关键性

Yeah, I think Senator Gillibrand is probably one of the most important people to watch right now. Uh she's pro crypto. uh she was very involved in uh the formation of of legislation from the very beginning, but she's also been adamant against ethics. So uh I'm watching her and I think if if there's a way that uh folks can reach a deal with her, I think it's going to be really impactful.

主题五：债券义警 + Warsh 确认 + 利率市场

All right. So I would be remiss not to talk about a segment we've titled bond vigilantes box wash if we have Gordon here. And the reason I say that is WSH was confirmed. So 54 to 45 vote, the closest Fed chair vote in the modern era. Uh the only Democrat to cross the line was Senator Federman on this one. So not the people crossing the line around clarity.

The first FOMC is in middle of June, but hours after his confirmation, the 25 billion 30-year auction broke 5%. We had 30-year yield hit 5.12 at the high, the first five handles since before the financial crisis of 2008. The 10-year is currently sitting at 4.59. The two-year is at 4.08. Uh CME Fed Watch has 50% odds of a hike by later in the year, which is a complete inversion of the cut narrative, though I think there is a lot to be said about how to interpret that data that we can get into. It is not purely one linear cut.

各方评论

Um, so let's talk about commentary. So Edgar Denny, the man who coined the phrase bond vigilantes, I believe, said they are now setting policy and the Fed may be forced to raise in July to appease the long end. Uh, Vincent An of Wisdom Fixed Income said WSH wanted the option to cut on day one. The bond market just took that option off the table. Uh, Morgan Stanley is saying rate cuts are off the table until 2027. And Ryan Swift of BCA is saying if Walsh pushes dovish during this blood bath, inflation expectations will break out and the Fed loses control of the long end.

Um there are some who view this as a good thing. Phil Blanto, uh Reuters breaking views basically saying vanishing cuts by time and that stripping power from an overinterventionist Fed might be a good thing. But this is a very complex space like interest rates are a very misunderstood thing in traditional markets and there's a lot of complexity to these dynamics.

So I want to lob the ball up Gordon first just as an economist, a market observer, somebody who obviously has deep experience with the Fed. What do you make of what the market is saying about US interest rates right now?

Gordon 拆解期限溢价

Sure. Um I'll give you, you know, answer from my background. I I used to uh work at the Fed uh board in DC and uh you know the one of the first things you look at uh in those positions is what has driven uh the interest rate move. Is it the term premium or is it the expected rate change? If you look at if you take the iconic ACM model and break it down, uh most of the 30-year uh rise in yield is driven by the term premium is uh turn premium right now is I think it's like in the 80 bips or so, which is quite high relative to a couple years ago is negative.

Like what does it mean? It means that the term premium is basically reflecting supply demand. Whereas the expected short rate is expectation of how much the fed is actually going to uh hike or or or or not uh over time. Uh so so much of the increasing the yield being reflected in term premium means that what we're observing is a market reflection of uh perhaps a few things with regards to uh supply and demand. awareness. There'll continue fiscal issues uh continue expansion in fiscal uh aspect and uh perhaps over a long-term uh lose of uh credibility of uh the ability to control inflation and also perhaps some lack of uh foreign demand uh as it was uh I think if you look at just the balance of payment has changed quite a bit over the last year or so.

So that's on the kind of the demand side and in terms of supply side this is where uh it's also quite interesting uh soon to be governor wars has also been a proponent of um actually shrinking the fed balance sheet uh meaning that uh kind of reversing QE which essentially since 2008 has been an expansion in balance sheet although with e and flows in overall size uh that comes at a time when you know you're getting this pressure on the long-term yield as well.

稳定币作为美债边际买家

Um this narrative that others have put out I think that even the US treasur has has uh mentioned in the past of stable coin being the marginal uh buyer of treasury. I think actually has a bit more meat than uh you know people give credit to uh not only because the stable coin circulation is increasing but also because of the maturity uh aspect. uh stable coin maturity is uh the asset that's held is generally very short short-term T bills and reverse repoles which free up the opportunity give up the opportunity for the US treasury to issue more in the shorter term versus longer term. Now if you actually do the duration waiting that actually could mean a significant shift in the dollar duration kind of reduce the supply of longdated dollar duration in the market and uh hence perhaps help moderate uh the the current uh rate pressure upwards.

Um so in a way it's all kind of linked together. uh but I think it's not to think about the rates in itself as a single number but you should break it down into two but also think about the balance sheet aspect how they're playing with the expected changes in u the both the fed balance sheet but also the private sector demand

Austin 论债券买家结构变迁

I think an important part of that that people often don't think about is exactly what you said which is not just who is buying treasuries but asking the next more granular question of who is buying which type of treasury specifically because exactly as you just said, stable coins are going to have a preference for T bills thanks to the Genius Act and the restrictions there. But also, even if they're not, they're doing reverse repo, which is a form of funding that can take treasuries as collateral, but you have larger haircuts on longerdated versus shorterdated. So, there's some preferencing there as well.

the same time that's going on, you know, back from my days on the street, some of the biggest buyers at like the 30-year point are both insurance companies and sovereigns, right? And the preferences of those are shifting over time. Sovereigns just towards holding less longdated US debt. You know, a lot of geopolitical concerns potentially moving around in there, but also insurance companies role with the call it demographic profile of the country that they're serving. So, as the boomers age out and the next largest group of people is probably millennials and we see sort of like a curve transformation for insurance, we could see less demand for the third year.

So, I'm watching term premium. Gordon, it's funny to hear you say, "Hey, term premium at 80 bips is pretty high compared to recent because conversely, it's pretty low compared to at least some truly historical measures where you could see like 1.5 or more. So I think the curve itself changing shape is part of the story here that people should not miss.

Rahm 论投资观点与利率见顶

So Rahm I want to ask you then on an investing standpoint how are you feeling about the long bond right now?

Yeah a few things. So first off uh yeah I agree it's supply demand driven investors are saying I need more compensation for greater inflation risk. That's it. And they know the Fed isn't biased to cut rates. So they're saying something's got to give. And what's going to give is rates are going to go up. That's what's happening. Uh so why all you see it because of oil it's impacting memory prices, gasoline, a lot of different areas. You're seeing an inflation make a comeback.

U I did find it kind of uh ironic that Worsh was uh approved in such a lopsided manner. Elizabeth Warren her litmus test is will you cut rates? This guy wants to cut rates. Like he should be a shoe. So, uh, he he fit the he fit the profile perfectly, uh, which is kind of uncanny. It's more about sending a political message, uh, more than anything else.

Overall though, I would say I expect that the rates are topping out right around here. And if that's the case, then rate sensitive names which have been hammered. You mentioned insurers, Austin, for example, right? Insurers are a balance sheet business like a bank. So when rates go up, the value of their holdings drop, the value of their bonds drop. Uh the value of the assets to fund their future liabilities drop, right? So they've been

But what we're starting to see in the most recent even two or three days is that long duration high free cash flow assets have rallied. There's been a a pullback of over a point in the major indices the last two days. pick and index all of them except these long duration high free cash cash flow assets have been rallying. So the equity markets are saying that they believe the long rates are going to come in which is kind of funny because you usually think of the bond market being smarter than the equity market. I think the equity market actually is right here. I think Kevin Worsh his confirmation was a kind of a capitulatory event. The only thing we haven't seen here is a Ray Dalio video that's gone viral talking about the end times. uh that's missing on the bottom. But

awesome.

I was gonna say we'll we'll know.

Chris 论长期通缩压力与政策协调

Yeah, let me let me chime in. I think long term there's a number of very deflationary pressures coming to hit the economy starting with AI and then on top of that energy prices. The amount of investment going into cheap energy is profound right now. Elon's going to channel it in space. You're going to get compute coming out of that. um that you've never seen before. So I feel like in the long term that's going to be quite deflationary and worse talks about that particularly around AI. It's the near-term that's the problem.

Um you've got sovereigns selling treasuries. Why? Because in certain cases they want to decouple. In other cases they need the money. Oil prices are high and we know why there.

Um, now the difference with this administration in in the postwar era is number one, he's he's reevaluating how we should be looking at inflation in the first place. We've spoken to the trueflation guys in the past. I think we're going to get much more thoughtful on what inflation is. Second, you know, the cooperation between Treasury and Fed and the Fed is going to be like you've never seen before with Besson. And I think that coordination should help arrive at better just holistic policy responses. And that doesn't mean that wars isn't going to be independent. I think coordination, you can be independent and be coordinated at the same time. And I think you should be.

Uh so I'm very positive there. So and last thing is like this geopolitical stuff. Um it it can get worse very quickly or it can get better very quickly. My sense is that it's going to get better because midterms are coming. The American people don't love it. Um, and it's a matter of of it's a it's a tough tough problem, but um, but I think Trump is axe to to deescalate, not escalate if he can.

Agreed.

收尾

All right. Well, we're right up on time, so I'm going to leave it right there. First, I wanted to say in particular, Gordon, thank you for joining us today. The timing here was excellent to talk about these particular subjects. So, I hope our viewers benefited from your knowledge. And then secondly, for everybody, thanks for joining us on Bits and Bips.

So, stay with us. Laura's up next on Unchained with FIA's Walt Lukan and Chris Perkins, breaking down ICE, not the one deporting people, and CME's KYC complaints against Hyperlid, the SpaceX preIPO pers debate, and how the C

Thank you for watching and hope you enjoyed this episode of Bits and Bips. Just remember, nothing we say here is investment advice. And please check unchainedcrypto.com/bits andbs for more disclosures.
