item.description:
## 一、Token 是 AI 时代的"千瓦时"，但它正在重演工业革命最大的认知陷阱
Freda 把 dollar per token 类比为工业时代的 dollar per kWh，这个比喻精彩，但**她自己揭示的反讽更深**：今天投资人以"烧了多少 token"作为景气度指标，恰恰等同于一百年前有人炫耀"我家用电量很大"——这是把成本当成价值的典型误读。
让我意外的不是这个观察本身，而是**它与 Jevons 悖论的反向呼应**：当蒸汽机效率提升时，煤炭总消耗反而暴涨。Freda 给出的预言是"右下两项（token/task 和 $/token）都会大幅下降，但前两项（用户数、任务数）的爆炸会盖过一切"——这意味着 AI 不会出现 Jevons 悖论的简单复刻，而是一个**"效率提升 + 单位价格暴跌 + 任务定义本身被重新发明"的三重叠加**。这是工业革命没有出现过的结构。
## 二、"把电机塞进蒸汽机的位置"——这一集最值钱的隐喻其实指向投资本身
电力发明后 40 年生产率没提升，因为工厂只是把电机塞进蒸汽机原来的位置，没有重新设计流水线。Freda 用这个史实解释为什

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Did Modern Humans Help Make Neanderthals? – David Reich
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最让人意外的反转：基因告诉你"他们是表亲"，文化却在说"他们是兄弟"

David Reich 在这里其实做了一件颠覆人类学常识的事——他在暗示"物种身份"可能不是由基因决定的，而是由"传承链"决定的。常规叙事是：基因近 = 亲缘近。但他指出尼安德特人 95% 的核基因组属于丹尼索瓦人系，可线粒体 DNA（母系）和 Y 染色体（父系）却是现代人系，再加上勒瓦娄哇石器技术——这就像一个被收养的孩子，血型随生父，但姓氏、语言、家训全是养父家的。

真正令人战栗的洞见是：尼安德特人可能是被"现代人文化波"殖民、改写、再吸收的一个旧族群。换句话说，他们不是我们的远亲，而是被我们"文化输血 + 母系基因输血"的一群本地人。这种"少数血统主导身份认同"的现象在族群形成中是反常的，但 Reich 给出的"波前扩张 + 局部基因稀释"模型，可以同时解释"为什么只有 5%"和"为什么这 5% 偏偏是线粒体和 Y 染色体"两个看似矛盾的事实。

二、跨领域连接：这是一场 30 万年前的"开源运动"——Levallois 技术是史前的 Linux

把视角从古人类学拉到技术传播史会出现一个惊人的同构： - Levallois 技术（中石器革命）= 一种"高质量、远距离原料、标准化打制流程"的技术协议。 - 它从高加索/东非这个"技术核心区"向四面八方扩散。 - 携带它的人群在欧洲被本地基因 95% 稀释，在非洲被 20% 稀释——但协议本身完整保留。

这和今天 开源软件协议、字母系统（腓尼基字母→希腊→拉丁→西里尔）、甚至宗教传播 的模式高度一致：载体可以被替换，meme 却保真度极高。Reich 实际上是在告诉我们：基因像硬件，文化像协议层，协议可以脱离原始硬件继续传播。如果这成立，那么"什么是人类的某一支系"就不再是生物学问题，而是信息论问题——谁继承了那条技术-认知协议，谁就是那条传承链的延续。

更刺激的延伸：如果 30 万年前就有"技术协议脱离原始携带者"的现象，那么今天 AI 通过文本继承人类思维模式，本质上是同一种现象的极端版本——载体（碳基→硅基）被彻底替换，协议（语言、推理、文化）却被保真传递。Reich 描述的尼安德特人，是这条规律最早的化石证据。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：人类起源叙事正在从"树"变成"网"，但我们的语言还停留在"树"

主流叙事——包括教科书——仍然用 分支树（phylogenetic tree） 来讲人类起源，因为树形结构容易画、容易记、容易做考试题。但 Reich 这段访谈的真正颠覆在于：他在悄悄地把"树"换成"网"，再换成"波场"。

注意他用的词：deems（小地理单元）、wave front（波前）、introgression（渗入）、95% replacement。这不是生物学语言，这是流体力学和扩散方程的语言。他实际上是在用偏微分方程式的直觉重写人类起源——一个"现代人浓度场"在欧亚非大陆上扩散，浓度随距离衰减，但携带的"文化场"几乎不衰减。

这里有一个少有人指出的元洞见：我们对"人类起源"的认知瓶颈，不是化石不够多，而是叙事工具落后于数据复杂度。古 DNA 已经把祖先关系变成了一张高维稀疏图，但大众科普还在用"我们是谁的后代"这种线性家谱叙事。Reich 反复用"playing with this idea / probably wrong"这种保留态度，其实是在为一个叙事范式转移铺路——未来十年，"人类起源"会从"故事"（story）变成"地图"（map），再变成"场"（field）。

而那 5% 与 95% 之间的不对称——少数血统、多数文化——也悄悄回应了一个当代议题：身份从来不是由统计学多数决定的，而是由传承结构决定的。这一点，无论是讨论古人类、移民社会，还是讨论 AI 如何继承人类文明，都是同一个深层结构在不同尺度上的回响。

📝 转录文本

一、问题的提出：我们也许搞错了"古人类与现代人"的关系

The thing I'm thinking about a lot recently is the possibility that maybe we're not thinking right — in the right way — about the relationship between archaic and modern humans.

The standard model is one like this: Denisovans, these archaic humans that were found from ancient DNA, and Neanderthals descend from a common ancestral population 5 or 600,000 years ago. And these two separated earlier, maybe 700 to 800,000 years ago, from the ancestors of modern humans, people like us. So that's the big result of a lot of studies since 2010.

But there's also evidence of an interbreeding event that happened maybe 200 to 300,000 years ago. And that actually resulted in modern humans contributing DNA to the ancestors of Neanderthals. So this is maybe 5% of the DNA of Neanderthals comes from this interbreeding event, and a lot of studies have shown this.

二、考古学的暗示：尼安德特人在文化上更像我们

I'm very interested in this because, actually, from the archaeological record, Neanderthals and modern humans sort of look quite similar to each other — much more similar to each other than a lot of them do to Denisovans, these archaic humans in East Asia.

So a lot of the history, people have thought that Neanderthals are our sister. But then, in 2010, the sequencing of the Denisovan genome made it very clear that on average Denisovans are closer to Neanderthals than to modern humans. So this was a very confusing result. Most people now think that Neanderthals and Denisovans descend from a common ancestral population that separated earlier from the ancestors of modern humans.

So I'm interested in the possibility that actually the right way to think about Neanderthals is somehow as culturally modern humans, even though genetically they're mostly Denisovans.

三、中石器革命：一个跨大陆的技术指纹

The model I'm thinking about is motivated by this archaeological phenomenon known as the Middle Stone Age revolution.

So, if this is Africa and this is, I don't know, Europe — we know that the new way of making stone tools, with these cores that were very carefully mined far away from the locations they were used, made out of high quality stone like flint, start being used 3 or 400,000 years ago, first in the Caucasus, places like Georgia today, or East Africa.

And this way of making stone tools, which is quite revolutionary and is known in Europe as the Middle Paleolithic and in Africa as the Middle Stone Age, is associated with much more widespread use of fire and also moving stone around at much further distances than before.

I'm interested in the idea that this is something that's shared between Africa, between modern humans, and Neanderthals — somehow some shared cultural feature that's absent in East Asia. And that might have a relationship in the genetic data and is somehow related to this 5% DNA.

四、核心假说：一次发明、四向扩散、文化保留、基因被稀释

So the idea I'm interested in is the possibility that there is a population here that invents the Middle Stone Age / Middle Paleolithic — sometimes called Levallois technology — and that people from this population expand into Europe and they mix with the local archaic humans who are there. And that is what this 5% interbreeding event is: it happens 2 to 300,000 years ago, and it produces a population that as it expands across this landscape in Europe mostly picks up the local DNA and becomes mostly archaic genetically, but retains its modern human culture — the way of making stone tools and some of its traditions.

五、线粒体与 Y 染色体的悖论

One of the things that's super interesting about this is that if you actually look at the genetics — the whole genome — the Neanderthals and Denisovans cluster. But if you look at the mitochondrial DNA, which people get from their moms (and they get from their moms), Neanderthals and modern humans cluster.

So if you look at the mitochondrial DNA, Denisovans and modern humans share an ancestor well more than 700 or 800,000 years ago, as you expect from the history. And if you look at the Y chromosome, that you get from your dad, Denisovans and modern humans share an ancestor more than 7 or 800,000 years ago, which is consistent with this history.

But if you look at the Neanderthal mitochondrial DNA, it's only 3 to 450,000 years. If you look at the Y chromosome, it's only 3 to 450,000 years.

So what the current genetic work is asking us to believe is that even though this is only 5% of the whole genome, it reintroduces mitochondrial DNA and Y chromosomes — and they jump up to 100% frequency. It's kind of a crazy claim, because the probability of this occurring by chance is low: maybe 5% × 5%, so a very small number.

And so it's sort of what we actually all believe, but it's a very surprising event. Somehow it's accreted all the findings in the whole literature so that we make ourselves believe this, but it seems unlikely on first principles that somehow only 5% would introduce both the Y chromosome and mitochondrial DNA.

六、来自 Sima de los Huesos 的直接证据

And it really looks like this. There's this amazing data from this site in Spain that's 3 to 400,000 years old, a site called Sima de los Huesos. They have a nuclear genome that looks Neanderthal-like — most of the genome — but their mitochondrial DNA and Y chromosome is Denisovan-like.

So it really looks like there was a population related to modern humans that pushed into this Sima de los Huesos-like population, displaced its mitochondrial DNA and Y chromosome, but kept the rest of its genome. So it really looked like something like this happened.

七、波前扩张模型：少数侵入者如何被本地基因吞没

So the idea I'm playing with — and probably is wrong, who knows — is that there's a landscape (this is maybe Europe), and you can break it up into a hundred or so demes, little areas. And modern humans get introduced at the bottom right corner, in the Middle East or something, and they spread into Europe.

As this population spreads, there's a wave front of expansion, and they're interacting with the local archaic humans. Even if there's a small amount of interbreeding, the theory — from lots of simulations and studies of all these different species like mammals and birds and so on — shows that when there's even a little amount of interbreeding as there's an invasion or movement of expansion of one group into the territory occupied by the other, there's massive introgression of local genes.

These pioneers at the wave front, they'll sometimes interbreed with the local population. There's so many of them around that their DNA will get swamped by the local group. So by the time they make it to the other side, they're largely local.

八、母系传承与文化传承的耦合

And so maybe what we're seeing is that this is what's happened. You have a modern human population that's matrilineal, for example, where transmission of making stone tools this way is happening from your mother to the kid, and that's why they're retaining their mitochondrial DNA. But by the time they get to the other end of Europe, they're mostly archaic — mostly local archaic. So you end up with a 95% population replacement.

So this would explain why the mitochondrial DNA is shared between Neanderthals and modern humans, and it would also explain why the mixture proportion is only 5%.

九、对称的另一面：现代人本身也是混血的

But the really interesting thing is that there's other evidence from studies of modern humans — that modern humans are also admixed. The right way to think about this is that modern humans are a mixture of two groups maybe 1.5 million years ago, and that they come together 200 to 300,000 years ago, with maybe 20% ancestry from this archaic African group and 80% ancestry from this early modern lineage. And the same group then mixes with Neanderthals — and it's 5% modern here and 95% local there.

So you actually have this key population that makes the Middle Stone Age, or Levallois technology — this one that appears here — and it expands in all directions: into Europe here, into Africa here, 2 to 300,000 years ago, bringing this technology, bringing these new ideas, bringing perhaps some genetic adaptations.

It expands into archaic humans in Europe; it mixes with the local population; it gets 95% replaced but still retains its cultural features and maybe some genetic features. And it expands in Africa too — and here it's not 95% replaced, it's only 20% replaced. Probably the reason that happens is that this group is much, much more diverged — it's much more archaic, it's 1.5 million years diverged rather than 7 to 800,000 years diverged. As a result, there are many more incompatibilities genetically and much more barriers to gene flow. But there's still a lot — maybe 20% — and we have evidence that this is a big mixture that happens.

十、一个更早、更被忽视的"革命"

So what you're actually seeing is a modern human expansion both into Europe and into Africa. In one place, it forms Neanderthals; in one place, it forms the ancestors of everybody living today. But all of these groups are descended from this key, sort of revolutionary event that happens here.

We often talk about the revolutionary events 50 to 100,000 years ago — the more symbolic behavior and so on and so forth — that sort of first appear in Africa and the Middle East and spread beyond. But there's also this earlier event. And this event is contemporaneous with the breakup of all the different groups also in Africa today — the Khoisan Southern Africans and the Central African rainforest hunter-gatherers. So one wonders whether this is an equally important formative event.

十一、结论：尼安德特人或许是我们的"表亲"，而不是堂亲

And it also, if that's true, makes you think of Neanderthals as actually somehow our cousins — that they actually share our Y chromosome, they share mitochondrial DNA, they share formation of this 2 or 300,000 year-old event, they share a toolkit.

So even though the genome is telling us that they're cousins of Neanderthals and the Denisovans, the actual correct way to think about them maybe, in an important sense, is somehow as the close cousins of modern humans.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最让人意外的反转：基因告诉你"他们是表亲"，文化却在说"他们是兄弟"
David Reich 在这里其实做了一件颠覆人类学常识的事——他在暗示**"物种身份"可能不是由基因决定的，而是由"传承链"决定的**。常规叙事是：基因近 = 亲缘近。但他指出尼安德特人 95% 的核基因组属于丹尼索瓦人系，可线粒体 DNA（母系）和 Y 染色体（父系）却是现代人系，再加上勒瓦娄哇石器技术——这就像一个被收养的孩子，血型随生父，但姓氏、语言、家训全是养父家的。
真正令人战栗的洞见是：**尼安德特人可能是被"现代人文化波"殖民、改写、再吸收的一个旧族群**。换句话说，他们不是我们的远亲，而是被我们"文化输血 + 母系基因输血"的一群本地人。这种"少数血统主导身份认同"的现象在族群形成中是反常的，但 Reich 给出的"波前扩张 + 局部基因稀释"模型，可以同时解释"为什么只有 5%"和"为什么这 5% 偏偏是线粒体和 Y 染色体"两个看似矛盾的事实。
## 二、跨领域连接：这是一场 30 万年前的"开源运动"——Levallois 技术是史前的 Linux
把视角从古人类学拉到**技

item.title: [Business of Sport] Players vs The Slams: The Pay Dispute Threatening to Rip Tennis Apart
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽略的真相：球员其实在为"前辈的剩余红利"领工资

最反直觉的观察是：Sinner 和 Sabalenka 拿到的天价奖金，本质上是费德勒、纳达尔、小威、博格、麦肯罗用三十年构建的"品牌信托基金"的分红。视频中嘉宾点破了一个残酷事实——这一代顶尖球员还没达到前辈那种"凭一己之力撑起一个时代"的全球文化影响力。换句话说，他们正在收割上一代人种下的果实，却以"我们是产品核心"的名义要求更高比例。

这制造了一个有趣的悖论：真正应该获得"涨薪权"的，恰恰是那些没法用涨薪来证明自己的人——也就是排名 50-128 的球员，因为他们是赛事生态多样性的真正承载者。但替他们喊话的，却必须是 Sabalenka 这种"喊话效率最高、动机最可疑"的人。这就是为什么舆论会卡住。

二、跨领域连接：大满贯其实是"教会式机构"，被误读成了"NBA 球队"

最深刻的盲点在这里：球员潜意识里把大满贯类比成 NBA 球队（老板赚钱→该分给球员），但大满贯的真实结构更接近大学捐赠基金或宗教组织——温网 90% 的利润流向 LTA 做青少年推广。

这让我联想到一个奇特的跨领域平行：大满贯和米其林指南的商业逻辑高度相似。米其林星不直接付钱给厨师，但获得星星的厨师可以靠声望在别处变现；米其林本身把利润用于维护"评级体系的神圣性"。大满贯就是网球世界的米其林——它出售的不是球员的劳动，而是"被这里认证过"的稀缺性。所以 Sabalenka 真正在做的，不是要求加薪，而是要求米其林按厨师营业额的 22% 分账——这在结构上是错位的。

而 LIV 高尔夫的失败恰恰证明：当你把"教会"改成"球队"，短期能给球员发更多钱，长期会让认证本身贬值。球员越成功，他们越不敢真的罢工——因为他们罢的不是工资，是自己未来的"圣徒身份"。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：罢工威胁是一种"反身性的自我证伪"

这里有一个精妙的博弈论结构值得点出：这场罢工讨论本身就在解构罢工的可能性。

逻辑链是这样的： - 如果顶级球员真的集体罢工 → Zverev 这种"次顶级"球员立刻意识到这是夺冠的历史窗口 → 不会跟进 - 如果顶级球员意识到次顶级不会跟进 → 罢工就变成"白送冠军给对手" - 所以这个威胁必须永远停留在威胁阶段才有效，一旦验证就立刻失效

这和核威慑的悖论一模一样——核武器的价值在于不被使用。Sabalenka 的"罢工牌"和冷战时期的核武器结构同构：可信度建立在永不兑现之上。一旦兑现，威慑结构就崩塌。

更深一层：这也解释了为什么大满贯采取"防御性沉默"的回应策略是理性的。他们不需要谈判，只需要让时间流逝——因为时间站在"威胁者必须自我克制"这一边。这是网球版的"等待戈多"，而戈多永远不会来，因为戈多来了，等待者就失业了。

意外的衍生洞察：女子网球长期由东欧球员主导，这其实是另一个"信托基金"现象——苏联体育体制崩塌后，被国家化训练浸润过的几代女性运动员，用纪律性优势收割了西方商业网球的奖金池。她们也是在领"前一个系统遗产的红利"。这与顶级球员领"前辈品牌红利"是同一个隐喻的不同变体——整个网球经济，可能比任何人意识到的都更像一个"代际套利市场"。

📝 转录文本

引子：球员与大满贯的薪酬之争再起

It's been a really important topic for a long time. It's been, should we say, a battle between the tennis players and the tournaments, most notably the Grand Slams, between how much players are compensated for in comparison to how much revenue the tournaments are making.

So, last week, the reason we're talking about it now is Aryna Sabalenka, the women's world number one, stated that she was, well, the players were very disappointed with the French Open's prize money.

Put it a bit stronger than that. They were willing to strike, go on strike. Go on strike because they felt that the representation, or well, the proportional representation for players to total revenue was significantly under where it should be.

And this is kind of opened a bit of a box for a number of players to now come out and go, well, one person said it, and so I'm going to back it. Coco Gauff, Jannik Sinner on the men's side as well. I do get the feeling now that with these Grand Slams making the most money they've ever made, and the French Open now particularly being around the corner, it seems to have got an extra bit of traction that makes it particularly interesting to cover.

数字拆解：17% 的天花板与法网的"涨钱不涨比例"

So, the big challenge for tennis players with the Grand Slams is that they do not get more than about 15 to 17%.

It's more like 17 now. 17% of revenue. Now, I know the French Open is a bit lesser, but there are one or two others which are a bit higher than that.

But, it's so interesting you correct me on that. So, right now, the big argument here is that while the French Open increased the prize money by 10 million euros, the percentage of revenue that the players are getting is actually decreased from about 15 and a half percent to just under 15%. Yet, the prize pool went up by 10 million euros.

So, the total prize pool for this year is 61.7 million euros for the French Open. That represents 14.3% of total revenue. If you're putting this into context of broader sport, and we talked about American sports and how they've compensated the athletes in the past, quite often you can get up to 50%. The ask of the players, to me is pretty reasonable considering the contribution that players make to tennis.

So, what they really want is they want a 22% of revenue contribution from the Grand Slams to the prize pool. So, to put that into context, if the French Open was to give 22%, it would go from 61 million euros to 88 million euros. This year, they are predicted to make 400 million euros. We're not talking about them requesting 50% of the pool of revenue going back to the players, but we're talking about them having greater say, or sorry, greater receipt and greater say, as it happens.

顶级球员发声的尴尬：钱不是为自己要的？

This has caused some people to push back because it's come from the top players. The world number one, Aryna Sabalenka, the world number one, Jannik Sinner. You may say I'm an idiot. I believe Charles—

Charles, I wouldn't say I wouldn't I wouldn't I wouldn't say that.

I don't think Sabalenka minds whether she gets an extra two to 300 grand based on her winning or not. What I do think this conversation really stretches to is how sustainable it is for the majority of players to play tennis further down where they are getting smaller packets, relying on Grand Slams to fund their year's worth of tennis because if you lose in the first round of somebody's tournaments, you can make anywhere between 60 to 80,000 pounds, let's say. That is a huge part of their ability to keep competitive on tour and to actually make a living out of it.

If the Grand Slams—if the French Open gave 27 million more euros to that, there would be a significant amount of money that they may be able to put into that total prize pool, which takes some of that money up a little bit more. And if each Grand Slam gave first round losers an extra 20,000 pounds, that's an extra 80,000 pounds a year. I get that that may be fanciful if it's coming from the world number one and you think that this is just brilliant capitalist corporate mentality. But for the players to be asking for this I don't think it's unreasonable.

钱去哪儿了？大满贯不是股东公司

Where do you think this money currently goes?

So, that's the key. Where do you—So, the underlying assumption here, and I'm not saying it's your assumption, I'm saying that the underlying assumption of the players seems to be that there is profiteering going on by Roland Garros and by the All England Club and by the US Tennis Federation and Tennis Australia. That they are nicking all this money which would rightly be the players and I don't know, stuffing in their back pockets, paying the chief executive another 10 million a year or, you know, having much plusher boardrooms or whatever it might be.

That is not the case. Let's be quite clear here. So, if you look at Wimbledon, right, which is also round the corner, relatively speaking, and it's our home Grand Slam, so it's something that we sort of keep a close eye on. 90% of Wimbledon's profits are given to the Lawn Tennis Association, which is Britain's governing body of tennis, for grassroots tennis promotion. Right? So, that's encouraging young kids to play the game.

双向沟通失败：大满贯不解释，球员没区分

Now, I think there's communications problems on both sides here, right? And I think you've alluded to one of them already.

So, the first communications problem is I don't think currently that the Grand Slams individually, and particularly as a group, communicate what it is they're trying to do. What are we doing here? What's the point of our existence, right? We're not just—We're not like a football club which is or American football franchise which is owned by a really rich guy, and if he pays the players less, he gets more, right? That's not the situation. These are not shareholder organizations where there's a dividend being paid out to wealthy owners.

So, there's a lack of communication going on there, and all I see from the Grand Slams individually is quite defensive, quite—"Well, we put it up by 10% this year." Okay, but that in itself doesn't answer the question, which is if you put it up by 10% and actually it should go up by 30%, then you haven't really sort of solved the problem here.

So, there's a communication problem there. And then there's a communication problem on the players' side, cuz you've put it quite nicely there, which is if it were the case in a sport where historically the lower-ranked players have actually been relatively poorly compensated, although actually that has changed quite a bit recently. We'll come back onto that in a moment.

But let's just say that Aryna Sabalenka and Jannik Sinner said, "We earned 20 million a year each last year in prize money before we get on to endorsements. We earned 20 million a year each in prize money this year. And far be it from us to say that we deserve more. But we feel it incumbent on us to speak up on behalf of our fellow pros, the ones that fall on hard times, the ones who aren't quite as good as us, etc., but who we need to make a circuit. And we want to see these guys and these girls being paid more money. And we want to see their pensions being looked after better. And actually we'd be very comfortable with the first-place prize money staying exactly where it is."

I think there'd be a lot of public sympathy for that situation. It's not what they've said. They haven't said that. Explicitly not, okay?

And as I said also, the Grand Slams saying, "Well, bear in mind that actually the money that we're talking about here currently gets given to encourage kids to play this sport, which is where you guys and girls have all come from. So, please just be aware that we are actually fundamentally charitable organizations." That point is also not being made.

So, it ends up in this rather unseemly sort of, you know, arm wrestling terms of whether it should be 8% extra this year or 9% extra this year, which I just don't think really gets us anywhere.

谁创造了营收？球员 vs. 赛事运营的核心争议

I think they're asking for proportional representation on growth of revenue to growth of prize money.

But are they individually responsible for that growth in revenue? There's a strong argument to say that the athletes are absolutely—

chance, no chance, Charlie.

So, you don't—Okay, so you—But the athletes are the core product.

Oh, yeah. So, okay, you're basically saying that Jannik Sinner is somehow much more attractive than Roger Federer was. Really? Absolutely not. What's happening here? Quite the opposite, in fact. Roger Federer was a much bigger draw than Jannik Sinner will ever be. What's actually happening here is that the Grand Slams, and we've discussed actually Tennis Australia being particular about this, have become better at commercializing their own event. That's what's happened.

I mean, no one can say with any degree of seriousness that Aryna Sabalenka is a bigger commercial draw than Serena Williams. I mean, forget it. No chance.

Is that what they're saying?

No, what you've just said is that if it's the players who are responsible for the increase in revenue. It's not. It's absolutely not. I—you would argue right now that with the possible exception of Alcaraz, that there isn't really any of the very top players who is on that level of Serena and Federer and Nadal who are really themselves as individual people massively globally famous.

I don't think that those players yet have achieved that kind of—and all that Borg and McEnroe were and Connors in the past or Becker and Lendl. They're not quite there yet. I'm not criticizing them for that. They're just not quite there yet. They haven't won as many Grand Slams. They haven't been around quite as long. Their personalities in Sinner's case, less in Sabalenka's case, are not quite as well developed. Whatever. No criticism of them.

They are reaping the rewards in what they get paid from the work that Borg and McEnroe and Becker and Connors and Nadal and Federer did before them in making tennis this massive global thing. Bully for them. Congratulations. If Tennis Australia is super smart at adding an extra week onto the front end of the Australian Open, which makes it into a three-week tennis festival rather than a two-week tournament, that has got nothing to do with Aryna Sabalenka and Jannik Sinner. That's Tennis Australia being smart to make more money, to put more money into grassroots tennis in Australia.

Why should they be giving that money to the top players? I don't think they should. I think they should face them down.

大满贯的"品牌不可替代性"：观众真的在乎谁参赛吗？

And there's another point here. There's one last point I want to make, which is that Grand Slam audiences, and we discussed this before with the Masters. What's the biggest star? The US Masters or Bryson DeChambeau? Are people not going to watch the Masters cuz Bryson decides to go on YouTube? I bet there will not be a single person who says I'm not going to watch the US Masters cuz Bryson DeChambeau is not playing in it. I would love to see the concept tested that Wimbledon viewing figures will massively decrease cuz Yannick Sinner is not playing. Don't believe it.

So I completely agree and I think the point I was going to bring up next was—

No, no, no, no, you're getting a bit of a rant there.

No, I love it. It's—

No, but this is—The pint—The pint's halfway down. It's—

[laughter]

It's starting to—He's already—He's in rant mode.

No, no, but I think I do think this is an impassioned topic.

LIV 模式 vs. 大满贯：当传承遇上球员议价权

The biggest challenge that you've got with something like LIV that we have criticized previously is that the players are the absolute stars cuz there's no heritage to the tournaments they are playing in. And so the executive have basically left themselves beholden to the negotiation power of those players.

Yes. Where I find this interesting is to that point—Look, you can say boycott and that's to be very clear, right? That is what the players are talking about in this discussion. They are saying we will strike. They're floating it. They haven't quite gone as far as saying that's what they will do, right? That's been the narrative in the last week, right? We are prepared to go that far to that length.

To which case you just get a separation, right? Fine. Grand Slams have no players, players have no Grand Slams players.

And no, Grand Slams will still have players. Like what? Grand Slams will still have players.

Exactly. So how far down does that strike go? I'd be fascinated to see that because fundamentally tennis and as with golf is a selfish sport and the winning of a major is the making of a career and the making of a fortune.

You also have to look at Aryna Sabalenka's current earnings of 20 million a year and say, how much of that, even if she thinks the prize money is not high enough, how much of that 20 million is currently coming from the majors? And the answer is a massive chunk of it, about half.

罢工的博弈论困境：替补选手的"千载难逢"

And how great would it be, right, if you did have, let's say the top 20 players all go on strike. It'd be very interesting. In fact, let's be honest, if the five in the men's game, if Sinner and Alcaraz went on strike. Well, Alcaraz is injured, so. Zverev is in. Yeah. Zverev is going, "This is my—This is my shot," right? So, that's your number three seed, who's probably going, "Well, with those two out, I may actually finally fulfill my Grand Slam prophecy."

So, collective strike action all bundling together for this greater good. If actually any of those players who didn't strike suddenly participated cuz the Grand Slam still going on and suddenly realized, "Hey, do you know what? I've got a chance to win $4 million." Well, they're highly likely to go and participate in that tournament.

Absolutely right. So, again, all of these threats, where does it actually net out?

I think this to me sounds like a sensible negotiation in an industry which rarely sees sensible conversation around this type of topic. So, they are asking for 22%. They're not coming to the table with a completely unrealistic proposal. Now, they supposedly wrote letters last year to the four Grand Slams. They didn't receive a single response on the points that they've raised. You want surely in this environment where talent is increasingly more influential to secure the quality of that product.

Messi 例外论：把个例当规律的认知陷阱

Do you think so? I don't think it is. And I think LIV has been and the Saudi Pro League and various other things have been the control study, the test case for the idea that it's not about the competition, it's about whichever players happen to be there at one time.

And I think where this has sort of come from is the freak one-off case that's Lionel Messi, the greatest footballer in history who is simultaneously a genius, a graceful person, a legend, everyone combined to Messi. And in the world's power of a continent.

And in the world's most popular sport.

So, Messi and his journey in going to Miami and once attracting vast amounts of attention to Miami and there being huge competition. People would want to go and see Messi one time and all this type of stuff. I think that is given a general sense and an idea that this applies more broadly to really great players of other sports as well and indeed football.

And I'm just not quite sure that that's the case and I think the US PGA tour versus LIV has rather proven that to me at least, which is that I think it's incumbent on the big brands, the big tournaments to hold their nerve on this and say, "We believe that the best players should want to play in our event."

I'd love to see what the percentage of revenue that the Masters makes that it devotes to prize money. I don't think it'll be much more than that. I'd have to go back cuz obviously as we said before, they leave an awful lot of revenue on the table, right? So, we know that, but fundamentally—

low. It was low. I remember it was—

Right. But fundamentally, it's still a tournament that's making 150, 200 million bucks, right? So, are we saying that they're giving 100 million dollars in prize money at the Masters? Of course not. They're not, right? But ultimately, they hold the line of saying, "If you don't want to play at Augusta National, if when you receive that invitation, you decide that you don't want to play here, if when you receive that invitation to come and play at the All England Club in Wimbledon, if you decide that actually you'd rather not, knock yourself out."

There'll be a lot of players that will. There'll be a lot of players that will.

历史回响：澳网曾经也"无足轻重"

Interestingly, going back a little bit in time and certainly I mean to be honest with you, a bit before my time, but certainly I don't know about people your time, the Australian Open was a bit of a take it or leave it event for some of the top players. It was probably when travel was far more complex, right?

Yeah, exactly.

And the time of year and so on and so forth and the prize money wasn't great and so, some players decided they did want to play in Australia. Did the Australian Open say, "Well, the answer to this is could we bring our prize money?" Well, they could have done, but they decided not to.

数据更新：奖金真实涨了多少？

I think it is also worth reflecting what has happened in prize money, right? So, if you look at Wimbledon, which has broadly similar prize money levels to what you were discussing, so it's 55 million pounds of prize money, which is almost exactly double what it was in 2014. Okay, so in 2014, you're talking about 25 million. Now you're talking about 55 million. If you add inflation to that number in 2014, bear in mind this was an epic era where we've been talking about Federer and Co. And that inflationary number of 25 million by now would be about 34 million. So, there has been a big increase, right?

In terms of first round money, it's gone up by 128% in that time. So, this is the people again talking about the journeymen, the ones who don't necessarily always make it to the later stages and if therefore this underlying money as you say forms a big part in their whole personal business model, it's gone up by 128%. Qualifiers up by 309% during that time. So, that's for the ones who get far in the qualifying rounds. Like there have been very, very considerable increases in prize money.

大满贯 vs. ATP/WTA：议价结构的根本差异

Now, what you're saying is, yeah, but the tournaments themselves are making even more money, so they should be devoting even more back into the players.

And when it comes to the ATP Tour and the WTA Tour, where each individual tournament has either no heritage or at least some heritage but nothing worth writing home about. So, they have to compete for the top players. They have to make the decision like any top player could easily make the decision not to play in my tournament. So, unless I pay them as much as I possibly can, they won't come. And if they don't come, I won't fill the stands and I won't get an another TV deal.

This is not a problem facing the Grand Slams. They're going to fill their stands and they're going to get their TV deals, which are often on very long terms anyway. So, the jeopardy just isn't there for the Grand Slams. And I just hope that they're able to get their heads together and make the case that actually what we're doing here is we're trying to grow the game of tennis as a whole.

And actually, whether Aryna Sabalenka earns 20 million bucks a year or 23 million bucks a year, that's a matter for her. And if she decides, like Bryson DeChambeau, that she wants to go and play on YouTube, she should knock herself out. She can crack on with that. What we're about here is providing a competitive tennis tournament which attracts the best players in the world who do want to be here and paying them what, by any stretch of imagination, is a very considerable sum of money.

反驳与平衡：底层球员的现实仍然艰难

I do think there is a broader discussion and probably maybe it's one for another day. And I think we've said before we'd have a much bigger look at the finances of tennis in the ATP's baseline program and the minimum salaries, etc. But while you put a very strong argument there and convinced me in lots of ways. We're still looking at players that are fighting hard to earn a proper living and be really competitive on tour. Yeah.

So, I'm then partially looking at it and going, "Okay, while all of your justifications are correct, I still want a thriving tennis environment for the top 128 players in my competition." And right now, if I'm—let's just say okay, let's say I lose in all the first rounds of the Grand Slams and I make 250,000 pounds a year, right? That after tax goes down to—

Charlie, it's got a lot better. So, actually, cuz I was thinking in your way of thinking when I approached this topic cuz certainly 5 or 10 years ago, it was sort of renowned in sports business that tennis players ranked between 50 and 100 basically were struggling. Okay, but it turns out actually, the situation has changed quite considerably.

So, we spoken already about how Alcaraz and Sabalenka are making 20 million bucks a year, okay? Fantastic. Well done. You're the best player in the world in your gender at a sport which is globally competitive. You deserve it. Absolutely no problem with that whatsoever, by the way.

The world number 30 last year in men's tennis was a British guy called Cameron Norrie and he made 2 million US last year. Okay, so that's quite a big drop off, but even so, you'd say a pretty good living even after expenses and so on and so forth.

What was really interesting actually is that the world number 100 in women's tennis, Anna Bondar, made 650,000 bucks. Right? What did I say to you earlier when you sent this to me?

Yeah, you said it's not as big a discrepancy as you thought. You said, "Is that annual or is that career earnings?"

No, annual. Annual earnings.

So, that was way bigger than I—

And that's been—So, do you remember what I said earlier when I said first round money's up by 128%? There has been a considerable effort to improve the lot of the journeyman players to the point where if you look at 650 grand, you say, "Okay, after paying some tax on it, probably 400 grand. After paying travel expenses and coaches fees physio and blah blah blah, maybe 200 grand net." Not saying that she's getting rich out of it, but she's definitely making a living out of it and she's the 100th best player in the world.

So, look, It's an improving environment.

It's been an improving environment at all levels.

PTPA 与领导层问题：发声的人选错了？

It's, you know, a legitimate debate. I'm not saying it's not a legitimate debate. I think where I would rather see it go from the player perspective is if they formed a players association and if the head of the players association was not Aryna Sabalenka and Jannik Sinner and if the head of was more like a mid-ranking type player.

Novak Djokovic and Vasek Pospisil. So, the PTPA, the Professional Tennis Players Association, really brought this properly and tried to unionize that group of players. Pospisil obviously of a far lesser ranking than Djokovic. Djokovic has actually stepped back from that now. I think partially because of the optics of having a player that earns incredible money and always has done on tour. But, that's maybe where my naivety might and where you turn around and go, "No, I completely disagree."

I just I struggle to see a world where Sinner and Sabalenka purely go on this fight because they want a few hundred grand more while they could make that on an Instagram post.

Well, it's more than a few hundred grand more because if you're talking about going from 60 to 80, that's a 40% increase, right? So, they're actually talking about going from, you know, 20 million a year to 30 million a year. You know, if you're talking about it as a year in the year as a whole. Now, I know that not all their earnings are Grand Slams, so maybe it's from 20 million to 25 million.

And I'm not saying, by the way, that that is their sole intent cuz they have spoken about pensions. They have spoken about various other things, but I think it'd be super helpful if the leading players clarified that they were absolutely fine with what they would be being paid. What they were looking for is for the rest of the tour to be better looked after.

Anyhow, I rest my case and I fully expect other people to disagree with me on it.

收尾闲谈：大满贯的独特魅力与女子网坛的东欧基因

Okay. Excellent. It's fascinating. It's something that, particularly going into a tennis summer—

Yeah, cuz you got Roland Garros and then Wimbledon coming up just literally in the next couple of months. So, do you love it? Do you love your [clears throat] tennis?

I I love what I love watching top tennis.

Yeah.

I'm not somebody who will turn on Eurosport or whatever to go and watch, um, you know, the Warsaw Open, um, whatever it might be. I, you know, if it's on, fine, it's on, but I'm not that fascinated by it.

But, when it comes to Grand Slam tennis, I do watch Grand Slam tennis. Um, it's somehow the the ambiance of the whole thing. They've all got their different styles, don't they? Those those Grand Slams have all got their different styles. And just the history and just going back and remembering the great stars who you've seen in those events and it just imprints in your memory.

You know, I remember the first memory I have of Wimbledon, which was on the TV, not live, um, was of watching—Perry?

[laughter]

Somebody wearing Fred Perry clothing, Charles.

[laughter]

Um, actually it was not cuz it was John McEnroe. He was definitely not wearing Fred Perry clothing, um, stomping around wearing a bandana, um, shouting and swearing at the umpire and the linesman and that must have been like, you know, in the '82, '83, '84, that type of era. Um, and it's it just imprints itself on your memory. Um so, yeah, very very fond of of the Grand Slam events, but probably not such a great um viewer of of the lesser events.

incredibly powerful as a sport over the years of of individual sport in particular for female sport. Yes. We talked previously about um the top earning athletes. Yeah. The top earning female athlete—

is almost always a tennis player.

player. Yeah, always tennis players.

Interesting question, um which I can't answer, but maybe a people in our audience who are more sort of into tennis might be able to answer. With the exception of the Williams sisters, and there have been more than other examples, but sport overwhelmingly dominated by some, you know, Russians, Czechs, Slovaks, Belarusians. Steffi Graf. German in this case. Yeah, of course, yeah. Yeah, but it's it has it has been sort of very much from that Eastern Europe is very strong for—

From that—I mean, even right now, let's go, you got Swiatek, you got Sabalenka, you got Rybakina. Yeah. Um Yeah. Then you've got Pegula and Gauff, two Americans. Yeah.

But yes, I mean, you know, But going back even to Navratilova and and Jana Novotna and Hana Mandlikova, and it's it's it's been a sport which has been at the female end very heavily dominated by what might be historically called Eastern Bloc female players, which is perhaps not entirely been the case on the male side.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽略的真相：球员其实在为"前辈的剩余红利"领工资
最反直觉的观察是：**Sinner 和 Sabalenka 拿到的天价奖金，本质上是费德勒、纳达尔、小威、博格、麦肯罗用三十年构建的"品牌信托基金"的分红**。视频中嘉宾点破了一个残酷事实——这一代顶尖球员还没达到前辈那种"凭一己之力撑起一个时代"的全球文化影响力。换句话说，他们正在收割上一代人种下的果实，却以"我们是产品核心"的名义要求更高比例。
这制造了一个有趣的悖论：**真正应该获得"涨薪权"的，恰恰是那些没法用涨薪来证明自己的人**——也就是排名 50-128 的球员，因为他们是赛事生态多样性的真正承载者。但替他们喊话的，却必须是 Sabalenka 这种"喊话效率最高、动机最可疑"的人。这就是为什么舆论会卡住。
## 二、跨领域连接：大满贯其实是"教会式机构"，被误读成了"NBA 球队"
最深刻的盲点在这里：**球员潜意识里把大满贯类比成 NBA 球队（老板赚钱→该分给球员），但大满贯的真实结构更接近大学捐赠基金或宗教组织**——温网 90% 的利润流向 LTA 做青少年推广。
这让我联想到一个奇特的跨领

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Will On-Chain Repo Reach $1 Trillion? One Reason Yes, One Reason No
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的"时间套利"才是 repo 真正的护城河

大多数人讨论 onchain repo 都聚焦在"杠杆"和"流动性"，但 Craig 的叙述里藏着一个更深的命题：repo 的本质不是借钱，而是把"时间"做成一种可交易的资产。

当资产处于 unbonding period、redemption queue、或某个解锁窗口时，资产本身是存在的，价值也是确定的，唯一缺失的就是"现在"这个维度。Repo 把"未来的确定性"贴现成"当下的流动性"，而不强迫你卖出头寸。这意味着 onchain repo 的真正对标物不是银行间拆借，而是——保险。它对冲的是"时间错配"这一独立风险因子。

意外的推论：一旦这个市场成熟，链上世界会出现一条新的"无风险曲线"，不是基于国债，而是基于质押退出周期的时长溢价。这条曲线甚至可能反过来定价以太坊本身的安全预算。

二、Aave 的死亡螺旋，本质上是"急诊室没有镇痛剂"

Craig 用 Aave 的清算瀑布作例子，但他没明说的是一个更尖锐的洞见：当前 DeFi 的清算机制是一种"一刀切的截肢手术"——市场只给你两个选项：要么交税（卖出），要么死（被清算）。

而传统金融在 2008 年之后建立了一整套"分级缓冲层"：discount window、repo、SLF、TAF……这些工具的存在让流动性危机和清偿危机能够被解耦。DeFi 至今没有这一层，所以每次黑天鹅都会把"暂时流动性紧张"误诊为"资不抵债"，然后用清算机制把它变成资不抵债。

跨领域连接：这与医院急诊分诊系统（triage）的演化史惊人地相似。19 世纪战地医院只会截肢，直到 morphine 普及后才出现"先稳定、再判断"的分级处置。Onchain repo 对 DeFi 来说，就是那一针 morphine——它的价值不在治病，而在为系统争取判断的时间。

三、阻挡万亿市场的不是技术，而是"语法"

Craig 给出的反方理由非常反直觉：onchain repo 达不到万亿规模的最大障碍，不是流动性、不是监管、不是智能合约风险，而是缺少一份"标准化的 GMRA 模板"。

这背后藏着一个被加密世界长期低估的事实：所有万亿级市场，本质上都是某种"共同语法"的胜利。 - ISDA 主协议造就了衍生品市场 - GMRA / MRA 造就了 repo 市场 - HTTP 造就了 web - TCP/IP 造就了互联网

而 DeFi 现在的状态像是前 ISDA 时代的衍生品市场——每家协议都在用"方言"做同一件事，Morpho、Aave、Fluid、Maple 各说各话。资本可以跨链流动，但法律语义和清算口径无法跨协议流动。

更出人意料的洞见是：真正能把 onchain repo 推到万亿的，可能不是某个 DeFi 协议，而是某个"无聊的"标准制定组织——一个加密世界的 ISDA。谁先把标准写出来并被三家头部协议采纳，谁就拿到了下一个十年最大的隐形税基。这甚至可能是一个比任何 L2 都更具垄断性的位置。

📝 转录文本

一、从 Aave 危机谈起：repo 如何缓冲去杠杆时刻

Craig，你之前提到的一点我想再追问一下——关于 repo 在去杠杆时刻、尤其是在市场承压期间能起到怎样的作用。我立刻想到的就是 Aave，以及它现在为重启那些市场所做的一切。你认为，一个成熟的 onchain repo 市场在那种情境下会如何帮助？

Craig： 是的，绝对会。这是一个很好的例子。比如说，当时之所以会出现围绕 death tokens 和 swaps 的整个二级市场——很多 Fluid 在做的事情——本质上都是建立在"交易出你的头寸"之上的。但有很多情况下，你并不想交易出一个头寸。你想保留同样的头寸，你只是现在需要流动性。

想保留头寸，只是需要"此刻的流动性"

而一个成熟的 repo 市场或 repo 设施，就能让你做到这一点。比如：

"好，我现在需要我的 ETH，我愿意把我资产负债表上的某个东西，或者某个 debt token，无论是什么，拿去 repo，以换取我在那边履约所需的 ETH，或者去补充抵押品，或者偿还这笔贷款以免被清算。"

当时这件事里有很多不同立场的人。这就是一个典型的例子——大家本来都对自己的头寸很满意，然后这个黑天鹅事件降临。如果当时大家有这种选择，绝大多数人都宁愿保留头寸，多付一点利息，让一切继续运转。

流动性挤兑的连锁反应

但你面对的是一个整体的流动性动态，结果引发了各种问题： - 人们被迫抛售 - 资产开始下跌 - 利率开始飙升 - 一些本来不会被清算的人被清算了 - 出现脱锚风险 - 对所有 LSTs、对执行层等等都形成压力

这些本来都不必发生。不是说 repo 能解决一切，但在那种情境下它本可以解决很多。

二、ETF、质押与 RWA：repo 的应用版图

短期流动性设施这件事，在 ETF 那边也有同样的需求。有很多兴趣来自管理大额 ETH、Solana 等可质押资产的 ETF 和大型国库的人——他们想把这些资产都质押起来，但他们需要某种 repo 设施，来确保即使资产处在 unbonding period，他们也能立即拿到流动性。

同样的逻辑也适用于 RWA 中的赎回队列（redemption queues）。某个东西在赎回队列里，要么在解除质押，要么在赎回，要么在等待某种局面落地——但你现在就需要流动性，你愿意为此付一点利息，而不是在二级市场上卖出头寸。

Morpho 视角下的去杠杆机制

我想说的最后一点是：因为 Morpho 的设计方式，除了 Aave 那种典型情况之外，Morpho 是我看到这种去杠杆概念最明显的地方。

vault curators 必须平衡他们的流动性状况，他们必须分配 LTV——流动性是他们脑中的一个巨大的考量因素，除了风险之外，他们能分配多少都取决于不同资产的流动性档案。所以： - 流动性提高 → 在 morpho 市场上可以给更高的 LTV 和更高的分配 - 流动性枯竭 + morpho 的浮动利率机制启动 → 人们的交易开始爆掉，面临清算风险 - 他们必须能去杠杆——要么把流动性拿出来，要么补充新的资本

那要怎么做？所以我们现在在自己的产品集中就有去杠杆设施——不仅是针对我们自己的 credit token，也针对其他资产，正是因为这种动态。

Repo 反过来提升市场资本效率

这件事会反过来提升整个市场的资本效率。因为有了 repo，vault curators 对自己所分配资产的流动性档案更有信心，他们就可以向系统注入更多的流动性。流动性不必因为难以应对的赎回窗口等问题而被困住。

三、五年内的万亿目标？

主持人： 明白。那么，传统 repo 市场是以万亿计的。Matteo，你觉得 onchain repo 在未来 5 到 10 年能做到多大？

Matteo： 在未来五年内，我相当有信心地说，它会达到万亿这个量级——至少一万亿。这是合理的，因为现在已经有一整条管线的传统金融公司和参与者在研究这类 onchain 应用。所以我甚至不会惊讶它在五年之内就达到。

正如我之前所说，这将会是一种混合（hybrid）模式。我们会看到越来越少的差异，越来越多的共同努力来改善这种状况。所以是的，我认为这在未来五年内是可达成的。

四、一个看多的理由，一个看空的理由

主持人： 好。Craig，对你来说——给我一个它会到达万亿的理由，和一个它不会的理由。

看多：必要性已经在显现

会到达的理由，就是我刚才提到的那些动态，以及一些已经在发生的事情。你已经看到很多平台开始推出这类设施，看到像我们这样的市场参与者参与其中。所以这就是 bull case：这是必要的，而且它已经开始发生了，这些设施正在被提供出来。

看空：标准化是巨大的挑战

不会到达的理由，我觉得是——repo 在所有情形下是否真的是 repo？还是说人们其实在用各种不同的方式实现这件事？

中央清算 repo 对银行而言的一个好处是：

它可以像钟表一样运行，你可以把它自动化到很高的程度，可以把它作为你国库管理策略的一部分；资本在流动，全部受同一份协议管辖，有标准化的条款。

要让我们这个行业达到那种程度——这是一个巨大无比的挑战。我知道有些人，比如我之前在 Membrane 工作的同事，他们一直在尝试推动大家采用标准化的 GMRA repo 表单，试图建立这样一个市场。

但要在当下建立这样一个标准化的市场，是一件极其困难的事。Onchain 也许恰恰是最佳的起点，但要让一整个生态站起来——基本上等于在建立一个新交易所，或者需要现有交易所中的某一家来推动这件事。这是一个巨大的进入壁垒。

五、节目预告

如果你一直喜欢 Bits + Bips，别忘了这档节目正在迁移到它自己在 X、YouTube 以及你最喜欢的播客播放器上的 feed。

如果你还没订阅 Bits + Bips 自己的频道，现在就去订阅，按下订阅按钮，这样你才能继续追上我们一周两次的直播以及宏观与加密的深度解读。

Bits + Bips 在 Unchained feed 上只会再出现几周。所以今天就订阅，为新节目的发布做好准备。所有链接都可以在 unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips 上找到。

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Bits + Bips] $5B On-Chain Credit: What's Actually Working and What's Still Missing
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个反直觉的观察：onchain credit 正在悄悄地"反向 DeFi 化"

表面上，大家以为 onchain credit 的演化方向是"越来越 DeFi"——更去信任、更可编程、更自治。但这场对话里隐藏着一个反向信号：真正在规模化的 onchain credit，正在主动重新引入 paperwork、permissioning 和法律层。Craig 自创的 "CFI/HiFi" 一词不是玩笑——它暴露了一个秘密：成熟的 onchain credit 在结构上越来越像 1980 年代发明 repo agreement 的华尔街，而不是 2020 年的 DeFi summer。5.13 亿到 5 亿增长的真正驱动力，不是智能合约更聪明了，而是律师们终于愿意把传统结构"包装上链"。 这意味着 onchain credit 的胜利方式，可能恰恰是放弃"纯 onchain"这个执念。

跨领域连接：repo 市场 ≈ 集装箱革命，而稳定币才是新的"标准箱"

Craig 提到的"stable coin repo"——也就是临时把 PYUSD 换成 USDE、USDT 换 USDC——表面看是个流动性小工具，但它的本质和 1956 年集装箱发明 是一回事。集装箱本身没有任何技术含量，它的革命性在于"统一接口"让全球货运的摩擦坍塌。多稳定币世界 + repo 机制 = 价值转移的"集装箱协议层"：每个稳定币是不同船公司发的箱子，repo 市场就是港口里那台让你五分钟内把货从一个箱子倒进另一个箱子的吊车。这也解释了一个被忽视的悖论——稳定币越多样化（看似碎片化），repo 市场的经济价值反而越大。所以未来 onchain 利率曲线的真正定价锚，可能不是国债，而是稳定币之间的临时互换溢价。

隐藏的系统性真相：流动性问题正在被"重新分层"，而非解决

行业普遍把 RWA 的流动性困境讲成"我们需要二级市场"。但 Matteo 描述的解决方案里藏着一个更深的结构性事实：onchain credit 正在做的不是"创造流动性"，而是把流动性按时间维度切片再重新打包。Morpho 上的 re-hypothecation、bridge facility、deleveraging facility——这些都不是新流动性，而是把同一份 collateral 的"时间权利"切成不同到期日卖给不同风险偏好的人。

这和电网的需求侧响应（demand response）市场惊人相似：电力本身不可储存，但通过把"什么时候用电的权利"金融化，整个系统的有效容量被放大了 3-5 倍。onchain credit 的下一个爆发点，可能不在于发行更多 RWA，而在于对"流动性时间权"本身建立衍生品市场——一个目前所有人都在做、却几乎没人用这个框架描述的事情。

一个被低估的预测：Lender of Last Resort 不会消失，但会"协议化"

Matteo 委婉地说"central authority 仍然需要"。但更激进的判断是：未来的 onchain lender of last resort 不会是央行，而会是某个 stablecoin issuer 的资产负债表——Circle、Tether、或者一个 Athena-like 实体。这听起来荒谬，但 1907 年 J.P. Morgan 个人救市、1913 年才有美联储——私人 last resort 先于公共 last resort，这是历史规律。我们正站在 onchain 版本的 1907 时刻。

📝 转录文本

当前 onchain credit 的格局

主持人： Let's turn to the onchain markets. So, uh, Matteo, um, this is your area of expertise, although I guess that would obviously apply to Craig as well. Um, but can you just kind of like level set for us? Um, what does online or what does onchain credit look like right now?

Matteo： Yeah, that's a really great question, and I think that we can have, you know, a lot of like parallels that we can draw with repo markets in general. The current state of private credit on chain is that we see real progress at the moment, but still I would say we're still early as a market structure itself.

You know, as of today we see that we have more than 5 billion AC distributed across more than 2,000 different onchain credit assets, and that's a meaningful growth. But it also points to a market where there's still infrastructure building phase and not the scaling phase.

借款方与承销端的成熟

What's actually working at the moment is that the borrower side has matured significantly. Institutional counterparties like Falcon X are accessing credit on chain with clear terms, defined collateral arrangements and legal structures that hold up to institutional scrutiny. That's a real change from, you know, the first generation of functioning credit, where the borrowers were often crypto native entities with thin credit history and limited accountability in general.

So I would say, regarding the current state of credit, also the underwriting layer is being professionalized. There's multiple professional underwriters now that, from a credit and reporting perspective, bring traditional credit underwriting incentives or discipline to onchain products, which is quite significant for the ecosystem in general.

可组合性开始显现真实价值

And apart from these — like the better infrastructure, the better underwriting methodology, more legal clarity for the final borrower and enforceability — even composability is starting to demonstrate real value. And that's exactly the parallel with the repo market, because the fact that you can use an onchain credit facility or even a RWA asset in broader terms as collateral in a different over-collateralized protocol, in a DeFi protocol — well, that does something that is beyond sitting in a bulletin yield. It's a meaningful proof of concept for what the next phase for launching credit looks like.

Yeah. So there's definitely improvements from what I see looking at even like two, three years ago on the onchain credit. There's still room for improvement. I would say there are still aspects that need to be improved from a market structure perspective, but that would be the current state, I would say.

不同口味的 onchain 私募信贷

主持人： Um, I'm looking at rwa.xyz right now. I mean, like you said, the number is about 5.13 billion. It looks like by far the biggest is Syrup USDC — that's Maple Finance's lending pool for stable coins. And, uh, I mean, Craig, I see Falcon X has one. Uh, I'm familiar with — is Acred considered here, one of the Hamilton Lane ones? Does that fall under this category too?

Craig： Should be.

主持人： Um, so I mean, how is this stuff being used today? Because this type of credit, I mean, this is different obviously than, um, like taking out margin on a derivatives type of trade and things like that. How is this being used today?

Craig： Um, and do you mean from our perspective as an issuer, or—

主持人： Both. I mean, if you could talk about specifically how people are using your credit vault, I'd love it. Um, you don't have to mention names, but just even more generally — how is onchain private credit being used today?

三类典型用例

Craig： Yeah, it's interesting, and I think there's a few different buckets of use cases. So, like, uh, you mentioned Syrup USDC, and that's why there's kind of these different flavors. And depending on the metric, you know, we have to make sure usually that we're talking kind of apples to apples, because there's a lot of flavor now of onchain private credit.

So there's some where it's essentially, you know, folks that are delegating or investing in some kind of a yield strategy, but with the idea that you're going to have a receipt token or something that's going to be generating yield, or you could stake it and generate yield. So that's like if you think about USDe with Ethena, or USDS with Spark, or Syrup USDC — you know, it's essentially someone's going to go run a yield strategy. But there's kind of these — it's quasi pegged to a stable coin and sometimes people will consider them stable coins. It's a separate debate. But it's kind of a receipt for your yield-sharing strategy.

Then you have more like traditional private credit strategies that have come on chain. Um, there's like JTRY, a lot of what the securitizing and Centrifuge folks have done. There's Apollo AC, you know, even to some extent Polo Finanara's onchain credit facility — like, kind of folks that are running these strategies in the traditional TradFi world and are actually finding a way to sort of bridge it on chain. For them it's a way to access additional AUM, and for the onchain community it's a way to actually invest in private credit through some kind of an onchain vehicle. Those tend to have stricter permissioning, but not always — there's interesting evolutions going on in that.

So there's kind of folks that — okay, I want to get into a traditional private credit strategy, or I want to get into a traditional treasury bond strategy or a CLO or something like that that I would normally do through a brokerage, but instead I'm going to be able to now access that on chain. And that tends to be a different profile.

Falcon X 的混合模式与意外的二级市场

And then with our facility and some of the others that have come out, it's a bit of a mix in a sense of both concepts actually, is where I'd look at it. You know, we don't have a daily liquidity concept. We don't necessarily have a stable coin or anything like that that's going to be involved. But what you can do is you can effectively participate in the yield generated through a portion of our loan book that's been tokenized.

And so, you know, this is an interesting merger because what we like about it is — you can have these facilities, and we do bilaterally with folks or through the traditional financial rail. Lien had a big ABS just through traditional rails. But there's actually a lot of capital, a lot of interest, and a lot of unique utility that comes in when you can access the onchain markets too.

And we've experienced that, frankly, in some cases by accident, where we've realized that, hey, folks go, "Well, maybe I can borrow against this token. Maybe I could do a repo type facility. Maybe someone will lend." That's outside of Falcon X — folks that may want to go do that. But it creates benefits for us too as you sort of have this secondary market that's evolving that you didn't have as much in traditional finance. You had a secondaries market, you could, but it would be hard to go and like borrow against your private credit. But the onchain markets in some ways are a lot more agile in that sense, for lending against these assets, even the traditional ones.

Repo 的法律特殊性如何上链

主持人： Okay. So I certainly see a lot of the overlap. I mean, the idea of people being able to put up assets, borrow against it. You can smart contract everything so that there's like sort of liquidation prices and ways to roll things over. You can even create like a digital escrow account to hold things, um, to release funds when certain conditions are met. A lot of overlap.

But I do want to talk about a couple of things that make the repo market in traditional finance very unique, and I'm curious how you think they apply to crypto. I mean, for one — repos, I mean, they stand for repurchase agreements. They have a very specific sort of legal — so we're looking for, like, they operate in a very unique legal space. Uh, I'm not sure why Craig's smiling, but I'm excited to hear. Um, and I guess maybe you know where I'm going, where like the owner — or I guess the lender — actually has rights to those assets and can sell them directly. You don't have to go through some sort of laborious bankruptcy process, like in case there's a default, which is unlikely because these are very short-term loans.

Um, but smart contracts — we all know they're called smart contracts, but they're not necessarily smart, and in the legal sense they are not contracts. So how does that apply here?

Repo vs. Master Lending Agreement

Matteo： Uh, Craig, go ahead.

Craig： Oh, yeah. I was just going to say — so this is one of the things you were alluding to. I would get asked questions all the time by people, especially in my former role at Membrane where we would end up talking to a bunch of different lending desks that are getting established. And if you ask anyone the question of "do I do it in a repo agreement when you're first starting the business, or do I do it in a master lending agreement?" — that is a big question.

The answer is, there's a few different reasons why you might want to do repo, but bankruptcy — avoiding a bankruptcy stay is actually one of the core components of that, as well as its classification on your balance sheet and how it's considered from a risk perspective.

But because of that, the reason I was smiling is, you have to document it in a very specific way as a repurchase agreement for it to truly be considered a repo and not just a loan. It's been interesting in my experience having seen repo agreements in the crypto space — and we can do either — but you find folks, you have to kind of play this translation that goes into it. You can translate a loan into a repo agreement. Like, we've seen term repos now; there's an argument that you have to really watch how that's papered up to not just be considered a term loan and truly be considered a repurchase agreement.

So it's been very interesting to kind of see a lot of that more than I had in the past in my career, and see how different financial institutions, especially folks coming in from traditional finance who tend to like repo a little bit more — they feel that it's safer — will come and try to adapt it to the crypto markets. And then there's other reasons: taxes, etc., why sometimes someone may want to do something as a loan instead, or regulatory status or something like that.

"CFI / HiFi" 与混合模型

Um, how to answer your question though about how you actually go and effectuate this on chain? I mean, I'm a CFI, I'm a HiFi person — in the sense of DeFi and CFI in a sense. And so—

主持人： I'm going to steal that term. I like that.

Craig： Yeah. Yeah. It's actually my old CEO Carson Cook used to — he coined it for me.

But I think that, you know, it doesn't all necessarily have to be done on a smart contract basis. I think you could probably have a pretty robust repo market that touches the onchain ecosystem that doesn't have to all be programmatic. Like, what you need is a product that's going to work and scale and make sense for the people that are going to be deploying liquidity into the ecosystem.

If that can all be done on chain and people can be comfortable, and then great — phenomenal. Like, then people will do it. But if you have some component that has to go offchain for some reason, or everyone has to sign a piece of paperwork and then it can all be run by smart contracts or something like that, like, that's fine too. And I think that is going to kind of be a nuance, because to have a lot of the larger players — they may touch on chain, us included — but a lot of times you want some kind of a paperwork piece that's involved, to some extent, and possibly to reap some of the benefits that come with why folks do repo generally.

So, it was a long-winded answer, but I just don't think we have to have a one-size-fits-all model as this space evolves.

我们需要"最后贷款人"吗？

主持人： Gotcha. Makes sense. And Matteo, just in light of everything that's how the repo market in TradFi has changed in the last few years — a natural question to ask is whether we need some sort of central bank backstop or some sort of big liquidity pool. Do you see the need for that? Or is there a way to handle this on chain without a "quote unquote" lender of last resort?

Matteo： Well, um, that's a really good point because, you know, liquidity is one of the most visible gaps at the moment, I would say. Tokenization improves transferability, but it doesn't automatically create an active market around it.

So I actually agree with Craig in the sense that there's a line between DeFi and CFI that is kind of fading over time, and we see more and more overlaps, hybrid models between DeFi and CFI, which just makes sense because we need to force adoption of smart contract application but it needs to make sense for the market itself.

To answer your question regarding having like a central authority that backstops the liquidity — I would say that it becomes less important in a market structure built on chain that allows participants to provide secondary liquidity and tap from a global pool of liquidity. So in a sense these expand also the audience that can participate into these kinds of markets. So it makes less important the participation of a central authority that provides backstop liquidity.

But I would say it doesn't completely eliminate the need of that. So you still need to have — it's good to have a central authority that allows to have backstop liquidity. And I think that if we look at from an onchain perspective with more composability and also global kind of infrastructure that can be available, each coordination effort from different central authorities to make sure that they can provide altogether at the same time a backstop liquidity.

So I would say it becomes less significant from an onchain perspective, but still it doesn't eliminate the need of it.

产品落地：稳定币 Repo 与 RWA 流动性

主持人： Okay. Um, I think at this point it would make sense to just have — maybe Craig, you could start — just explain the setup that you guys are rolling out with your version of sort of like onchain repo. And I have a few more questions after that, but yeah, please just sort of paint a picture for the audience.

为什么现在是 repo 的时机

Craig： Yeah, and maybe I can set the stage and give it to Matteo to kind of go into the productization. But I think one of the core reasons that this is really interesting at this time in the crypto ecosystem — because repo has been around forever, like this has been a dynamic that there's repo in TradFi, not necessarily as much in crypto.

But I think what's interesting is now you have — like, stable coin repo is something that I've been hearing a lot about. Which is, folks want to swap out their PYUSD for USDe, or — I mean, we run into this all the time. You know, my desk doesn't want to go in and burn something. We have something coming along the way, but we have to go settle USDT with this client right now. Wouldn't it be great if we could access a repo market and just quickly swap our USDC for USDT, and then, you know, we'll handle it afterwards.

Especially as all these new stable coin products are rolling out, there's a lot — and no one wants you to go and burn stable coins, and you may not want to. And so you kind of have an interesting dynamic actually where a robust repo market could be actually extremely valuable for those types of things, that type of activity. And it's a lot of times kind of the short-term stuff as well as the long-term stuff. So with the abundance of these new stable coins, I think that's one driving factor.

RWA 的流动性痛点

And then the other driving factor is the illiquidity, like the liquidity profile of a lot of these new RWAs. And this is something that we've gotten especially involved in — and again, Matteo will go into a bit here with what we're rolling out — is you have this gigantic problem right now where folks want daily liquidity, especially folks that are going in levered looping, or as part of just their overall treasury management strategy, they need the peace of mind that this is a temporary deposit.

Well, but the problem is — that's not really — and ourselves are included in this — that's a huge pain to go and offer a lot of times, and it might just not work in your fund strategy of how you're deploying. You may have quarterly liquidity and maybe it's gated, and you just will have a different profile on what you can offer.

Well, does that mean that those folks just can't get invested in those products anymore? Maybe not. Maybe you can create different bridge facilities, repo facilities, lending facilities as ways to create this liquidity, or even deleveraging facilities. There's a separate thing we could go into — for things like if the Morpho markets are blowing out, you may temporarily want to delever. You could do that in a repo.

And so that I think is another big driving factor for why these markets make sense, because a lot of people are trying to solve this illiquidity problem with these new RWAs. And I'll hand it to you on the product.

多种二级流动性机制

Matteo： Yeah. Yeah. Absolutely agree on the — there are definitely a ton of different projects and teams that are working on the secondary liquidity problem, in the sense that it's definitely tangible for an allocator perspective that with the tokenization of the credit facilities you need to have also this kind of instant available liquidity, I mean, in some forms.

Um, the composability again of these kinds of products helps us in some ways, in the sense that I'm seeing different flavors, different models applied to being able to provide this instant liquidity. You can have coordination mechanisms that allow to, you know, coordinate a callback with the borrower itself, which if you look at a traditional credit facility it would have taken days and days of structuring, legal reviews and everything. But from an onchain perspective instead, it's just a contract call, an onchain transaction that appears.

So that's just one methodology to handle this kind of instant liquidity. You can have repo markets on Morpho, for example, where you can post the position that you have — that the collateral that you posted on Morpho already, again. So you are rehypothecating that collateral into another market, which effectively gives you instant liquidity that can allow the leveraging for other operational purposes.

And in general, having market participants that provide — and you know, even a simple — allows to—

主持人： I'm sorry, Matteo. We're having a little trouble. Uh, I'm sorry, Matteo, I think we're having a little trouble hearing you. Um, why don't you try one more time?

Matteo： Can you hear me now?

主持人： It's a little better. It's a little better now.

Matteo： Yeah, sure, sure, sure. Okay, okay. No, yeah, I was saying that also having participants in the market that can provide directly secondary liquidity, and you know, bear the counterparty and duration risk — which, you know, in traditional finance there's many different firms that do this kind of role in the market.

Well, what I'm seeing in DeFi and onchain finance in general is that the combination of all these kinds of projects and teams working towards this kind of goal of providing instant liquidity for RWA assets is quite significant. And I think that in the next couple of years we're going to see quite of an improvement that would kind of make a no-brainer for an originator to just use an onchain structure instead of a traditional structure.

节目订阅提示

If you've been loving Bits and Bips, don't forget that the show is transitioning to its own feeds on X, YouTube, and your favorite podcast player. If you're not already subscribed to Bits and Bips on its own channels, go there now and hit that subscribe button so you can keep up with our twice-weekly live streams and macro-meets-crypto breakdowns.

Bits and Bips will only be on the Unchained feed for a few more weeks. So subscribe today to be ready for launch. You can get all the links at unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips.

观看原视频 →

## 一个反直觉的观察：onchain credit 正在悄悄地"反向 DeFi 化"
表面上，大家以为 onchain credit 的演化方向是"越来越 DeFi"——更去信任、更可编程、更自治。但这场对话里隐藏着一个反向信号：**真正在规模化的 onchain credit，正在主动重新引入 paperwork、permissioning 和法律层**。Craig 自创的 "CFI/HiFi" 一词不是玩笑——它暴露了一个秘密：成熟的 onchain credit 在结构上越来越像 1980 年代发明 repo agreement 的华尔街，而不是 2020 年的 DeFi summer。**5.13 亿到 5 亿增长的真正驱动力，不是智能合约更聪明了，而是律师们终于愿意把传统结构"包装上链"。** 这意味着 onchain credit 的胜利方式，可能恰恰是放弃"纯 onchain"这个执念。
## 跨领域连接：repo 市场 ≈ 集装箱革命，而稳定币才是新的"标准箱"
Craig 提到的"stable coin repo"——也就是临时把 PYUSD 换成 USDE、

item.title: [邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain] 比特幣沒有天花板！為什麼美股「創新高」才是好買點？Anthony Pompliano【邦妮區塊鏈】
item.dc:creator: 邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"僵尸链"现象揭示了加密世界的反达尔文悖论

Pompliano 抛出了一个表面看似常识、实则暗藏深意的观察：在传统经济中，失败的公司会破产、清算、把人才和资本释放回市场；而在加密世界，失败的项目却可以"不死"。它们以幽灵链的形态游荡，吸附着原本应该被市场再分配的智力与资金。

这其实是区块链最大的悖论：它原本被设计为"不可关闭"，但这种不可关闭性也意味着"无法死亡"。一个不能死的系统，反而阻碍了进化——因为达尔文式的资源重分配机制被冻结了。换句话说，抗审查性 = 抗自然选择性。这是一个被整个行业刻意回避的结构性缺陷。

二、"高点买入"原则与生物学中的"富者愈富"高度同构

他提到的"在历史新高那天买入，反而是一年中表现最好的买点"，这条违反直觉的统计事实，在生物学里叫做优先连接（preferential attachment）——也就是 Barabási 描述的无标度网络生长机制：连接越多的节点，越容易获得新连接。

同样的机制，出现在引用次数（Matthew Effect）、城市规模（Zipf 定律）、生态位中的物种丰度。所以"动量交易"在物理意义上根本不是一种"策略"，它是对一切复杂网络扩张方式的下注。这意味着 Bitcoin 不是在挑战股市规律，它是在重复宇宙中最古老的一种增长模式。

三、最让人意外的洞察："Bitcoin 没有顶 因为美元没有底"其实是一句关于"参照系坍塌"的宣言

这句听起来像段子的话，藏着一个物理学般的真相：当你用一个正在崩溃的尺子去测量一个固定长度的物体时，物体的"数值"会无限增长——但物体本身没动。Bitcoin 的"上涨"在很大程度上不是 Bitcoin 在变大，而是测量它的工具在溶解。

这把对话从金融拉到了认识论：我们以为我们在观察资产价格，实际上我们在观察"货币这个语言"的崩塌速度。这就解释了一个反直觉现象——为什么 Pompliano 把 AI 和 Bitcoin 放在同一个公司论点里：

  • AI 是进攻：在加速创造名义价值的速度
  • Bitcoin 是防守：在锚定真实购买力的尺度

它们看似毫不相干，但其实是同一枚硬币的两面——AI 让美元贬值得更快（生产力溢出 + 资本支出 + 印钞配套），Bitcoin 则负责接住贬值带来的能量外溢。这是一个隐藏的能量守恒：AI 创造的速度，必须由某种货币硬度来吸收，否则系统会通胀失控。 Pompliano 没明说，但他的整套商业模型已经默默押注了这一物理学式的对称结构。

四、彩蛋观察：他无意中描述了一个"代币化的祛魅过程"

他说，代币化最终的赢家不是代币本身，而是被代币化的资产（比如苹果股票）。这意味着加密行业经历了一个从神秘主义到管道工的祛魅过程——从"区块链将改变世界"到"区块链只是更好的清算管线"。

这与互联网史惊人相似：1999 年人们以为"门户网站"会统治世界，结果赢家是把互联网当作隐形管道的 Amazon、Netflix。真正改变世界的技术，最终都会变得无聊。 加密行业现在正进入它的"无聊期"——而无聊，恰恰是成熟的开始。

📝 转录文本

开场金句

Bitcoin's got no top cuz the dollar's got no bottom. Most of the crypto coins will never make an all-time high ever again. And uh the faster people realize that the better.

In crypto, we have this weird dynamic where things can be zombies. They can kind of basically become ghost chains or whatever. And so you can hold on to the dream for a long time, but it doesn't mean the dream is reality.

The US equity market is a great thing to study because it shows that strength gets more strength, returns, but get more returns. And so when you look at the stock market, when is the best day to buy in the stock market any given year? At an all-time high?

嘉宾介绍

I'm back with co-host Bonnie Chang at the Consensus Miami, one of the premier crypto conferences in the world. Joining me and Bonnie right now is Anthony Pompliano, host of the Pomp Podcast.

Host: Welcome to the show, Anthony. Anthony: Absolutely. Thanks for having me. Host: Good to see you. Thank you for joining, Bonnie and I.

一、关于"大多数山寨币再也回不到历史高点"

Host: All right, you've been tweeting a lot about crypto recently. "Most of the crypto industry is dead and never coming back. Eventually, people will realize it." Tell us more.

Anthony: Well, what else is there to say? I think a lot of people are confused. They think that uh most of the crypto coins will never make an all-time high ever again. And uh the faster that people realize that the better.

It doesn't mean that everything is going to go to zero. Just means that most of the long-tail assets didn't find product market fit. So they're basically zombies at this point.

Host: One of the comments to that tweet was pretty good. I was reading the thread and they said: "Hey, look, this is a fleshing out period. The good projects are here to stay and every cycle you flush out the scammers and the projects that have no future. Maybe this is good for the industry."

Anthony: Well, of course, it's good to get rid of all the things that don't work. But I don't think that that means that all of a sudden a bunch of things come back.

My general view is that:

  • Bitcoin is going to survive,
  • stablecoins will survive,
  • equity infrastructure will survive, and
  • tokenization will survive.

Those four themes are pretty much where most of the value, if not all the value, is going to occur moving forward.

The problem becomes that there's still an entire industry of people who are trying to make altcoins, you know, and other types of assets, something — but they're never coming back. They're dead. They're zombies.

If this was not in crypto, they would go bankrupt. They would file for bankruptcy. They would go out of business. That capital and that uh talent would get reallocated to other opportunities.

In crypto, we have this weird dynamic where things can be zombies. They can kind of become ghost chains or whatever. And so you could hold on to the dream for a long time, but it doesn't mean the dream is reality. And I think people just need to recognize that.

二、"Bitcoin 牛市正在发生"

Host: A few weeks ago, your video was titled "Bitcoin Bull Market Is Happening Now." Tell us more.

Anthony: Well, Bitcoin bottomed at 60K. Now we're back to 80,000. So it's up 30% off of the bottom.

And I think that what people don't usually give enough credit to is — the US equity market is a great thing to study, because it shows that strength gets more strength, returns beget more returns.

And so when you look at the stock market: when is the best day to buy in the stock market any given year? At an all-time high. If you buy at an all-time high day, you are likely to outperform buying any other day of the year.

Well, same thing with Bitcoin: if you buy Bitcoin when it has strength to it, it is starting to go higher — that momentum carries through.

And so when I tweeted that, we weren't at 80K. I think we were somewhere in the high 60s. Well, now we're even higher. And I think we'll continue to go higher through the end of the year.

Do we get back to an all-time high or not? I'm not sure. But what I do know is that when you have a big flush like you had in the Bitcoin price — you had the fear and greed index go to single digits, which I had never seen before — and now you have this kind of very persistent bid and this grind back up to 80K, it tells you that it is likely that we are going higher, not lower.

So I think that people just need to get comfortable with the fact that Bitcoin is a very different asset today than it was a decade ago.

三、关于量子风险与"人"的力量

Host: Are you worried about anything? Quantum risk, any risk that people talk about?

Anthony: Every single risk that you could outline, the single biggest mitigator is the humans that are involved in Bitcoin.

The humans are smart. We figure out how to do mining. We figure out how to do security. We figure out how to do transactions. We figure out how to do all these different things.

So every time I hear that there's some technology risk, I just think: I will bet on Bitcoiners over that technology any day of the week. Because Bitcoiners are very smart.

We were able to create and help thrive an asset that reached over $1.5 trillion valuation. There's no CEO. There's no board of directors, there's no management team, no marketing budget, etc.

And so if we can do that, Bitcoiners can do anything in the world. And I think that that is the ultimate mitigator for all these different risks — the Bitcoiners will figure it out.

四、除了 Bitcoin，还押注什么？

Host: Is there anything except Bitcoin that you're betting on?

Anthony: Well — stablecoins, equity infrastructure, and tokenization.

You know, people forget, but back in 2016, 2017, 2018 timeframe, I was very, very bullish on tokenizing all these assets. And it wasn't that I thought they competed with Bitcoin. I always thought Bitcoin was kind of this cryptonative or Bitcoin-native asset that was very unique — it was going to be this great store of value.

But as I've talked about for a long time: if you go back pre-1980s, everything was analog. You had a physical stock certificate, a physical bond, physical deed to your home, etc. And then in the 80s into the 90s, we electronified everything. So we went to electronic CUSIPs for all of those different assets.

Now we're just going to digitize them. They're going to become digital tokens. But as a user, I don't really care what the back-end plumbing is. All I know is that I'm buying Apple stock — I want Apple exposure.

And so that tokenization is going to be a big theme. It's just not going to be the way that people thought. It's not like some brand new coin that can't exist in the equity market — it's just simply allowing the plumbing to get better.

Host: I mean, tokenization is great, but where does the value go? Are we doing the infrastructure?

Anthony: Some of the equity infrastructure will accrue value, but mostly it's to Apple — or to whatever the asset is, right? You're not buying Apple stock in a tokenized form because it's tokenized. You're buying it because you want Apple stock.

And so ultimately, the same thing in the traditional markets is going to happen here: you're just going to have value accrue to wherever it is treated best.

五、关于美联储新成员 Kevin Warsh 的言论：AI 与 Bitcoin

Host: The soon-to-be new Fed chair Kevin Warsh said that AI and Bitcoin will be the next drivers of the American economy. This is coming from a would-be Fed chair. What do you make of these remarks? How important is it, or what is the significance of this coming from the Federal Reserve?

Anthony: Look, we got a public company called ProCap Financial. That's literally our entire thesis — AI and Bitcoin are going to end up being the two driving forces in finance.

And if you think about it:

  • AI is offense. It's going out, it's building products, it's creating services, it's trying to go and drive solutions to the problems that our customers have.
  • Bitcoin is defense. You then take that revenue and that profit, you put it on your balance sheet in the form of Bitcoin, to protect the economic value that you've worked so hard for.

And so I see this across the economy: if you go and you look at what is a huge part of the economy right now — it's AI. And the more that we build out data centers and power generation and all these different things, that is leading to job creation, that's leading to more investments, that's leading to all these benefits in the US economy.

六、收尾金句

Host: Okay. You know what I love about your channel is you talk about Bitcoin will always go up because governments will not stop printing.

Anthony: Yeah — Bitcoin's got no top cuz the dollar's got no bottom.

Host: I love that. And that could be — I know he has to go — that could be a great ending. Anthony: Sounds good, guys. Thanks so much for having me. Host: Thank you, Anthony. Take care.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、"僵尸链"现象揭示了加密世界的反达尔文悖论
Pompliano 抛出了一个表面看似常识、实则暗藏深意的观察：在传统经济中，失败的公司会破产、清算、把人才和资本释放回市场；而在加密世界，失败的项目却可以"不死"。它们以幽灵链的形态游荡，吸附着原本应该被市场再分配的智力与资金。
这其实是**区块链最大的悖论**：它原本被设计为"不可关闭"，但这种不可关闭性也意味着"无法死亡"。一个不能死的系统，反而阻碍了进化——因为达尔文式的资源重分配机制被冻结了。换句话说，**抗审查性 = 抗自然选择性**。这是一个被整个行业刻意回避的结构性缺陷。
## 二、"高点买入"原则与生物学中的"富者愈富"高度同构
他提到的"在历史新高那天买入，反而是一年中表现最好的买点"，这条违反直觉的统计事实，在生物学里叫做**优先连接（preferential attachment）**——也就是 Barabási 描述的无标度网络生长机制：连接越多的节点，越容易获得新连接。
同样的机制，出现在引用次数（Matthew Effect）、城市规模（Zipf 定律）、生态位中的物种丰度。所以"动量交易"在

item.title: [Business of Sport] What’s Going on at Real Madrid
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：皇马是"成功的囚徒"——胜利本身在制造他们的衰败

表面上人们会说：皇马的问题是球员太差、教练太弱、Perez 太老。但真相恰恰相反——皇马的问题是"赢得太多"。

注意一个被所有人忽略的悖论：当 Vinicius 没拿到金球奖时，俱乐部集体抵制颁奖典礼。这不是"输不起"，这是一种更深层的病态——他们已经把"应得"内化为身份本身。Rodri 拿了英超冠军 + 欧洲杯冠军，按任何客观标准都是合理赢家，但皇马的反应不是"我们下次再争"，而是"宇宙错了"。

这就形成了一个我称之为 "熵增式成功陷阱" 的结构：每一次胜利不是加固系统，而是侵蚀系统的反馈机制。因为当你 5 年赢 5 个欧冠，你失去了从"失败信号"中学习的能力——而组织学习理论告诉我们，没有失败信号的系统最终会因为信号失灵而崩溃，就像不会感到痛的人会被烫伤却不知道。

Mbappé 度假事件之所以是引爆点而非孤立事件，是因为它揭示了一个更可怕的事实：这家俱乐部已经训练出了一种"个人主义最大化"的玩家文化——而这恰恰是他们自己 20 年合同战略的副产品（详见第三点）。

二、跨领域连接：皇马 = 阿根廷经济 + 哈布斯堡王朝 + WeWork

这听起来荒唐，但请跟着我的思路：
