Like I think I think if we like what what you need is a product that's going to work and scale and make sense for the people that are going to be deploying liquidity into the ecosystem. If that can all be done on chain and people can be comfortable um and and then then great phenomenal like then then people will do it. But if you have some component that has to go offchain for some reason or everyone has to sign a piece of paperwork and then it can all be run by smart contracts or something like that like that's fine too and and I think that is going to kind of be a nuance like because there's like to have a lot of the larger players like they may touch on chain us included uh but a lot of times you want some kind of a paperwork piece that's involved uh to to some extent and possibly to reap some of the benefits that come with why folks do repo generally.

So, um, it it was a long-winded answer, but I I just like I don't think we have to like have a one-sizefits-all model as this space evolves.

链上是否需要"最后贷款人"？

Gotcha. Makes sense. And and Matteo, uh, just in light of everything that how the repo market, uh, in Trafy has changed in the last few years, uh, a natural question to ask is whether we need some sort of central bank backs stop or some sort of big liquidity pool. Uh, do you do you see the need for that? uh or is there a way to to handle this onchain without a quote unquote lender of last resort?

Well, um that's a really good point because you know liquidity is one of the most visible gaps at the moment I would say. So, you know, tokenization improves transferability, but you know, it doesn't automatically create an active market around it.

So I actually agree with Craig in the sense that there's a there's a line between DI and CFI that is kind of fading over time and we see more and more overlaps hybrid models between D5 and CFI which it just makes sense because we don't need to force adoption of smart contract application but it needs to make sh make sense for the market itself.

uh to answer your question regarding having like a central authority that backs stops the liquidity. I would say that it becomes less important in a market structure built on chain that allows participant to uh provide secondary liquidity and tap from a global pool of liquidity. So in a sense these expand also the audience that can be part that can participate into these kind of markets.

So it makes less important the participation of a central authority that provides back stop liquidity but I would say it doesn't completely eliminate the need of that. So you still need to have um you still like it's it's good to have a central authority that allows to have backup liquidity

and I I think that if we look at from an onchain perspective with a with a more composibility and also global kind of infrastructure that can be available it coordination uh effort from different central authorities to make sure that they provide altogether at the same time a back stop liquidity.

So I would say becomes less significant from an onchain perspective but still it doesn't eliminate the need of it.

稳定币 Repo 与 RWA 流动性问题

Okay. Um I think at this point it would make sense to just have you I maybe Craig you could start just explain um the the setup that that you guys are rolling out with your um version of sort of like onchain repo and I I have a few more questions after that but but yeah please just sort of paint a picture for for the audience.

Yeah and maybe I can and maybe I can set the stage and and give it to Matteo to kind of go into the the productization.

Um but the the I think one of the core reasons that that this is really interesting I think um at this time in um in the crypto ecosystem uh because repo's been around forever like this has been a a like there's always been this dynamic that there's repo and tradi not necessarily uh as much in in in uh in you know crypto but I think what's interesting is now you have uh like stable coin repo is something that I've been hearing hearing a lot about which is folks want to swap out their PYUSD for USD or um uh or I need I mean we run into this all the time.

You know, my desk doesn't want to go min and burn something. We have something coming along the way, but we have to go settle USDT with this client right now. Wouldn't it be great if we could, you know, access a repo market and just quickly swap our USDC for USDT and then, you know, we'll handle it afterwards. like especially as all these new stable coin products are are rolling out um there's a lot of uh and and no one wants you to go and burn stable coins and you may not want to and so like you you kind of have an interesting dynamic actually where uh a robust repo market could be actually extremely valuable for those types of things um that type of activity and it's all a lot of times kind of the short-term stuff as well as the long-term stuff so with the abundance of these new stable coins I think it's been um it's been uh that's that's one driving factor.

And then the other driving factor is the illi liquidity like the liquidity profile of a lot of these new RWAs. Um and this is something that we've gotten especially involved with it and again you know Matteo will go into a bit here with with what we're rolling out is you have this gigantic problem right now where um folks want daily liquidity uh especially folks that are going in levered looping or uh as part of just their overall treasury management strategy. They need the peace of mind that this is a temporary deposit. Well, but the problem is that's not really and ourselves are included in this. That's a huge pain um to go and offer a lot of times and it might just not work in your fund strategy of how you're deploying. You may have quarterly liquidity and maybe it's gated and uh um and uh you you just will have a different profile on what you can offer.

Well, does that mean that those folks just can't get invested in those products anymore? Maybe not. Maybe you can create um uh different bridge facilities, repo facilities, uh lending facilities as ways to create this liquidity or even deleveraging uh facilities. There's separate thing we could go into for things like if the morpho markets are blowing out, you may temporarily want to delever. You could do that in a repo.

Um and so like that I think is another big driving factor for why these markets uh make sense because a lot of people are trying to solve this illquidity problem um uh with with these new RWAs and I'll hand it to you on on the the product.

链上即时流动性的多种实现路径

Yeah. Yeah. Absolutely. absolutely agree on the like there there are definitely a ton of different like projects and teams that are working on the secondary liquidity problem in the sense that it's definitely tangible for an allocator perspective that with the tokenization of the credit facilities you need to have also uh this kind of like uh instant available liquidity I mean in some forms um the composability again of this kind of products uh helps us in uh in some ways in the sense that I'm seeing like different flavors different models applied to being able to provide these instant liquidity.

Uh you can have like coordination mechanism that allows to um you know coordinate a call back with the borrower itself which if you look at a traditional credit facility it would have taken like days and days of structuring legal reviews and everything but from an onchain perspective instead is just a contract call a onchain transaction that that appears.

So uh but that's just one me methodology to to handle this kind of instant liquidity. You can have uh repo markets on morpho for example where you can post the position that that you have that the collateral that you posted on morpho already again. So you are re repoticating that collateral into another market which effectively gives you like instant liquidity that can allow the leveraging operational purposes you know and um in general like having market participants that provide and you know even from a simple allows to

I'm I'm sorry, mate. We're having a little trouble. Uh uh I'm sorry, M. I think we're having a little trouble hearing you. Um why don't you try one more time? Can you can you hear me now?

It's a little better. It's a little better now.

Yeah, sure. Sure, sure, sure.

Okay. Okay. No. Yeah, I was saying that also having like participants in the market that can provide directly secondary liquidity and uh you know beer the counterparty and duration risk which you know in traditional finance there's many different firms that do this kind of role in the market.

uh well what I'm seeing in DeFi and onchain finance in general is that the combination of all these kind of projects and teams working towards this kind of goal of providing instant liquidity for RWA assets is is quite significant and I think that in the next couple of years we're going to see uh quite of an improvement that would kind of make uh you know quite of a no-brainer for a for an an originator to just use an onchain structure instead of a of a traditional structure.

Repo 如何缓解去杠杆压力

Okay, great. Um, we're almost out of time. I have a couple of quick follow-ups or or things to finish up with. Um, Craig, one one thing you you mentioned, I wanted to ask you about uh sort of how Repo could help with like deleveraging moments, especially during periods of market stress. Uh, immediately made me think of a and everything that's happening there, trying to restart those markets. Um, how do you think like a mature onchain repo market would help in a situation like that?

Yeah. No, a absolutely. I I mean it's a great example like for instance you you because you had this sort of whole secondary market that started going around with with the debt tokens and the swaps a lot of like what fluid was doing. Um but that was fundamentally built on trading out of your position.

Uh there's a lot of situations where you don't want to trade out of a position. Um you want to keep the same position. You just need the liquidity right now. Um, and so that a a mature repo marketer or a mature repo facility would have let you do that. Okay, I've got uh I need my ETH right now and I'm willing to go and post, you know, whatever it may be either off my balance sheet or that's even in in a some other debt token, whatever it may be. I'm willing to go and repo that to get to get the ETH that I need for my for my obligation over here or to post this additional collateral or or to pay this loan back so I don't get liquidated or or whatever it may be.

There were a lot of you know a lot of folks on different sides that like it's that that's a prime example. Everyone was happy with their positions. Then this black swan situation comes about and um folks may like if folks had the option, everyone probably would have loved to have kept their positions and maybe paid a little bit of interest for it or something but just kept things going. But you have a general liquidity dynamic which then causes all sorts of problems uh where people are selling. Now you have assets that are selling off. Now you have interest rates that are spiking. Now you have people that are getting liquidated that otherwise would have never been liquidated. Like you you have this whole kind of cascade. you have a deep pegging risk that's that's going on and now you have pressure on all the LSTs and and the execute and things like that like none of that had to happen um uh like not that repo solves everything but it would have solved a whole lot in that situation.

and you run into the same thing with like these short-term liquidity facilities like with the ETFs there's a lot of interest um with ETFs with folks that are managing large treasuries of ETH Salana stakeable assets um that would love to have all of at stake, but they need some kind of repo facility essentially that's going to go and guarantee them that they'll be able to have the liquid right away, even if something's in an unbonding period. And and you could take the same thing in the redemption cues in in RWI. It's the same thing. It's something's in a redemption queue somewhere. It's either being unstaked or it's being redeemed or you're waiting for the A situation to play out. Um, but you need the liquidity now and you're just going to pay a little bit of interest for it as opposed to selling out of your position on on a secondary market.

Um, and um, so I I think it I think the last thing I'd say is this this because of how Morpho is designed, it's where I've seen this kind of deleveraging concept outside of obviously like the A situation probably the most because you have situations where um, you may have vault curators that are going and um, you know, they have to balance their liquidity situation and they have to assign LTVs that like like liquidity is a massive thing in the back of their minds in addition to the riskiness. of these various vaults and how much they can allocate is all dependent on the liquidity profile of different assets and so they can give a higher LTV and they can do a higher allocation in the morpho markets when the liquidity profile increases.

and so um because what'll happen is the liquidity situation dries up the um and the morpho markets are doing the ticking interest rate situation that they do and now people's trades are blowing up and people are at risk of liquidation and what they and they need to be able to delever that situation. They need to be able to get their their liquidity either out or get additional capital in.

And so and so how would you do that? And so like we um we currently have like within our product set off for deleveraging facilities for folks not just necessarily against uh our credit token but also for other uh other assets because of this dynamic. Um, and what that'll do is it'll actually increase the capital efficiency of the market overall because now the Morpho because the vault curators feel confident um in what they're allocating to in the liquidity profile thanks to repo, they can now actually extend more liquidity into the system. It doesn't have to stay trapped because of the um of the difficult redemption windows and so on and so on.

五到十年的市场规模预测

Gotcha. So I mean the traditional repo market measured in the trillions. Uh Matteo, how big do you think onchain repo could get in the next say five to 10 years?

Well, in the next uh five years, I would say um I'm pretty confident to say that it would reach the uh trillion figure at least one trillion figure. It's something that it just makes sense with the current pipeline of you know firms and traditional players that are looking into on these kind of onchain applications. So, uh, I wouldn't be surprised to even see it like before the next five years.

Um, and that that's going to be as as I mentioned before, it's going to be kind of like an an hybrid approach. We're going to see more and more, you know, kind of like less differences and D5, but more a united approach to work together on improving this kind of situation. And um and so yeah, I I would say it's something reachable within the the next five years.

Okay. And uh Craig, for you, give me one reason why it will get there and one reason why it will not.

I think it it will get there because of those dynamics that that I mentioned and things that are already happening. You're you're already seeing a lot of these platforms go and release these types of facilities. You're seeing people like us and and other market participants be involved. Um and so you that that's the bullc case is this is necessary and it's already kind of begun to happen and these facilities are being are being offered.

Why it won't happen um I think is because is that necessarily actually I guess repo in all cases um or are people going and implementing this in a variety of sort of different ways? I think one of the one of the benefits of centrally cleared repo for the banks is that it's like something you can do like clockwise and you can automate it to an extent and you can use it as part of your treasury management strategy and capital's flowing and it's all governed under the same agreement and there's standardized terms um and um and and that's to get our industry on that and I'm not sure like that is a gargantuan challenge.

I know that there's folks actually my uh alma mo at membrane uh they they're I know they've worked on this to try to get folks on a standardized gymra repo form uh to try to kind of create this but to create this sort of like standardized market in general right now is a extremely difficult thing to do onchain is probably one of the best places to start frankly um but that's standing up and getting an entire ecos basically starting a new exchange or you would have one of the exchanges that would that would need to do it um That's a massive barrier to entry.

结束语

Yeah. And well, I guess we'll have to have you guys back on in 5 to 10 years and uh and we'll we'll see we we'll see what plays out. Um but thank you both for for joining. Um we're going to have to end it here, but uh everyone um thank you for watching and listening. Stay where you are.

This is the this is it for bits and bits the interview this week but in a moment Lara is coming back to do an unchained interview with Alex Wesley of Artemis who's been making the boldest call um we've seen in a while for repricing Coinbase Circle and Kraken is AI native finance infrastructure. You'll want to stick around. Come on. Come on.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被忽视的"翻译损耗"：Repo 在链上的真正难题不是技术，而是法律语义
大多数人讨论链上 Repo 时，本能地把它当作"DeFi 借贷 + 标准化合约"问题。但 Craig 在节目里那个意味深长的微笑揭示了一个被严重低估的事实：**Repo 之所以是 Repo，而不是贷款，本质上是一种"法律语义的特权"**——它能在破产法庭中绕过 automatic stay（自动冻结），让债权人直接处置抵押品。这不是金融工程，这是法律工程。
这意味着链上 Repo 真正的"基础设施"，不是 Morpho 这样的 vault，而是**判例法和文件模板**。一个被 GMRA（Global Master Repurchase Agreement）正确包装的链上交易，与一个被叫做"repo"但实际写法是 loan 的链上交易，在系统性风险层面是两种完全不同的物种。换言之，**链上 Repo 的瓶颈不在 Solidity，而在 Word 文档**。这是一个让加密原住民非常不舒服的真相。
## 二、出人意料的观察：2019 年 Repo 危机和 DeFi 清算瀑布是"同一种病"
节目里 Mat

item.title: [SemiAnalysis Weekly] Ep. 012 - Cerebras IPO (AI Cloud TCO)
item.dc:creator: SemiAnalysis Weekly
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、晶圆的拓扑结构，正在变成公司的组织结构

最容易被忽略的一点：Cerebras 这块晶圆和 Cerebras 这家公司，是同构的。

过去四十年，半导体行业做的事情叫"拆解"——把芯片拆成 chiplet、把内存拆成 HBM、把连接拆成 interposer 和 NVLink、把机器拆成机架。Cerebras 对这条主线说"不"，它把一切重新焊回一整片硅上：84 个 reticle 不出晶圆、不过网络、数据永远留在 die 内。

而现在请看这家公司在做什么：自己设计硅、自己在加州工厂做专有组装、自己钻孔、自己建数据中心、自己开 API endpoint、自己当 NeoCloud。它把行业花了四十年拆开的产业链，又重新捆回了一家公司里。 产品的架构哲学和组织的架构哲学，是一模一样的"反拆解"。这是 Conway 定律的镜像版本——通常是组织结构决定产品结构，而 Cerebras 是产品的物理信仰倒灌进了商业模式。这也解释了它真正的风险：晶圆的好处是"一个东西全包了"，公司的风险也是"一个东西全包了"——硅、系统、供电、散热、编译器、数据中心运维、API 定价，每一层都是一道独立的 scaling 难题，而它没有一层可以外包出去。

二、被困在 5nm，恰恰是它没说出口的护城河

转录里有个一闪而过、但极其反直觉的点：SRAM 微缩已经死了，而晶圆一半面积是 SRAM,所以 Cerebras 的下一代 CS4 干脆继续用 5nm 的 WSE3。表面看这是"技术停滞"。

但换个系统视角：这恰恰让它躲开了 3nm 的产能血战。 NVIDIA、Apple、所有人都在抢 3nm,而 Cerebras 安安静静地待在一个没人争的节点上,从几百片晶圆扩到几千片都不会撞供应链的墙。这是生物学里典型的"次级生态位"策略——一个物种打不过主战场,就去占一个别人看不上的niche,反而活得最稳。技术上的劣势,被供应链动力学翻译成了竞争上的优势。

更妙的是那个"啊哈"细节:这家攻克了 reticle stitching(半导体最难的工艺之一)的公司,真正的扩产瓶颈居然是——在晶圆上钻螺丝孔。因为晶圆和 PCB 热胀冷缩速率不同,必须用大量螺丝孔来均匀施压,而在一片有源晶圆中间钻孔又不能钻裂它,所以这是个慢工。这是 Goldratt 约束理论最锋利的一刀:当你解决了系统里最华丽的难题,约束不会消失,它只会迁移到最不起眼的环节去。 一家硅工艺天才公司,被一个机械钻孔工序卡住了脖子。

三、Cerebras 卖的根本不是速度,是一份"看涨期权"

普遍的叙事是 Cerebras 卖"快"。但看 OpenAI 那笔交易的结构:750MW 分三批 250MW 交付,token-as-a-service 计价,而且 OpenAI 握有把它加到 2GW 的选择权。

这不是一笔采购合同,这是一份期权合约。Cerebras 真正的产品,是卖给前沿实验室的一份"保险"——保的是"万一 fast token 浪潮起来了,我没掉队"。播客里那句话点破了:"如果你的生意是给前沿实验室做'别错过快 token'的下行保护,这是个很有意思的位置。"用金融的语言说,Cerebras 是前沿实验室买的一个 put option,对冲的是"agentic 范式突变"这个尾部风险。

而这又牵出一个跨领域的判断:fast token 本质上是一种"位置性商品"(positional good),不是效用商品。 数据摆在那里——Opus 4.6 fast 比标准模式只快了不到 2 倍(66 vs 40 tok/s),价格却是 6 倍;Jensen 的 slide 还在画"快 100 倍、贵 1000 倍"的超线性曲线。纯从效用看这根本不成立,"世界的钱会被花光"。但赛马的价值从来不是它的绝对速度,而是它比别的马快。fast token 的定价权不来自速度本身,而来自它在"谁迭代得更快"这场军备竞赛里制造的相对位置。一旦"不用 fast token 的机会成本"变得足够大,需求会非线性爆炸;在那之前,它就是个昂贵的凡勃伦商品。

四、它最大的长处和最大的短板,是同一个物理事实

最后一个收束性的洞察:Cerebras 的"数据留在 die 内"——是它能跑出 1000+ tok/s 的原因,也是它跑不了高并发大模型的原因。1.2 Tbps 的 I/O 带宽是 2018 年 CS1 就冻结的设计决定,7 年后这个决定反过来约束着它的推理架构。同一片晶圆,在 roofline 图最左端(低算术强度、decode batch-1)无人能敌,在右端(256 并发、百万 token 上下文的 DeepSeek v4,要 24 套系统、2400 万美元 capex)却毫无性价比。

这是系统论里一条很硬的真理:一个系统的"定义性优势"和它的"定义性约束",几乎永远是同一个属性的两面。 Cerebras 不是"又快又有短板",它是"因为这样所以快、也因为这样所以有短板"。理解了这一点,就能理解它为什么注定是个"中小模型 + 中小上下文"的数十亿美元生意,而不是"让全世界最大模型都飞起来"的故事——后者那个叙事,是讲给 IPO 听的。还有个时间维度的彩蛋:这家公司 2018 年是为 HPC、CFD、分子动力学造的东西,是个典型的"找不到问题的解决方案"。它真正做对的事,是活得够久,久到世界自己长出了那个问题(agentic coding)。深科技 moonshot 的本质,往往不是预测未来,而是一场"时间套利"——赌的是自己能不能熬到市场追上自己。

📝 转录文本

Ep. 012 — Cerebras IPO (AI Cloud TCO)

频道：SemiAnalysis Weekly | 链接：https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ST4OtItJyYs

Intro

Hello everyone. Welcome back to Semi Analysis Weekly. We're here with episode 12. I got Myron and Howie with me, and we're going to talk about Cerebris on the eve of the IPO.

These guys have a need for speed, fast tokens. So we're going to cover what that means: what's the street talking about on the lead up to the IPO, why Cerebris is important. We're also going to dig into what the chip actually is, what this wafer scale engine actually is, the system and BOM and some of the content inside, when it wins and when it doesn't in terms of the workloads. And yeah, I'll talk a little bit about some of the deals they've signed with OpenAI and Amazon and stuff like that.

But guys, welcome to the show. Big article today.

Great to be here after a long night trying to push this article. But yeah, I'm excited it's out. It's a big one.

It's a pretty cool one as always. And there's some real interesting things happening to Cerebris.

Need for Speed: Why Fast Tokens, Why Now

Basically, like you said, there's a need for speed. Fast tokens are a thing now. Before we kind of just dismissed it as it's cool, but not necessary. But now we're seeing that people are really willing to pay more for speed. And that's what Cerebrus is really good at.

Yeah, people are willing to pay more for speed just with tuning for interactivity on existing HBM-based accelerators like GPUs and GPUs, where the trade is like two to two and a half times faster for six X the price. But Cerebris kind of extends that to like potentially 10 X faster, maybe more depending on the model and depending on the profitability you want to run the endpoint at, and potentially exponentially more.

I mean, we don't know exactly what the profitability of the 4 5.3 codec spark endpoint with OpenAI is, as the biggest public example of people using Cerebris for inference. But if you say conceptually, what are the SRA machines promising? It's generally 10 times faster at some exponential on top of 10 times the price, maybe 20 times the price or up to like 50 times the price per token. So it'll be interesting to see them play with that part of the Pareto curve as this gets rolled out.

Yeah. So I mean, we've played around a bit with the endpoint, right? And then we got, I think, on OSS 120B, you know, like 1100 tokens a second. And if you look at our inference X benchmarking for all the NVIDIA and AMD GPUs, they top out interactivity wise at 500. It's literally off the charts performance, right? GPUs can't do this.

Yeah, at any price. But Howie made the point earlier that there's some upper bound and some lower bound at which people are willing to pay for stuff, and then it just doesn't become worth it anymore — or willing to save money, and then it doesn't become worth it anymore.

Yes, exactly. So one of the points that CEO Andrew Feldman was making is that in the future, everyone would want fast tokens. And he put an analogy to like the internet, right? Where if you gave me — I mean, I'm based in Singapore — so if you gave me even 100 meg internet, you would have to pay me and I still wouldn't take it, right? Like I'll take gigabit and above, right? So his analogy was that eventually people won't even bother paying for slow tokens. And so everyone will move up the stack in terms of the tokens per second.

But of course, there's definitely a bound to that. You know, you will never run video generation on Cerebra, so there'll still be plenty of market for that. But at least, you know, he had a solution looking for a problem and now the problem has come. And we'll see how far that goes.

The Marquee Customers: OpenAI & Amazon

Yeah. I mean, there's so much demand. It's making everybody look good right now. The Wafer: Reticle Stitching & Yield Recovery

Yeah. So maybe we could dig into the chip now. Like, we love the wafer. For the general audience here, what's it mean to have a wafer scale chip, and maybe how is it different than GROK? Like, they're both SRAM machines, but can one of you guys break down — what is the wafer? Why do we love the wafer so much?

Yeah, Myron, could you share a screen of the article and then you can pull some images?

Yeah, yeah, I'll throw it up. Yeah, so Cerebra's special sauce is truly in the radical stitching. So as you know, your ASML lithography machine can only pattern a certain size of area, which is 26 by 33 millimeters. So that's what's known as a radical limit. So as you can see here, the Cerebras wafer, what they've done is they've patterned the wafer with 84 of these patterns, right, these dye radicals.

And their special sauce is they're able to stitch them together with a special method that is done in collaboration with TSMC, where the upper metal wiring layers — they are connected in such a way that not only are they horizontally and vertically connected to each other, but they also have ways to route around defects, right, which helps them to yield the wafer. Otherwise, you know, if they use a traditional method, one catastrophic defect can basically — you have to throw away the whole wafer.

So these two key innovations, right — the radical stitching and the yield recovery — are what enables them to have such a huge amount of SRAM, huge amount of compute, on a single wafer, on a single chip.

And then the fundamental benefit of this is, of course, as you know, in AI, we are extremely data movement bottlenecked. And so the most efficient way to transfer data is to just stay within the chip, right? Even your HBM or like your chiplet solutions over the Coors Interposer, even that is still nowhere near as efficient as just staying within the die. So if you can have a huge die, then you can have a huge amount of compute without needing to transfer a lot of data in and out.

So the analogy they like to give is that one wafer almost represents like a whole NVL72 rack, right? It's like having 84 chips in the same network and you don't have to move off the wafer. So huge savings in terms of communication power. And also you get a lot of benefits when you're able to parallelize a model across an entire wafer. So yeah, a lot of techniques there as well, which some of our guys have more experience with in terms of how the models can perform so well on a wafer.

Tiny Cores & Defect Routing

Yeah. So this next image goes through just what it looks like, right? So one of the ways that they achieve this high yield, or like not catastrophically bad, is to use a very small core.

So if you imagine your core, if it's like six square millimeters, which is like the size of a NVIDIA SM — if that is defective, if there's one defect on that, I have to disable the whole SM. Whereas if I have a really tiny core, which in this case is, you know, around 200 by 200 microns in width and height, then if there's a defect there, I just have to disable a very, very, very small part of the dye.

With this, they have this grid, right? So you have 10,700 cores. In fact, there's more than that. One of the presentations Cerebrus gave was that there's around 970,000 cores on the wafer and they enable only 900,000, right? So that's the number that they market — but they have over-provision, they have extra cost on the die, on the wafer, so that they can get the yield and ship enough wafers.

So this routing mechanism, right, in order to bypass the defective cost is also another reason why they can achieve the good yield. Because for the software, right — if you imagine I have a defect in the core, in the wafer, my software, I don't need to, or I don't want to have to make my software aware of this defect, right? Like the software should just want to see a continuous two-dimensional Cartesian grid.

So this innovative routing system allows them to bypass a defective call, right, while still showing to the software that it is still a grid. So that means, for example, if I have a defect, I will shift the entire routing grid offset by one call so that — if you actually draw the grid out topologically — it still looks like a continuous 2D mesh without any missing gaps. Right. So that's one of the innovations there.

Building the System: Power, Cooling & Mounting

Yeah. So that's just the wafer. And then the next part, if we scroll down to the hardware and how they are able to build this right into a machine.

So, you know, the traditional packaging, it's a dye on a substrate, like your ABF, Ajinomoto substrate, and then that gets attached to I.O. Whereas for the wafer scale system, you have a whole other problem with regards to temperature expansion and power delivery, because how are you going to get the power in and out when the whole thing is going to generate a huge amount of power, talking 25 kilowatts and even more in the next generations.

So here we see the current implementation, which actually is quite old — it's from 2018, when they were very first designed and they've kept it since then, but this will change in the future design. But you can see here the WSE3 engine block on the top right, the wafer is actually placed vertically, right? And one side faces the front, one side faces the back, with power coming in from one side, right, and then cooling coming from the other side.

Yeah, power's the purple there on the left side, right?

Yeah. The PSUs, yeah. This is in fact the first true example of vertical power delivery, right? Because the power is coming in perpendicular to the silicon, which is necessary for this kind of wafer scale design. So there's also some of the things that they've had to innovate, right, by necessity to achieve this practical implementation of a wafer scale design.

And then in order to mount the wafer onto the PCB for power delivery, because the wafer will expand and contract with temperature at a different rate to the PCB and the surrounding system, they needed to implement this custom flexible connector, right? It's almost like an interposer, but it has pins that can move around so that when the wafer heats up and cools down, the connections are maintained. So this is another proprietary innovation that they have to come up with, right?

And cooling — you have a really complex cold plate that distributes the water onto each of the 84 dyes and then does the direct impingement downwards. So you end up with a pretty interesting design that's not seen anywhere else. And that's how they're able to achieve this.

Networking & I/O Limits

So then the last one, which I will talk about, is networking and how much I.O. goes out of the wafer.

So as you know, for the wafer scale design, you can see the wafer here, you only have the perimeter to have I.O. out of the wafer. So in this case, there's a north side and a south side with special FPGAs in order to convert the wafer signal into something that the outside world can understand, which in this case is Ethernet over optical modules.

In this particular instance, Cerebras has gone with 1.2 terabits per second worth of bandwidth of the wafer, which was also a design decision from 2018 in the original CS1. And we'll talk about the implications of that in the inference section later.

If you look at the bandwidth to compute ratio, it seems to be far, far lower than our current modern accelerators. So why can't they add more bandwidth — or I guess this is just a kind of natural limitation of doing this radical stitched wafer scale implementation, right?

Yeah, so firstly for wafer patterning, right, you can't really pattern different reticles or different masks onto the same wafer, meaning that I can't have half of my wafer be compute reticles and half of my wafer be I.O. reticles, right? Like it all has to be uniform based on the manufacturing flow as well as some of the chemical deposition and intensity, things like that. So this means that if you wanted to have a lot of I.O., that means all 84 dice must have provisioned some of the area for I.O. But then only the perimeter ones will actually get used. So that's not a very efficient use of the silicon area, right? So that's one thing.

And then the other thing that we should explain in the article was to do with the routing, right? Your 2D routing, where if you placed I.O. in the middle, as with the example we mentioned, then you have to route around these I.O. elements which would introduce additional complexity.

Yeah. So in the case of Cerebras, they don't really want to mess with the design, which they've, you know, perfected with the CS1. So, but we'll see if they make any changes in the future, right? Regarding some of the things they were talking about regarding optical wafers and DRAM wafers, which is quite aspirational, but it seems like the next logical roadmap.

How Do They Scale From Here?

Yeah. Before we move on, one thing I would like to address is — some people have been asking, okay, so now they've made a chip the size of a wafer. How do they scale from here? Right? Like how do they improve performance? You can't increase the area anymore. Like your TPUs and your NVIDIA GPUs, you're getting bigger and bigger, but they see Cerebrus is already at the limit, right?

So one way is to go vertical, which is what we've shown here with IO. And then another way is, of course, to go to panel level systems where you can essentially have four of these side by side, which is another kind of moonshot aspirational thing, but it is one of the possible avenues of scaling.

So what about just — sorry — what about just like traditional Mosul scaling? Right, like, although it has slowed down?

But I think one of the big things is that SRAM scaling is dead. And then a lot of the area on a wave scale engine is SRAM. So it's 50 percent. So that means that you're just going to the next node, say from N5 to N3, a lot of — they just lose a lot of the benefits and it's pretty hard for them to increase on chip memory capacity because of this.

Yeah, that's true. So in the investor roadshow, they did disclose that the next generation system, CS4, will use the exact same WSE3 wafer, which is on 5 nanometer. So clearly they agree as well, right? If not, they would also jump to the 3 nanometer bandwagon.

But they did mention other ways of increasing the amount of memory, which is to do with the area efficiency. So if I can get my compute with a new node, if I can get my compute area down, I have more room to place my SRAM. So there's one way to increase SRAM by maybe like 20, 30%. And then on top of that, you have these scaling factors such as the vertical SRAM, like AMD's 3D cache, or even a future 3D DRAM, right, which is like 10 to 15 times the bit density per area of SRAM. So these are some of the more difficult ways, but still ways to achieve this scaling, right?

So, but on the other hand, this is kind of a good thing, because by not moving to 3nm, Ceribus is kind of like — they're not going to face the shortages, right, on the wafer, which our accelerator model has already shown, right? So it's almost like a blessing in disguise where they're able to grow and to scale from hundreds to thousands of wafers without facing kind of bottlenecks on the supply chain, right? The other one being, of course, this has no HPM and no DRAM. So it is quite easy to scale, right?

The Token: Roofline & Arithmetic Intensity

Haas is doing okay. Yeah, this year. Yeah. Sorry. I'm going back too far to Mazepan. Anyway. Urikali — not Ukrainian, Russian. Okay.

Let's talk about fast tokens and what it means. So maybe first where the wafer wins and then second where it has some problems.

So the key thing that we tried to point out here is really the structure of the chip itself. And tried to introduce the concept of a roofline plot for people who aren't familiar. It's basically showing the ratio of compute performance measured in teraflops to arithmetic intensity of a given kernel, which — you know, it moves up linearly with the flops until it hits some limit in terms of how much memory bandwidth you have in the system.

And what's arithmetic intensity? Can you remind us?

Arithmetic intensity of a kernel — a kernel being a piece of software that runs on the device side, so runs on the GPU or runs on the TPU or in this case on the wafer — is, yeah, it's a way of describing how many flops versus how many bytes are being moved in the kernel. So how much bandwidth you need versus how much compute you need.

And these ratios are pretty well understood by performance engineers all the way back for HPC workloads, as we were just talking about, but obviously in AI workloads too. Because that like for loop that you're running on the device side has some — like the canonical example is matrix multiplication, a gem, it's got some shape. And if it's a nice big square, you know, the input matrices are like nice and big in terms of their dimensions, then you have very high arithmetic intensity because you always have to do a lot of flops on every byte that you move, right?

But a lot of machine learning is not this way. And it's not always even matrix math. A lot of it involves vectors. So matrix with a dimension of one. And what that means is that relative to the amount of data you move, you don't actually need to do that many flops per amount, per byte that you move onto the, into the registers on the chip.

And so if we add Cerebrus and Grox and really anybody else that is building an SRA machine to these plots, what you'll see for the viewers is that on the very far left side of the plot at very low arithmetic intensity, that optimal ratio of the workload shape that should run on the GPU or the TPU or the wafer scale engine best is actually really low arithmetic intensity.

And the reason to point this out is that basically what it means is that no matter how many GPUs you add to the theoretical system that has eight H100s in an NVLink system, or 72 GB200s in a GB200 NVL72 system, the amount of flops that you can realize on a very low arithmetic intensity kernel is not high. You hit some limit in terms of usually memory bandwidth, but at the system level, the network overhead and things like that.

And so the summary, Myron, that you said earlier is like SRA machines like Grok and Surrey Rift and many other chip startups out there — the concept of them is that they can do things that the others can't at any price. And that's specifically run these kernels with low arithmetic intensity.

So we add these and we try to explain like, you know, what the ratios actually are in terms of flops per byte. And we go through some example gem shapes for LLM inference, and the final chart in the article shows like the arithmetic intensity pointed out of some common gem shapes, where decode batch one — meaning a decode kernel, when the model produces output for a single user — is very low arithmetic intensity. And that's what Cerebrus is built for: really fast performance for relatively low amounts of users.

Where the Wafer Wins, and Where It Doesn't

Okay. So now that we've talked about like the good stuff — going really fast and how it works in terms of getting to the point where you have a thousand tokens per second or something on decode, the output portion — where does it not work?

And generally this is something along the lines of running really big models for a whole bunch of users at really high concurrency and playing really high batch size, which is the paradigm of LLM inference today — the non fast mode LLM inference.

Yeah, on screen we've got a screenshot where we show an estimation to say like the DeepSeqv4 model. We talked about how expensive these wafers are. Let's put a single WSC — like the whole system — as costing a million bucks, capex, right? Much more expensive than a $20,000 wafer because you've got to add in a whole bunch of other stuff, a whole bunch of other expenses after you just buy the wafer from CSMC or something, right?

And the result is — like you can run DeepSeq v4, this new model, on one of these really expensive systems: if you use 4-bit weight quantization and 16-bit KB cache for this model, and you want to support 256 concurrent users or concurrent requests at a time with an agentic coding workload at a full like million token context, you're going to need 24 systems. 24 million dollars of capex just to support 256 concurrent users, right? Which is like eight racks of cerebris in the data center, just to run one copy of the model. You probably want to run multiple copies of the model.

So a pretty significant upfront investment for somebody to commit to the wafer technology is like probably measured in the minimum price of 100 million dollars if you want to even start trying out how to run these really big models on these wafers. And with $100 million, you can get so many GB300 racks, right? You'll have tons more memory, you can have multiple copies and a lot more KB cache to support huge context.

It's a big bet to start the development process, right? Because the way that you start optimizing a model — and the way that you get that curve that we've tried to show improves over time with inference X — is by getting developers access to these systems. And if you need a team of developers working on how to optimize one of these models, and the buy-in is 25, 50, a hundred million dollars to get in, there's just a limited set of customers who are even gonna explore that.

And so we think there's a feasibility problem here, right? It's not that like technically there's no way possible for you to run a trillion parameter model at a million token context on a set of wafers and get really great performance — 1000 tokens per second or something per user. It's just that the upfront cost is going to be a lot, and then potentially the cost per million tokens is also going to be a lot, that you're going to need to pay for by having the users pay a significant premium.
