## 一、"代码不重要"是这场访谈里最大的谎言——而且每个人都在用行动拆穿它
所有创始人嘴上说 "code doesn't matter anymore"，但仔细听，他们几乎全都悄悄回到了亲手写代码的状态。有人维护内部工具是为了"能可信地谈论产品该怎么改"，有人说"像很多创始人一样，不得不亲自回来写代码，just to **rebase** my assumptions"。注意那个词——**rebase**。他们不是在拿 git 术语开玩笑，而是在描述一件严肃的事：人类正在把自己当成一个需要定期重新校准的传感器。代码从"产出物"降格成了"感觉器官"。这很像影像诊断普及之后，资深医生反而更坚持亲手触诊——不是因为触诊更高效，而是因为不亲手摸，临床直觉会悄悄漂移而你毫无察觉。真正稀缺的不是写代码的能力，而是"知道此刻模型能做到什么"的体感，而这个体感的半衰期只有几周。
## 二、Conway 定律正在反向运行——公司在把自己改造成一个 API
经典的 Conway 定律说：软件结构会镜像组织的沟通结构。但访谈里发生的恰恰是反过来的事。一个创始人说他们"一个接一个地在内部造 agent

item.title: [Lex Clips] The insane difficulty of reverse engineering video codecs | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、比特精确：一个 1992 年的技术决定，如何在三十年后造出一家"垄断企业"

表面上 bit exactness 只是个工程正确性问题——每个解码器对同一段码流必须吐出一模一样的字节。但请注意 JB 那句几乎被一带而过的话：FFmpeg 是"赢家通吃"。这两件事其实是同一枚硬币的两面。一旦规范强制要求比特精确，解码器之间就再也无法在"画质"上竞争——所有实现的输出按定义完全相同。竞争维度于是被迫从"谁解得更好"坍缩为"谁解得更全"。而"覆盖面"这个维度有极强的正反馈：每多支持一个编解码器，整个库对所有人的价值都上升（JB 原话：每加一个新 codec，其价值高于这个 codec 本身）。结果就是数学上注定的单一赢家。反观浏览器——HTML 渲染没有比特精确要求，于是 Firefox、Chrome、那几个日本引擎可以在"渲染得怎样"上各有千秋，多元生态得以存活。所以那个被 Yuri Resnik 承认的"MPEG-2 时代最大错误之一"（没做比特精确），从市场结构的角度看，反而恰恰是多样性的保护伞。 令人意外的结论是：1992 年标准委员会房间里那个纯技术性的取舍，悄悄替整个行业选择了"垄断还是多元"。

二、"二进制即规范"——一次被忽视的认识论翻转

Costa 反复挂在嘴边的 "binary specification"，值得停下来咂摸。在正常的软件世界里，因果链是：规范 → 实现，文档是真理，代码是它的影子。Costa 把这条链整个倒了过来：二进制就是规范，文档"不需要"。这不是偷懒，而是一种哲学立场——地图被烧掉了，于是疆域本身成了唯一的地图。这跟法律里的"原旨主义"、跟宗教里"唯文本"的解经传统是同构的：当权威的解释者（微软、苹果、那家做 GoToMeeting 的公司）已经沉默或消失，剩下的人只能把那个不会说话的造物当作终极经文，逐字、逐指令地训诂。逆向工程师，本质上是数字时代的经文校勘学者。

三、黑箱里的"课程学习"，与作为认知前提的谦逊

两个细节放在一起看会发光。其一：Kieran 说他"纯靠运气"找到一个全是平坦块（flat blocks）的动画样本，因为它没用复杂工具，于是能"先到达某处，再一点点累加"。其二：整个解码链（熵解码 → 帧内预测 → 运动预测 → IDCT）在被打通之前，你"很长时间什么都看不见"。把这两点合起来，你会发现：逆向工程师在没有任何理论指导的情况下，自发地重新发明了机器学习里的 curriculum learning（课程学习）——先喂最简单的样本，建立一个可用的初始模型，再逐步加难度。一个人在德国的房间里、用反汇编器做的事，和今天训练大模型时的数据课程设计，是同一个认知策略的两次独立发现。而那句被 Lex 敏锐捕捉到的"谦逊"——高手们总把一切说得很容易——我的判断是：它不是修养，而是结构性的认知前提。如果 Kieran 在动手前就准确感知到 GoToMeeting 是"两个数量级以上的复杂度"，他根本无法启动。"我那个简单啦"不是谦虚，而是大脑为了让自己敢于开始，所必须维持的一种善意的自我欺骗。

四、代码里的笑话：当造物没有故事，逆向者必须既当考古学家又当作者

最后一个出人意料的角度。Costa 逆向出来的代码"全是笑话"，塞满了关于 JB、Kemp、Costia 的玩笑。为什么一个冷冰冰的解码器会变成一份社交文本？因为二进制 blob 是被刻意抽掉了所有叙事的——没有注释、没有变量名、没有作者的声音。逆向工程师先把叙事从机器码里彻底剥离，然后在重写时，又把叙事（以幽默的形式）重新注入。这跟中世纪抄经员在手稿边缘画的涂鸦、跟地下墓穴墙上的刻字，是同一种冲动：人类无法忍受一个没有故事的造物。 还有一道地缘上的暗纹值得说破：专有的"墙"几乎都建在美国（Windows Media、Apple FairPlay、GoToMeeting、DVD 的 CSS），而挖隧道的人——Benjamin、Costa（乌克兰人，住德国）、Jon Lech Johansen（挪威）、VideoLAN（法国）——几乎都在欧洲。Lex 把这行当比作考古，但需要再精确一点：考古面对的遗物从未打算躲藏，而逆向面对的是被刻意加密、且其守护者往往已经消失的造物。这是一种"对抗性考古"——你在和一个已经不在场的对手博弈，为的是把人类自己刚刚锁起来的记忆重新解锁。

📝 转录文本

逆向工程的几个时代

That's where a lot of the reverse engineering was happening.

It start— it started in the 2000s with the Windows media stuff, because that was proprietary. It started with the real media, with Benjamin Lson—

Kashiskov.

Kashiskov — that era, those were the key, that was the key groundwork.

And then in— in the 2010s was kind of the Paul Mahal, Costa era, building, doing some of the most difficult codecs. Um, JB maybe can talk about Go to Meeting four and Go to Meeting five, and—

What—

These are— what's the Go to—

Costa：来自乌克兰的天才

So, so, so, so, like, let's talk about this amazing Ukrainian guy called Costa, who was, uh, at the time living in Germany, and who was in love with Sweden, right? And the guy was the most— he— he's like— like a lot of the people in the community are very clever; he's one of those who are like borderline geniuses, right?

He was able to reverse engineering extremely complex codecs. Um, and he— he does that, and we do a a bit of engineers with Kan, but clearly not at this level. No.

Um, he reverse engineered binary blobs which are 20 megabytes.

Yeah. So, just for reference, uh, a 1 megabyte binary blob to reverse engineer is probably, order of magnitude, a month of work. And this guy is doing 20, 30 megabyte blobs. Maybe we'll talk about that in a minute, about the subtleties of how you do that. But this guy is doing it for very difficult and very obscure codecs—

GoToMeeting 的悬赏

—and— and did that for fun, right? And— and, um, so Go to Meeting, uh, was a big problem with VC, because that was like the number— number one, uh, feature request for a long time. So I put a bounty, and the guy at some point say, okay, GB, I'm going to do it. And in a matter of two months. And then he explained how he did it. He was just like, "Oh, I looked at the code, like, this looked like a a DCT that I used to see on WMV and so on." He did that. And the funniest part is that the code he has written is a ton of jokes, and there is— there is a ton of JB— right, my name— and— and Kemp, um, and Kemp and Costia jokes inside the code. The code is beautiful, right?

谦逊的高手们

So, one of the things I— I want to call is, uh, I've gotten a chance to speak to some of the developers, some of the assembly language level, uh, people, and they all— always make everything sound like it's kind of easy. There's a a kind of humility, because— I maybe— just the level of what's required to do this stuff is so high that everything else seems easy, I guess, is the lesson to take away from that.

So in the community, like, some of the most impressive people are the ones doing reverse engineering, and the other ones doing the assembly— folks, right? Um, and both of those type of people are—

amazing.

Um, x264, for example, became amazing because of a guy called Lauren Merritt, who is— was from University of Washington, I think—

at the time.

Yeah. And who was like— who made everything great and fast doing a ton of assembly. Um, yeah, so this is like the— the— the— the golden era, I guess, where so many things got—

"二进制即规范"

If you look at Costa, for example, he looked at the world as a binary specification. He didn't need documentation or anything. It's, "I have a binary and I can figure all of this out," and— and he regularly used the phrase "binary specification." "Ah, you know, it's not a problem," and he went— and he would go away, and he would come back, and he would do interesting stuff.

Can you actually speak to the details, or any— any— add color and texture to what it takes to reverse engineer— you're— a blob?

如何逆向一个 blob：以 GoToMeeting 为例

Yeah. So, so, let's look at Go to Meeting, for example — is a good one. Because, um, I record a meeting. Uh, on Go to Meeting, for example. How do I play it back without needing this Go to Meeting player? They may not even be a player. I may— I may need to send a recording of a meeting to someone that doesn't have a player or whatever.

So, f— first of all, there's a ton of other stuff there. There's a actual video conferencing client you need to go and find. It may be easy, it may not be easy to find the actual module doing the decompression. You need a way to actually dump the YUV data from the module. So often it re— it involves opening in a disassembler, trying to guess where the hooks are to incorporate that module and run that module natively to decode a sample file. So figure out where this module is doing the decoding process, and— and find a way to hook in and output the raw YUV data, because you will need that—

—as a point of comparison for when you actually do the reverse engineering, because you'll need to be bit exact, or in some cases close to bit exact.

And then you— you open up your disassembler, use a lot of intuition to go and figure out, you know, where the DCT is, where's Entropy Coding. There— there is a kind of— not a rule book, but there's always a pattern of some sort. Um, for example, Go to Meeting, you know, it will be a a lot of screen codec tools. There's also different variants. So often— I think there's— what's Go to Meeting four, five—

two, three, four, I think.

2, three, four.

Yeah.

来自 Perplexity 的背景补充

So I should mention here, going to Perplexity. Go to Meeting uses its own proprietary codec for older recorded sessions, historically stored in WMV files that require a special decoder to play properly on Windows. Without this decoder installed, Windows Media Player and some editors cannot decode the video track. So, you may only hear audio or see a black screen. Boy, do I remember that.

But this is reverse engineering that. This is key, right? Because the Go to Meeting is something that not many people know anymore, right? You know about Zoom and— and Teams and so on. But, like, now let's fast forward 10 years, 15 years. And, like, this is a gotome meeting.exe for Windows 32 bits, right? Which is like, "Oh yeah, but I'm on Android, I'm on an iPad, I'm somewhere else," right? How are you going to do that? I'm going to be on Ris 5 — on— those are blocked, but there are tons of files we need to support for the future. And this is why those type of work are in— exceptionally useful for humanity.

像考古学家一样工作

I just have to say, though, that reverse engineering process is mindblowing. It's crazy. It's like— it's a kind of, like, you know, I've been reading a lot, and I interview archaeologists. I mean, you— you just have so little signal.

Yes. Yes.

You know, over time you get so much experience, you understand the structure of the original code, so you can kind of start inferring basics—

but you're like— like— like archaeologist with a little brush trying to reconstruct the entire human.

Kan is too humble, but Kieran has done some reverse—

engine ofform. Yeah. At the time. Um—

synform. Nice.

Yeah. At the time, before— um, actually led to the open sourcing of that work. Um, so in parallel to doing the binary side, you obviously have samples. In many cases, you don't have many samples. Um, so you have to figure out what all the different flavors are, and you may have a— soap form, for example, is actually a collection of different approaches and toolkits within that codec, cuz often it— it grows naturally. And— and the hard part is finding the sample that gets you kind of somewhere to start without having to implement 10 different other things.

So, start there. I think, thankfully, at the time I found a sample by pure chance that had a lot of flat blocks. It was animation. So that really helped a lot, because it wasn't using particularly complex coding tools, etc. And you could kind of get somewhere, and then— and then build up, and build up, until you figure, "Hey, here's a few bits here. I missed this. I missed this— this if branch that it does," and go, "Oh—"

在样本与机器码之间推断

So when we say samples, you mean a sample of videos. And then— and then you're tracking, trying to infer, like, what is this codec doing by observing the sample, and then looking at what— at the— at the machine—

the machine code, saying, "I have— by this bite is six, take this branch"—

and in a different sample. Oh, nuts. And— and— and so you see, this is nuts. Then you go to things like Go to Meeting, it's like—

"mine was easy," imagine, right?

Two order of magnitude of more complexity. A guy alone—

somewhere in Germany doing that.

And for a long time you work— you're in a black box, because a decoder— for a long time— because there is so many step, from the entropy decoding, the intra prediction, the motion prediction, the IDCT, and so on. For a long time you don't see anything, right? So you're debugging purely in memory, you're debugging guess work, and you may have the buffer that the coefficients are stored in completely wrong, and so you may be going down a complete rabbit hole thinking it's this, and then, "Oh damn, that's not— that's— that's something else." And—

and you're doing that on binaries that are tens of megabytes, millions of instructions, right?

So you're stepping through the debugger, like, one by one, you know, instruction by instruction, going, "Hey, this instruction changes this, this does this"—

posing the program on the CPU level—

it on the CPU level, watching what's going on, trying to figure out—

从虚拟机中转储内存：DeCSS 的启发

Sometimes you need to, like, be in a VM. So, yes, you can pause the VM.

Yeah, pause the VM, dump the memory, cuz it could— some of the codecs could have encryption. There could be, um, like a DRM on there. So you need to dump the memory from a virtual machine. Like, when I joined the Central in 2003, John Le Johansson basically broke the DVD specification and created DCSS. Showed us how he was breaking a DRM which was MP4 Fair Play from Apple. What he did on his laptop— and I was young, I was 21— was just, like, mindblowing, because he was basically debugging Windows inside a type of VM with, like— wow, it's incredible. It's mind-blowing and inspiring.

社区互助与"求样本"

Does it get, like, from your experience and from what you've seen in the community, does it get discouraging? Does it get—

People help you. People send you samples. People are keen. Sometimes you don't have access to an encoder, so this is even more difficult, because you just— you just ask, and you have to ask for samples. I remember video land— you used to tweet for samples at one stage. "Hey, I need this obscure sample," and—

for a long time I was, "Oh, I need this codec, and I need this codec"—

and— and— and if you were really lucky, you would find— like, if you were— if you were unlucky, you'd get like one, or you'd get nothing, or you get one or two. And then— they would— sometimes you'd find a gold mine, like, "Yeah, my company has a 100,000 of these files because we depended on it for some reason." Um, and so those are the— those are kind of the best, if— if— because then they can test bit exactness across the huge range of coding tools.

什么是 bit exactness（比特精确）

Can you explain bit exactness?

Bit exactness. So, um, most— but not all— video codecs, certainly from about the 2000s onwards, have a bit exact definition. So every implementation must produce exactly the same bits, bit for bits, in exactly the same data that comes out of a decoder—

for like a large number of samples—

for a given sample. So Lex's implementation, JB's implementation, and my implementation of H.264 264 must match bit exactly.

That wasn't the case in the '90s of Meg 2. Probably fair to say one of the biggest mistakes the video industry made— and I think people who were in the room in '92— I— most of both of us were in diapers, I suspect— but [laughter]— um, have acknowledged— um, I would give a shout out to Yuri Resnik. He's acknowledged that was one of the big mistakes of the era.

FFmpeg 的"赢家通吃" vs 浏览器引擎

And you're saying the encoders needed to be able to run tests, and then the— the— the bit exactness. I mean, that's a nice thing to guarantee. Like, there's a parallel sort of development here on, uh, the way the web browser works, which is— a, you know, takes HTML and displays it, and there's no bit exactness there across the different engines.

I would— I would point out, actually, FFmpeg is unique in the sense that it's— it has been a winner takes all scenario. You have brow— browsers is a good analogy, because it has to parse a lot of different content and render it in a particular way, like a decoder. But there still are multiple browser engines. There's Firefox's one, there's Chrome's one, there's a few Japanese ones that are pretty decent. That's not been the case in multimedia in general. Um, across a wide range of codecs, FFmpeg is kind of won it all, I suppose, in a sense— because of— because of the fact that you can get— every new codec added is actually worth more than the value of that codec itself, because it makes the whole thing better.

Yuri Resnik 其人

Man, this is really cool. Uh, going to Perplexity: Yuri Resnik is a multimedia and signal processing researcher. Got his PhD in computer science from Kev University, with over 150 papers, in more than 80 granted US patents, contributor to major multimedia standards, including H64, MPEG 4, AVC, H265, uh, MEG 4 ALS, G718.

G71 is telco stuff.

And so he was more connected to—

real audio, real video, right? That was—

Oh yeah,

very important at that time.

Zen coder, Brightite Cove, context, man. I need to hang out with Yuri. He's legit.

Yeah.

And he's like one of the most nice person ever, right? Like— like, um, for example, for— for my— for my startup that I'm doing right now, called Kyber, right? I met Yuri because I met him every year at the Mile High video conference, which is in Denver. Um, and he— he gave me, like, so much good ideas and good things. He's like really amazing person. He tell— he tells us how— how, you know, how great it is to be, you know, even know us. And, you know, you look at that, and it's— I think it's— I think it's the other way around, Yuri.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
### 一、比特精确：一个 1992 年的技术决定，如何在三十年后造出一家"垄断企业"
表面上 bit exactness 只是个工程正确性问题——每个解码器对同一段码流必须吐出一模一样的字节。但请注意 JB 那句几乎被一带而过的话：FFmpeg 是"赢家通吃"。这两件事其实是同一枚硬币的两面。一旦规范强制要求比特精确，解码器之间就再也无法在"画质"上竞争——所有实现的输出按定义完全相同。竞争维度于是被迫从"谁解得更好"坍缩为"谁解得更全"。而"覆盖面"这个维度有极强的正反馈：每多支持一个编解码器，整个库对所有人的价值都上升（JB 原话：每加一个新 codec，其价值高于这个 codec 本身）。结果就是数学上注定的单一赢家。反观浏览器——HTML 渲染没有比特精确要求，于是 Firefox、Chrome、那几个日本引擎可以在"渲染得怎样"上各有千秋，多元生态得以存活。**所以那个被 Yuri Resnik 承认的"MPEG-2 时代最大错误之一"（没做比特精确），从市场结构的角度看，反而恰恰是多样性的保护伞。** 令人意外的结论是：1992 年标准委员会房间里那个纯技术性的取舍，悄

item.title: [Lex Clips] VLC turned down $30+ million dollars to keep VLC ad-free | Lex Fridman Podcast
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

真正守护 VLC 的，不是 JB 的道德，而是"没有人创造过它"

大多数人把这个故事读成"一个英雄用意志力拒绝了金钱"。但 JB 自己悄悄给了你一个完全不同的版本——他说他拒绝的真正原因，是觉得自己"不够正当（not completely legitimate）"去卖它。这是一个让人意外的反转：VLC 之所以没被广告吞掉，不是因为有一个足够强的守门人，而是因为它根本没有一个"有资格卖它的人"。 VLC 不是被某个人创造的，它从 Network 2000 → VideoLAN 学生项目 → VLC 客户端"自然涌现"出来。一个没有清晰所有者的东西，结构上就免疫于被收购——因为任何人想卖它，都会立刻撞上"你凭什么"。分散的起源，本身就是一种抗捕获的免疫系统。这比"找一个有德性的 CEO"要稳固得多。

Token Ring 不只是一段技术怀旧——它是整个故事的拓扑结构

注意 JB 花了很大篇幅解释 token ring：没有路由器，每台机器都要打开消息、检查"是不是给我的"、再传给下一个。慢，但人人参与。这恰恰是那个校园的治理结构——学生把"供电、电视台、超市、宿舍分配"这个"令牌"一圈圈传下去；也恰恰是这个项目谱系本身的形态：Network 2000 把令牌传给 VideoLAN，VideoLAN 传给 VLC，VLC 传到 JB 手上，JB 又传给叙利亚和印度乡下的开发者。技术的拓扑、机构的拓扑、和这段口述史的拓扑是同构的。更跨界一点说：JB 继承的从来不是代码，而是一种"拒绝反射"——École Centrale 的校友拒绝为了务实而搬去 Clermont-Ferrand，学生"强行逼迫（strong-armed）"大学开源，JB 拒绝几千万美元。这是同一个动作的三代复现。这个血脉从一个"不"中诞生，也只会用"不"来延续自己。

"只有骗子广告公司来找我"——这是一面镜子，也是经济学里的"柠檬市场"

JB 说了一句很轻、但极重的话：如果当年是 Netflix 来谈，故事可能不一样；可来的"只有 shady ads company"。这里藏着一个残酷的系统性真相：你收到的诱惑的质量，等于外界对你身份的判断。 VLC 被感知为一个"眼球聚合器"，而不是一个高端品牌，所以靠近它的只有抽取型玩家。这几乎就是 Akerlof 的"柠檬市场"——免费的公共基础设施，在结构上只会被"次品买家"求购，因为正经玩家会自己造轮子而不是来寄生。于是 JB 的"好雷达"其实不是道德直觉，而是他从一开始就只被投喂了同一种选项，逼着他要么堕落要么彻底拒绝，没有中间地带。

最后一个意外：他用"自私"这个词，恰恰说出了整个系统的引擎

JB 反复强调"我不是不爱钱"，还说拒绝是"very selfishly——我需要晚上能安心睡觉"。把道德重新定义为一种个人可持续性机制，而不是利他。这跟他描述校园时那句话是同一台发动机："你 22 岁，你得让校园有电，所以你会真的在乎。"恰当尺度下的自利＝公共品。VLC 活下来最危险的时刻不是高峰，而是 2005 年只剩两个开发者的低谷——那也正是"卖掉它"在财务上最显得"情有可原"的时刻。能在最该妥协的谷底说不，才是它后来拥有数十亿用户的真正原因。增长策略没有保住 VLC，在最该崩溃的那一年没有变现，才保住了它。

（说明：按要求，洞察见上方 insights 标签；以下为分段美化后的转录原文，仅增加段落结构、必要标点与小标题，未翻译、未压缩、未改写事实。）

📝 转录文本

The Reddit Legend

So, as the legend goes, JB, you uh repeatedly turned down millions of dollars to uh keep VLC open source, free for everyone, without ads. So, take me through the reasoning behind that decision of leaving millions of dollars on the table.

Yeah, that's like almost a meme, right? On on Reddit.

There literally is a meme on Reddit. 9GAG and yeah, yeah, I see, there's you >> [laughter] >> looking like a wizard in the in the VLC hat on Reddit. "This is JB, the creator of VLC media player. He refused tens of millions of dollars in order to keep VLC as free. Thanks, Jean-Baptiste Kempf. You can even summon him on Reddit."

Yeah, and usually if you see, right? It's usually like people tagging me, right? And and then there is me and then like I say, "Good morning." I got 24K upvotes, which is great, right? My comment on Reddit is >> [laughter] >> At least on that account.

What Is the Story About VLC?

Um, so the question is, needs to be answered first. It — what is the story about VLC, right? Um, because yes, it is true. I refused dozens of millions of dollars. Yes, several time. Yes, I could be a multi-millionaire and be somewhere on the beach.

Um, but I did not do it because I thought it was not moral and it was not the right thing to do. And this is very important for myself, is to be like, I work for the greater good. I work for people, and I don't want — it's not just by my — by myself.

But the reason is also because I — I did not feel that I'm completely legitimate to do that, and let me explain you why.

The Origin: École Centrale Paris

VLC story is a very weird story. In France, we have university and we have a type of top colleges, um, and those type of excellency schools are engineering school, business schools, and basically lawyers and medical, right? But they're outside of university. And in order to enter those, you spend 2 years working like crazy — math, physics — to enter those best engineering school.

One of the school is called the École Centrale Paris. It has changed name since, but it was called the École Centrale Paris. And because it was Centrale, they had to move it because it was too small after the World War II. And they moved it and wanted to move it to the center of France, in place called Clermont-Ferrand. And the alumni decided that this was not okay, right? It is the the school that Eiffel — right? The one who did the Eiffel Tower — attended to, right? So, they said, "No, no, we are amazing, great school. We cannot do that."

And so, they bought a piece of land south of Paris, uh, very near Paris. Um, and it was a campus managed by a non-profit of the alumni.

A Student-Run Campus

Okay? Because of that, everything on the campus was managed by students. The university did nothing, right? So, uh, radio, uh, TV, uh, supermarket, uh, library, um, defining who was going to into which rooms — um, everything was managed by the students.

That's an amazing experiment that it all didn't go to hell quickly. It somehow flourished.

It worked great, and I learned so much in my life doing those side activities, right? Because you're — you're 22 and you need to run your campus, else you don't have electricity, right? So, you care about that, right?

Token Ring and the Need for Speed

But anyway, in the '80s, um, they did a full experiments of deploying a network — uh, mostly sponsored by IBM and 3Com — which was a token ring network. So, token ring is something that probably almost no one knows about anymore. It's a networking technology where you don't have routers, right? Everyone is linked, um — it's type like really a ring. And when you want to send a message, you talk to your neighbor who's going to put the message to the next one, who's going to put the things to the next one, in term of ring.

The issue with token ring is, of course, is that it's very slow, because every computer on the network needs to open the message, see if it's okay, "is it for me? No, it's not," and then send it back like a token which is traveling around the ring.

In the '80s, you're doing some telnet and sending mails as university, that's okay, right? But starts the '90s — and the '90s, um, and starts video games. And when you have li- — high latency video games, basically you die, right? So, in 1994, 1995, around Doom and Duke Nukem coming around, they want a faster network.

So, the students go and see the university and say, "You know what? We want a faster network. We need to work and also play video games." Um, and the university tells them that basically, "Oh, I'm sorry. We cannot help you, because you understand, the campus is not ours. You manage it, so do something." And you should see some basically partners of the university and basically go away.

The Network 2000 Project

And they go, and they actually go and see um the the the CIO of uh Bull, which is a large uh French company and who's doing some TVs in France. And he says, "Well, you know what? The future of video is satellite." Well, today we know it's not, but at least it was a good idea in 1995, the first of satellite dish.

And he says that instead of having like one satellite dish and big decoder for each of the students, which are 1,500 — what about you build like, you put an enormous dish and only one decoder, and you send the video directly on the network. And that required a very fast network. Today, it's obvious, but at the time was like the first to do video streaming.

So, they built this project which was called Network 2000. >> [snorts] >> And of course, we're — we're in the '90s, right? Everything is futuristic. It's called 2000. >> Yeah.

And so, they do the Network 2000 project. It's completely hacked. It crashes after 45 seconds. That's okay — the demo is 40 seconds. It leaks memory. That's okay — they put 64 megabyte of RAM instead of the age of 16 you have. And the demo should have stopped there, and that was a Network 2000 project by the students.

What was the format of the video that they had to work with?

MPEG-2, because satellite is MPEG-2 — TS for transport, MPEG-2 video, and MPEG-2 audio at that time.

From VideoLAN to VLC

And the project should have stopped there. Everyone was happy. They had like amazing ATM network at 155 — 55 megabits per seconds. They had probably one of the best network in Europe at that time. And they stopped the project.

6 months or a year later, two students arrive and say, "Well, you know what? Maybe other people care about video streamed on a local network." And they create the VideoLAN project — VideoLAN. And one of them is called uh Christophe Massiot, that is a good friend of uh Boskyan and me. And they start the project. It's not even open source yet. And they spend around 3 years to get the school to agree to make it open source, because the the university wanted to get some, because of the IP and copyright of the students, wanted to basically monetize these MPEG-2 decoders.

Just to be clear, so what was the main application? Streaming on a local network.

It was streaming on a local network.

Way — that's just like, to state the obvious, this is before YouTube. This is before —

10 years before YouTube. You have a Pentium 60 or 75, right? You — you — you — the main machine was 486 DX at 33 MHz, right?

Bear in mind, television was the main form of video at the time. You could get new channels in the '90s — having even one new channel, when you grew up with four channels, having a fifth or a sixth was a big deal. And so, having this satellite service with, you know, dozens, even hundreds of channels was so groundbreaking.

Especially because this is university where you had ton of different nationalities, right? So, there was a ton of people who wanted — So, in the end, they had like several dishes on different type of satellite, right? Because for example, a lot of people were coming from the Maghreb or the Middle East, and they they — So, they went to different type of uh of satellites.

Anyway, the solution worked great. And they started the VideoLAN project. The VideoLAN project has several — and some are completely crazy solutions, like one, how — how to create multicast on an unicast network, but let's not come to that. It's too too complex. But VideoLAN client part is what became VLC.

Actually, they basically strong-armed the the university to force it to open source, because university did not understand that. And in 2001, it's still early. But basically, yes, the university agreed in early 2001 to make it open source.

Joining in 2003

I joined the project in 2003, because that's when I joined the university. So, the first thing is, I'm not the one who created VLC, because actually no one did, right? It just kind of naturally emerged from the VideoLAN project.

We should mention that, like — again, you you you said it, just but to make it clear, VideoLAN as what it became was at the time as a set of technologies around video. The VLC, what you call the client — that's the thing that most normies uh —

That is correct.

— think of like as the thing which is like the thing that pops up when you click on video and you play it.

So, I arrive in 2003, and then I will create the open source um non-profit organization called VideoLAN. And I took everything out of the university to create it a non-profit project and something sustainable.

It's — yes, it's true that I spend more time than anyone on VLC and VideoLAN. That is sure. Um, but it's a continuity of a previous project, VideoLAN, the student project, which is a continuity of the Network 2000 project, which is a continuity of that and that.

2005: The Project Should Have Died

I'm sure there's moments along the way there you were thinking of like, what is the future of this from an open source perspective? Cuz as as the internet is blowing up and there is companies — I mean, for people don't remember, like, there's companies making huge amounts of money.

I can tell you that in 2005, the project should have died. Um, and I made it to continue the project. At some point, we were only two active developers. Um, and I thought it was a great technology and was useful, and it will be useful, and I made that my my my life and my my time.

Um, and I made that grow from a few hundreds of thousands of users, millions of users, to what we have now, which is probably billions of uh version of VLC around the world and used everywhere. Um, so that's a bit the story of VLC.

There is ton of very funny story around that. Many people from uh around the world working on it, like you said, in Syria or middle of nowhere in India.

The Shady Offers

But along the way, I got several offers, which were either to bundle uh toolbars, right? You remember those horrible toolbars, which were basically spyware, or changing your web browser or your search engine, or even like advertisement inside VLC. And I didn't like that, right? I am —

And people don't understand that is not — I'm not against money, right? I'm very happy to make money. I created several startups and one — I hope that is going to work very well. It's a fact that I believe that you need to win money ethically. There is a right way of doing that. And doing sneaky advertisement or stealing data is not the correct way, right?

For example, if Netflix arrive at some point and say, "Well, we want to put Netflix inside VLC" — probably the story would have been different, right? But they didn't. The only people who came to us were shady ads company. And if I do that, right? I would have ton of money, right? And then 3 years later, project is gone, right? Someone forks it and and something else happens.

So, it's not even necessarily ads or any of that. It's the shadiness of the dishonesty of the — So, you had a good radar. You had a good threshold of like, "No, this compromises the spirit of what this is supposed to represent."

Right and Wrong

But also it's — it's for me, right? I'm like, very selfishly, I need to go to bed at night and be happy about what I've done, right? Maybe it's my upbringing. Um, uh, maybe it's my parents' fault or whatever, right? But I believe there is right and wrong, right? And this was the right decision at the time. It still is. I want to be proud of what I've been doing. And like, if I had sold out, I would have betrayed so many other people's work.

Yeah. Well, I should say, me and uh most of the internet thank you for that decision. It's inspiring uh for others uh, I think, that um — that are pushing open source movement forward, that it's okay to do these kinds of huge sacrifices if you believe it's right. And I think in that case it was right, and it was the reason that VLC became as successful as it was. Cuz it — it's an embodiment. It's a symbol of like, you know, freedom and what the open source community can create.

Yeah, and be a service for so many people around the world. And this is important.

The 2000s: When Spyware Was Normal

We should emphasize, in the 2000s it was really normal to download a program and it secretly installed some spyware.

Yeah. It was — it was buried in very faint text, or in the license text box that nobody reads, that at the bottom: "Oh, we'll be installing this toolbar and changing all these things." And it was very common to — you know, you install a program to do something at the time, of any sort.

To put yourself in the mind of a developer at that time — I think it's very easy to — everybody listening to this, it's very easy at that time to convince yourself to take a few thousand dollars, a few thousand dollars to do it. To say no to much more money takes guts and takes vision.

The last offer I had was obscene. And they say, "Yeah, but imagine, with all that money, you could build something new open source, right?" It was like the mind trick was — it was difficult, but for me it was just like, "No, this doesn't work like that," or "this is not the right thing, so I don't do it." And again, right? It's not that I don't like money or whatever. It's just like, it wasn't right.

Well, once again, thank you, from me and from the rest of the internet.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 真正守护 VLC 的，不是 JB 的道德，而是"没有人创造过它"
大多数人把这个故事读成"一个英雄用意志力拒绝了金钱"。但 JB 自己悄悄给了你一个完全不同的版本——他说他拒绝的真正原因，是觉得自己"不够正当（not completely legitimate）"去卖它。这是一个让人意外的反转：**VLC 之所以没被广告吞掉，不是因为有一个足够强的守门人，而是因为它根本没有一个"有资格卖它的人"。** VLC 不是被某个人创造的，它从 Network 2000 → VideoLAN 学生项目 → VLC 客户端"自然涌现"出来。一个没有清晰所有者的东西，结构上就免疫于被收购——因为任何人想卖它，都会立刻撞上"你凭什么"。分散的起源，本身就是一种抗捕获的免疫系统。这比"找一个有德性的 CEO"要稳固得多。
## Token Ring 不只是一段技术怀旧——它是整个故事的拓扑结构
注意 JB 花了很大篇幅解释 token ring：没有路由器，每台机器都要打开消息、检查"是不是给我的"、再传给下一个。慢，但人人参与。这恰恰是**那个校园的治理结构**——学生把"供电、电视台、超

item.title: [邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain] 現在是比特幣秋季！巨鯨親揭ALL IN時間點！Michael Terpin 【邦妮區塊鏈】feat.@TheDavidLinReport ​
item.dc:creator: 邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、Sailor 的「免疫接种」，暴露了一个被精心隐藏的事实：MicroStrategy 根本不是投资公司，而是一家银行

Terpin 不经意间说出了最惊人的一句话——两年前 Sailor 私下告诉他，他之所以"永远只买不卖"，不是因为这是最优投资策略（Terpin 自己都承认"想拿更多币就该在顶部卖、底部买回"），而是因为"一旦做了买入以外的任何动作，华尔街买家会觉得他动摇了 thesis，就不再无条件给他写支票了"。

请把这句话品三遍。这意味着"钻石手信仰"从来不是信仰，而是一份债券契约（bond covenant）——是 Sailor 为了维持融资信用而必须表演的"央行可信度承诺"。而所谓的"pivot / inoculation"之所以发生，恰恰是因为融资基础从机构优先股换成了 STRC 的散户——散户拿着 11.5% 的股息，他们要的是"按时兑付"，不是意识形态纯洁性。于是 Sailor 必须装上一个"必要时可以卖币"的逃生阀。这和一个国家"脱离金本位"的剧本一模一样：你维持可兑换的虚构，直到债主的结构变了。 更进一步：当 Terpin 说"STRC 给跌幅设了地板，所以我不再认为会跌破 4 万"时，他实际上在宣布一件比特币被设计出来就是为了永远避免的事——比特币自发地长出了一个"最后贷款人"，一支"平准基金"，一个去中心化资产体内野蛮生长出来的美联储。

二、跨界连接：Satoshi 的减半与美国大选，是惠更斯的两只摆钟

Terpin 坚持"减半对齐美国大选周期不是偶然"。如果他是对的，那这就是一个极度反讽的系统性真相：比特币用尽全力打造了一个无需信任的时钟（白皮书里区块链的本质就是 timestamp server——一台盖时间戳的机器），结果这台精密机器却悄悄和地球上最依赖信任的机构（美国选举与财政放水周期）锁相同步了。

这在物理学里有个精确的名字——惠更斯耦合（Huygens' synchronization）。1665 年惠更斯发现，挂在同一面墙上的两只摆钟，无论初始相位如何,最终都会通过墙体传来的微小振动同步摆动。比特币的"四年周期"和美国的"四年大选"就是这两只摆钟，而那面共振的墙,就是全球流动性。你以为你买的是一个独立于政治的资产，其实你买的是一只被隔壁那只钟的振动悄悄"带动"的摆。这也解释了为什么 Terpin 反复在"我的周期论"和"Lynn Alden 的 M2 流动性论"之间说"它们是互补的"——他没意识到自己描述的不是两个理论，而是同一个耦合系统的两个端点。

三、最反直觉的观察：所有盼着"超级周期"的人，其实是在盼比特币"死掉"

Bonnie 那个数学问题问得极漂亮：3000x → 100x → 30x → 10x →（这轮勉强 2x），递减回报数学上必然趋近于零。Terpin 的救命稻草是"超级周期"。但他自己给超级周期下的定义才是埋雷的地方——他说超级周期必须满足"资产的核心 thesis 发生本质性改变"（黄金需要尼克松关闭金本位 + 美国人重新获得持金权两件事叠加）。

把这两句话拼起来，结论令人不寒而栗：比特币要想再次暴涨，前提是比特币必须停止做它现在的自己。 递减回报不是 bug，而是这个资产正在"成熟为完成态"——一个 96% 已被挖出、波动性被机构和 STRC 反复抹平的资产，本质上正在变成"做完了的事"。它要再上一个数量级，就必须死而复生为另一种东西（全球抵押品层？主权储备资产？终极基础货币）。所以那些一边抱着"数字黄金"叙事、一边热切盼望"super cycle"的持有者，逻辑上是在自相矛盾地为现有比特币的"消亡"投票。这是一种 death-and-resurrection 的多头逻辑。

四、附赠两个小"啊哈"：比特币不是太阳，是海床；而 Terpin 其实不是预言家，是考古学家

第一，注意 Terpin 的 AI 资金流模型：AI 代币会跑赢比特币三年 → 然后"利润会 roll back 回比特币"。在他自己的心智模型里，比特币不是加密世界的太阳（能量源头），而是海床（能量沉降的最低势能层）——投机的热量在 AI、预测市场、meme 里燃烧、翻腾，最终沉淀回比特币。比特币最大主义者以为自己持有的是恒星，Terpin 的模型里它是热力学的"地面态"。

第二，整段访谈里 Terpin 最焦虑的不是"会涨还是会跌"，而是"这一轮到底从哪天算起"——因为减半前的 ETF 新高（73,850）把他的周期计数搞乱了。他不停地数"35 个月""23 个月""coin days destroyed 指向 4.2 万"。这哪是预测？这是年代测定学，是数年轮的树木年代学，是 NBER 给衰退定起止日的那种工作。 于是终极反讽出现了：比特币本身是一台为了消灭"时间争议"而生的完美机器钟，可它脚下的人类却在这台精密钟之上，叠加了一套谁也读不准的"春夏秋冬"民间历法。精确的机器钟，孵化出了一套模糊的占卜日历——而整个市场的钱，押的都是这套日历读得准不准。

📝 转录文本

开场预告

Whales already sold. I think what is uh what's selling right now, the majority of it is liquidations. We are in the middle of Bitcoin fall. We have no change in the the u progress to the million dollar Bitcoin.

We just had Michael Sailor on. He claims that his buying strategies buying a Bitcoin does not move the price.

It puts a floor on how low it's going to go. Most whales are hodlers, but you know there's a lot of whales, right? If you look at the past bull cycles, each subsequent bull cycle had a lower growth rate than the previous one went from trough to peak. If we assume that this trend continues, does it not make sense that mathematically speaking the growth from crop to peak should approach zero at some point?

That is correct. That's why the super cycle part is important. A super cycle has to be five years or more and um it has to have an essential change in the thesis of an asset that causes the price largely to go up twice every four year cycle. They go and sell near the top, buy back after the bottom is already in. Few things have been consistent across every bottom. Clean days burned uh destroyed that says it's going to be around 42,000 at the bottom.

Oh, just went back to market for a minute. The um

Wow, you're so much fun, David.

嘉宾介绍

We're back at Consensus Miami. Pleased to welcome back to the show Michael Turpin for the second time in a year. Michael Turpin is the author of Bitcoin Super Cycle. Coined by CNBC as the godfather of crypto and the founder and CEO of Transform Ventures. Very pleased to welcome back to the show Michael and get his views on where Bitcoin is headed next. I'm David Lynn.

Bonnie Chang.

Michael, tell us where Bitcoin is headed next, but I'll let you uh kick it off with Bonnie first. She's got some questions for you.

一、Sailor 的「转向」与 STRC

Sure. The most timely one is uh Sailor's Pivot. What is your thought? I know you are a big investor in uh STRC. You talked about that product. Uh we last talked it was 3 months ago. Tell us what you think.

Sure. Um I think the pivot, if you want to call it, he says it's an inoculation. Um is because of where he's receiving his funding now. So originally — and I've I've discussed this with Michael at length, uh you know I've been to private events at his home — and uh you know he, I've always been an advocate: if you're if you want to get more Bitcoin at the end of the day you should salt the top and buy back at the bottom, which is where my uh my book has that thesis, uh my fund has that thesis. And um you know he had told me at the time, and this is probably two years ago, that uh you know that if he changed his thesis and did anything other than always buy, he can certainly buy more at the bottom and he has done that. But if he did anything other than buy, that certain, you know, Wall Street buyers would think he chased his thesis and they wouldn't be as willing to just perpetually write checks to him.

Now, that was when they were writing checks to um the preferred shares, the different instruments he had before STRC. STRC now has become really a retail phenomenon as well as institutional. And I think there the main um concern is, you know, how does he pay the dividend? It's 11 and a half%. I mean, it's almost three times what T-bills are and it's still pretty safe. And I think he's just having to show that if needed, he can sell Bitcoin to pay the dividends because there's a lot of it.

And uh that doesn't mean — I mean most DATs when they sold Bitcoin, it was because they had to at the bottom of the market. You know, their their boards in turmoil and crisis, "what a stupid idea buying Bitcoin." That's not the the case with Sailor or Micro Strategy. They're all firm long-term believers. And his thesis of borrowing at 11, 12 and having appreciation of more than 20% per year, um you know still works uh with the right financial engineering. And so you have to have that little inoculation, as he says, or escape valve, that if all of a sudden you need to go and sell, um you can. I just really don't see him doing it anytime soon.

三、Sailor 的买入是否影响价格 / OTC

What do you What do you have to say about that?

I didn't say it moved the price the day he buys it, but what I think it does is it puts a floor on how low it's going to go because I would think that he would buy a lot more if it actually went down to say 39 or something like that. And I think that every time he buys it, it doesn't immediately raise the price. It's actually been surprising how little it does raise the price because he's buying it over time and he doesn't declare it until — you, his Monday, when he says how much he bought. And it's OTC, right? I mean OTC masks a lot of um buying and selling behavior.

I mean historically a lot of the volatility in the era that whales control things um were that they would, you know, buy things OTC and then they would dump on the market and cause things down and then short at the same time. That's behavior that Wall Street knows how to do and they've done on other assets, and I think you could see around the 10-10 scenario that there was a bit of that gamesmanship going on.

If they accumulate them as permanent capital? Yeah. Because ETFs are certainly not permanent capital. They they do move in and out. 十、AI 重定价是否波及比特币 / 量子计算

AI has been re disrupting the entire software industry. The IGV uh ETF, which is a software index ETF, is down about 25% year to date. Mainstream media is writing that anything built on code is being repriced. So can you expect Bitcoin, which is also built on code, to see a similar repricing at some point?

No. Um, Bitcoin has withtood an awful lot of attacks. I don't think there's going to be some version of Mythos that's going to uh unwind the code in Bitcoin. It just has too much protection behind it. It's not just uh code. It's code and also all these blocks that have been permanently sealed.

So I mean quantum of course its threat is that it can go in and um you know figure out um you know what a private key is by just trying you know what would normally take billions of years, and that in the future could take a few minutes to go and guess every single variation of like a 45 character um alpha numeric uh string. Um but I think before that happens, which I believe we're still 15, 20 years away, uh — a, they're going to go after other shot 256 targets: the defense industry, uh, hospitals, banks. I mean, these are all built on shot 256. So, if you can crack Satoshi's wallet or you can crack JP Morgan, you know, you're probably going to have to go after JP Morgan first. But, you know, again, with Bitcoin, you have to go out after a wallet at a time. You're not going to crack every wallet in Bitcoin. Um, so it's a very distributed decentralized system.

So, I don't believe — I think that it's very possible that um you'll have AI um crack some significant um uh smart contract, and that will be a crisis that will uh drive the price of Ethereum way down and there'll probably be collateral damage on Bitcoin. I — I would see that as one of the main things that could happen uh between now and October that would then be your FTX moment. You know, let's say Leo cracked, right? How much is staked on Leo, and uh and we got sucked out and goes to North Korea. Um that would definitely cause a lot of collateral damage and I could see that bring us down, you know, into the 40s perhaps. But without that, without something significant, if it's just a garden variety, you know, hedge fund unwinding, um you know, we're probably going to, you know, probably only go down a little bit below 60-50s.

十四、减半与美国大选周期 / Satoshi 的设计

Sorry, how is that not an accident?

I said I don't believe it's an accident. He never — he never put it in, he never said specifically in the white paper that he was going to have this, but I believe that it's not an accident that you know the havingings have been 2012, 2016, 2020, 2024 — the next one's going to be 2028.

I don't believe that he planned to um have it move out of the presidential year so quickly because 2016 — I mean if you take away 2016 we've been pretty close to the four-ear cycle. There's never been a four-year cycle, right? It's a 210,000 block cycle. And the algorithm is supposed to go and make 10-minute blocks. And if they're exactly 10 minutes, you get about four years. And um they've been um — if they're if they're if they're less than that, you make uh you adjust the difficulty to make them longer. If they're longer than that, you you adjust the difficulty to make them — make them shorter. So, in other words, you add difficulty or subtract it to make it easier or harder for mining. Because otherwise you you might have a four-year cycle, then you might have a two-year cycle, then you might have an eight-year cycle. And so that's the whole thing about it — it also creates extra security for the network. That's — I have a whole chapter in my book about mining and and the economics of that.

That is very interesting because then the four-year cycle is based on US election. So Satoshi planned this around US —

十五、超级周期与法币贬值 / 商品超级周期

And you know we've had really three of them in the last 100 years and a super cycle overall, and this goes back to you know Elliot and the Elliot wave and what he said you know 100 years ago almost. And um you know that a super cycle — he he has the grand super cycle, the little mini super cycles — a super cycle is — has to be five years or more. And um it has to have an essential change in the thesis of an asset um that causes the price largely to go up.

Um and in the first super cycle in the last century for commodities was gold in the 70s because you had a fundamental change, two of them really. You had Nixon taking us off the uh the gold standard, and then in the same decade you had the relegalization of Americans abilities to own gold. So all of a sudden you had a new reason to buy gold and you had more people who were able to buy. And so that led to a quadrupling of the uh price of gold as you well know in the in the 1970s.

The second um super cycle was more broad-based across most commodities that were used in building um copper, nickel, etc. in the '9s because China had hyperindustrialization. And um in my book I — I — I discussed that CME had said I think in 23 that we may be starting uh a new super cycle based on money printing and monetary debasement. "We're not sure yet." Well, obviously what happened in 2025 is we're sure now.

十六、第四次转折（Fourth Turning）与每十年的技术颠覆

Have you heard of Neil How's Fourth Turning?

Yes.

So, for the audience, just to recap, the fourth turning argues that every 80 years, society reaches some sort of institutional breakdown. I wonder if Bitcoin has been rising in the last couple of years as a reflection of the coming of the fourth turning and is really just an erosion of societal trust.

You know, it's an interesting question. I mean, long before the fourth turning came out, there were a lot of thesis about the uh, you know, you have to wait sort of like two generations before people forget everything and things repeat. Um, and 80 years, you know, has been the — A lot of times people, you know, disagree about when the 80 years was. Was it, you know, 1929? Was it 1945? You know, when when did sort of the last uh 80-year cycle end?

Obviously, there have been dramatic things that have happened every decade for the most part. And um from technology, it's been very consistent for the last five decades or so that there's been at least one major new disruptive technology every decade, and they tend to start taking off in the early part of the decades and then like you know sort of like the bubble pops at the end.

Um if you look at uh you know just in the last few decades uh the uh the internet — you know kind of the web started in '91 even though the internet had been around as a you know DARPA experiment since the 60s — but the the worldwide web started in '91. When I got in in like early 93 it was still barely there. I had to you know get my web browser from Mark Andre's dorm room effectively; it was a university project. And uh by the end of the 90s you had the dot bubble and trillions dollars made and lost.

And so now you go 10 years later to the 2000s and you had a similar phenomenon with web two, right? Uh uh social media was just — was nothing at the start of the decade. It was little experiments with this little company called LinkedIn, then MySpace, and by you know, the end of the decade, you know, you had the the big unicorns and uh you know, and then that bubble uh you know, kind of popped — not not really because obviously it it kind of went down and now they're some of the largest uh entities in the world.

But the start of uh the 2010s, you know, Bitcoin was still very nent phase. I mean, you could have bought, you know, for pennies in 2010 and then by the end of the decade it was almost 20,000 — sorry, uh $60,000 by by 2020.

And so I'd say that this decade has been AI, right? 2022 is when uh um when the you know open AAI came out with its first commercial uh LLM and that broke all records for how quickly a product could be adapted. Netflix had the record for getting to a million users. It took about a year. Uh Open AI took 5 days and now it's closer to a billion. And so it's just been — I mean AI is like the internet has been you know percolating along for a long time. Uh I've I've followed AI for many decades. My uh my younger brother graduated from MIT's artificial intelligence lab and I thought in the 90s that it was going to take off with expert systems and prologue and it never did, and obviously LLMs is what was needed — now agents — to really uh you know it's now a vast majority of the increase in value in the S&P.
