十八、稳定币与 Tether 的起源

Um and then stable coins are again a a massive um new force in the economics of of crypto. The more that you have people having stable coins, they're now very easily able to buy Bitcoin, Ethereum, any other token because they they now finally have a wallet. And you've had this mass explosion.

I don't think — when I mean, as you know, I was involved in the dawn of Tether when uh Brock Pierce and and and Reev Collins and you know the team there uh Craig put together Tether in Santa Monica in early 2014, and then they end up selling into Bitfinex and spun it off as Tether. I mean, their thoughts in those days was, "we're going to be able to go and uh be able to have a coin that represents a dollar so that you can be able to trade between exchanges and not wait three days, and we'll make money on interest from banks." That was it. I don't think any of them thought that one day would be a, you know, making 20 billion dollars a year in uh in T bills, right, with 100 employees.

十九、比特币周期的未来：递减回报与超级周期

Michael, I'm curious how the future of Bitcoin cycles will progress. If you look at the past bull cycles, each subsequent bull cycle had a lower growth rate than the previous one from trough to peak. If we assume that this trend continues, does it not make sense that mathematically speaking the the the growth from trough to peak should approach zero at some point?

That is correct. That's why the super cycle part is important. So we have had logarithmically diminishing returns um and we've had arithmetically diminishing losses.

So your your first cycle, which is before the having and may or may not be as relevant because you didn't have a having yet, but um from the lowest quoted price — the first sale of Bitcoin for onetenth of a penny — to the high was 3,000x. Tenth of a penny to $30, and then it crashed down to a dollar — 97% drop. The second cycle after the first having you went up 100x from $12 to $1,200, and then you crashed 85%. The third cycle you ended up going up 30x and then you crashed 83%. The fourth cycle you ended up — so 3,000, 130 — next number is 10, right, in that sort of a progression.

And so I was projecting that we would have had a 10x increase um from the $8,700 um having in 2000 during COVID times, and um you know we ended up getting cut short at around 68, 69K. And I think that's because of macro — the bad macro is Biden's war on crypto, um you know the choke point 2.0, and the rapid rise of interest rates that — I'm surprising it only got clipped by you know 20%. Um but it did, and so — and you ended up having it go up, so it went up 8x instead of 10x. And you end up having this suppressed demand um show up at the end of the uh cycle — uh the fourth cycle — um the month before the ha — the the having — um with the ETFs u going live and us hitting 73,000 which was roughly 9x, and um and then you know we ended up having a 75% drop to the FTX lows of 15k.

二十、2024 周期为何被「腰斩」/ 10-10 黑天鹅

So I projected based on that that we were going to go up 3x plus or minus macro. Everybody and their brother thought that with Trump being elected it'd be tailwinds, positive macro. Unfortunately, they didn't realize that uh you know, President Trump has, you know, been great for crypto, but has been chaotic with his commun communications. And um every time he tweets something, you think by now people realize that he bluffs a lot, that he just plays — you know, read the art of the deal, you know, he'll ask for one thing and and like overstate, you know, what what bad things are going to happen to get the midpoint and make it seem like a win. This is very common in politics, but the media and the markets just get freaked out whenever, you know, and you end up having that leading to 10-10.

Even though um the high in in October happened exactly where it had the last couple of cycles, there was another um you know, sort of version of reading the data that would — that said it could have gone another month or two, um you know, if there was enough buying demand and if there wasn't a black swan. And you know 10-10 ended up being that black swan where you know you had this perfect storm of Trump tweeting, Binance selling like crazy and then cutting off its market makers ability to short — they they deleveraged them — and so that led to like just a freef fall in price. And u you know there was some tread uh coincidences that were going on in terms of like who was shorting what and uh you what messages they were sending to their brokers that uh you know appear that there's number of people who very quickly acted upon that uh event, and uh you know sold very quickly and and shorted and really caused that initial drop that you know started the bare market.

二十一、Jane Street 的 10 点抛售

My last question for you is your comment on the 10 a.m. dump by Jane Street.

Yes.

Uh that is what people think happened. I am not sure and I'd love your opinion on it.

Well, it's been widely reported. I — I don't have any proof of it, but it's been widely reported enough it sounded like uh uh like it probably happened. And uh and it's not happening anymore. Um the —

It stopped after —

It stopped, right? And prices have gone up since then. Um, but the the thesis was that uh Jane Street was systematically selling at 10:00 a.m. which is a half hour after the markets open, and then like many short sellers do, flooding the market with like "ah price is going to dump today because of XYZ news." Well, the price is going to dump because you were systematically selling at 10 a.m., and then they would have shorts in and they would make money.

But is it — is that legal?

Basically, it is legal to — if you're not doing it with a commodity, with a security. Um, you know, Bitcoin is not as well defined in terms of, you know, what you can do in terms of, uh, shorting, etc. I'm not a lawyer. Yeah. Um, and I know a lot of Wall Street banks pay fines and they're they're a fraction of what they make on the trade. So um you know all I know is that um there's still a lot of uh games that the whales played that are being played by Wall Street now, or certainly can be.

二十二、鲸鱼 / 华尔街的 OTC + 做空游戏

That argument is — so if we see the inflows of Bitcoin spot ETF, that is what retail investors see, that doesn't equally mean that they're not shorting with derivatives. So that is one of the concerns people have.

Well look, you have a lot of invisible parts of the market in stocks as well. You have dark pools, you got OTC desks, and historically the way that the whales would sort of um make money in a bare market would be — or in any market would be — they would, you know, um buy OTC and then short on chain in a smaller exchange and the rest would automatically follow, right? Because you have very quick arbitrage between the exchanges. And then at the same time, they would have a uh a large short position in, and they would like, you know, say it's — say the price is 85,000, they say they're going to go and um you know, short it that it's going to go down to 75,000. They have enough um Bitcoin that they can cause the dump by themselves and then they buy it back at — at where they dumped it to, and they also collect money on the short.

Is that why? Okay. There are a few people that are very famous on chain for losing money, but I would assume that they're doing something else on the exchanges.

We're talking about Mr. Win. Um, you know, there's a number of people on X who have been, you know, loudly saying they made a billion, lost a billion or whatever. So, uh, I can't speak to to the veracity of their, uh, of their data. I mean, uh, there's a number of, uh, YouTubers that go on and actually show their trades, but it doesn't mean they're not doing something other than those trades. So, uh, you know, there's there's a lot — the great thing about the Bitcoin market is that you, uh, you can see a lot of what happens onchain obviously, particularly with the ETFs, you know, a lot of it is, you know, behind the the walls of uh, CoinDesk, right? I'm sorry. Coinbase. And uh you know and and also in other derivatives markets that are not showing up unchained because they're they're trading uh you know stocks. I mean look, this has been common in gold for a long time too.

二十三、如果今天重写这本书

Anything you would change in your book if you had to rewrite it today? Last question for me.

I would probably go into some more details about what could have happened at the end of uh uh this cycle because I think I probably oversimplified um you know the most likely scenario being uh um that it was going to go to about you know about like you know the midpoint with neutral macro which would be about 193. I probably should have uh put in a few more formulas like uh like the 35month, and uh I just didn't want to make it too technical.

All right. Well, encourage everyone to check out Bitcoin Super Cycle and uh check out uh Michael's work, links down below. Also, check out Bonnie's channel. Subscribe —

And yours. Thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、Sailor 的「免疫接种」，暴露了一个被精心隐藏的事实：MicroStrategy 根本不是投资公司，而是一家银行
Terpin 不经意间说出了最惊人的一句话——两年前 Sailor 私下告诉他，他之所以"永远只买不卖"，**不是因为这是最优投资策略**（Terpin 自己都承认"想拿更多币就该在顶部卖、底部买回"），而是因为"一旦做了买入以外的任何动作，华尔街买家会觉得他动摇了 thesis，就不再无条件给他写支票了"。
请把这句话品三遍。这意味着"钻石手信仰"从来不是信仰，而是一份**债券契约（bond covenant）**——是 Sailor 为了维持融资信用而必须表演的"央行可信度承诺"。而所谓的"pivot / inoculation"之所以发生，恰恰是因为融资基础从机构优先股换成了 STRC 的散户——散户拿着 11.5% 的股息，他们要的是"按时兑付"，不是意识形态纯洁性。于是 Sailor 必须装上一个"必要时可以卖币"的逃生阀。**这和一个国家"脱离金本位"的剧本一模一样：你维持可兑换的虚构，直到债主的结构变了。** 更进一步：当 Terpin 说"

item.title: [No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups] Pax Silica: Inside the Trump Administration’s Tech Strategy with Jacob Helberg
item.dc:creator: No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

最反常识的一点：那 4000 英亩"经济安全区"在法律上其实是一座大使馆。

Helberg 不经意间说漏了一个堪称精妙的"系统漏洞"：国务院本来就有把土地、房产"纳入托管"的权限——外国政府正是这样赠予美国领事馆和使馆用地的——而"赠予财产的大小没有法律上限"。于是,菲律宾这块"曼哈顿三分之一大"的工业用地,被装进了一个原本只为几栋楼设计的法律容器里。这不是新立法,而是对既有授权的一次"未公开 API 调用"。用工程师的话说,美国没有写新代码,它找到了一个 zero-day——把外交豁免权当作产权安全的载体,让"美国普通法的可预测性"以治外法权的形式被空投到东南亚。Pax Silica 表面是供应链战略,底层却是一次主权的"侧载"(sideload)。

第二个隐藏结构:政府正在把自己重新定位成一支"跟随 VC alpha 的指数基金"——而没人察觉这里埋着反身性陷阱。

注意他对风投说的话:你们更擅长识别"谁是这个领域最好的玩家",我们"把它当作重要信号"来指导政府的资本配置。这等于政府公开宣布:我的下注会跟随你们的下注。可一旦市场知道观察者会跟随信号,信号本身就被污染了——VC 会被激励去投那些"能吸引政府跟投"的项目,而不是最优项目。这是 Goodhart 定律和索罗斯式反身性在产业政策上的精确复现:当一个度量变成目标,它就不再是好的度量。整场对话里,这是唯一一个被所有人——包括两位投资人主持人——温和地忽略掉的系统性风险。

第三个反直觉的死锁:这套"再工业化"计划内部藏着一个鸡生蛋的悖论。

他亲口说,在 4% 失业率下再工业化"将不得不高度自主化"——也就是说,这本质上是一个大规模机器人部署计划。可他同样亲口承认,机器人供应链"目前被中国完全主导"。于是逻辑闭环咬住了自己的尾巴:要重建工业基础需要机器人,而造机器人的供应链恰恰在对手手里。Pax Silica 真正要解决的,不是"多元化"这种公关词,而是一个 bootstrapping(自举)难题——就像一台电脑需要操作系统才能启动,但操作系统又得先被加载。菲律宾这块地,本质上是一段"引导加载程序"(boot loader),目的是把美国从这个死锁里撬出来。

跨领域连接:把"美国是全球 underdog"当成修辞是误读——它其实是一次精密的"身份工程"。

Helberg 推翻"修昔底德陷阱"里"美国=守成霸权"的设定,坚持美国是"屡屡被专家阶层judged 会衰落的弱者"。这听起来像爱国话术,但放进系统视角看,它是必要的基础设施:整个 Pax Silica 依赖"Trump time"、依赖创始人式的风险偏好、依赖"听到 45 次 no 还继续"的执行文化才能运转。policy 需要一种 culture 才能跑起来,所以 policy 反过来去制造和营销这种 culture。这是一个自指循环——和一家公司"因为要招某类人,所以把品牌打造成能吸引那类人的样子"是同一个机制。叙事在这里不是装饰,叙事是承重墙。

最后一个"啊哈":这个名字本身就泄露了它的焦虑。

Pax Romana、Pax Britannica、Pax Americana——都是霸权强加的和平。但他们偏偏选了 Pax Silica(硅砂之治)。讽刺有两层:其一,silica 是地球上最丰沛的物质之一,而整场谈话从头到尾讲的都是稀缺、集中度风险、卡脖子;这个名字承诺"丰裕的和平",内核却是"匮乏的恐慌"。其二,他自己点破了最关键的洞见——稀土的瓶颈不在矿(silica,原料),而在精炼(refining,通往 silicon 的那一步)。可他们却用原料、而非精炼品来命名整个战略。整套体系还笼罩着一种时间脆弱性:菲律宾两年谈判窗口、"本届政府结束前"解决定价、行政令易被推翻——所以才反复强调要建"evergreen(常青)系统"、要让前沿工业基地"最终脱离政府、作为私营服务存活下去"。说白了,政府在扮演一个孵化器,拼命想在自己这轮"政治融资"耗尽之前,让手里的下注"毕业"、达到 default-alive。它对中国说的是地缘战略,对自己说的其实是:跑赢我自己的政治时钟。

📝 转录文本

Cold Open

"We're not going to do government operated supply chains, because that's not how we shine as a country. Our superpower is really our private sector and our companies. The old Steve Jobs quote, that American products enchant and delight users around the world by the billions — that is really is our edge as a country. And so the answer's been trying to work in lockstep with our private companies and our builders to build platforms >> [music] >> that are commercially viable and that will can ultimately live outside of the government as a private service."

Introduction

The General Purpose are joined by Jacob Helberg, the Under Secretary of State for Economic Affairs. We spoke with Jacob before he was confirmed in this role, and we're very excited to have him back to discuss Pax Silica, which is a multi-nation effort to secure the AI supply chain for the United States and its allies.

Jacob, thanks so much for being here.

Yeah, thanks for joining us.

Thanks for having me.

What Is Pax Silica? The Philippines Announcement

So, let's get right into it. Three months ago you announced Pax Silica, super ambitious coalition. Can you explain what it is and where you are in it?

Yeah, absolutely. So, I gave a speech at the Hudson Institute that was really meant to be our blueprint for Pax Silica. Pax Silica is an economic security coalition that now has 14 countries, and the idea is really to have an ecosystems-based approach to our supply chains, and specifically the AI supply chain.

And in my speech at Hudson I outlined our different lines of efforts, including our policy road maps and our projects. And about a week and a half ago we basically did the first big, you know, what would be the tech equivalent of a product rollout, where we announced a forward deployed industrial base with our oldest ally in Asia, the Philippines.

We made this arrangement with them where they are granting us 4,000 acres, which is obviously very substantial. It's a third of the size of Manhattan, to do a very large industrial build-out. That's one-of-a-kind, that helps combine the predictability and certainty of the American common law system with the industrial comparative advantages that the Philippines offers. And the goal is really to secure inputs that are vital for our supply chains. And so we're super excited to be here in San Francisco, talk to builders and see ways that we can actually accelerate supply chain security for our tech companies.

The "Economic Security Zone": Two Phases

Is this set up as a special economic zone in the Philippines, or could you tell us more about the details beyond sort of the legal side that you mentioned?

Yeah, absolutely. So right now there are two phases to the plan. The first phase is the State Department taking into custody the zone. We are referring to it as an economic security zone because it is a very unique type of arrangement. The State Department has authorities to take in land and property into custody — sort of how foreign governments gift the State Department counselors and consulates and embassies. It's very unique to do a gift of 4,000 acres, but fortunately there's no statutory limits on how big or small property can be. And so that's phase one. So right now it's actually diplomatic property that is effectively, you know, governed by the same laws as our embassies are.

Phase two will be the long-term development and build-out of the land. And so we have a two-year window to negotiate the details with our Filipino counterparts on the investor protections that will apply to the land, the taxation regimes, and all of the different, you know, legal safeguards that investors will be able to benefit from for the long term. And the goal is, within that two-year window, to actually have a long-term framework that will be multiple decades.

Which Domains to Invest In: Robotics

It's amazing innovation. Are there specific domains that you think makes sense to invest in there, from a manufacturing, mineral processing, whatever it is perspective?

Yeah, so the goal is really to actually test a concept that could potentially be replicable. I mean, right now, when we think about the AI supply chain, a lot of people just think of chips, but the reality is that the AI supply chain actually includes thousands of inputs — like precision reducers and server motors and rare earth magnets and actuators — and our concentration risk as a country is incredibly high for basically all of those inputs.

And so the goal is to identify key geographies that actually have industrial strengths and bring unique capabilities to the table that could actually help us move the needle meaningfully in different segments of the supply chain. So the Philippines already has a native indigenous manufacturing ecosystem that's already quite deep. It's our oldest ally in Asia. So we have a very deep values alignment with the Philippines, and so we think that the nexus between the values alignment combined with their industrial advantages actually makes for a very compelling value proposition for a lot of companies.

We're talking with a number of companies focused on the robotics supply chain. As you guys know living out here — and I'm sure you guys include robotics companies in your portfolio — the robotics supply chain... robotics is an incredibly promising industry that really is poised to change a lot of things in manufacturing as well as in people's daily lives, and the supply chain is right now completely dominated by China. And so that's an area where we're particularly interested in potentially making a bet on.

Contrast with the Belt and Road Initiative

When most people hear economic security policy, one of the first things they think of is the Belt and Road Initiative. How would you contrast your strategy and the State Department's strategy here with that approach?

Yeah, so that's such a great question. And I think one of the benefits that we've been able to leverage developing the strategy today >> [clears throat] >> is the ability to study 25 years of China's Belt and Road Initiative — how they've done what they've done, what's worked, what hasn't worked. And >> [clears throat] >> you know, fundamentally —

You want to explain what that is, too, for our audience?

Yeah, so the Belt and Road Initiative is a very, very large foreign policy project of the Chinese government that basically involved using state-owned enterprises that are essentially extensions of the government to carry out massive infrastructure projects overseas. And the idea is using infrastructure projects as a tool of foreign policy to achieve foreign policy outcomes.

Was the big build-outs, for example, in Africa that —

Exactly.

— did it in a variety of countries there.

That's right. So whether it's creating mines or processing facilities, a lot of the times it involved building out very big roads, sometimes bridges, a lot of the times it included railways. And fundamentally what it was, was state-owned enterprises building government-operated railways, government-built roads and bridges. And the Chinese government, by virtue of its system, really built all of this in-house. Which for us, when we think about how we want to secure supply chains, we need to make a lot of investments that touch upon these kinds of industrial capabilities — but as Americans, we're not going to do this in-house inside of the government.

Effects of this for China was basically both building out their industrial base, but then also securing natural resources in some of these countries.

Exactly. They were able to build a network that ensured that their factories in China had all the inputs in order to actually really thrive. And Shenzhen today is really the world's factory floor, because they have deep relationships with suppliers and vendors on every continent, basically. And the infrastructure that they leverage allows them to get access in a way that's very, very competitive.

So the question for us is, how do we compete in that landscape at a time when we want to re-industrialize? And the answer is: we're not going to do government-operated supply chains, because that's not how we shine as a country. And where we've landed is, our superpower is really our private sector and our companies. And it's sort of the old Steve Jobs quote, that American products enchant and delight users around the world by the billions, and that's how... that is really is our edge as a country.

And so, how do we adopt a product-based, a product-centric approach to our foreign policy, where we can actually use that kind of approach to achieve foreign policy outcomes? And that's very much the lens of how we've decided to focus our economic security strategy. The answer's been trying >> [clears throat] >> to work in lockstep with our private companies and our builders to build platforms that are commercially viable and that ultimately can live outside of the government as a private service.

And so the forward-deployed industrial base is meant to be a platform for private investment that will be viable for the long term. It's the first of its kind. We're thinking about making a big play in the logistics space, in partnership with large corporates, and in June we're looking at potentially doing a broader rollout where we're going to roll out four or five different big lines of effort. You guys are both invited to Washington for it. And so, having a product-based approach is very much how we're thinking about it.

Where Belt and Road Failed: The Debt Trap

As somebody who's done a deep study of the pros and cons of Belt and Road, there's obviously access to infrastructure and inputs as some dimension of success here. What's been the failure point for Belt and Road?

Well, the failure point is — so first of all, obviously >> [clears throat] >> some of the projects have been useful for China. There is a lot of waste in the Belt and Road Initiative, and mainly it's because whenever you have central planning and really government bureaucrats effectively allocating large pools of capital, there's a lot of waste because vendors massively overcharge things. There's a lot of waste because there's a lot of roads to nowhere.

And what happens is, China will basically deploy capital to its own companies that then basically write IOUs to the host country. And the host country realizes that these projects have gained a reputation for being debt traps — because China will say, we'll build a road as a loan, except the company building the road is Chinese, and so China can basically decide what the price is. And as you guys know, when you build a house or when you do any construction project, a lot of projects run over budget a lot. And so when a host country thinks it's taking on X >> [clears throat] >> million dollars in liabilities and it actually ends up being 10X — that's when they kind of end up in quicksand.

And so the Belt and Road Initiative has really garnered a reputation for really being a tool of political leverage that a lot of countries are still digging themselves out of.

And so versus one of partnership.

Exactly.

Versus one where it's positive sum.

I think a lot of countries don't feel like they have enough upside in it. It is Chinese workers, Chinese companies, and a lot of the times it's Chinese equity — because the debt converts to equity if the host country defaults. They do a lot of the times. And so we're approaching it totally differently. And because we're approaching it by putting our companies in the driver's seat, it's actually, in a lot of ways, much more ethical — because the deal is structured in a way that's really meant to be a true joint venture. And one that will be optimized for commercial viability, not just for political purposes.

The Value Proposition for the 14 Countries

When you think about the 14 countries that are already part of Pax Silica, or this as a potential blueprint for others to be involved in American forward-deployed industrial bases — what's the value prop for them?

So the value prop is — one of the amazing things that we're seeing is, obviously the AI revolution is leading to huge growth. I mean, despite the volatility in the energy markets, the American economy has been proven incredibly resilient. And a big part of that is AI being this incredibly strong economic force that is already fueling over a third of our economic GDP growth right here in the US.

Overseas, we're seeing that growth translate to record demands for copper, record demands for cobalt, record demands for lots of different inputs that go into data centers, and record demand for electricians and all the rest. And so the takeaway for a lot of these countries is: if they find ways of actually having a bigger part and a bigger stake in that supply chain, at different layers that make sense for their companies and their economy, they can actually derive a lot of economic growth from that revolution.

Because the amazing thing about the tech industry, especially when we go through these inflection points, as you guys know, is the pie grows really fast. And so it's really not zero-sum, which actually makes it incredibly conducive to forge very mutually beneficial partnerships — because we're not approaching it as "what I gain, someone else loses." It's actually because the pie is growing, we're partnering together. And this is very much part of the ethos of how we're partnering with the Philippines. We are developing a partnership where we both have skin in the game and we both share in the upside of success. And so risk is evenly allocated, and so is upside. And so ultimately, it's very much a win-win proposition.

What Stays in the US vs. What Goes to Partners

How do you think about the areas where you want to partner with other countries versus have things happen in the US? I know, for example, there's been a long-standing effort to bring back fabs and the ability to manufacture semiconductors at sort of state-of-the-art linewidths and everything else in the US. That hasn't quite happened yet. I know there's active efforts to do that. What do you think is important to do in the US itself versus to partner with other countries, and how do you figure out what goes where?

Yeah, so that is such a great question. And I would answer that question a few different parts, but the first part is maybe starting with some stats. America accounts for somewhere in the neighborhood between 20 and 30% of global consumption on any given quarter. It's obviously huge, because we're 4% of the world's population, but we're basically a fourth to a third of the world's total consumption.

Or other things like that?

Other things than Celsius, or >> [laughter] >> no-name companies, you know. We consume a lot of stuff. I mean, Americans like to consume. And we're a risk-taking culture, we're a high-consumption society. And our production levels are not 20 or 30%. We produce a lot less than that.

And so part of what we're seeing is: if we narrow the gap between what we consume and what we produce, we will reindustrialize America — because that will be a massive reindustrialization plan that will inevitably include a lot of semi-autonomy or full autonomy. Because if we industrialize an economy where unemployment's already at 4%, it will have to be very, very autonomous, which is possible. Singapore has proven that that's possible. They have highly autonomous ports, factories. And so that's number one.

Then there's the question of what happens with the other 70% of the world's consumption that is currently mostly concentrated in China. And so the idea is, if we want to have a supply chain system where America and its allies in the global economy has access to suppliers that are more evenly distributed, that are reliable, trustworthy, transparent — I think a lot of countries actually really see that as a business opportunity for them to also be able to have more production in their respective regions.

And so part of the idea for the forward-deployed industrial base and these economic security zones is actually to have a hub-based approach, where we can leverage the industrial attributes. So, for example, if regions have a lot of rare earth minerals, or, for example, in the case of the Philippines, a very deep indigenous manufacturing ecosystem — things that they're really good at, or have unique advantages in — we can leverage that to really help capitalize on that and make sure that they can actually contribute to the supply chains for areas that they're really good in. And ultimately help foster regional hubs — because, you know, the population of Africa, for example, is growing massively. South America is also growing very, very quickly, places like Brazil, Argentina.

And so I think having a hub-based approach is very promising. And ultimately, America remains the economic engine of the global economy. And so I think the parts that we re-industrialize here will definitely be the biggest driving forces.

And you're totally right — the effort to bring back semiconductor fab production here is already well underway. And so that will continue. It's already in flight, and there's a whole host of reasons why it wouldn't necessarily make sense to try to replicate that elsewhere, because it's highly technical. The global supply of talent available in that area is very finite and limited. And it's also extremely capital intensive. So it wouldn't make sense to replicate that elsewhere before it's finished here. But, >> [clears throat] >> the unfortunate and good news is, the supply chain is vast, and there's a lot of things that still need to be built. And so the world, I think, is actually really, really ripe with opportunity in that sense.

Rare Earths and Other Subsidized Inputs

And how do you think about other inputs into what you're talking about? So, for example, you mentioned rare earth magnets. And my sense — and I haven't verified this, so please correct me if it's wrong — is that A) rare earths are not actually rare; B) the total market size is in the few billion dollars a year; and C) that market is heavily subsidized by China in order to basically control supply. It's sort of a lever from like a political basis. And so are there considerations around other mechanisms that the government can use? I know there have been, but I'd love to hear how you all are thinking about other aspects of the supply chain that may be raw materials, that may be heavily subsidized or propped up by foreign governments, etc., and how we should address those areas in addition.

Absolutely. And so you're exactly right. The really interesting thing about rare earths is, obviously as you point out, they're not that rare. I think some people have said that they're everywhere — it's not totally true either. I mean, they're not everywhere, but they're in lots of different places. The sort of key thing that really drives the economics of those industries is how much energy do you need to pump into the ground in order to extract a given mineral at a given quality grade. And then what's actually really rare is the refining process — because the number of processing facilities for these minerals is very, very limited outside of China. They exist, but in very limited quantities. And you're right, China obviously subsidizes the hell out of them.

And so it's very exciting to be able to share with you that the Trump administration's actually really had a very, very comprehensive approach. I mean, we've been really focused on the mineral security issue pretty much since day one of the administration. We did the largest critical minerals summit in the history of the State Department in February — on February 4th — with over 55 countries that participated. We signed critical mineral MOU deals with dozens of countries who have lots of different kinds of minerals.

And now one of the things that we're doing, and have aggressively pursued across the administration, is allocating capital and investments into a lot of these projects to make sure that they have access to the resources to expand production. So that's on the supply side.

On the demand side, the administration is hard at work at negotiating deals with countries to address the pricing issue. Because as you guys know from the businesses that you invest in, you can invest a lot of money in a startup, but ultimately a lot of the economics of whether a company can be viable is really based on the price that that company is able to provide a service for. And so the pricing mechanism is really, really central to unlocking long-term commercial viability. And I'm incredibly confident that we actually will resolve the pricing issue for the minerals market before the end of this administration.

The Role of Private Capital and Venture Capital

You mentioned that you are directly investing in domains of importance where you're trying to trigger better supply capability with us and allies. How do you think of the role of — asking for a friend — private capital in these initiatives?

Yeah, so I think you guys have such an important role to play, mainly because >> [laughter] >> you know, so much of whether a project materializes or not, as you >> [clears throat] >> guys know, it hinges on execution capacity. Can a company really execute on a very aggressive, ambitious plan?

And one of the amazing superpowers of the venture capital business is, you guys are kind of hardwired to be able to assess a lot of the personality attributes of founders and operators — to tell some of the things that are harder to read from a deck or spreadsheet on, does this person really have what it takes to be able to execute, and address execution risk, underlying scientific risk, all the different risks that inevitably exist in any company.

And so we actually would really love for the venture capital ecosystem to help be part of the solution, because I think you guys are better positioned to assess who are the best players in the space. And we can take that as an important signal to help inform capital allocations that we make on the government side, to make sure that we allocate money in the right places and in the most efficient way possible — especially, you know, as good stewards of taxpayer money.

The other thing is, I think beyond assessing who the best players are, on the innovation side: there's a number of really great companies, especially out here in the Bay Area, who are working on new materials and rare earth free magnets and lots of other kinds of innovations. And I think that's where we have a lot of room and potential for potentially coming out with a "rabbit out of a hat" situation, where we somehow end up solving this issue in a way that was completely unanticipated through innovation. And that will ultimately be a solution that will really be born out of the tech industry, not the government. And so those are things that we obviously want to be attuned to and help be supportive in any way we can.

Prioritizing Near-Term vs. Long-Term

When you think about — you mentioned limited bandwidth, of course, and economic security in the AI supply chain being an enormous topic — how do you think about prioritizing '27, '28 versus five years out? Because this is something we think about as well, where, you know, if you look at next-gen lithography and robotics and let's say new semi designs, there are some longer-term plays that are more capital intensive, and so we balance our views on that and nuclear and such with software that is going to ship this year.

Uh-huh. Yeah, so I think one of the approaches that the administration's taken — that hopefully will really be the foundation for a lot of short-term change as well as just provide long-term support — is helping shape the environment, creating a macro environment that basically makes innovation, iteration of innovations, as well as deployment of innovations a lot easier and less expensive.

So the administration's approach to expanding our domestic energy supply is obviously part of that — expanding nuclear. The administration's really undertaken a lot of efforts to basically cut red tape, accelerate the deployment of American nuclear energy. And from memory, >> [clears throat] >> it was actually one of the first executive orders that the president signed, where they said that they wanted to quadruple our domestic nuclear supply, as well as a lot of actions to deregulate. The One Big Beautiful Bill included a lot of tax incentives.

And through the State Department, we're looking to help play a constructive part in shaping that environment by creating these evergreen systems — basically like the forward-deployed industrial base — that will be a long-term platform. And hopefully a replicable one that we could pursue in other geographies. And the idea is that, for the long term, our technology companies will have these platforms to operate in that will really be a competitive strategic edge that they can use to build very, very quickly and actually get to market faster.

Making It Stick Across Administrations

How do you think about, in general, transitioning through administrations? So, you mentioned a number of executive orders. Typically when an administration shifts, you end up with a lot of those executive orders undone in both directions, right? Somebody comes in and they kind of cancel a bunch of executive orders that were passed or signed by the prior administration, and they write a bunch of other ones that may be at odds with them. And so often the key is legislation or other approaches. How do you view making this stick, if you think it's so important for our country's future?

Well, there are some things that I think will be hard to undo. I mean, tax reform is very sticky. And so — one of the interesting attributes about my role at the State Department is, we're actually not allowed to comment on electoral politics.

Yeah.

So it's kind of this interesting, you know —

Now, so I guess ignoring —

— statutory limitation that we're subject to.

What Business People and Entrepreneurs Should Know

When you — we have the audience of a lot of folks in the AI ecosystem, but also a lot of folks in the general business and investing ecosystem, of companies of different scale. What do you think American business people and entrepreneurs should know about these policies, in terms of how they affect them?

The goal of Pax Silica is, we want to use it as a platform to expand market access for our companies. Because even amongst our allies, sometimes our companies face a lot of hurdles with exporting their products and services. So we want to hear feedback from them on what's working and what's not working in places like Japan, South Korea, India, and things >> [clears throat] >> that we could do better in the policy-making space, to make sure that we can actually provide some support for our companies to be more competitive in those markets.

We also want to hear — it's been great to already get a lot of feedback from a number of different companies on partnerships that are already in flight and opportunities that they see on the table on the supply chain security side. So there's a lot of cross-pollination and partnerships that are already being forged between American companies and companies in India, in Japan, in South Korea, even in Singapore. And so understanding those partnerships helps us really understand how executives are thinking about their supply chain strategy.

Everyone uniformly is obviously thinking about ways of being much more deliberate about their supply chain decisions, in ways that factor in the realities of the need to not be overexposed to China because of the political dynamics at work. And so understanding where they see opportunities for what segments of their supply chain helps us get a better understanding of what's working, and what can we help grow as quickly as possible at scale. And so that part is very useful.

So I would say: expanded market access, strategic partnership opportunities on the supply chain side, and then we also want to make sure that we actually create a space where we can talk about important intellectual property conversations. You guys are obviously very involved in the AI industry, and so I'm sure you've been spending a lot of time thinking about the whole model distillation debate, which is super important to actually protect the economic value of these hundreds of billions of dollars in investments in AI companies. And so that remains a really important unresolved area of policy, where I think getting the input from the builders who are the closest to the development of this technology is super important.

The Biggest Surprise at the State Department

When we last spoke to you on No Priors, you were just beginning your tenure at the State Department. What is the biggest surprise been? You've been very active since you started.

Well, the biggest surprise is honestly how entrepreneurial the Trump administration has been. Because I think the stereotype for people who work outside of the government usually really focuses on this idea that the government is super bureaucratic. And obviously — that's not to say... the government is a massive enterprise. It's lots and lots of people involved, lots of processes.

But the really extraordinary thing is, we have a president who also spent most of his life in the private sector, and who really likes speed. I mean, the running joke, especially with our current counterparts overseas that deal with the Trump administration, is that we like to move in "Trump time" — because the president, when he likes something, he wants it yesterday. And so that part's really been amazing, because the appetite to try new things, and the appetite for risk, and to move really, really fast is highly unusual by government standards, and really speaks to the philosophy that President Trump brings to bear in the Oval Office. And so that part's really been extraordinary.

That, combined with the really great leadership that we've been able to benefit from at the cabinet level — from Secretary Bessent, Secretary Lutnick, Secretary Rubio, Secretary Burgum — it's an incredibly collegial team. And they really operate with a level of professionalism that's been incredibly inspiring for people like me in the sub-cabinet to be able to benefit from, and also work very constructively with our bureaus.

America as a "Global Underdog"

One thing that you've said recently surprised me, just in phrasing: you referred to America as a global underdog, I believe. That's not the way I think of America. Can you explain yourself?

Yeah, so I think it's interesting, because the US >> [clears throat] >> often thinks of itself as the navy, and, you know — versus a pirate.

Versus a pirate.

And so, Allison Graham wrote this book years ago >> [clears throat] >> called the Thucydides Trap, where he kind of characterized America as the established power, and he characterized China as the rising power. And I actually think one of the really big flaws in that whole line of thinking is — >> [clears throat] >> I really wouldn't say we're an established power. Because I think for most of our history — one of the things that really makes America quite unique and very different is, from >> [clears throat] >> the earliest days of our founding, we've always been a nation of underdogs.

We started out as 13 disorganized, unruly colonies rebelling against an empire of polite society, and the educated expert class, who said that our project as a republic would never succeed. And if there really is one through line across our history, it's that almost any decade in the history of our country, there have been a class >> [clears throat] >> of experts that predicted our decline, or that we've reached the limits of our power — whether it was the great financial crash, whether it was the Iraq war, the first and second oil crisis, the Vietnam war, you name it.

And the amazing thing is, Americans perform really, really well when we feel like our backs are against the wall and it's crunch time. I mean, I'm sure you'll remember during COVID, there were these very dramatic Atlantic magazine covers about the great unraveling of America. And against the odds of everyone in the mainstream press, we came out with a vaccine in under a year that actually performed much, much better than every other alternative out there, and came out of COVID much earlier. China, obviously, was bogged down in its zero-COVID policy, the effects of which it's still feeling today.

And so the reason I say this is because I think, culturally, it's something that makes America very, very unique. And I think it's something that really hits close to home for Silicon Valley, because Silicon Valley embodies the quintessence of the underdog mentality. Every founder —

Soul.

Yeah, every founder started out as having a contrarian idea that was kind of seen as heretical by the expert class — the sort of polished Harvard professors of the world that think that they sort of have these well-founded sciences. And you have people who, some of them are dropouts, they dare >> [clears throat] >> to question old orthodoxies, and they hear "no" 45 times before getting to a "yes," and they keep going. And so it really is part of the DNA of what makes a founder a really great founder.

And I think it's that kind of spirit, that new frontier mentality, that makes Silicon Valley unique — but that has really characterized America as a nation, that's allowed us to always bounce back whenever we've experienced a setback. And so it makes me really optimistic about the future of our country.

And I think my message to founders, executives in Silicon Valley, as well as our partners overseas, is: I think that is the kind of spirit we want and are looking for when we want to partner with people. And it's also the kind of ethos that people should expect from us — when they ask themselves, what can I expect from Americans versus executives >> [clears throat] >> from another country, say China. And I think the answer is, with America a lot of the times it's very positive sum, because our companies already work on positive sum partnerships all around the world. And it will involve a lot of tenacity and creative thinking. And that's how American founders work.

Closing

Thanks so much for joining us today.

No, no. Thank you. Thanks.

Thanks for doing this follow-up.

Find us on Twitter at No Priors Pod. >> [music] >> Subscribe to our YouTube channel if you want to see our faces. Follow the show on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or wherever you listen. That way you get a new episode every week. And sign up for emails or find transcripts for every episode at no-priors.com.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
**最反常识的一点：那 4000 英亩"经济安全区"在法律上其实是一座大使馆。**
Helberg 不经意间说漏了一个堪称精妙的"系统漏洞"：国务院本来就有把土地、房产"纳入托管"的权限——外国政府正是这样赠予美国领事馆和使馆用地的——而"赠予财产的大小没有法律上限"。于是,菲律宾这块"曼哈顿三分之一大"的工业用地,被装进了一个原本只为几栋楼设计的法律容器里。这不是新立法,而是对既有授权的一次"未公开 API 调用"。用工程师的话说,美国没有写新代码,它找到了一个 zero-day——把外交豁免权当作产权安全的载体,让"美国普通法的可预测性"以治外法权的形式被空投到东南亚。Pax Silica 表面是供应链战略,底层却是一次主权的"侧载"(sideload)。
**第二个隐藏结构:政府正在把自己重新定位成一支"跟随 VC alpha 的指数基金"——而没人察觉这里埋着反身性陷阱。**
注意他对风投说的话:你们更擅长识别"谁是这个领域最好的玩家",我们"把它当作重要信号"来指导政府的资本配置。这等于政府公开宣布:我的下注会跟随你们的下注。可一旦市场知道观察者会跟随信号,信号本身就被

item.title: [Lex Clips] Future of holograms, brain signal playback, smell, and touch in multimedia software | Lex Fridman
item.dc:creator: Lex Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

FFmpeg 会活下去，VLC「也许」——这个差别比它看起来更残酷

注意他回答时的那个微妙裂缝：FFmpeg 是斩钉截铁的「yes」，VLC 却是犹豫的「maybe」。这不是谦虚，这是一个系统性真相的泄露——没有界面的东西，比有界面的东西更长寿。FFmpeg 是一套语法（library / 编解码的底层规则），VLC 是一张脸（application / 用户看得见的入口）。脸会过时，语法不会。这和拉丁语的命运惊人地一致：罗马帝国（界面）崩塌了，但拉丁语法（基础设施）渗进了所有欧洲语言活到了今天。他其实是在说：想造百年之物，就别造让人能看见你的东西。

他们不是在「扩展功能」，而是在悄悄重新定义「人」

表面上这是一段关于「VLC 要不要支持气味、触觉、脑电波」的技术讨论。但真正发生的事情是一次本体论政变。当他说「multimedia is a digital representation of several streams for the human senses」、当他说 demuxer 会多一个「odor track」、会有「left and right nose track」——他做的不是加功能，而是把人的整个感官系统重新定义成了一堆「带时间戳、可被采样的流」。这里有个让人意外的反转：FFmpeg 进军「立体气味」根本不算「扩张」，因为你的鼻子本来就是双声道的、你的身体本来就是一台多轨设备——是格式落后于解剖学，现在只是格式终于追上了身体。而最深的一层在更里面：他说「codecs to compress neural information」——大脑本身就是一套压缩算法，它一直在做有损编码。所谓脑机接口的 codec，不是发明新东西，是逆向工程一套已经运行了几十万年的私有格式。

真正的产品不是软件，是「时间」——FFmpeg 偷偷是一台钟

这是我想让你停一下的地方。他给多媒体下的定义里，藏着一个被所有人忽略的词：「everything that is timed」。VLC/FFmpeg 处理的从来不是「视频」或「音频」，而是同步——让画面、声音、震动、未来的气味和脑波在同一根时间轴上对齐。所以当 Neuralink 真的来了，难题根本不是「读取大脑」，而是「让大脑数据和别的东西对上拍子」。从这个角度看，FFmpeg 本质上是一台钟，一个人类经验的时序层。这也解释了为什么是「maintainer 而非 contributor」这个区分如此关键——他们守的不是代码，是架构的节拍器。

标准化是「老二老三的复仇」，而 Dolby 是它的反面教材

他描述的标准化曲线——新领域先冒出五个标准、热度退潮、然后「followers, usually not the leader」做出统一标准、最后逼得 leader 就范——表面像 Gartner 炒作周期，但他点破了一个被掩盖的权力结构：标准化从来不是技术演进，而是行业老二老三对垄断者的政治起义。开放标准是弱者的联合工会。而 Dolby 正是这枚硬币的反面：一家曾经「定义了声音」的工程公司，退化成「mostly lawyers and licensing」。这里有个跨领域的连接——把它放进生物学看：开源维护者和专利授权方，其实是靠同一种养分（标准）存活的两种代谢策略：一种靠扩散进食，一种靠设卡收租。他把 Dolby 类比 HP 不是随口吐槽，而是在指认一种系统性的衰老路径——当一家公司开始靠「定义」收租、而不是靠「定义」继续创造，它就从器官变成了肿瘤。 而 FFmpeg 选择的，是反向的那条路：把自己变成谁都能用、谁都收不了租的空气。

📝 转录文本

100 Years From Now: FFmpeg, Yes; VLC, Maybe

Do you think VLC and FFmpeg will be here 100 years from now?

FFmpeg, yes. Yep, FFmpeg, yes. VLC, maybe.

Where Is It All Going?

What's the future of — what, where is — where is FFmpeg going? Where is VLC going? Like, in the next — if you think about like 5 years, 10 years, 20 years?

5 years, 10 years is easy. The question is after that, right? Um, the question is: do we arrive at something called holograms, right?

Yeah, so will VLC and FFmpeg expand to whatever — yes — multimedia? Yes.

From Holograms to Brain-Computer Interfaces

The multimedia might become — sorry for the pothead expansion of topic, but you know — um, if you look at something like Neuralink with brain-computer interfaces, it's very possible that we start to consume — what multimedia means is whatever codec, whatever data that our brain wants to consume through the brain-computer interfaces. That's one. Then virtual reality, of course.

You will have VLC for Neuralink. Yeah. And you'll have — you'll have FFmpeg, input format: human brain. Yeah, there's going to be codecs for the brain.

Sure, 100%. Of course. Yeah, to compress neural information.

Codecs Beyond Audio and Video

Today there is, like — there are new codecs for, for example, what we call point cloud, right? Or volumetric videos, right? There is a ton of research on what we call RGBD, right? So, codecs for depth that is useful for robotics and for 3D things. There is a ton of codecs for compression of 3D elements. Compression for astronomy.

Uh, for example, on VLC we also have already a VR and XR version of VLC. And also on Kaiba — right, we talk about Kaiba. On Kaiba we also would like to do streaming of XR content for the glasses who cannot have enough power, or inside the Apple Vision or the Quest. Um, so we already work on streaming 3D XR interactive low latency.

There is something called volumetric video, point cloud videos. So it's not stopping. And yes, at some point we will manage 3D data inside VLC and FFmpeg, right? It's obvious. So that's where it is moving. Like, the community is open. Not everyone in the community sees that, but like, as Kihan and I — we are, we are entrepreneurs, we know where it's going. We see that, right?

What Is Multimedia, Really?

So I — I suppose that there is a tension probably inside FFmpeg, is like: "Hey, listen, folks, we're really good at doing video and audio. So, like, why expand? Like, let's do the thing we're really good at doing."

In order to answer that question, we need to answer the definition of what is multimedia.

Yeah.

And multimedia is a digital representation of several streams for the human senses. And we will do that, right? So imagine there is now a way to not have a mic, but have an odor sensor and a diffuser of odors. Mhm. It will get into FFmpeg.

So your demuxers — yes. Yeah. Yes. Of course, your demuxer has a new track type that is basically odors, right? And you already have smell, touch, audio — you'll have a left and right nose track, you'll have a left and right audio pair. It's easy. Yes, of course. The stereo smell. Stereo smell, yeah.

Haptics and 4D Cinema

So in VLC, for example, we already have a plugin for haptic. It's mostly for the what we call 4D cinemas, right? You know those ones on hydraulic — I don't know how you say that. It's all the hydraulic arms — hydraulic arms. Anyway, everything is moving. Like, you have it in theme parks, right? And there is a data feed synchronized, which is basically transporting this information. There is a standard for that. There are many standards, right?

This is — you make me so happy.

And so of course, like, we have a plugin which is not in the normal version of VLC, that is basically transporting those types of movements — which is physical movements, which is haptic movements, right? It is a human sense, so it will get in.

That's such an exciting feature. What was it — I mean, it's a small community of developers. How do you pull that off? Like, if you're a contributor to FFmpeg or VLC, it feels stressful. Like, it — it's just looking at Twitter, it's like — it's a huge amount of work to make it work on all these different operating systems. It's an incredible effort.

Maintainers, Not Contributors

No, see it in the other way direction. We are not the contributors, we are the maintainers. Right? So we maintain for everyone, meaning that, for example, every year there is around 150 people who contribute to VLC and maybe 300 on FFmpeg, right? Our goal at this small team is to get all the contribution in. So if there is more usage, there will be more contributions, and those people will do the right modules, the new formats and so on.

We care about the architecture of VLC, the architecture of FFmpeg, right? Now we're doing things in VLC which is a spatial audio, right? We did the demo not not long ago. There was n- changes needed on the architecture, and we did the first spatial audio module. When it's going to add the second one, it's going to be easy. Or the third one, it's going to be easy, right?

Our goal — and it's going to be the same for odors or haptic, right? We need to work the architecture so that modules can be added to add future capabilities. So yes, we are — we are multimedia frameworks, so that's not just audio and video. It's everything that is timed and represents something that you can sense. And if it's brain waves, it's going to be brain waves. I think that's inevitable, sorry.

Pushing the World to Standardize

I lost us so many funds because — so FFmpeg and VLC are pushing companies and pushing the world to standardize. So, for example, to standardize brain waves. Whereas — to standardize it, it would push — like, I hope Neuralink comes up with a standard for multimedia via brain-computer interfaces, or for robots with haptic.

By experience, what happens is always the same, right? You start — it's a new topic that is like five different standards, because everyone starts to do this. The hype goes down — because every time the hype goes down, then people start to say, "Well, you know what? We need to do a standard." People — because two or three companies, usually not the leader, but the two or three followers, do a standard. And then we implement the standard, and then it's the end of the curve, it starts to be more popular. And then the leader is kind of pressured into it, because it is better to do a standard.

Example: 3D audio, right? Six or seven years ago it was everything about 3D. You had the cardboard on Android, you had — you had two audio formats. They all dead, right? And now it's coming back with actual use cases, and we learn from the mistakes of the past standard. So it will be the same everywhere. And not try to avoid closed —

On Dolby

I saw somewhere you didn't have too many nice things to say about Dolby.

No, I don't.

What is — can you educate me on why — where they went, what they do bad that made you mad?

It used to be an amazing company doing tons of great things with amazing engineers. They defined what sound was. Mhm. And now it's mostly lawyers and licensing things.

Oh, so they're — yeah, so they're closing stuff off. They're trying to make money off of licensing.

Innovate — as much as they did, and so on. It's a bit like — I'm sorry to say, right? Like HP, right?

[laughter]

Not true.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## FFmpeg 会活下去，VLC「也许」——这个差别比它看起来更残酷
注意他回答时的那个微妙裂缝：FFmpeg 是斩钉截铁的「yes」，VLC 却是犹豫的「maybe」。这不是谦虚，这是一个系统性真相的泄露——**没有界面的东西，比有界面的东西更长寿**。FFmpeg 是一套语法（library / 编解码的底层规则），VLC 是一张脸（application / 用户看得见的入口）。脸会过时，语法不会。这和拉丁语的命运惊人地一致：罗马帝国（界面）崩塌了，但拉丁语法（基础设施）渗进了所有欧洲语言活到了今天。他其实是在说：**想造百年之物，就别造让人能看见你的东西。**
## 他们不是在「扩展功能」，而是在悄悄重新定义「人」
表面上这是一段关于「VLC 要不要支持气味、触觉、脑电波」的技术讨论。但真正发生的事情是一次本体论政变。当他说「multimedia is a digital representation of several streams for the human senses」、当他说 demuxer 会多一个「odor track」、会有「left and ri

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Is the AI Rally a Bubble? Ram, Chris, and Austin Disagree
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最大的反常识：这次"泡沫"长在了私募市场，而不是公开市场

大多数人盯着英伟达、Micron 的 K 线惊呼"2000 年又来了"，但视频里 Ram 抛出的真正颠覆性观察是——63 家 AI 独角兽实验室，零美元收入；而公开市场的买家其实在用"远期市盈率 8 倍的 Micron"做风控。这意味着传统的"散户疯狂—机构理性"叙事被彻底反转：这一轮是私募 LP 在为公共物品（基础模型）做慈善式补贴，公开市场反而成了纪律的最后堡垒。

跨领域的连接：这其实是 17 世纪荷兰东印度公司模式的反向再演——当年是公众散户为远洋探险承担长尾风险，今天是顶级 VC 为人类的"认知大航海"承担风险，而散户通过买"卖铲人"（半导体）反而获得了最确定的现金流。风险资本和公共资本的角色，第一次在历史上彻底对调。

二、被忽略的隐藏结构：资本不是流入 AI，而是流入了"Capex 接收方"

Chris 用了一个极其精炼的二分法——Capex Payer vs Capex Receiver——但他没有展开的隐藏含义是：现在的市场本质上是一台"现金流转译机"，把超大规模平台公司过去十年积累的垄断利润，强制转化为半导体复合体的资本开支。这不是泡沫，这是一次史无前例的、由私人企业主导的"准国家级产业政策"。

跨领域连接：这与 1950 年代美国冷战时期国防部把军费转译给德州仪器、Fairchild 几乎是同构的——只不过这一次的"五角大楼"叫做 Microsoft、Google、Meta，"冷战对手"叫做中国。当你意识到企业 Capex 已经在扮演冷战级国家投资的角色时，"S&P 中半导体占 18%"就不再是泡沫指标，而是军工复合体 GDP 占比的现代翻译。

三、最容易被忽略的"时间炸弹"：监管不是风险，监管是巨头的护城河期权

Chris 看似随口提的一句话其实是整段最锋利的洞察——"一旦监管来临，大者恒大将被锁死"。市场目前在为"无监管创新"定价，但真正聪明的资本应该在为"未来某届政府落下监管闸门的那一刻"定价。这意味着：今天估值最贵的不应是模型公司，而是那些在监管落下前已经把分发渠道、企业权限体系、数据合规框架搭好的公司——也就是 Ram 提到的微软 agentic 框架、埃森哲式的"启用层"。

一个真正出人意料的推论：Claude 当前在模型上的领先反而可能是它最大的脆弱点。因为先发的模型公司通常会被监管"按住头"做合规模板（参考 Netscape vs IE，AOL vs MSN），而后来者带着分发优势在合规明确后吃下市场。在 AI 时代，"领先"和"幸存"是两条几乎正交的曲线——这是几乎没人在公开讨论的非对称真相。

一个跨域彩蛋

视频后半段那段 Coinbase 广告（用篮球教练 Lethal Shooter 比赛预测 5 个比特币），其实和主题形成了一个奇妙的镜像：它把"预测的不确定性"游戏化、零售化、Bitcoin 化——这恰好就是 AI 资本周期的微缩模型：少数人用纪律和信息优势收割多数人的 FOMO。当投资节目的广告本身都在演示同一个结构时，你就知道叙事已经渗透到流通介质本身了——这才是真正值得警觉的"系统信号"。

📝 转录文本

一、AI 是解释整个市场的唯一变量

It's just AI. The entire market is explained by AI. AI picks and shovels, AI infrastructure. Names like Micron and SanDisk and CN and Lumentum and industrials linked to it. That's it.

It's been extraordinary. The movement you've seen — like Micron, which is doing a 10x in public markets as a large cap security — is unprecedented. It's extraordinary. And you can make the argument that Micron is still cheap at a forward P/E of eight times. And South Korea also has been running. That's it.

It's like — you know, you've seen those movies where the tsunami's coming, all the water drains from the beach and you can walk deeper into the ocean. That's what's happening. It is really extraordinary. The last time I saw this was when Nvidia re-rated in May through July of 2024. The capital is flowing from everywhere else. Doesn't matter what else. Into this theme.

And you've got record call option activity, record 3x levered ETF activity. And yeah, it's a truly extraordinary market phenomenon that we're seeing.

二、牛市中的最大风险是"鞭打效应"与 FOMO

Overall, I mean, like, it's a bull market. That's the main takeaway. Like, what matters? Well, it's a bull market. That's the main takeaway. And it's creating opportunities elsewhere in the market, too.

The main risk you have is whipsaw risk. Right? And everyone has anxiety. Right? If you own semiconductors, you say, "Am I supposed to sell now? Because I'm up 80% in 3 months?" Or if I don't own it, "Do I buy now? Cuz nothing's working except AI."

It's max anxiety, max frustration, max FOMO, max fear. You know, the markets are an extraordinary animal here, and it has just put everyone in a vise with this semiconductor rally. Semis are now 18% of the S&P. It's never been higher as a market cap. The last time we had this record was 11% in 2014.

三、两种叙事之争：这是不是 2000 年的翻版？

Well, all right. So this leads me to two schools of thought that I've been watching.

One — I'll phrase it this way because this goes around on Twitter as a quote sometimes — is: not enough people are emotionally prepared for this not to be a bubble. Right? We'll put that on one side.

On the other side, the last time we saw price action like this concentrated in a single sector in the United States was probably 2000. And we all know how that ended.

So, Chris — Ram was talking — I'm going to throw this one to you. If you had to pick between one of those two archetypes, which one are you?

四、Chris 的回应：基本面与历史不同，但极端必至

It's not the same. You know, back back in the crisis, the bubbles were being made about things without utility. It was engineering on top of engineering. And I was there, and there was just a lot of — I guess I see more fundamentals here, and I see more fundamental innovation here.

AI is changing our civilization as we know it. It's unlocking democratization of knowledge. It's going to facilitate new innovations across every single discipline. So there's real fundamental value here. And there is an absolute arms race, and again, we're seeing the United States take the lead again, which is another reason why we're outperforming. Not only with AI — we're outperforming with energy and everything else, and it's all correlated.

But like all things in markets, you end up going to extremes at a certain point. I don't know where we are in that cycle just yet. We will go to an extreme. There will be — it will pop. Like we had the same thing with the dot-com bubble, where, "Man, this is amazing technology, it's going to change the world" — and it did. Like, look at us now. Look at us now. But near term, it did hit a bubble.

Look, I still think that — I still personally think we're on the trajectory up because the amount of investment that's going into infrastructure, and we're still coming up with new innovation after new innovation in real time. So I don't think we're through it yet.

五、Ram：这次不是泡沫——市场会学习

This is not a bubble. See a lot of people talking about this. It's also very clearly not a bubble. It's the anxiety.

We're not going to live through the dot-com era because people have lived through that era who are still managing money today. Also, markets learn. Like, the valuations in the dot-com era were truly nutty. Like, theGlobe.com went public and on the IPO day went up 600% in a day. There are companies like GeoCities that went public. The bankers that — they were taking public as stories around that, and JP Morgan…

Yeah, these are real businesses. And you don't have the dark fiber issues with the dot-com era. So you can say GPUs are the dark fiber now — that's like the analogy. The difference is: back then the dark fiber was being so over-invested by telecom and venture-backed startups. Here the primary spenders are the most profitable companies in the world, number one. Number two, increasingly governments. And those profitable companies are showing a return on that through their cloud business.

Like Google's cloud business grew 60%. Microsoft's cloud business grew 40%. So they're making money on that. There are counterparty questions around OpenAI. But Meta just assumed a contract — they stepped in and said, "Hey, we'll take that data center lease." So the demand for compute is so high that it's hard to see how the capex cycle breaks down anytime soon.

六、监管将成为整合的最大力量

Yeah, the other thing — I think we're in an interesting era now because of the lack of regulation, it allows for innovators and startups to jump in. But you're starting to hear more and more about regulation. I think that will scare a bunch of people.

And to the extent that we start seeing regulation in the future — maybe not this administration, but the next, who knows — it's going to be a huge consolidation force. With my experience, once you have a regulation, the big already-really-big are going to get even bigger, and there's going to be even more consolidation.

So I'm hopeful now that we see a couple of these startups bust through before they're absolutely put out of business through that fixed cost of: "Sorry guys, you have a new model, you have to get it approved." "Oh, but mine's already approved cuz I've been doing it for 5 years."

七、真正的泡沫在私募市场，而不在公开市场

Here's another fun one for you. I know, Chris, you're on the venture side — prior to current role, maybe doing venture now here too — but here's a fun fact for you.

There are 63 unicorn AI labs, billion-dollar valuations, like Thinking Machines, founded by the former CTO of OpenAI; Ilya's got one. None of them make a penny of revenue. So there is a bubble — it's just in private markets.

Public market investors have exercised far more discipline. And yes, in public markets there are issues too — like Caterpillar's not worth 35 times earnings rate. But it's private markets — that's where the bubble is.

The other fun fact for you is that the number of these LLMs that are in Europe is zero. It's zero. I'm trying to ask: which is the scarier fact? That we have 63 unicorns that don't make any money, not a penny of revenue? Or that Europe has none?

I'd rather live in the former. I'd rather have the innovation borne by venture capital investors that are subsidizing the public good for everybody else. Hopefully they'll make money on that. I doubt it. Amazon didn't make money till it did, either. Right?

八、价值创造 vs 价值归属：两个分开的问题

And well, another theme from 2000 though — and actually one we've talked about with blockchains before too — is that the value may be large, but are we attributing value accrual to the right people?

Because, Ram, to your point, OpenAI may be stepping away from contracts. We may have a number of model companies that are currently unicorns that don't have clear pathways to revenue, but that doesn't mean they're not creating value. It may just be that they're not creating value for their shareholders.

They're clearly creating value for Nvidia, for Intel, for Micron, for SanDisk. And they may also be creating value for the people who are going to distribute those things, like the Apples of the world. They're creating value for Google and Microsoft in their cloud businesses.

So, to me, I think you've got to ask two separate questions investing in this market.

  • Question one: where is the value creation overall? Is AI writ large a bubble? Do we have the right number in aggregate?
  • Question two: then have we allocated this correctly? Like, is it in the right companies?

And I will tell you, I am much more skeptical of number two than I am of number one — if that makes sense.

九、Capex Payer vs Capex Receiver：资本流动的真正分界线

Yeah, the capital's going to capex receivers, as in, capex payers and their capex. So that's the dividing line. The capex payers — these cash-flush companies and governments — and the capex receivers — they're the semiconductor complex. So they're the winners, but that'll shift too.

Like, you know, it's 18% of the S&P now. Usually when you start to see headlines like that, it's time to look in other pastures. You know, when technology's share of the S&P hit its level in 2021 in Q4, that was the exact same time to rotate. So there are significant bargains elsewhere.

十、AI 启用层与企业级 agentic 框架的机会

And you haven't seen the AI enablement layer really activate or get rewarded yet, right? You're going to need companies like the Accentures and these consulting companies to deploy their professional services arms to just integrate and re-transform workflows, do the process mapping. AI can't do that.

You have to have highly regulated governance on these AIs — the AIs need policy controls. And I just saw recently Microsoft rolled out their AI agentic solution. So they're coming after Claude, right? Claude is in the pole position. They leaped OpenAI. Microsoft has all the IP from OpenAI, and they just rolled out an agentic AI framework that has what enterprises need:

  • They can lock down an agent.
  • They can permission it.
  • They can have a policy around it.
  • The AI agent might or might not have certain data — they can revoke it at the enterprise level.

That's actually how enterprises operate.

十一、历史镜像：Netscape → IE 的剧本会重演

If you look back to the late '90s, first there was Netscape Navigator. We had that moment with the launch of ChatGPT a few years ago. Then Microsoft showed up with the browser a few years later.

The big guys always show up later. But they don't stop, and it doesn't matter if their form factor is late or it's not as effective. They know their customer well and they're integrated well. So that's an example of those opportunities that are still out there where I think they're still attractively priced.

十二、节目结尾与赞助内容

If you liked this segment, please like, subscribe, and tune in every Monday at 4:30 p.m. Eastern time. I'm Austin Campbell, the host of Bits and Bips, along with my friends Ram Ahluwalia and Chris Perkins and our slate of exceptional guests. Every week we're going to discuss macro, crypto, and the collision of worlds, covering topics that move markets and shape the financial landscape.

[music]

Coinbase 赞助内容：

Who in the group chat has the absolute worst sports takes? You guys, it's got to be me. I don't watch sports unless it's a global event, but Coinbase is giving me a chance to out-predict a pro basketball coach for a share of five Bitcoin. And honestly, I'm built for this. I have beginner's luck, energy, and absolutely nothing to lose.

Coinbase is bringing in pro basketball coach Lethal Shooter to see if you can actually out-predict a pro for a share of five Bitcoin. Get more correct than Lethal Shooter, split five Bitcoin, and get a chance at a private coaching session in LA to perfect your jump shot.

Coinbase One is the ultimate membership to make the most of your money and has been amazing for me:

  • Zero trading fees on thousands of crypto assets
  • 3.5% APY on USDC
  • Boosted staking and lending rewards
  • Up to 4% Bitcoin back with the Coinbase One Card

If you trade crypto regularly, the basic annual membership can pay for itself. Plus, the $1 million streak prize pool is still live. You can also get 20% off the first year of Coinbase One annual plans and a $50 Bitcoin bonus when you spend $100 on a new Coinbase One Card in the first 30 days through May 31st.

Make your predictions and split five Bitcoin. Get started at coinbase.com/unchained. That's coinbase.com/unchained. Join today at coinbase.com/unchained to start your predictions.

## 一、最大的反常识：这次"泡沫"长在了私募市场，而不是公开市场
大多数人盯着英伟达、Micron 的 K 线惊呼"2000 年又来了"，但视频里 Ram 抛出的真正颠覆性观察是——**63 家 AI 独角兽实验室，零美元收入**；而公开市场的买家其实在用"远期市盈率 8 倍的 Micron"做风控。这意味着传统的"散户疯狂—机构理性"叙事被彻底反转：**这一轮是私募 LP 在为公共物品（基础模型）做慈善式补贴，公开市场反而成了纪律的最后堡垒**。
